sections.title: FCA Principles for Businesses
sections.content:
Following implementation of the ‘Consumer Duty’ there are 12 FCA Principles for Businesses, which in substance express the main characteristics of the “Fit and Proper” standard set for firms. The Principles are

also designed as a general statement of the regulatory requirements in new or unforeseen situations and in situations in which there is no guidance.

1. Integrity A firm must conduct its business with integrity.

2. Skill, Care and A firm must conduct its business with due skill, care and Diligence diligence.

3. Management and A firm must take reasonable care to organise and control Control its affairs responsibly and effectively, with adequate risk management systems. 4. Financial Prudence A firm must maintain adequate financial resources.

5. Market Conduct A firm must observe proper standards of market conduct.

6. Customers’ Interests A firm must pay due regard to the interests of its Customers and treat them fairly. 7. Communications A firm must pay due regard to the information needs of with Clients its clients and communicate information to them in a way which is clear, fair and not misleading. 8. Conflicts of Interest A firm must manage conflicts of interest fairly, both between itself and its Customers and between a Customer and another client. 9. Customers: A firm must take reasonable care to ensure the suitability Relationship of Trust of its advice and discretionary decisions for any Customer who is entitled to rely upon its judgement. 10. Clients’ Assets A firm must arrange adequate protection for clients’ assets when it is responsible for them. 11. Relationship with A firm must deal with its regulators in an open and Regulators cooperative way and must disclose to the FCA appropriately anything relating to the firm of which the FCA would reasonably expect notice. 12. Consumer Duty A firm must act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers.

All persons must be familiar with the FCA Principles for Businesses (see Section 1.3) and, where relevant, the Code of Conduct Rules (see Section below).

These Principles are to be adhered to at all times, both in letter and in spirit. This imposes an additional responsibility beyond the requirement to comply with the procedures set out in this Manual. It will never be adequate to defend conduct that fails to conform to the Principles simply by maintaining that that conduct satisfied the prescribed procedures - compliance with both at all times is mandatory.

You must report to the Compliance Officer if:

1. You suspect that you, or someone in the Firm, has breached one of the Principles; 2. You consider that any of the Firm’s procedures are inconsistent with the Principles; or 3. You are asked, directly or indirectly, to act in a way that you consider breaches any Principle.

Breaches of the Principles can lead to disciplinary action by the FCA against the Firm and damage to the Firm’s reputation. Every individual is responsible for taking all reasonable steps to prevent any of these situations arising by strict adherence to the Principles at all times. Failure to do so will be treated as a breach of your employment contract and may lead to your dismissal.

sections.title: Treating Customers Fairly (“TCF”)
sections.content:
The Firm is obliged to treat all of its customers fairly, in accordance with Principle 6. It is the responsibility of the Firm’s senior management to ensure that this is being done. Senior management must ensure that a TCF culture is embedded throughout the Firm. The Firm acknowledges that it is the responsibility of everyone in the Firm to comply with TCF. See also ‘Consumer Duty’ below.

The Firm has a policy in regard to TCF. This policy can be found at Appendix D.

sections.title: The Consumer Duty
sections.content:
Principle 12 (see ‘FCA Principles for business’ above) is ‘Consumer Duty’.

The Consumer Duty (‘Duty’), is an FCA initiative which essentially builds upon TCF (see above). However, unlike TCF, the Duty is focussed on ‘retail customers’ with the requirement to ensure the delivery of the four ‘customer outcomes’ (as defined in PRIN 2A):

 the governance of products and services (PRIN 2A.3)  price and value (PRIN 2A.4)  consumer understanding (PRIN 2A.5)  consumer support (PRIN 2A.6)

It is important to note that the Duty applies to all firms that have a ‘material influence’ on these customer outcomes in what is referred to as the ‘distribution chain’. The latter captures all firms with ‘material influence’ that are involved in the manufacture, provision, sale and ongoing administration and management of a product or service to the end retail customer. It will therefore be appreciated that the Duty can apply to firms that do not have any retail clients by virtue of the fact that they are in a ‘distribution chain’ which has a retail end user e.g. a firm that does not have any direct retail clients that designs a product or a service for retail customers would be subject to the Duty.

The Duty will not apply if a product meets the definition of a ‘non-retail financial instrument’ or in respect of activities related to products or services that do not qualify as ‘retail market business’. The definitions of these terms can be found in the FCA Handbook.

The FCA has produced Guidance (FG22/5) to assist firms in understanding the application or otherwise of the Duty to them including what might constitute a ‘material influence’. The Consumer Duty also links in to Rule 6 of the ‘Code of Conduct’ (see ‘Code of Conduct’ below) which is “You must act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers”.

Alamut has considered its business model and has determined that the Consumer Duty is/is not applicable to the firm.

sections.title: The SEC Fiduciary Duty requirement
sections.content:
As an ERA, the Firm and its employees have a fiduciary duty and other obligations to the Firm’s clients. This means that the Firm must, among other things:  Render disinterested and impartial advice;  Make suitable investment decisions (or recommendations) for clients in light of their investment objectives and restrictions;  Exercise a high degree of care to ensure that adequate and accurate representations and other information about investments are made to and selected for clients;  Have an adequate basis in fact for any and all recommendations, representations, and forecasts;  Refrain from actions or transactions that conflict with the interests of any client, unless the conflict has first been disclosed to the client and the client has (or may be considered to have) waived the conflict; and  Treat all clients fairly and equitably.

A breach of any of these duties or obligations may, depending on the circumstances, expose the Firm, its supervisory personnel, and any employee involved in the breach to disciplinary actions by the SEC and/or US state securities authorities, and to potential criminal and civil liability, as well as subject the employee to the Firm’s sanctions up to and including termination of employment.

For example, a company that violates the Advisers Act may, upon conviction, be fined up to $500,000 for each violation, or the gross amount of any gain. An employee or agent of the company convicted of a violation of the Advisers Act may, upon conviction, be fined up to $100,000 for each violation or the gross amount of any gain, or imprisoned for not more than five years, or both.

This fiduciary duty adheres to the same principles as the FCA principles set out in Section 1.3.

sections.title: The Compliance Officer
sections.content:
Thomas Alcock is the Firm’s Compliance Officer. Should you have any queries or concerns in respect of regulatory or compliance matters, you should consult the Compliance Officer.

The Compliance Officer is responsible for ensuring that this Manual is kept up to date and abreast of any new or amended Rules or Regulations.

Should you be in any doubt as to the application of any Rules, whether these have been imposed by the Firm or directly by the FCA or the SEC, you should consult the Compliance Officer immediately. The Firm will treat any instances of non-compliance seriously and these may ultimately lead to the dismissal of members of staff.

sections.title: FCA Authorisation and Approval
sections.content:
The Threshold Conditions Before a regulated activity may be carried on by a firm the FCA must be satisfied that it can meet, and continue to meet, certain minimum standards, known as threshold conditions, set out at COND 2. Failure to meet these minimum conditions on an ongoing basis may result in the Firm losing its permission to conduct regulated activities.

 Location of offices The Firm must maintain its head office (and registered office, if relevant) in the UK.  Effective The Firm must be capable of being effectively supervised by supervision the FCA. In particular having regards to its close links to another entity/person.  Appropriate The Firm’s financial and non-financial resources must be resources appropriate in relation to the regulated activities it carries on.  Suitability The Firm must be fit and proper having regard to all the circumstances.  Business model The Firm’s business model, or strategy for doing business must be suitable for such a firm carrying on such regulated activities.

Scope of permission The FCA’s Scope of Permission Notice (“SOPN”) sets out the regulated activities that the Firm is authorised to undertake, any limitations on such activities and also the types of Client for whom the Firm may act (in Alamut’s case, it is Professional Clients and Eligible Counterparties). If you are uncertain as to whether you are permitted to carry out a particular activity it is essential that you consult the Compliance Officer prior to carrying out that activity.

The Firm has been authorised as a MIFIDPRU investment firm, with the following Scope of Permission:

Activity Example Advising on investments (except on Providing investment advice to a Client or Pension Transfers and Pension Opt potential investor on the merits of buying, Outs) selling, and subscribing to a particular investment. Agreeing to carry on a regulated Agreeing to carry on the activity specified in the activity Part 4A Permission. Arranging (bringing about) deals in Making arrangements for another person to investments buy, sell or subscribe for a particular investment that would have the direct effect that a particular transaction is concluded. Dealing in investments as agent Buying, selling or subscribing for an insurance policy as agent. Making arrangements with a view to Putting in place ongoing arrangements to transactions in investments facilitate the entering into of transactions. Unlike ‘arranging (bringing about) deals in investments’ (see above) there is no requirement for any transaction to actually take place. This activity is very broad in scope and aside from internal procedures can range from e.g. introducer arrangements to assisting an investor complete necessary documentation. Managing investments Managing a securities portfolio on a discretionary basis on behalf of a client. Managing an unauthorised alternative Managing an unauthorised alternative investment fund investment fund on behalf of a client. Arranging safeguarding and Arranging on a continuing basis for others to administration of assets actually carry out the safeguarding and administering of a client’s assets.

The Firm is able to undertake these activities in relation to the following specified investments:

Security 1. Share 2. Debenture 3. Government and public security 4. Unit 5. Warrant 6. Certificates representing certain security 7. Rights to or interests in investments (Security)

Contractually Based Investments 1. Option (excluding a commodity option and an option on a commodity future) 2. Future (excluding commodity Future) 3. Contract for difference 4. Rights to or interests in investments (Contractually Based Investments) 5. Rolling spot forex contract

The Firm is not permitted to:

1. Deal as principal; 2. Safeguarding and administration of assets (without arranging).

Senior Managers and Certification Regime (“SMCR”)

The SMCR, which is applicable to the Firm, is essentially divided into two regimes:

 Senior Managers regime (persons performing such roles will need FCA approval)  Certification regime (persons performing such functions will not need FCA approval) The application of the SMCR is dependent on whether a firm is categorised as a ‘Limited Scope firm’, a ‘Core Firm’ or an ‘Enhanced Firm’ – Alamut is categorised as a ’Core Firm’.

Senior Managers Regime A ‘Senior Manager’ under the SMCR is a person holding a ’Senior Management Function’ (“SMF”). As mentioned above, such persons require FCA approval before performing such roles.

The SMFs relevant to the Firm are as follows:

SMF27 Partner SMF16 Compliance Oversight SMF17 MLRO SMF1 CEO

Every person performing an SMF:

 will be provided with an agreed Statement of Responsibility which will set out their role and responsibilities;  will have a duty of responsibility, the effect of which means that if the Firm breaches a requirement the Senior Manager responsible for that area could be held accountable if they did not take ‘reasonable steps’ (see ‘Code of Conduct’ below) to prevent or stop the breach - although the burden of proof rests with the FCA

In addition to the above, prescribed responsibilities have been identified by the FCA and which must be allocated to Senior Managers (although, unlike a Statement of Responsibility, not every Senior Manager will necessarily have a prescribed responsibility allocated to them). For a ‘Core Firm’ such as Alamut there are a total of six potential prescribed responsibilities, although not all will be relevant to every firm. SMFs are also subject to an annual assessment by the Firm – see ‘Fitness and Propriety’ below.

Certification Regime The Certification regime captures those individuals who are not Senior Managers but whose roles could have a significant impact upon customers, the Firm or market integrity (‘FCA certification functions’). The regime reinforces the concept that it is the responsibility of firms, rather than the FCA, to ensure that staff are fit and proper. For this reason these individuals will no longer be ‘approved’ by the FCA but instead will need to be ‘certified’ by the Firm before assuming such roles and subsequently at least once a year by the Firm that they are suitable to continue to perform their role. As the name suggests, such staff are provided with a certificate by the Firm in line with SYSC 27.2.9. See ‘Fitness and Propriety’ below.

There are eight FCA certification functions although, again, not every one of those will be relevant to every firm.

The FCA certification functions relevant to the firm are as follows:

 Client-dealing  Material risk takers  Managers of certification employees

Not every non-Senior Manager role within the Firm will necessarily fall within the Certification regime e.g. the client-dealing FCA certification function will not apply to purely administrative roles even though they might involve customer contact nor to some persons whose role does not require a significant amount of discretion, judgement or technical skill. See SYSC 27.8.22A & 22B for further information.

Although they are two different regimes, it is feasible that a person that is a Senior Manager could also be subject to the Certification regime where the latter role is ‘very different’ to what they are undertaking as a Senior Manager e.g. an SMF27 (Partner)/SMF3 (Executive Director) whose role meets the definition of the client-dealing function. On the other hand, an SMF27/SMF3 would not necessarily fall under the Certification regime for being a ‘material risk taker’ (a certification function) as that would likely be deemed to be part and parcel of their SMF role.

Code of Conduct The ‘Code of Conduct’ (“COCON”) applies to:

 persons subject to the SMCR e.g. Senior Management or Certification Functions;  most employees that fall outside the SMCR.

The only employees not subject to COCON would be e.g. post room staff, receptionists etc. Reference should be made to the table in COCON 1.1.2 for further information.

The Conduct rules that apply to all staff subject to COCON are as follows:

Rule 1: You must act with integrity Rule 2: You must act with due skill, care and diligence Rule 3: You must be open and cooperative with the FCA, the PRA and other regulators Rule 4: You must pay due regard to the interests of customers and treat them fairly Rule 5: You must observe proper standards of market conduct Rule 6: You must act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers.

As well as the above, Senior Managers are also subject to:

SC1: You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible is controlled effectively SC2: You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible complies with the relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system SC3: You must take reasonable steps to ensure that any delegation of your responsibilities is to an appropriate person and that you oversee the discharge of the delegated responsibility effectively SC4: You must disclose appropriately any information of which the FCA or PRA would reasonably expect notice

COCON 4.2 provides specific guidance on senior management conduct, including the ‘reasonable steps’ aspects of SC1-SC3. In addition, DEPP 6.2.9-E provides further useful guidance on aspects the FCA will take into account.

Regulatory references Before a person can be appointed to undertake a Senior Management Function or before a certificate can be issued to a person performing a certification function then the firm is required to ‘take reasonable steps’ to obtain appropriate references their current employer and anyone who has been their employer in the past six years. However, this does not necessarily mean that a new reference is required whenever a certificate is renewed (SYSC 22.7.5). There is a similar obligation on firms to provide such references relating to the above circumstances as soon as is reasonably practical.

See SYSC 22 for further details – SYSC 22 Annex 1 provides a template for regulatory references to be provided by firms.

Fitness and Propriety FIT is the part of the FCA Handbook that describes the criteria the Firm should consider when assessing the fitness and propriety of persons (see table in FIT 1.1.4):

 before undertaking Senior Management Functions and Certification Functions;  in continuing to perform those functions.

The Fit and Proper criteria (see FIT 2.1 to 2.3 for further information) relate to:

1. Honesty, integrity and reputation; 2. Competence and capability; and 3. Financial soundness.

The above is guidance which will be applied by the FCA when assessing a person’s fitness and propriety which will be relevant to applications to the FCA for approvals in respect of Senior Management Functions.

However FIT 1.3.3 advises that ‘substantially the same criteria’ should be used by the Firm when assessing the fitness and propriety of staff undertaking Certification functions and of candidates for Senior Management Functions before application for approval is made to the FCA.

Approved Persons Although the Firm is subject to the Senior Managers and Certification Regime, the Firm’s Appointed Representatives are subject to the separate Approved Persons Regime. ‘Approved Persons’ need to be ‘approved’ by the FCA before they can carry out ‘Controlled Functions’.

There are 7 Controlled Functions, although not all of them are relevant to this firm’s Appointed Representatives.

The relevant Controlled Functions are as follows:

FCA Governing Functions CF3(A) Chief Executive function CF4(A) Partner function

Customer Function CF30(A) Customer function

All Approved Persons are required to adhere to the FCA Rules set out in the FCA Handbook, particularly those pertaining to fitness and propriety (see above). For the avoidance of doubt, COCON does not apply to Appointed Representatives.

The Statements of Principle and Code of Practice for Approved Persons (“APER”) To assist Approved Persons to fulfil their obligations, the FCA issued the Code of Practice for Approved Persons. APER sets out seven basic Principles (‘Statements of Principle’) – not to be confused with the Principles for Business set out earlier in this chapter.

1. An approved person must act with integrity in carrying out his accountable functions. 2. An approved person must act with due skill, care and diligence in carrying out his accountable functions. 3. An approved person must observe proper standards of market conduct in carrying out his accountable functions. 4. An approved person must deal with the FCA, the PRA and with other regulators in an open and co- operative way and must disclose appropriately any information of which the FCA or the PRA would reasonably expect notice.

5. An approved person performing an accountable higher management function must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which they are responsible in their accountable function is organised so it can be controlled effectively. 6. An approved person performing an accountable higher management function must exercise due skill, care and diligence in managing the business of the firm for which they are responsible in their accountable function. 7. An approved person performing an accountable higher management function must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which they are responsible in their accountable function complies with the relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system.

An ‘accountable function’ not only includes the FCA ‘controlled functions’ but is extended to include any other functions in relation to the carrying on of a regulated activity in relation to which that person is an approved person.

Principles 1 to 4 above apply to all approved persons. In addition, Principles 5 to 7 apply to those persons performing an ‘accountable higher management function’. An ‘accountable higher management function’ is any FCA controlled function that is a significant-influence function i.e. excluding the CF30 ‘customer- dealing function.

It is imperative that you are familiar with these Principles if you are an FCA Approved Person, as contravening them may result in disciplinary action against you personally. If the FCA considers an Approved Person’s misconduct to be sufficiently serious it can also ask for their “Approval” to be withdrawn and they would no longer be eligible to work in that Controlled Function.

sections.title: FCA Supervision
sections.content:
The FCA adopts a proportionate approach in its supervision of firms and as such, except for firms with the greatest potential impact on consumers and markets, individual firms will not have dedicated FCA supervisory teams (as is the case for this firm). Instead firms are supervised on a ‘portfolio basis’ under which firms that share a common business model are supervised as ‘members’ of a particular ‘portfolio’. These portfolios include e.g. an ‘alternatives portfolio’ (being firms that predominately manage alternative investment vehicles such as hedge funds or private equity funds) and an ‘asset management portfolio’ (which is comprised of FCA authorised firms that predominantly directly manage mainstream investment vehicles, or advise on mainstream investments) - the FCA recognises that certain issues will be common to more than one particular ‘portfolio’ as a result of business models overlapping.

Although a ‘portfolio firm’ will not have their own dedicated FCA supervisor, portfolios of firms will have a designated portfolio lead supervisor who has oversight of the firms in a particular portfolio and will communicate with firms in their portfolio on relevant issues e.g. by way of a ‘Dear CEO’ letter setting out the FCA’s key concerns and expectations for firms in the portfolio.

sections.title: Brexit and EU Directives & EU Regulations
sections.content:
Before the end of the Brexit transitional period (31 December 2020) the UK was directly subject to EU Regulations e.g. MiFIR, EMIR, Market Abuse etc. Generally these Regulations ceased to apply to UK firms after the end of this transitional period. However to ease the burden on firms, the effect of the UK EU Withdrawal Act is that these EU Regulations became UK law (‘retained law’). Necessary adjustments (‘onshoring’) to this retained law have been made e.g. by way of a UK Statutory Instrument (“SI”) to reflect the fact that ESMA no longer hold power of direction over the UK ensures that the retained law works in a UK-only context.

In contrast, EU Directives are not binding on any Member State but rather required each Member State to transpose a Directive’s requirements into local law. For this reason there always has been e.g. a UK MiFID, a UK AIFMD etc. albeit that the convention was to simply refer to ‘MiFID’, ‘AIFMD’ etc.

The intention of the UK was that the end of the Brexit transitional period should not result in any substantive changes to any EU Directives or Regulations that UK firms had become used to.

The UK approach means that to understand the requirements of the UK version of an EU Regulation one needs to read together both the original EU Regulation and the UK adjustments (the above mentioned ‘onshoring’).

To avoid any confusion, unless stated otherwise, any references to an EU based Directive in this Manual e.g. MiFID II (2014/65), AIFMD (2011/61) etc. should be read as meaning the UK equivalent of that Directive. Similarly, any reference to an EU Regulation e.g. Market Abuse should be read as meaning the UK equivalent of that Regulation.

Although the above sets out the impact of EU Regulations on UK firms, be aware that some EU Regulations are extra territorial in that they always apply to non-EU firms. By way of example the EU Market Abuse regime captures financial instruments traded on an EU regulated market and does not simply only apply to EU firms.

Note that any EU supporting ‘Technical Standards’ still remain, albeit amended to make them UK specific where necessary. In addition ESMA Q&As and Guidelines remain relevant, although the FCA has explained that they will need to be read in the context of Brexit.

chapters.title: MARKET ABUSE
chapters.summary: 2. MARKET ABUSE
chapters.content:
2. MARKET ABUSE

## 2.1 Introduction

The Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls Sourcebook (“SYSC”) 6.1.1 requires the Firm to implement and maintain adequate policies and procedures for countering the risk that the Firm might be used to further Financial Crime. Financial Crime is defined in the Handbook to consist of:

 Fraud or dishonesty; or  Misconduct in, or misuse of information relating to, a financial market; or  Handling the proceeds of crime; or  The financing of terrorism

This chapter concerns itself specifically with ‘market abuse’. Please see the following chapter for ‘Financial Crime’.

## 2.2 What is Market Abuse?

The market abuse regime is concerned with preventing and deterring individuals from acting in an unscrupulous way towards the markets. The regime applies to all individuals.

An individual found guilty of committing market abuse can face a range of sanctions including unlimited financial penalties, public censure and imprisonment.

It is important to note that market abuse consists of both civil and criminal offences. This chapter looks at both in turn.

## 2.3 The Market Abuse Regime

The UK’s market abuse regime was revised as a result of implementation of the EU Market Abuse Regulation 596/2014 (“EU MAR”) in July 2016. Although, following Brexit, the Regulation is no longer binding on the UK it has been converted into ‘UK retained law’ in line with SI 2019/310 (see section 1.8 of Chapter 1 of this Manual). As such, all relevant persons are expected to comply with the Regulation as amended by SI 2019/310 (combined - ‘UK MAR’).

UK MAR establishes a common framework on market abuse addressing:

 insider dealing;  the unlawful disclosure of inside information; and  market manipulation.

In the Handbook, ‘market abuse’ is addressed in MAR. However, in keeping with its previous approach to EU Regulations, the FCA does not copy out the regulations but rather ‘signposts’ readers to the appropriate parts of UK MAR. In addition, MAR provides various examples of ‘behaviours’ that e.g. might amount to ‘insider dealing’ (MAR 1.3.20 onwards) as well as ‘factors’ to be taken into account when e.g. determining whether a ‘behaviour’ amounts to ‘unlawful disclosure’ (MAR 1.4.5).

UK MAR does not require the person engaging in the behaviour in question to have intended to commit market abuse.

In addition to MAR, chapter 8 of the Financial Crime Guide (“FCG”) within the Handbook concerns itself with ‘Insider dealing and market manipulation’ and provides self-assessment questions for firms covering areas such as ‘Governance’ and ‘Risk assessment’.

## 2.4 Scope

UK MAR applies to financial instruments (as specified in Section C of Annex I of MiFID II – Directive 2014/65):

admitted to trading (or for which such a request has been made) on:

 a UK or EU regulated market;  a UK or EU MTF;

or

 traded on a UK or EU OTF.

It also applies to financial instruments not covered by the above but whose price or value depends on, or has an effect on, the price or value of such instruments above (including, but not limited to, credit default swaps and contracts for difference).

UK MAR also applies to behaviour or transactions, including bids, relating to the auctioning (on an auction platform authorised as a regulated market) of emission allowances (or other auctioned products based thereon) including when auctioned products are not financial instruments.

The prohibition of market manipulation (Article 12 and 15) also applies to:

i. spot commodity contracts, which are not wholesale energy products (as defined in Regulation EU/1227/2011), where the transaction, order or behaviour has, or is likely or intended to have, an effect on the price or value of a financial instrument; ii. types of financial instruments, including derivative contracts or derivative instruments for the transfer of credit risk, where the transaction, order or behaviour has, or is likely to have, an effect on the price or value of a spot commodity contract where the price or value depends upon the price or value of those financial instruments; and iii. behaviour in relation to benchmarks.

EU MAR applies to any transaction, order or behaviour concerning any financial instrument referred to above, regardless of whether or not the activity takes place on a ‘trading venue’ (being a regulated market or a MTF or an OTF).

The prohibitions (e.g. Article 15 on market manipulation) and requirements (e.g. Article 17 on public disclosure) apply to actions and omissions, in the European Union and in a third country.

Although UK MAR captures financial instruments that are traded on both UK and EU markets, as mentioned in section 1.8 in Chapter 1 of this Manual, EU MAR is extra territorial i.e. the EU Market Abuse regime captures financial instruments traded on an EU regulated market and does not simply only apply to EU firms.

## 2.5 Insider Dealing

Insider dealing arises where a person possesses inside information and uses that information by acquiring or disposing of (whether for own account or that of a third party and whether directly or indirectly) financial instruments to which that information relates. Insider dealing also applies to instances where inside information is used to cancel or amend an order that was placed before the person concerned possessed the inside information. The definition extends to bids in relation to auctions of emission allowances or other auctioned products based thereon.

The use of a recommendation to trade etc. also amounts to insider dealing where the person using the recommendation knows or ought to know that it is based on inside information.

Inside information Inside information is information of a precise nature, which has not been made public, relating (directly or indirectly) to one or more issuers or to one or more financial instruments and which, if it were made public, would be likely to have a significant effect on the prices of those instruments or related derivative

instruments. Where commodity derivatives are involved the ‘significant effect’ extends to the related spot commodity contract. The definition also includes emission allowances and auctioned products.

Unlawful Disclosure of Inside Information This will arise where a person possesses inside information and discloses that information to any other person except where the disclosure is made in the normal exercise on an employment, a profession or duties e.g. a market sounding made by a disclosing participant (see below). The onward disclosure of recommendations (see ‘Insider Dealing’ above) also amounts to ‘unlawful disclosure’ where the person disclosing the recommendation knows or ought to know that it was based on inside information.

## 2.6 Market Manipulation

This will capture a wide variety of actions including the placing of orders etc. (or disseminating misleading information) to give a false impression of the supply or, or the demand for, a financial instrument or related spot commodity contract. The prohibition against market manipulation does not apply when carried out for a legitimate reason and conforms with accepted market practice (see MAR 1 Annex 2).

Legitimate behaviour For the purposes of insider dealing and unlawful disclosure of inside information EU MAR provides that it shall not be deemed that because a legal person is in possession of inside information that that person has used that information to engage in inside dealing where that legal person:

 has established and maintained adequate systems and controls to ensure that the natural person who made the trading decision (or another natural person who may have had an influence on that decision) was not in possession of inside information; and  has not encouraged, influenced etc. the natural person who undertook the transaction.

There are additional ‘legitimate behaviour’ provisions for persons:

 in possession of inside information when acting as a market maker or counterparty or where they execute orders on behalf of third parties in the normal course of day-to-day business or where a public takeover or merger is concerned;  conducting transactions in discharge of an obligation that has become due (provided not being for the purposes of circumventing the insider dealing prohibition) where the obligation results from an order or an agreement concluded (or where a legal or regulatory obligation) arose before the person possessed inside information.

Market abuse ‘behaviours’ There are various behaviours that may indicate market abuse under the market abuse regime. It is important to note that an individual does not have to intend to commit market abuse, in order to engage one of the behaviours; it is possible to unwittingly fall foul of the regime.

Please see Appendix E for examples.

The examples should be read in the light of:

 the elements specified by MAR as making up the relevant type of market abuse;  any relevant descriptions specified by MAR which do not amount to market abuse;  any MAR-related provisions specified in any Commission legislative text; and  any applicable guidelines made by ESMA.

Exceptions The prohibition on market abuse does not apply to trading in own shares in buy-back programmes or trading in securities or associated instruments for the stabilisation of securities where the conditions in MAR Article 5 are met.

Behaviour conforming with certain rules in the Takeover Code are ‘unlikely’ to amount to market abuse - please refer to MAR 1.10.3 onwards for further information.

## 2.7 Holding Inside Information

In general, no staff member in possession of inside information may be involved in trading, selling, making recommendations, or publishing research with respect to affected financial instruments. It may also be necessary to restrict who these persons participate with. As a general matter, staff should seek to avoid unnecessarily coming into possession of information that will result in limitations being imposed on their activities. Research analysts in particular should be sensitive to the possibility that they may be prohibited in using or disseminating inside information where it has been disclosed selectively rather than to a large group of industry analysts or by press release which may provide an unfair trading information advantage. Selective disclosure should be distinguished from normal situations of corporate disclosures.

## 2.8 Criminal Offences

Although the ‘market abuse regime’ is a civil regime, the UK also has a criminal regime in respect of ‘insider dealing’ by virtue of the Criminal Justice Act 1993 (“CJA2) s52 (where the acquisition or disposal in question occurs on a regulated market or that the person dealing relies on a professional intermediary or is himself acting as a professional intermediary). In appropriate circumstances the FCA may decide that behaviour relating to insider dealing is serious enough to justify a criminal prosecution. An offence under the CJA is punishable by up to seven years imprisonment or a fine or both.

CJA s53 provides certain defences against a charge of insider dealing including, but not limited to, that:

 he would have done what he did even if he had not had the information;  he did not expect the dealing to result in the making of a profit or the avoidance of a loss; or he believed on reasonable grounds that the information had been disclosed widely enough to ensure that none of those taking part in the dealing would be prejudiced by not having the information.

## 2.9 Reporting Suspicious Transactions or Orders (“STOR”)

MAR requires both market operators and firms operating a trading venue to establish and maintain effective arrangements, systems and procedures aimed at preventing and detecting insider dealing, market manipulation or any attempt thereof. Any such orders and transactions, or cancellation or modification thereof that could constitute market abuse, or attempted market abuse, as above must be reported to the FCA ‘without delay’.

MAR requires persons ‘professionally arranging or executing transactions’ to establish and maintain effective arrangements, systems and procedures to detect and report suspicious orders and transactions. Where there is reasonable suspicion that an order or transaction in any financial instrument (whether placed or executed on or outside a trading venue) could constitute market abuse, or attempted market abuse, as above then this must be reported to the FCA ‘without delay’.

SUP 15 Annex 5 provides indications of possible suspicious transactions or orders.

STOR notifications to the FCA must be made via Connect.

## 2.10 Handling Inside Information

As mentioned above, any improper disclosure of inside information amounts to market abuse. This is so regardless of whether a person actually intends to encourage the person to whom they are disclosing the information to deal.

This reinforces the importance of maintaining robust controls over the persons with whom inside information may be shared within an organisation. The details of controls and procedures are provided in the ‘Market Conduct and Rumours’ Policy.

Nevertheless, Alamut maintains Information Barriers, Restricted Lists, and other operational measures to limit market abuse. The risk of market abuse arising is covered in the Market Abuse Risk Assessment, and the efficiency of Alamut’s systems and controls to combat market abuse are tested on a regular basis as part of the Compliance Monitoring Programme. Nevertheless, only those persons who need to have access to sensitive data to perform their function are given inside information. Further, ‘strategic leaks’ or rumours are unacceptable and will not be tolerated, even where they appear to be in the Firm's interest.

Relevant Persons have primary responsibility for ensuring the confidentiality of inside information but may be justified in disclosing inside information to certain categories of recipient (in addition to those of its own employees who require the information to perform their functions) providing those persons owe a duty of confidentiality. Mere reliance on confidentiality undertakings without more may, however, no longer be sufficient. Alamut requires that parties to whom inside information is disclosed have robust controls to prevent leakage.

## 2.11 What to do if actual or possible Market Abuse has occurred

To protect the interests of Alamut, all persons are required to cooperate in:

 Securing any relevant evidence, including tapes of telephone and electronic communications, e-mails, etc.;  Informing relevant regulators of the situation and the steps the Firm is taking to investigate — and coordinating the Firm's response to different regulators and exchanges;  Ensuring a thorough, speedy and independent investigation of the facts is undertaken;  Whether action needs to be taken to suspend members of staff while matters are investigated (subject to advice on the Firm's employment law obligations);  Whether individuals involved should be advised of the need for separate legal representation; and  Managing internal and external communications about the issue.  Where market abuse is suspected or discovered, senior management should self-report the incident to the FCA immediately. The FCA, particularly where it suspects criminal wrongdoing, will wish to coordinate any investigation with the Firm and be the first to interview potential witnesses and offenders.

## 2.12 Market Rumours Policy

Market rumour cases generally involve two of the market abuse behaviours (as set out above under the Market Abuse Behaviours Section). The first involves disseminating false or misleading information (MAR 1.8). The second involves creating a misleading impression or market distortion (MAR 1.9). See Appendix F for our Market Rumours’ Policy.

Definition The FCA provides no definition of rumour” in its Guidelines. Instead it leaves this to firms and states that the policy should provide examples. For the sake of this policy, a rumour is classified as information from an unverified information source, such as from internet bulletin boards. A rumour would be unverified where the information was not verified by an appropriate official of the company concerned.

Creation and passing of Rumours On no account should staff create any rumours.

Although Alamut actively discourages passing on rumours, significant disclaimers should be used if this is unavoidable including:

 The origin of the information;  The information is clearly stated to be a rumour;  No additional credence or embellishment is given to the rumour; and  The information is clearly stated to be unsubstantiated/not verified.

This means avoiding sensational or exaggerated language and checking factual statements carefully before issue. This applies equally to formal written communications and to communications issued via Bloomberg, instant messages, emails or chat rooms.

An individual should be cautious that a rumour is not inside information before deciding whether to pass on the information, and where necessary check with the Compliance Officer.

Trading on Market Rumours The Firm is allowed to trade on market rumours. However, individuals at the Firm who are in receipt of a market rumour should consider the source of the rumour and whether it could constitute market abuse to trade on the rumour.

When considering this, individuals should also confirm whether the information is in the public domain and therefore available for general view and thus trading would not be an offence, or whether the information is specific or precise, but not generally available, thus potentially being either “inside” or “relevant information not generally available”, which may be considered an offence.

## 2.13 Principles for Business

In addition to the market abuse regime, the FCA’s Principles for Businesses are also applicable when considering issues around market abuse. For example, Principle 5 provides that a firm must observe proper standards of market conduct, whilst Principle 1 requires a firm to conduct its business with integrity.

## 2.14 Issuers and the Market Abuse Regime

Under MAR an issuer means a legal entity which issues, or proposes to issue, financial instruments (in the case of depositary receipts representing financial instruments the issuer will be the issuer of the financial instrument represented).

References to ‘issuer’ in the following means an issuer for whose financial instruments:

 it has requested or approved admission for trading on a regulated market;  it has requested admission to trading on a MTF; or  it has approved trading of on a MTF or OTF (where only traded on such venues).

Issuers and Public Disclosure An issuer must inform the public ‘as soon as possible’ of inside information which directly concerns the issuer. Such information must be maintained on its website for at least five years (although in the case of issuers whose instrument are traded on a SME growth market – being a MTF so registered under MiFID II - the information may be posted on the trading venue’s website instead where this facility is offered).

Delay in disclosure is permissible only if compliant with relevant provisions in MAR – see Articles 17(4) – 17(7).

Where inside information is disclosed to a third party in the normal course of business, and that third party does not owe a duty of confidentiality, then public disclosure must be simultaneous (intentional disclosure) or prompt (non-intentional disclosure).

Issuers and Insider Lists An issuer, or those acting on their behalf, are required to maintain and to keep up to date a list (‘insider list’) of all persons who have access to inside information or those performing tasks through which they will have access to inside information e.g. advisers, accountants or credit rating agencies.

The list must be retained for at least five years after it is drawn up or updated and be made available to the FCA upon request (‘as soon as possible’).

The obligation does not apply to issuers whose instruments are traded on an SME growth market – being a MTF so registered under MiFID II – provided that it takes reasonable steps to ensure that persons with access to inside information acknowledge the legal and regulatory duties entailed and is aware of the penalties for insider dealing and unlawful disclosure. The issuer must also be able to provide the FCA with an insider list upon request.

Issuers and Manager’s Transactions There are notification requirements in respect of certain transactions effected by a ‘person discharging managerial responsibilities’ (“PDMR”) – or persons closely associated with them - within an issuer (or emission allowance market participant or related auction platform/auctioneer). A PDMR is defined as a person within an issuer (or an emission allowance participant) or any of the other persons referenced in MAR Article 19(10) that is a member of the administrative, management or supervisory body of the entity or a senior executive who has regular access to inside information and power to take managerial decisions affecting the future development and business prospects of the entity.

Disclosure applies to:

 (for issuers) every transaction conducted on their own account relating to the shares or debt instruments of the issuer or to derivatives or other instruments linked thereto;  (in respect of emission allowance market participants) every transaction conducted on their own account relating to emission allowances, to auction products based thereon or to derivatives relating thereto.

The above scope of disclosure, although based upon MAR, is very broad. However, note that Article 10 of Delegated Regulation 2016/522 (see ‘Useful Links’) identifies a broader non-exhaustive list of transactions that should be identified with which all PDMRs should familiarise themselves with.

The notification requirement applies to any subsequent transaction once a total amount of €5,000 has been reached within a calendar year. If so wished, all transactions can be notified on a voluntary basis.

Notification (no later than three business days after the date of the transaction) is to be made to both the issuer (or emission allowance participant) and to the FCA or relevant competent authority if applicable (the latter will be the competent authority of the Member State where the issuer (or emission allowance participant) is registered).

The issuer (or emission allowance participant) must ensure that the notified information is made public ‘promptly’, and no later than three business days after the transaction.

For the purposes of this requirement, ‘transaction’ will also include: pledging or lending of instruments; transactions undertaken by another person on behalf of the PDMR (including under discretion); and certain life insurance policies.

A PDMR must not conduct any transactions on own account during a closed period of 30 calendar days before the announcement of an interim financial report or a year-end report.

In exceptional circumstances trading during a closed period may be permitted on a case-by-case basis e.g. severe financial difficulty. However, any such trading must be in accordance with MAR Article 19(12) and Delegated Regulation 2016/522 (see ‘Useful Links’). In the first instance any PDMR claiming exceptional circumstances must discuss the matter with the Compliance Officer.

See DTR 1, 2 & 3 for further guidance on PDMR notifications and control of inside information.

## 2.15 Market Soundings

Market soundings is the term used to describe the communication of information, prior to the announcement of a transaction, in order to gauge the interest of potential investors in a possible

transaction and the conditions relating to it such as its potential size or pricing to one or more potential investors by:

 an issuer;  a secondary offeror (where the quantity or value makes the transaction distinct from ordinary trading and involves a selling method based on the prior assessment of potential interest from potential investors;  an emission allowance market participant; or  a third party acting on behalf or on the account of any of the above.

Disclosing Market Participant (“DMP”) Unlawful disclosure of inside information is prohibited under MAR (see above). However, a disclosure of inside information made in the course of a market sounding is deemed to be made in the normal exercise of a person’s employment, profession or duties – and hence falls outside the definition of ‘unlawful disclosure of inside information’ - where the DMP complies with the following:

 the person must consider whether the market sounding will involve the disclosure of inside information. A written record of the conclusion (which must be provided to the FCA upon request) must be made and the reason for that conclusion;  consent of the receiving party must be obtained to receive inside information;  the receiving party must be informed that they are prohibited from using that information to trade on own account or the account of a third party or cancel or amend an order which has already been placed;  the receiving party accepts that by agreeing to receive such information that they are obliged to keep that information confidential; and  a record is maintained of all information, including the above points, given and to whom disclosed, as well as the date and time of each disclosure.

A market sounding can be made by any means e.g. email, meetings etc. However, where made by telephone then recorded lines must be used where there is access to such lines and where the recipient has consented to the recording. Only equipment supplied by the DMP must be used.

Before making any form of contact, agreement on the information to be imparted will be agreed and adhered to in respect of all persons receiving that sounding.

Where any information imparted in the course of a market sounding ceases to be inside information, as assessed by the disclosing market participant, then the latter must inform the recipient accordingly as soon as possible and maintain a record of the information given.

For the record, notwithstanding the above, the recipient of a market sounding is required to assess for itself whether it is in possession of inside information or when it ceases to be in possession of inside information.

All such records must be kept for at least five years.

Market Sounding Recipient (“MSR”) The contact point for receiving market soundings in Alamut is the Compliance Officer. After the initial contact it will be the responsibility of the Compliance Officer to notify the DMP whether we would wish to receive future market soundings whether in relation to all potential transactions or particular types of transactions.

Although the DMP will assess whether inside information is involved (and, possibly, when previously imparted information is deemed to no longer be inside information), Tom Alcock is also required to independently assess whether information is, remains or ceases to be, inside information.

For each market sounding received, a list will be drawn up of persons within Alamut that are in possession of the information communicated.

In those situations where our assessment is that we are in possession of inside information we must identify all the issuers and financial instruments to which that inside information relates.

If communication with the DMP was by way of unrecorded meetings or telephone conversations then the DMP is required to keep written minutes or notes. Alamut, as a MSR, should sign these minutes or notes where the contents are agreed. If we do not agree with what has been produced then we must submit our own version to the DMP within five working days after the market sounding.

Alamut has in place documented internal procedures relating to market soundings to:

 ensure information received is internally communicated only through pre-determined reporting lines on a need-to-know basis;  ensure that Alamut’s required assessment of whether or not we are in possession of inside information is undertaken by clearly identified persons and that the relevant staff are properly trained for that purpose;  manage and control the flow of inside information within Alamut (including the training of relevant personnel on the required internal procedures and on the market abuse regime) in compliance with the EU MAR obligations concerning insider dealing and the unlawful disclosure of inside information.

All such records must be kept for a period of five years.

## 2.16 Monitoring

Alamut may undertake monitoring including considering a routine sampling of phone calls, emails, instant messages and Bloomberg messages. This could also include a retrospective review after suspicious price movements and trades.
