sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
The Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls Sourcebook (“SYSC”) 6.1.1 requires the Firm to implement and maintain adequate policies and procedures for countering the risk that the Firm might be used to further Financial Crime. Financial Crime is defined in the Handbook to consist of:

 Fraud or dishonesty; or  Misconduct in, or misuse of information relating to, a financial market; or  Handling the proceeds of crime; or  The financing of terrorism

This chapter concerns itself specifically with ‘market abuse’. Please see the following chapter for ‘Financial Crime’.

sections.title: What is Market Abuse?
sections.content:
The market abuse regime is concerned with preventing and deterring individuals from acting in an unscrupulous way towards the markets. The regime applies to all individuals.

An individual found guilty of committing market abuse can face a range of sanctions including unlimited financial penalties, public censure and imprisonment.

It is important to note that market abuse consists of both civil and criminal offences. This chapter looks at both in turn.

sections.title: The Market Abuse Regime
sections.content:
The UK’s market abuse regime was revised as a result of implementation of the EU Market Abuse Regulation 596/2014 (“EU MAR”) in July 2016. Although, following Brexit, the Regulation is no longer binding on the UK it has been converted into ‘UK retained law’ in line with SI 2019/310 (see section 1.8 of Chapter 1 of this Manual). As such, all relevant persons are expected to comply with the Regulation as amended by SI 2019/310 (combined - ‘UK MAR’).

UK MAR establishes a common framework on market abuse addressing:

 insider dealing;  the unlawful disclosure of inside information; and  market manipulation.

In the Handbook, ‘market abuse’ is addressed in MAR. However, in keeping with its previous approach to EU Regulations, the FCA does not copy out the regulations but rather ‘signposts’ readers to the appropriate parts of UK MAR. In addition, MAR provides various examples of ‘behaviours’ that e.g. might amount to ‘insider dealing’ (MAR 1.3.20 onwards) as well as ‘factors’ to be taken into account when e.g. determining whether a ‘behaviour’ amounts to ‘unlawful disclosure’ (MAR 1.4.5).

UK MAR does not require the person engaging in the behaviour in question to have intended to commit market abuse.

In addition to MAR, chapter 8 of the Financial Crime Guide (“FCG”) within the Handbook concerns itself with ‘Insider dealing and market manipulation’ and provides self-assessment questions for firms covering areas such as ‘Governance’ and ‘Risk assessment’.

sections.title: Scope
sections.content:
UK MAR applies to financial instruments (as specified in Section C of Annex I of MiFID II – Directive 2014/65):

admitted to trading (or for which such a request has been made) on:

 a UK or EU regulated market;  a UK or EU MTF;

or

 traded on a UK or EU OTF.

It also applies to financial instruments not covered by the above but whose price or value depends on, or has an effect on, the price or value of such instruments above (including, but not limited to, credit default swaps and contracts for difference).

UK MAR also applies to behaviour or transactions, including bids, relating to the auctioning (on an auction platform authorised as a regulated market) of emission allowances (or other auctioned products based thereon) including when auctioned products are not financial instruments.

The prohibition of market manipulation (Article 12 and 15) also applies to:

i. spot commodity contracts, which are not wholesale energy products (as defined in Regulation EU/1227/2011), where the transaction, order or behaviour has, or is likely or intended to have, an effect on the price or value of a financial instrument; ii. types of financial instruments, including derivative contracts or derivative instruments for the transfer of credit risk, where the transaction, order or behaviour has, or is likely to have, an effect on the price or value of a spot commodity contract where the price or value depends upon the price or value of those financial instruments; and iii. behaviour in relation to benchmarks.

EU MAR applies to any transaction, order or behaviour concerning any financial instrument referred to above, regardless of whether or not the activity takes place on a ‘trading venue’ (being a regulated market or a MTF or an OTF).

The prohibitions (e.g. Article 15 on market manipulation) and requirements (e.g. Article 17 on public disclosure) apply to actions and omissions, in the European Union and in a third country.

Although UK MAR captures financial instruments that are traded on both UK and EU markets, as mentioned in section 1.8 in Chapter 1 of this Manual, EU MAR is extra territorial i.e. the EU Market Abuse regime captures financial instruments traded on an EU regulated market and does not simply only apply to EU firms.

sections.title: Insider Dealing
sections.content:
Insider dealing arises where a person possesses inside information and uses that information by acquiring or disposing of (whether for own account or that of a third party and whether directly or indirectly) financial instruments to which that information relates. Insider dealing also applies to instances where inside information is used to cancel or amend an order that was placed before the person concerned possessed the inside information. The definition extends to bids in relation to auctions of emission allowances or other auctioned products based thereon.

The use of a recommendation to trade etc. also amounts to insider dealing where the person using the recommendation knows or ought to know that it is based on inside information.

Inside information Inside information is information of a precise nature, which has not been made public, relating (directly or indirectly) to one or more issuers or to one or more financial instruments and which, if it were made public, would be likely to have a significant effect on the prices of those instruments or related derivative

instruments. Where commodity derivatives are involved the ‘significant effect’ extends to the related spot commodity contract. The definition also includes emission allowances and auctioned products.

Unlawful Disclosure of Inside Information This will arise where a person possesses inside information and discloses that information to any other person except where the disclosure is made in the normal exercise on an employment, a profession or duties e.g. a market sounding made by a disclosing participant (see below). The onward disclosure of recommendations (see ‘Insider Dealing’ above) also amounts to ‘unlawful disclosure’ where the person disclosing the recommendation knows or ought to know that it was based on inside information.

sections.title: Market Manipulation
sections.content:
This will capture a wide variety of actions including the placing of orders etc. (or disseminating misleading information) to give a false impression of the supply or, or the demand for, a financial instrument or related spot commodity contract. The prohibition against market manipulation does not apply when carried out for a legitimate reason and conforms with accepted market practice (see MAR 1 Annex 2).

Legitimate behaviour For the purposes of insider dealing and unlawful disclosure of inside information EU MAR provides that it shall not be deemed that because a legal person is in possession of inside information that that person has used that information to engage in inside dealing where that legal person:

 has established and maintained adequate systems and controls to ensure that the natural person who made the trading decision (or another natural person who may have had an influence on that decision) was not in possession of inside information; and  has not encouraged, influenced etc. the natural person who undertook the transaction.

There are additional ‘legitimate behaviour’ provisions for persons:

 in possession of inside information when acting as a market maker or counterparty or where they execute orders on behalf of third parties in the normal course of day-to-day business or where a public takeover or merger is concerned;  conducting transactions in discharge of an obligation that has become due (provided not being for the purposes of circumventing the insider dealing prohibition) where the obligation results from an order or an agreement concluded (or where a legal or regulatory obligation) arose before the person possessed inside information.

Market abuse ‘behaviours’ There are various behaviours that may indicate market abuse under the market abuse regime. It is important to note that an individual does not have to intend to commit market abuse, in order to engage one of the behaviours; it is possible to unwittingly fall foul of the regime.

Please see Appendix E for examples.

The examples should be read in the light of:

 the elements specified by MAR as making up the relevant type of market abuse;  any relevant descriptions specified by MAR which do not amount to market abuse;  any MAR-related provisions specified in any Commission legislative text; and  any applicable guidelines made by ESMA.

Exceptions The prohibition on market abuse does not apply to trading in own shares in buy-back programmes or trading in securities or associated instruments for the stabilisation of securities where the conditions in MAR Article 5 are met.

Behaviour conforming with certain rules in the Takeover Code are ‘unlikely’ to amount to market abuse - please refer to MAR 1.10.3 onwards for further information.

sections.title: Holding Inside Information
sections.content: In general, no staff member in possession of inside information may be involved in trading, selling, making recommendations, or publishing research with respect to affected financial instruments. It may also be necessary to restrict who these persons participate with. As a general matter, staff should seek to avoid unnecessarily coming into possession of information that will result in limitations being imposed on their activities. Research analysts in particular should be sensitive to the possibility that they may be prohibited in using or disseminating inside information where it has been disclosed selectively rather than to a large group of industry analysts or by press release which may provide an unfair trading information advantage. Selective disclosure should be distinguished from normal situations of corporate disclosures.

sections.title: Criminal Offences
sections.content:
Although the ‘market abuse regime’ is a civil regime, the UK also has a criminal regime in respect of ‘insider dealing’ by virtue of the Criminal Justice Act 1993 (“CJA2) s52 (where the acquisition or disposal in question occurs on a regulated market or that the person dealing relies on a professional intermediary or is himself acting as a professional intermediary). In appropriate circumstances the FCA may decide that behaviour relating to insider dealing is serious enough to justify a criminal prosecution. An offence under the CJA is punishable by up to seven years imprisonment or a fine or both.

CJA s53 provides certain defences against a charge of insider dealing including, but not limited to, that:

 he would have done what he did even if he had not had the information;  he did not expect the dealing to result in the making of a profit or the avoidance of a loss; or he believed on reasonable grounds that the information had been disclosed widely enough to ensure that none of those taking part in the dealing would be prejudiced by not having the information.

sections.title: Reporting Suspicious Transactions or Orders (“STOR”)
sections.content:
MAR requires both market operators and firms operating a trading venue to establish and maintain effective arrangements, systems and procedures aimed at preventing and detecting insider dealing, market manipulation or any attempt thereof. Any such orders and transactions, or cancellation or modification thereof that could constitute market abuse, or attempted market abuse, as above must be reported to the FCA ‘without delay’.

MAR requires persons ‘professionally arranging or executing transactions’ to establish and maintain effective arrangements, systems and procedures to detect and report suspicious orders and transactions. Where there is reasonable suspicion that an order or transaction in any financial instrument (whether placed or executed on or outside a trading venue) could constitute market abuse, or attempted market abuse, as above then this must be reported to the FCA ‘without delay’.

SUP 15 Annex 5 provides indications of possible suspicious transactions or orders.

STOR notifications to the FCA must be made via Connect.

sections.title: Handling Inside Information
sections.content:
As mentioned above, any improper disclosure of inside information amounts to market abuse. This is so regardless of whether a person actually intends to encourage the person to whom they are disclosing the information to deal.

This reinforces the importance of maintaining robust controls over the persons with whom inside information may be shared within an organisation. The details of controls and procedures are provided in the ‘Market Conduct and Rumours’ Policy.

Nevertheless, Alamut maintains Information Barriers, Restricted Lists, and other operational measures to limit market abuse. The risk of market abuse arising is covered in the Market Abuse Risk Assessment, and the efficiency of Alamut’s systems and controls to combat market abuse are tested on a regular basis as part of the Compliance Monitoring Programme. Nevertheless, only those persons who need to have access to sensitive data to perform their function are given inside information. Further, ‘strategic leaks’ or rumours are unacceptable and will not be tolerated, even where they appear to be in the Firm's interest.

Relevant Persons have primary responsibility for ensuring the confidentiality of inside information but may be justified in disclosing inside information to certain categories of recipient (in addition to those of its own employees who require the information to perform their functions) providing those persons owe a duty of confidentiality. Mere reliance on confidentiality undertakings without more may, however, no longer be sufficient. Alamut requires that parties to whom inside information is disclosed have robust controls to prevent leakage.

sections.title: What to do if actual or possible Market Abuse has occurred
sections.content:
To protect the interests of Alamut, all persons are required to cooperate in:

 Securing any relevant evidence, including tapes of telephone and electronic communications, e-mails, etc.;  Informing relevant regulators of the situation and the steps the Firm is taking to investigate — and coordinating the Firm's response to different regulators and exchanges;  Ensuring a thorough, speedy and independent investigation of the facts is undertaken;  Whether action needs to be taken to suspend members of staff while matters are investigated (subject to advice on the Firm's employment law obligations);  Whether individuals involved should be advised of the need for separate legal representation; and  Managing internal and external communications about the issue.  Where market abuse is suspected or discovered, senior management should self-report the incident to the FCA immediately. The FCA, particularly where it suspects criminal wrongdoing, will wish to coordinate any investigation with the Firm and be the first to interview potential witnesses and offenders.

sections.title: Market Rumours Policy
sections.content:
Market rumour cases generally involve two of the market abuse behaviours (as set out above under the Market Abuse Behaviours Section). The first involves disseminating false or misleading information (MAR 1.8). The second involves creating a misleading impression or market distortion (MAR 1.9). See Appendix F for our Market Rumours’ Policy.

Definition The FCA provides no definition of rumour” in its Guidelines. Instead it leaves this to firms and states that the policy should provide examples. For the sake of this policy, a rumour is classified as information from an unverified information source, such as from internet bulletin boards. A rumour would be unverified where the information was not verified by an appropriate official of the company concerned.

Creation and passing of Rumours On no account should staff create any rumours.

Although Alamut actively discourages passing on rumours, significant disclaimers should be used if this is unavoidable including:

 The origin of the information;  The information is clearly stated to be a rumour;  No additional credence or embellishment is given to the rumour; and  The information is clearly stated to be unsubstantiated/not verified.

This means avoiding sensational or exaggerated language and checking factual statements carefully before issue. This applies equally to formal written communications and to communications issued via Bloomberg, instant messages, emails or chat rooms.

An individual should be cautious that a rumour is not inside information before deciding whether to pass on the information, and where necessary check with the Compliance Officer.

Trading on Market Rumours The Firm is allowed to trade on market rumours. However, individuals at the Firm who are in receipt of a market rumour should consider the source of the rumour and whether it could constitute market abuse to trade on the rumour.

When considering this, individuals should also confirm whether the information is in the public domain and therefore available for general view and thus trading would not be an offence, or whether the information is specific or precise, but not generally available, thus potentially being either “inside” or “relevant information not generally available”, which may be considered an offence.

sections.title: Principles for Business
sections.content: In addition to the market abuse regime, the FCA’s Principles for Businesses are also applicable when considering issues around market abuse. For example, Principle 5 provides that a firm must observe proper standards of market conduct, whilst Principle 1 requires a firm to conduct its business with integrity.

sections.title: Issuers and the Market Abuse Regime
sections.content:
Under MAR an issuer means a legal entity which issues, or proposes to issue, financial instruments (in the case of depositary receipts representing financial instruments the issuer will be the issuer of the financial instrument represented).

References to ‘issuer’ in the following means an issuer for whose financial instruments:

 it has requested or approved admission for trading on a regulated market;  it has requested admission to trading on a MTF; or  it has approved trading of on a MTF or OTF (where only traded on such venues).

Issuers and Public Disclosure An issuer must inform the public ‘as soon as possible’ of inside information which directly concerns the issuer. Such information must be maintained on its website for at least five years (although in the case of issuers whose instrument are traded on a SME growth market – being a MTF so registered under MiFID II - the information may be posted on the trading venue’s website instead where this facility is offered).

Delay in disclosure is permissible only if compliant with relevant provisions in MAR – see Articles 17(4) – 17(7).

Where inside information is disclosed to a third party in the normal course of business, and that third party does not owe a duty of confidentiality, then public disclosure must be simultaneous (intentional disclosure) or prompt (non-intentional disclosure).

Issuers and Insider Lists An issuer, or those acting on their behalf, are required to maintain and to keep up to date a list (‘insider list’) of all persons who have access to inside information or those performing tasks through which they will have access to inside information e.g. advisers, accountants or credit rating agencies.

The list must be retained for at least five years after it is drawn up or updated and be made available to the FCA upon request (‘as soon as possible’).

The obligation does not apply to issuers whose instruments are traded on an SME growth market – being a MTF so registered under MiFID II – provided that it takes reasonable steps to ensure that persons with access to inside information acknowledge the legal and regulatory duties entailed and is aware of the penalties for insider dealing and unlawful disclosure. The issuer must also be able to provide the FCA with an insider list upon request.

Issuers and Manager’s Transactions There are notification requirements in respect of certain transactions effected by a ‘person discharging managerial responsibilities’ (“PDMR”) – or persons closely associated with them - within an issuer (or emission allowance market participant or related auction platform/auctioneer). A PDMR is defined as a person within an issuer (or an emission allowance participant) or any of the other persons referenced in MAR Article 19(10) that is a member of the administrative, management or supervisory body of the entity or a senior executive who has regular access to inside information and power to take managerial decisions affecting the future development and business prospects of the entity.

Disclosure applies to:

 (for issuers) every transaction conducted on their own account relating to the shares or debt instruments of the issuer or to derivatives or other instruments linked thereto;  (in respect of emission allowance market participants) every transaction conducted on their own account relating to emission allowances, to auction products based thereon or to derivatives relating thereto.

The above scope of disclosure, although based upon MAR, is very broad. However, note that Article 10 of Delegated Regulation 2016/522 (see ‘Useful Links’) identifies a broader non-exhaustive list of transactions that should be identified with which all PDMRs should familiarise themselves with.

The notification requirement applies to any subsequent transaction once a total amount of €5,000 has been reached within a calendar year. If so wished, all transactions can be notified on a voluntary basis.

Notification (no later than three business days after the date of the transaction) is to be made to both the issuer (or emission allowance participant) and to the FCA or relevant competent authority if applicable (the latter will be the competent authority of the Member State where the issuer (or emission allowance participant) is registered).

The issuer (or emission allowance participant) must ensure that the notified information is made public ‘promptly’, and no later than three business days after the transaction.

For the purposes of this requirement, ‘transaction’ will also include: pledging or lending of instruments; transactions undertaken by another person on behalf of the PDMR (including under discretion); and certain life insurance policies.

A PDMR must not conduct any transactions on own account during a closed period of 30 calendar days before the announcement of an interim financial report or a year-end report.

In exceptional circumstances trading during a closed period may be permitted on a case-by-case basis e.g. severe financial difficulty. However, any such trading must be in accordance with MAR Article 19(12) and Delegated Regulation 2016/522 (see ‘Useful Links’). In the first instance any PDMR claiming exceptional circumstances must discuss the matter with the Compliance Officer.

See DTR 1, 2 & 3 for further guidance on PDMR notifications and control of inside information.

sections.title: Market Soundings
sections.content:
Market soundings is the term used to describe the communication of information, prior to the announcement of a transaction, in order to gauge the interest of potential investors in a possible

transaction and the conditions relating to it such as its potential size or pricing to one or more potential investors by:

 an issuer;  a secondary offeror (where the quantity or value makes the transaction distinct from ordinary trading and involves a selling method based on the prior assessment of potential interest from potential investors;  an emission allowance market participant; or  a third party acting on behalf or on the account of any of the above.

Disclosing Market Participant (“DMP”) Unlawful disclosure of inside information is prohibited under MAR (see above). However, a disclosure of inside information made in the course of a market sounding is deemed to be made in the normal exercise of a person’s employment, profession or duties – and hence falls outside the definition of ‘unlawful disclosure of inside information’ - where the DMP complies with the following:

 the person must consider whether the market sounding will involve the disclosure of inside information. A written record of the conclusion (which must be provided to the FCA upon request) must be made and the reason for that conclusion;  consent of the receiving party must be obtained to receive inside information;  the receiving party must be informed that they are prohibited from using that information to trade on own account or the account of a third party or cancel or amend an order which has already been placed;  the receiving party accepts that by agreeing to receive such information that they are obliged to keep that information confidential; and  a record is maintained of all information, including the above points, given and to whom disclosed, as well as the date and time of each disclosure.

A market sounding can be made by any means e.g. email, meetings etc. However, where made by telephone then recorded lines must be used where there is access to such lines and where the recipient has consented to the recording. Only equipment supplied by the DMP must be used.

Before making any form of contact, agreement on the information to be imparted will be agreed and adhered to in respect of all persons receiving that sounding.

Where any information imparted in the course of a market sounding ceases to be inside information, as assessed by the disclosing market participant, then the latter must inform the recipient accordingly as soon as possible and maintain a record of the information given.

For the record, notwithstanding the above, the recipient of a market sounding is required to assess for itself whether it is in possession of inside information or when it ceases to be in possession of inside information.

All such records must be kept for at least five years.

Market Sounding Recipient (“MSR”) The contact point for receiving market soundings in Alamut is the Compliance Officer. After the initial contact it will be the responsibility of the Compliance Officer to notify the DMP whether we would wish to receive future market soundings whether in relation to all potential transactions or particular types of transactions.

Although the DMP will assess whether inside information is involved (and, possibly, when previously imparted information is deemed to no longer be inside information), Tom Alcock is also required to independently assess whether information is, remains or ceases to be, inside information.

For each market sounding received, a list will be drawn up of persons within Alamut that are in possession of the information communicated.

In those situations where our assessment is that we are in possession of inside information we must identify all the issuers and financial instruments to which that inside information relates.

If communication with the DMP was by way of unrecorded meetings or telephone conversations then the DMP is required to keep written minutes or notes. Alamut, as a MSR, should sign these minutes or notes where the contents are agreed. If we do not agree with what has been produced then we must submit our own version to the DMP within five working days after the market sounding.

Alamut has in place documented internal procedures relating to market soundings to:

 ensure information received is internally communicated only through pre-determined reporting lines on a need-to-know basis;  ensure that Alamut’s required assessment of whether or not we are in possession of inside information is undertaken by clearly identified persons and that the relevant staff are properly trained for that purpose;  manage and control the flow of inside information within Alamut (including the training of relevant personnel on the required internal procedures and on the market abuse regime) in compliance with the EU MAR obligations concerning insider dealing and the unlawful disclosure of inside information.

All such records must be kept for a period of five years.

sections.title: Monitoring
sections.content: Alamut may undertake monitoring including considering a routine sampling of phone calls, emails, instant messages and Bloomberg messages. This could also include a retrospective review after suspicious price movements and trades.

chapters.title: FINANCIAL CRIME
chapters.summary: 3. FINANCIAL CRIME
chapters.content:
3. FINANCIAL CRIME

## 3.1 Financial Crime

The Handbook definition of ‘financial crime’ is any kind of criminal conduct relating to money or to financial services or markets, including any offence involving:

 Fraud or dishonesty; or  Handling the proceeds of crime; or  The financing of terrorism  Misconduct in, or misuse of information relating to a financial market

The Handbook includes a Financial Crime Guide (“FCG”) and a section on Financial Crime Thematic Reviews (“FCTR”) – see ‘Useful Links’. This chapter of the Compliance Manual will not cover market misconduct (see the previous chapter on ‘Market Abuse’).

## 3.2 Fraud

Fraud is the act of obtaining by deception money or assets belonging to another which will benefit the fraudster and expose the victim to a loss. Fraud may be committed against individuals and firms, for example, through:

 The use of false or stolen identities to defraud financial services organisations; or  The use of the internet, for example, by setting up websites purporting to belong to a reputable institution.

The FCA requires firms to take reasonable care to establish and maintain effective systems and controls for countering the risk that the Firm might be used to further Financial Crime.

The Firm provides investment management services and does not hold or control client money or other assets. This minimises the risk of financial fraud. Nevertheless, the Firm has implemented the following systems and controls to combat Financial Crime:

Preventing Fraud There are numerous ways the Firm protects itself against Fraud. These include:

 Established and clear reporting lines;  Regular analysis of risks;  Segregation of duties;  Employment screening;  Taping and monitoring of phone calls;  Staff training;  Compliance monitoring programme;  Detailed KYC processes; and  IT security measures.  See the Firm’s ‘Fraud’ Policy at Appendix I.

## 3.3 Anti-Money Laundering – General

Purpose A firm must take reasonable care to establish and maintain effective systems and controls for compliance with applicable requirements and standards under the legislative provisions and regulatory system and for countering the risk that the Firm might be used to further financial crime. The best defence that Alamut can have to ensure that it is not being used to further financial crime is to Know Your Customer (“KYC”) through appropriate Customer Due Diligence (“CDD”) which is a process that continues throughout the duration of the business relationship.

The Firm’s procedures must enable it to identify, assess, monitor and manage its money laundering risk. They should be proportionate, taking into account the nature, complexity and scale of the Firm’s business. Overall responsibility to reduce financial crime lies with Senior Management, although the role of the Money Laundering Reporting Officer (“MLRO”) (see below) is to act as the focal point for all activity within the Firm relating to anti-money laundering.

What is Money Laundering? Money laundering is the process by which criminals or individuals acting on their behalf attempt to conceal the true origin and ownership of the proceeds of their criminal activities. If undertaken successfully it also allows them to maintain control over those proceeds and, ultimately, to provide a legitimate cover for their source of income. The obligations to prevent “Money Laundering” apply equally to the prevention of Terrorist Financing and Proliferation Financing and this Chapter should be read as such. The latter activity refers to providing funds or financial services for the development, manufacture etc. of chemical, biological, radiological or nuclear weapons (‘CBRN’) in contravention of a relevant financial sanctions obligation.

Money laundering can take many forms including:

 trying to turn money raised through criminal activity into ‘clean’ money (‘classic money laundering’)  handling the benefit of acquisitive crimes such as theft, fraud and tax evasion  handling stolen goods  being directly involved with any criminal or terrorist property, or entering into arrangements to facilitate the laundering of criminal or terrorist property  criminals investing the proceeds of their crimes in the whole range of financial products

Overriding Responsibilities There are two overriding responsibilities on all staff made under the arrangements of the ‘Anti-Money Laundering’ Policy:

 Client Identification and Identity Verification Reasonable steps must be taken to check the client’s identity to show that they are who they claim to be and that they are trading for a legitimate business purpose. This means that Alamut needs to be able to demonstrate that it has done sufficient CDD checks on the client, but also that they understand the client’s business, the nature of the transaction and source of funds. In addition to new clients, CDD measures should be applied “at appropriate times” to existing customers on a risk-sensitive basis or when circumstances of the customer change.

The Fifth Money Laundering Directive also requires CDD to be applied to existing customers:

 When the Firm has a legal duty in the course of a calendar year to contact that customer for the purposes of reviewing information regarding beneficial owners  When that duty arises under the tax cooperation regime (2011/61)

 Recognising and Reporting Suspected Money Laundering Individuals must make a report promptly to the MLRO if the individual knows or suspects or has reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting that a client, or the person on whose behalf the client is acting, is engaged in money laundering. If staff are unsure as to what constitutes a suspicious activity they should contact the MLRO for guidance. Failure to follow the Policy may result in a personal criminal liability.

## 3.4 Laws and Regulatory Regime Relating to Money Laundering

Relevant money laundering legislation in the UK is derived from EU Directives, specifically the Fourth and Fifth Money Laundering Directives. The former effectively contains the body of the EU requirements in respect of anti-money laundering whilst the latter amends various elements of the former. As such, both documents need to be read together. Legislation also includes the UK’s Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on Payer) Regulations 2017 (as amended – see below), the

Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (as amended) and the Serious Organised Crime and Police Act 2005. Further provisions in regard to Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing are contained in the Criminal Finances Act, the Counter Terrorism Act 2008, the Anti-Terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001 and the Terrorism Act 2000. In addition, HM Treasury maintains a ‘sanctions list’ (see later). A brief overview of the most relevant legislative and regulatory provisions is included below.

For the record, the Sixth Money Laundering Directive (“MLD6”) required the UK to transpose the obligations therein into UK law by 3 December 2020. However the UK has decided to opt out and hence MLD6 will have no impact in the UK. The Directive is concerned with defining criminal offences and associated sanctions – the UK is of the view that UK domestic legislation is at least equal to the requirements under MLD6.

The FCA Handbook does not contain prescriptive rules on AML controls but instead requires a firm to ensure that it has established appropriate policies and procedures that are comprehensive and proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of its activities that enable it to identify; assess; monitor; and ‘manage money laundering risk’. The latter is the risk that a firm may be used to further money laundering.

When considering whether a breach of its rules on systems and controls against money laundering has occurred, the FCA will have to regard whether a firm has followed the guidance issued by the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (see later).

In summary, it is a criminal offence:

 To acquire, use, possess, conceal, disguise, convert, transfer or remove criminal property from the jurisdiction, or to facilitate its acquisition, retention, use or control by another person;  To fail to disclose to the appropriate authorities’ knowledge or suspicion of money laundering; and  To tip off a suspected person.

“Criminal conduct” is defined as conduct which:

1. constitutes an offence in any part of the United Kingdom; or 2. would constitute an offence in any part of the United Kingdom if it occurred there.

This not only includes serious criminal conduct, but also any other offence which is committed and where benefit is gained, of whatever size, for any person. The basis for reporting suspicions that criminal conduct has arisen, or that funds arise from criminal conduct, is that the activity would be a criminal offence in the UK.

“Criminal property” means property that a person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe that it is derived from criminal conduct.

Broadly, a violation of any of the legislative or regulatory provisions can result in severe sanctions in respect of individual acts, or the failure to put in appropriate procedures to combat money laundering. The FCA, in conjunction with other bodies, has the power to bring prosecutions for breaches of the criminal regime described above.

3.5 The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 and associated Regulations The 2017 Regulations, which replaced the Money Laundering Regulations 2007, have been amended by subsequent Regulations (see ‘Useful Links’) – for convenience all are referred to as ‘the Regulations’. The Regulations require that Alamut establishes and maintains specific policies and procedures to guard against its business being used for the purposes of money laundering. They specify the range of procedures that Alamut should implement to fulfil this obligation.

The procedures include:

 Applying CDD measures when establishing a business relationship, carrying out occasional transactions (above €15,000, whether in a single operation, or multiple operations which are linked), suspecting money laundering, questioning the originality of documentation, and ultimately applying other due diligence measures on an appropriate and risk sensitive basis;  Conducting ongoing monitoring of a business relationship;  Applying “enhanced” due diligence if the situation warrants it;  Not to carry out transactions or establish a relationship where due diligence has not been satisfactorily conducted;  Record-keeping of all CDD as evidence; and  Provision of regular training.

Further provisions are in respect of: Assistance The combined effect of the UK statutes makes it an offence for any person to provide assistance to a money-launderer to obtain, conceal, retain or invest funds if he knows or suspects, or in the case of terrorist activities should have known or suspected, that those funds are the proceeds of an indictable offence (see note below).

Such assistance is punishable on conviction by a maximum of 14 years imprisonment or a fine, or both. It is a defence that the person concerned reported his knowledge or suspicion to the National Crime Agency (“NCA”) or under approved internal arrangements at the first available opportunity. Destroying or disposing of relevant documents which could amount to the offence of prejudicing an investigation carries a maximum penalty of 5 years imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine, or both.

Tipping Off It is also an offence for anyone to prejudice an investigation by informing the person who is the subject of a suspicion, or any third-party, that a disclosure has been made, or that the police or customs authorities are carrying out or intending to carry out a money laundering investigation.

Making enquiries of a customer, to verify identity or to ascertain the source of funds for a particular transaction will not trigger a tipping off offence before a suspicious activity report has been submitted in respect of that customer. If a suspicious activity report has been made, great care should be taken to ensure the customer does not become aware of that fact.

The punishment on conviction for this ‘tipping off’ offence is a maximum of 5 years imprisonment or a fine, or both.

Failure to Report In the case of drug trafficking or terrorist activity, it is an offence for a person who acquires knowledge or suspicion in the course of his trade, profession, business or employment not to report the knowledge or suspicion as soon as reasonably practical after the information came to his attention.

Failure to report in these circumstances is punishable on conviction by a maximum of 5 years imprisonment or a fine, or both.

The legislation protects those reporting suspicions of money laundering from claims in respect of any alleged breach of customer confidentiality.

The Regulations transpose the Fourth Money Laundering Directive (2015/849) and establishes the UK supervisory authorities in relation to the Transfer of Funds Regulation (2015/847). The relevant date for transposition of the amendments brought about by the Fifth Money Laundering Directive (2018/843) was 10 January 2020.

## 3.6 The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (“JMLSG”) Guidance

The JMLSG is made up of leading UK Trade Associations in the Financial Services industry. Its aim is to promulgate good practice in countering money laundering and to give practical assistance in interpreting the UK Money Laundering Regulations through the publication of its guidance notes.

As mentioned above, in taking into account whether the Firm has appropriate systems and controls against money laundering the FCA will have regard to whether the Firm has followed the relevant provisions in the JMLSG Guidance.

These provide invaluable guidance on e.g. what is meant by, and when it is required for, ‘customer due diligence’ (JMLSG Guidance 5.3); ‘simplified due diligence’ e.g. in respect of certain regulated firms in the financial sector (JMLSG Guidance 5.4); and ‘enhanced due diligence’ e.g. where a client is based in a high risk third-country (see ‘Useful Links’) (JMLSG Guidance 5.5). They will also provide guidance on the differing approaches needed for individuals; trusts; corporates; etc. The guidance notes have been approved by HM Treasury.

The guidance is currently in three parts: Part I (general guidance); Part II (sector-specific guidance); and Part III (specialist guidance).

It should be noted that the Fourth Money Laundering Directive requires firms to determine the level of risk posed by a client prior to applying simplified due diligence to such a client and provide justification for such assessment. A justification for applying simplified due diligence may be that the client is listed or regulated but now a firm needs to record such reasoning.

The JMLSG Guidance (Part I) provides ‘Risk Factor Guidelines’ – see Annex 5-III (simplified due diligence) and Annex 5-IV (enhanced due diligence).

## 3.7 USA Patriot Act

The US criminal money laundering laws, in particular the USA Patriot Act 2001, have extra-territorial effect. Where Alamut has any established activities in, or linked to the USA, whether through a branch, subsidiary, associated company or correspondent banking relationship there is a risk that U.S. regulations and sanctions may apply. This includes dealing with clients that are US citizens. Alamut must also make due regard to any US financial sanctions and provisions from the Office of Foreign Assets Control (see below).

## 3.8 Systems and Controls to Mitigate Money Laundering Risk

Money Laundering Risk is the risk that a firm may be used to further money laundering. In identifying its money laundering risk, and then adopting procedures to mitigate that risk, the Firm should consider a range of factors including the nature of the Firm’s customer, distribution channels, complexity and volume of transactions, processes and systems and its operating environment. As noted above, one of the FCA’s statutory objectives is to reduce financial crime and terrorist financing. The systems and controls that the FCA requires regulated firms to adopt to meet that statutory objective are contained in SYSC. In addition, reference should be made to the FCA’s published guidance on financial crime (FC). Alamut maintains a Money Laundering Risk Assessment which is reviewed at least annually for any changes. This document serves to identify the risk of Money Laundering occurring at Alamut.

In addition to the above, the assessment of the risk of money laundering should be informed by relevant findings in the UK’s National Risk Assessment. This also contains guidance on overseas jurisdictions.

3.8.1 Appropriate Systems and Controls The Firm will adopt the following systems and controls in ensuring that the Firm is not used to further financial crime:

 Appropriate training for employees in relation to money laundering;  Providing the Senior Management / governing body with sufficient information, this includes an annual report from the Firm’s MLRO;

 Documentation of the Firm’s risk profile in relation to money laundering; and  Appropriate measures to ensure that Money Laundering risk is taken into account in its day to day operations.

## 3.9 Reporting of Suspicious Activity: NCA

If a member of staff comes across a transaction or an activity which is regarded as suspicious or unusual (or which could reasonably be regarded as such), then in the first instance, the member of staff should refer the transaction and/or activity in question without delay to the MLRO.

The MLRO will consider the issue, together with any other relevant information available (including any information about the client’s identity) and, if the suspicion is justified, make a report to the National Crime Agency (NCA).

## 3.10 Identification of Clients, Politically Exposed Persons (“PEPs”) and Sanctions List

Alamut should find out the identity, and verify the identity, of its potential clients by obtaining sufficient evidence of identity of any client who comes into contact with it to be able to show that the client is who he claims he is. If that client appears to be acting on behalf of another, sufficient evidence should be obtained of both their identities. All clients or those acting on behalf of a client must be identified in accordance with applicable CDD Procedures.

The money laundering framework includes specific provisions when a customer is a trust or a corporate body which includes the need to identify ‘beneficial owners’ or, where relevant, the ‘senior managing official(s)’ – see the JMLSG Guidance (see below) for further information. Companies House maintains the publicly available PSC Register (“persons with significant control”) which will assist with legal entities. There is a similar register for trusts (“Trust Registration Service”) maintained by HMRC although this is not public and, if relevant, details would need to be requested from the trustees of the trust.

In addition, consideration should be given to any applicable Financial Sanctions as detailed by HM Treasury’s Consolidated List (see ‘Useful Links’).

Further at times Alamut may need to check against the Sanctions Lists maintained by the (US) Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) which is available from the United States Treasury (see ‘Useful Links’).

This check must be carried out as soon as possible after staff of Alamut has had an initial ‘firm’ contact with the client. The verification of the identity of the customer and, where applicable, the beneficial owner, must take place before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of an occasional transaction. The type of evidence that should be sought will depend upon the nature of the client and the proposed activity. Further guidance on appropriate identification evidence for different types of clients can be found in the JMLSG Guidance. If you are in any doubt as to an appropriate procedure please contact the MLRO immediately.

If the client does not provide any evidence, no business may be done with or for him, and the understanding reached with the client should be ended immediately, unless the NCA has been informed.

There are a few exceptions to this identification check (opening of a bank account; life assurance or where necessary not to interrupt normal business in low risk cases - refer to 5.2 of the JMLSG Guidance– where verification can be made after the initial business activity). However, these exceptions will not apply if staff have any knowledge or suspicion of money laundering or in circumstances where it would be reasonable to have knowledge or suspicion.

PEPs can pose a higher money laundering risk – although PEP status itself does not incriminate individuals or entities.

A PEP is defined as “an individual who is entrusted with prominent public functions, other than as a middle-ranking or more junior official”. Immediate family members, or known close associates, of such a

person are also captured. Prominent public functions include, but are not limited to, heads of state, ministers and members of parliaments. Reference should be made to regulation 35 of the Regulations and section 5.5.13 onwards of the JMLSG Guidance for further details. (It should be noted that as a result of the Fourth Money Laundering Directive, the definition of a PEP has been extended and now captures domestic PEPs. In addition, an individual can be classified as ceasing to be a PEP after such person has left office for one year though firms may apply a longer period of time where it is felt appropriate).

An initiative emanating from the Fifth Money Laundering Directive (2018/843) is that Member States are required to maintain a list of those functions that qualify as “prominent public functions” with the European Commission maintaining an EU-wide single publicly available list.

Should anyone in Alamut become aware of the possibility of us establishing a relationship with a PEP the matter should be referred to the senior management for a decision to be made on whether to progress the potential relationship.

Transparency International’s web site contains a ‘Corruption Perceptions’ Index which ranks countries according to their perceived level of corruption and which may be of assistance in assessing the risk a PEP might pose.

Any suspicion or knowledge of money laundering should immediately be reported to the MLRO.

## 3.11 National and International Findings: FATF and Egmont

So that the Firm’s Systems and Controls remain appropriate and up to date the Firm should take account of government and Financial Action Task Force findings (“FATF”) (which is an organisation intended to combat money laundering) in respect of money laundering. Those findings give essential information about countries or governments whose money laundering arrangements are inadequate or materially deficient. The Money Laundering Reporting Officer (in conjunction with the Compliance Officer) will feed any such information through to staff. Staff should always note that information and act upon it.

In addition, the website of the Egmont Group of Financial Intelligence Units (“Egmont”) may be a useful reference source. Egmont is an organisation of Financial Intelligence Units (“FIUs” - the NCA is a member) which provides a forum for FIUs around the world to improve cooperation in the fight against money laundering and financing of terrorism.

With the introduction of the Fourth Money Laundering Directive, the ability for financial institutions to rely on a ‘white list’ of countries outside the EU where the AML regulations were considered equivalent to those within the EU Member States has been removed. Under the Fourth Money Laundering Directive, financial institutions must conduct country-specific risk assessments for any jurisdiction outside the EU where they undertake business. JMLSG Guidance 1.9 to 1.16 provides useful guidance on international anti-money laundering standards. In particular, JMLSG Guidance 1.13 provides that countries may be assessed using publicly available indices from, for example, HM Treasury Sanctions, FATF-high risk and non- cooperative jurisdictions, MoneyVal evaluations, Transparency International Corruption Perception Index, UK trade and investment overseas country risk pages and quality of regulation.

The EU publishes a ‘black list’ which details high risk third-country jurisdictions which have strategic deficiencies in their national anti-money laundering regimes. In contrast the UK Money Laundering Regulations directs us (Regulation 33(3)(a)) to the listings maintained by FATF to which reference should always be made (see ‘Useful Links’). The Fifth Money Laundering Directive imposes prescriptive enhanced due diligence measures on firms is respect of business relationships or transactions involving such high-risk third countries.

## 3.12 Using or Relying on Third-Party Sources for CDD Checks

Note that for this section ‘relying on’ means taking advantage of CDD work undertaken by another entity whereas ‘outsourcing’ refers to appointing a third-party to undertake CDD work on behalf of the firm.

Section 39 of the Regulations allows Alamut to rely on third parties to have applied any necessary customer due diligence checks provided that where Alamut relies on a third party to carry out such CDD measures, Alamut must obtain from the third party all the information needed to identify the client or beneficial owner.

Additionally, Alamut must enter into written arrangements with the third party being relied upon which:

 Enable Alamut to obtain from the third party immediately on request (or at the latest within two business days) copies of any identification and verification data and any other relevant documentation on the identity of the client or beneficial owner;  Require the third party to retain copies of the data and documents referred to above for five years beginning on the date on which the third party is relied on by the Firm.  In such cases of reliance, Alamut remains liable for any failure to apply correct CDD measures.

There are strict limitations on what types of third parties can be relied upon; typically they will be FCA authorised entities e.g. a wealth manager but certain non-UK entities can be relied upon and reference should be made to s39(3) of the Regulations and Chapter 5.6 of JMLSG Guidance (Part I) for further guidance.

Section 39(7) of the Regulations allows Alamut to outsource (as opposed to ‘relying on’) CDD measures to a service provider subject to Alamut remaining liable for any failure to apply such measures.

There may be certain instances when Alamut may outsource CDD checks to a third-party firm such as an Administrator for conducting and confirming the identity of the client provided this is done in accordance with applicable UK standards. Alamut must review the third-party’s methodology and procedures prior to such an arrangement occurring. The third-party must supply Alamut with copies of any CDD/AML checks done upon Alamut’s request. Alamut will be required to monitor the client and report any suspicious of financial crime or terrorist financing to the competent authorities.

## 3.13 Staff Awareness and Training

It is a requirement of the Regulations under (s24) that staff receive regular training on money laundering (which, as a reminder, in this Chapter also captures terrorist and proliferation financing) and on their responsibilities in this respect. In determining the regularity of such training we are obliged to consider the nature of our business, the size of our business and also the nature and extent of the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing to which we are subject. The MLRO is responsible for arranging such training and for maintaining the training records.

The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (“POCA”) emphasises the importance for regulated firms to ensure that all their staff receive regular anti-money laundering training. POCA s330(7) of the Act provides that employees who fail to disclose knowledge or suspicion of money laundering will not be personally liable if they did not suspect that money laundering was involved and their employer failed to provide them with sufficient anti-money laundering training. This may lead the Firm open to prosecution or regulatory sanction for not having adequate training and awareness arrangements in place.

## 3.14 Money Laundering Reporting Officer/Responsible Senior Manager or Director

The MLRO at Alamut is Thomas Alcock. The MLRO is principally a focal point for all activity within the Firm relating to anti-money laundering and is charged with responsibility for ensuring that the requirements relevant to money laundering are complied with, for monitoring the day to day operation of anti-money laundering and for dealing with the NCA and the FCA in respect of money laundering. The FCA expects this individual to be based in the UK and they must be approved by the FCA to perform that role. For the purposes of regulation 21 of the Regulations he is also the nominated officer to receive disclosures of suspicions or knowledge of money laundering or terrorist financing.

The MLRO will also report annually to Senior Management on the operation and effectiveness of Alamut’s money laundering systems and controls.

The MLRO must have a level of authority and independence within the Firm so that he has access to the necessary resources and information sufficient to enable him/her to carry out that responsibility.

The Firm must also allocate overall responsibility within the Firm for the establishment and maintenance of effective anti-money laundering systems and controls with a director or senior manager of the Firm - this can be the same individual who undertakes the MLRO function. For the purposes of SYSC 6.3.8R the MLRO is also the person so responsible.

POCA introduced an additional offence of “not reporting” by the nominated officer (i.e. the MLRO). Therefore, to protect themselves and the Firm, if the MLRO receives a suspicious activity report from a member of staff but does not consider it necessary to notify NCA, he must carefully record the reasons why this course of action was taken. Staff should always give the MLRO access to any information (including financial circumstances) of the client, including details of any transactions entered into with the client.

## 3.15 Consequence of not complying with the Anti-Money Laundering Obligations

Not complying with the Anti-Money Laundering obligation is considered a serious offence. Disciplinary actions may be taken, including immediate dismissal. Failure to comply with the money laundering obligations may also result in a criminal penalty (including imprisonment).

Failure by a member of staff may also expose Alamut to penalties, censure and enforcement action (including the imposition of fines) by the FCA.

## 3.16 Record Keeping

The Regulations (s40) and the JMLSG Guidance (Chapter 8) require or suggest that the following records be kept, for at least five years from the end of the relationship with a client or longer if required by law:

 Copy of evidence of the identity of clients or a record where this can be obtained or, where this is not practicable a record of how details of the evidence of identity can be obtained;  Transaction records (carried out with or for a client);  Records of any internal reports made to the MLRO and of any external reports made by the MLRO;  MLRO annual (and other) reports;  Where the MLRO has considered information or other matter concerning knowledge or suspicion that another person has engaged in money laundering, but has not made a report to the NCA, a record of that information or other matter; and  Dates of AML training, including nature of training, attendees and results of tests where appropriate.

These records are kept at the Firm’s offices and the MLRO is responsible for ensuring that these records are complete and up to date. Failure to keep the required records can leave affirm or person open to prosecution, including imprisonment for up to two years and/or a fine or regulatory censure. It should be noted that personal data processed for any purpose or purposes shall not be kept for longer than is necessary for that purpose or purposes.

You should not hesitate to report suspicion - by failing to make a report you are committing an offence. By not making a report, the firm and you will not be able to take advantage of the protections afforded by the legislation.

## 3.17 Money Laundering Monitoring

The Firm’s compliance monitoring programme will make periodic assessments of the adequacy of its systems and controls arrangements to prevent the Firm being used to further financial crime. The MLRO reports these findings to Senior Management and governing body on a periodic and at least annual basis.

## 3.18 Bribery

Members and employees may not give, agree to give, or offer any gift or other consideration to any person, including a public official or an employee in the private sector, as an inducement or reward for that person doing or not doing an act in relation to his principal affairs or business. It is an offence just to offer such a gift or other consideration, whether or not the offer is accepted or acted upon. The provision or acceptance of cash gifts is strictly prohibited. Attention is also brought to the Firm’s Gifts and Benefits Policy.

The Bribery Act 2010 The Bribery Act 2010 establishes four categories of offence:

1. Bribing another person; 2. Being bribed; 3. Bribing a foreign public official; and 4. Failure of a commercial organisation to prevent third parties paying bribes on its behalf.

The first three offences are capable of being committed by an individual or a company, whilst the fourth can be committed by a company only.

SEC’s Pay to Play Restrictions – Advisers Act, Rule 206(4)-5 Rule 206(4)-5 of the Advisers Act makes it unlawful for an investment adviser to receive compensation for providing advisory services to a government entity for a two-year period, after the adviser or any of its “covered associates” makes a political contribution to a public official of a government entity or candidate for such office, who is or will be in a position to influence the award of advisory business to the adviser. Covered associates could be any general partner, managing member or executive officer, or other individual with a similar status or function; any employee who solicits a government entity for the investment adviser and any person who supervises such employee; and any political action committee controlled by the investment adviser.

It is the Firm’s policy that contributions to candidates for public office, political parties or Political Action Committees (“PACs”) (as defined below) by the Firm and all its Employees are made in compliance with applicable law. The Firm will not participate in, nor permit Employees to, participate in pay to play practices. Any contribution, which includes any gift, subscription, loan, advance, deposit of money or anything of value, to candidates running for US state or local political office, candidates running for US federal office who currently hold a US state or local political office, or to political parties or PACs that may contribute to such campaigns (collectively, a “Political Contribution”) by the Firm or its Employees must be made in compliance with applicable law. In addition, Employees or members of the Employee’s immediate family (spouse, child, anyone living with any Employee or anyone deriving financial support from the Employee) may only solicit another person to make any Political Contribution or coordinate another person’s Political Contribution (including fundraising activities) in compliance with applicable law. Employees are not permitted to facilitate or encourage political contributions through third parties, including, for example, consultants, attorneys, other family members, friends or entities affiliated with the Firm as a means to circumvent this policy.

The Firm will not make contributions or payments or otherwise give any endorsement or support to political parties or candidates (including through intermediary organizations such as PACs or campaign funds) with the intent of directly or indirectly influencing any investment management relationship. The Firm will not directly or indirectly reimburse Employees or their immediate family members for individual political contributions or expenditures.

To comply with these laws, Employees and members of their immediate family may not contribute to, solicit or coordinate campaign funds for:  A candidate running for US state or local office;  A candidate running for US federal office who currently holds a US state or local political office; or

 A political party or PAC in the United States

without prior written approval from the Compliance Officer. All volunteer activities by Employees or members of their immediate family for any such persons or organizations must also be approved in writing by the Compliance Officer. This policy does not affect campaign contributions intended to be used for candidates for US federal office, such as the US House of Representatives, US Senate, or President of the United States, unless the candidate currently holds a US state or local office.

If an Employee or any member of his or her immediate family wishes to make, or solicit another person to make, or coordinate another person’s (for example, conducting any fundraising activity), a Political Contribution, the Employee must complete a “Political Contributions Pre-Clearance Form” (See Appendix P) and submit the completed form to the CCO for pre-clearance before making any Political Contribution or participating in any solicitation or coordination of Political Contributions (the form is available upon request from the CCO). Employees and immediate family members that wish to volunteer to assist a political party, PAC or candidate in the US must also complete the Political Contributions Pre-Clearance Form.

Definition of a PAC A PAC is generally an organization whose purpose is to raise and distribute campaign funds to candidates seeking political office. PACs are formed by corporations, labour unions, membership organizations or trade associations or other organizations to solicit campaign contributions from individuals and channel the resulting funds to candidates for elective offices.

New Employee Certification All new Employees must complete a “New Employee Political Contribution Disclosure Form” (see Appendix Q). Any Political Contributions by a new Employee in the prior two years may affect the Firm’s business. The CCO shall be consulted on all new Employee hires by the Firm in which the potential employee has made a Political Contribution in the two years prior to being hired. The CCO must determine whether such past Political Contributions will affect the Firm’s business.

Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (“FCPA”) The FCPA prevents US companies, citizens, foreign companies listed on a US stock exchange from making payments or providing anything of value to non-US government officials, political party officials or candidates for political office, in order to obtain or retain business, or influence any act or decision of a non-US official. A company which violates the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA may, upon conviction, be fined up to $2 million per violation. An employee or agent of the company convicted of a violation of the FCPA may be fined up to $100,000 or imprisoned for not more than five years, or both. In addition to criminal sanctions, the FCPA allows the US Attorney General to initiate a civil action in a United States District Court seeking an injunction against any act or practice which constitutes a violation of the FCPA.

The Firm’s Anti-Bribery Policy The Firm has adopted the following controls to prevent bribery taking place, “pay to play” arrangements and payments to non-US officials taking place:

 Clear, documented responsibility for reducing the risk of Financial Crime, including Bribery and corruption. In the Firm, this responsibility rests with Compliance Officer;  Senior management understand and is informed of the Bribery and corruption risks facing the Firm via the provision of Management Information addressing the Financial Crime risk;  Remuneration structures are designed to avoid incentivising staff members to gain business through bribes;  Regular reviews of the Risk Register by Senior Management, which comprise a review of the Firm’s exposure to Financial Crime risk and the systems and controls in place to mitigate this risk;  Risk based approval for third party payments and documentation of the reason behind all payments;

 Regular monitoring of the schedule of third party payments (large payments, a large number of small payments, payments to connected parties, payments to political connections, High Risk jurisdictions, unusual, complex or secret payments);  Focus on countries regarded as higher risk in terms of Bribery and corruption, in accordance with the Transparency International Corruption Perception Index: http://www.transparency.org/research/cpi/overview  Prohibition on the provision of cash to staff members (apart from de minimis amounts to cover small incidental expenses);  Prohibition on the receipt or giving of cash gifts;  Performing checks when recruiting new members of staff that are proportionate to their respective roles, for example, Criminal Records Bureau (“CRB”) checks;  Prohibition on all employees making political contributions, or giving gifts or business courtesies to any government officials without written permission from the Compliance Officer;  Annual monitoring of the fitness and propriety and financial soundness of members of staff; and  Providing staff members with relevant, understandable and effective training.  The Firm has an ‘Anti-Bribery’ Policy, which can be found at Appendix H.

## 3.19 Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023

The Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023 introduces two corporate offences in respect of ‘economic crime’. Under s196 if a ‘senior manager’ of a corporate body or partnership (‘organisation’) acting within the scope of their authority commits (or attempts to commit) an ‘economic crime’ then, from 26th December 2023, the organisation is also guilty of the offence. For this purpose a ‘senior manager’ means an individual who either plays a significant role in the making of decisions of the organisation or in the actual managing or organising of a substantial part of the organisations activities. What constitutes as an economic crime as defined in the Act captures a range of activities such as false accounting, money laundering (including failure to disclose suspicion of money laundering) and bribery – the full definition of can be found in s193(1) of the Act (see ‘Useful Links’). The second corporate offence is ‘failure to prevent fraud’ under s199 and which is only applicable to a ‘large organisation’ which, in brief, is an organisation that satisfies at least two of the following measures in the financial year of the organisation that precedes the year of the fraud offence:  Turnover of more than £36m  Balance sheet total of more than £18m  Number of employees being more than 250 The offence occurs where a person that is ‘associated’ with the organisation commits a fraud offence intending to benefit (directly or indirectly) either the large organisation or any person to whom (or to whose subsidiary undertaking) the associate provides services on behalf of the large organisation. The precise definition of a ‘fraud offence’ can be found is s199(6) if the Act (see ‘Useful Links’). For the purpose of this offence a person is ‘associated’ with an organisation if they are an employee, agent or subsidiary of the large organisation or the person otherwise performs service for or on behalf of the large organisation. However a large organisation will not be guilty of the offence if it was, or was intended to be, a victim of the fraud. Note that it is a defence if it can be proved that the large organisation had ‘reasonable procedures’ in place (or it was not reasonable “in all the circumstances” to expect the organisation to have such procedures in place. The ‘failure to prevent fraud’ offence will not come into force until the Government publishes guidance on what constitutes ‘reasonable procedures’.
