chapters.title: SENIOR MANAGEMENT ARRANGEMENTS SYSTEMS & CONTROLS (“SYSC”)
chapters.summary: 4. SENIOR MANAGEMENT ARRANGEMENTS SYSTEMS & CONTROLS (“SYSC”)
chapters.content:
4. SENIOR MANAGEMENT ARRANGEMENTS SYSTEMS & CONTROLS (“SYSC”)

## 4.1 Purpose

To:  Encourage firms’ directors or partners and senior executives to take appropriate practical responsibility for their firms’ arrangements on matters likely to be of interest to the FCA;  Increase certainty through taking reasonable care to organise and control business affairs responsibly and effectively through adequate risk management systems;  Vest responsibility for managing an effective and responsible organisation in specific directors/partners and senior managers.

This chapter should be read in conjunction with the section on the ‘Senior Managers and Certification Regime (“SMCR”) in chapter 1 and MIFIDPRU 7.2 – 7.3.

## 4.2 Senior Management Responsibilities

As a MIFIDPRU investment firm, Alamut is required to have a clear organisational structure in place with well defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility. Directors or partners and senior managers of a firm must take appropriate practical responsibility for their firm’s arrangements, which are likely to be of interest to the FCA and must take responsibility for effective and responsible organisation of their firm. The management body (defined in the Handbook but essentially the governing body and senior personnel) must:  Consist of at least two independent minds to formulate and implement the policies of the firm  Have overall responsibility for the firm  Approve and have oversight of implementation of the firm’s strategic objectives, risk strategy and internal governance  Ensure the integrity of the firm’s accounting and financial reporting systems, including financial and operational controls and compliance with the regulatory system  Oversee the process of disclosure and communications  Have responsibility for effective oversight of senior management  Monitor and periodically assess the effectiveness of the firm’s governance arrangements  Possess adequate collective knowledge, skills and experience  Commit sufficient time to perform their functions

The senior personnel of a firm must be of sufficiently good repute and sufficiently experienced as to ensure the sound and prudent management of the firm. Where necessary, adequate resources will be employed for the induction and training of members of the management body. It is a requirement that members of the management body do not hold more directorships than is appropriate taking into account individual circumstances and the nature, scale and complexity of the firm’s activities (SYSC 4.3A.5). For the record, for a firm that is classed as a ‘significant SYSC firm’* there are prescribed limitations on the number of directorships that can be held (although that limitation does not apply to members of the management body that represent the UK) - see SYSC 4.3A.6.

- Being any firm where one or more of the following conditions are met:

 its total assets exceed £530 million  its total liabilities exceed £380 million  the annual fees and commission income it receives in relation to the regulated activities carried on by the firm exceeds £160 million in the 12-month period immediately preceding the date the firm carries out the assessment under this rule;  the client money that it receives or holds exceeds £425 million  the assets belonging to its clients that it holds in the course of, or in connection with, its regulated activities exceeds £7.8 billion

## 4.3 Nomination Committee

Alamut does not have a nomination committee. As such it is required to engage a broad set of qualities and competences when recruiting members to the management body. Please see Alamut’s policy on promoting diversity on the management body (SYSC 4.3A.10).

## 4.4 Remuneration Committee

As an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm Alamut is not required to have a remuneration committee. Nevertheless the firm’s management body adopts and periodically reviews the remuneration policy and has overall responsibility for overseeing its implementation.

## 4.5 Risk Committee

As an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm Alamut is not required to have a risk committee. Nevertheless the firm’s management body adopts and periodically reviews the remuneration policy and has overall responsibility for overseeing its implementation. See also ‘Risk Control’ below.

## 4.6 Systems and Controls

What are Systems and Controls? The Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls module requires that a firm must take reasonable care to establish and maintain such systems and controls that are appropriate to its business. The type of systems and controls employed by the regulated firm will depend on a variety of factors including:  The nature, scale and complexity of the firm’s business;  The diversity of its operations, including geographical diversity;  Volume and size of transactions;  Degree of risk associated with each area of its operation.

A firm should carry out a regular review of its systems and controls. For Alamut, the Compliance Officer will carry out this review during periodic compliance monitoring.

## 4.7 Systems and Controls Relevant for Alamut

A full outline of the systems and controls in place is included in the firm’s Senior Management & Systems and Controls Document. Below is a brief overview of the primary systems and controls that have been put into place for Alamut which include: General Organisational Requirements A firm must have robust governance arrangements, which include a clear organisational structure with well defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility, effective processes to identify, manage, monitor and report the risks it is or might be exposed to. This includes internal control mechanisms such as sound administrative and accounting procedures and effective control and safeguard arrangements for information processing systems. In this respect, Alamut will apply an appropriate chain of direct management (control), which concludes with the governing body. Information Handling Alamut must have in place adequate systems and procedures to safeguard the security integrity and confidentiality of information, taking into account the nature of the information in question. Business Continuity Alamut must take reasonable steps to ensure continuity and regularity on the performance of its regulated activities. Alamut must establish, implement and maintain an adequate business continuity policy (‘BCP’) aimed at ensuring, in the case of an interruption to their systems and procedures, that any losses are limited. Where this is not possible, Alamut will enable the timely recovery of such data, functions, services and activities. The BCP will be tested on a regular basis proportionate to the nature of the firm. Alamut’s Business Continuity Policy is retained on file by the Compliance Officer. Accounting Policies A firm must have in place accounting policies and procedures which enable them, at the request of the FCA, to deliver to the FCA, in a timely manner, financial reports which reflect a true and fair view of Alamut’s financial position and which comply with all applicable accounting standards and rules. Alamut will ensure that its internal control mechanisms, etc., permit the verification of its compliance with rules adopted in accordance with requirements at all times. Regular Monitoring Alamut must monitor and evaluate the adequacy and effectiveness of their systems, internal control mechanisms and arrangements. This forms part of the firm’s Compliance Monitoring Programme.

Persons who effectively direct the Business Alamut must ensure that management is undertaken by at least two persons “of sufficiently good repute and sufficiently experienced as to ensure the sound and prudent management of the firm”. Responsibility of Senior Personnel When allocating functions internally the firm must ensure that senior personnel are responsible for ensuring the firm complies with its regulatory obligations. In particular senior personnel must assess and periodically review the effectiveness of the policies, arrangements and procedures put in place to comply with Alamut’s regulatory obligations and take appropriate measures to address any deficiencies. A firm’s arrangements should be such as to furnish its governing body with the information it needs to play its part in identifying, measuring and controlling risks of regulatory concern. Three factors will be the relevance, reliability and timeliness of that information. Senior personnel should receive on a frequent basis, at least annually, written reports on the compliance function and the risk management policies and procedures. This should cover the adequacy and effectiveness of measures taken to address any deficiencies in those policies including failures by relevant persons to comply with such arrangements or processes and mechanisms or follow such policies and procedures.

## 4.8 Data Security and Confidentiality

Firms must have in place systems and procedures to safeguard the security, integrity and confidentiality of information, taking into account the nature of the information in question. The Compliance Officer is responsible for data security arrangements within the Firm. The Firm has a documented data security procedure that describes these arrangements, and this is reviewed on an annual basis. The Firm has adopted a proportionate, Risk Based Approach to data security, that takes into account its customer base, business and risk profile.  All staff members are required to lock their PCs/laptops when not in use.  All staff members are required to make use of confidential waste bins for the disposal of confidential or sensitive data.  Access to IT systems containing customer data, both through laptops and desktops, is controlled using individual user accounts that are password protected.  The Firm employs anti-spyware software and firewalls to protect its IT systems.  All confidential and sensitive data held by the Firm in paper form is stored in lockable filing cabinets.  All information collected for KYC purposes may only be accessed by staff members who require this information to carry out their roles.  Systems prevent unauthorised access to buildings and IT systems when staff members leave the Firm.  All laptops and other portable devices containing customer data are encrypted.  The Firm’s staff training program is tailored to ensure that staff members understand its data security procedures.  Staff members have been made aware that all data security breaches must be reported to the Compliance Officer.  The Firm takes a Risk Based Approach to staff recruitment, with higher vetting standards for staff members who will have access to confidential or sensitive data.  When staff members leave the Firm’s employment, all their IT access rights are permanently disabled.  If data is lost, it is the Firm’s policy to inform affected customers of the data loss in writing, unless the data is encrypted, or where there is law enforcement or regulatory advice to the contrary. Confidentiality is of fundamental importance to the maintenance of the Firm’s integrity, reputation and professional standing. Staff members must not discuss with partners, shareholders, clients, contacts, family or friends any information they may have about the Firm’s clients or any business in which the Firm is involved. Employees have a duty to safeguard and treat as confidential all non-public information concerning the Firm as well as all investors/clients.

## 4.9 Data protection

The General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’) imposes directly binding obligations on the processing of personal data. Like most EU-derived Regulations, post Brexit there is a UK version of the GDPR as well as the original EU version. The UK version mirrors the EU version save that the former is in a UK context e.g. whilst EU GDPR applies in the context of the activities of an establishment of a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ in the EU or of EU data subjects by a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ that is not established in the EU, the UK version applies to the

activities of a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ established in the UK or to a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ of UK data subjects that is not established in the UK. Article 5 of the GDPR establishes the following principles relating to the processing of personal data:  Fairly and lawfully processed in a transparent manner;  Collected for specific and legitimate purposes, and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes;  Adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary;  Accurate and up to date;  Stored for no longer than is necessary;  Processed in a manner that ensures security of personal data, including protection against accidental loss and unauthorised or unlawful processing.

## 4.10 Employees and Agents

A firm’s systems and controls should enable it to satisfy itself of the suitability of anyone who acts for it. The staff of Alamut have been appropriately and properly assessed for suitability. In accordance with existing industry practice, references have been taken up where required. Only personnel with the skills, knowledge and expertise necessary for the role are employed. All relevant persons are aware of the procedures to be followed for the proper discharge of their responsibilities. Alamut must ensure that the performance of multiple functions does not and is not likely to prevent those persons from discharging any particular function soundly, honestly and professionally. The Compliance Officer monitors and evaluates the adequacy and effectiveness of Alamut’s systems, internal control mechanisms and arrangements concerning the segregation of duties.

## 4.11 Compliance, Internal Audit, Financial Crime & Risk

Compliance The beginning of this Compliance Manual contains the information about Alamut’s compliance arrangements. Internal Audit Due to the nature, scale and lack of complexity of Alamut’s business it is not deemed appropriate or proportionate to have an independent internal audit. To the extent that it applies, internal audit remains the responsibility of the Senior Management of the firm. Financial Crime Alamut’s policies and procedures include systems and controls that enable it to identify, assess, monitor and manage money laundering risk. To ensure this happens, regular assessments of the adequacy of these systems and controls are undertaken. Risk Control The management body of Alamut has overall responsibility for risk management and is required to devote sufficient time to the consideration of risk. Alamut maintains its risk management policies and procedures which identify the risks relating to Alamut’s activities, process and systems. Effective arrangements, processes and systems have been adopted to manage the risks that can arise from the investment process, business systems, internal and external sources, and staff. Losses to a client can have an affect on the reputation of Alamut and in some cases prompt regulatory, legal action or compensation. Further details of risk are detailed in Alamut’s ICARA which details applicable risks and mitigating factors and has been approved by the Board. The ICARA is reviewed on at least an annual basis or more frequently where major changes to the business or applicable provisions arise. Alamut’s risks are covered in the ICARA – see chapter on ‘Financial Resources’. Alamut monitors:  The adequacy and effectiveness of their management policies and procedures;  The level of compliance by them and their relevant persons with the arrangements, processes and mechanisms adopted; and  The adequacy and effectiveness of measures taken to address any deficiencies in those policies, procedures, arrangements, processes and mechanisms, including failures by the relevant persons to comply with such arrangements or processes and mechanisms or follow such policies and procedures.

## 4.12 Outsourcing

When relying on a third-party for the performance of operational functions which are critical for the performance of regulated activities on a continuous and satisfactory basis, Alamut must ensure that reasonable steps are taken to avoid undue operational risk. For our MiFID activities, and pragmatically for all our activities, we are subject to both the relevant sections of SYSC 8 of the Handbook and the directly binding rules in Articles 30 – 32 of the MiFID Org Regulation (2017/565 as amended) – see ‘Useful Links’. An operational function is important or critical if a defect or failure in its performance would materially impair the continuing compliance of Alamut with the conditions and obligations of its authorisation, or it other obligations under the regulatory system, or its financial performance, or the soundness or the continuity of its relevant services and activities. Article 30 of the MiFID Org Regulation provides further, limited, guidance on non-critical functions. If Alamut outsources critical or important operational functions it will remain fully responsible for discharging all of its obligations under the regulatory system. The respective rights and obligations of Alamut and the service provider must be clearly allocated and set out in a written agreement. Alamut must ensure the following conditions are met:  The outsourcing must not result in the delegation by senior personnel of their responsibility;  The relationship and obligations of Alamut towards its client under the regulatory system must not be altered;  The conditions with which Alamut must comply in order to be authorised, and remain so, must not be undermined; and  None of the other conditions subject to which Alamut was authorised must be removed or modified. The use of due diligence on the service provider and periodic reporting as to whether the service provider is meeting the relevant undertakings and regulatory obligations is important. Written agreements between the firm and the relevant service provider must comply with Article 31(2) & (3) of the MiFID Org Regulation. Additional requirements arise under Article 32 when a service provider is based in a third country. The Board retains oversight on a periodic basis to ensure that non-material outsourced functions continue to remain appropriate. It should be noted that there are no specific SEC outsourcing rules or requirements. However, a US investment adviser has a fiduciary duty to its clients, and it is incumbent upon an investment adviser to ensure that neither its client’s assets nor personally identifiable information is at risk when placed in the trust of an outside service provider.

Specific to the firm when ‘managing an AIF’ The agreement with the service provider should be in line with Article 75 of Regulation 231/2013 (AIFMD Level 2 Regulation as amended) which is binding on the firm. It is a requirement to notify the FCA of any such delegation before the arrangements become effective, providing the FCA with objective reasons for the delegation (FUND 3.10). An AIFM is not permitted to delegate its functions to the extent that it becomes a ‘letter-box’ entity – FUND 3.10.8 provides further guidance on this. At all times an AIFM remains responsible for those activities it has delegated. As such, effective monitoring of any delegate is required and, if requested by the Regulator, we have to be able to demonstrate that we can withdraw the delegation with immediate effect when this is in the interest of the investor. See also chapter on ‘AIFM specific requirements’ in this manual.

Note that an AIFM not only remains responsible for those activities it has delegated but is also responsible for ensuring compliance with the AIFMD (AIFMD Article 5).

Further information on outsourcing can be found in FUND 3.10 and, as per FUND 3.10.13, in Articles 75 to 82 of the AIFMD Level 2 Regulation.

## 4.13 Record Keeping

Alamut should have appropriate systems and controls in place to fulfil its regulatory and statutory obligations with respect to adequacy, access, periods of retention and security of records. In particular, it must be possible for any amendments to be readily ascertained when compared to the original(s) and, aside from this, it must not be possible for records to be otherwise altered or manipulated. Alamut must retain all records in a medium that allow the storage of information in a way accessible for future reference by the FCA for a minimum period in respect of MiFID business of five years. Other legislative or regulatory requirements may require records to be held for a longer period. Schedule 1 to each module of the Handbook sets out the record keeping requirements of that module e.g. SYSC Schedule 1.2, COBS Schedule 1.3, etc. See SYSC 9. In addition, Article 72 of the MiFID Org Regulation (‘Retention of Records’) is directly binding on the firm. Section 204 of the Advisers Act requires investment advisers to make and keep records and to make and disseminate such reports as the SEC may prescribe by rule. The SEC has indicated in the Implementing Release that ERAs will be subject to recordkeeping requirements that will be the subject of future SEC rulemaking. A projected list of the books and records to be maintained by the Firm pursuant to Section 204 is contained in Appendix O.

## 4.14 Conflicts of Interest and Control of Information

Alamut must take all appropriate steps to identify, and prevent or manage, conflicts of interest. Conflicts could arise both between clients and the firm (including relevant persons and group members) and between one client and another. In terms of this duty, Alamut must not place itself in a position where:  its own interests;  the interests of another customer;

conflict with the interests of the customer for whom Alamut is acting e.g. by making a financial gain or avoiding a financial loss. If a material relationship, arrangement or interest arises which would put the member of staff in such a position where he may be induced to act in a manner which is not in the customer's best interests, they should consult the firm’s Conflicts of Interest Policy. Important: In any circumstances where it is suspected that there is a conflict of interest or a potential conflict may arise, the Compliance Officer should be informed. Alamut keeps records and regularly updates a record of the kinds of service or activity carried out by or on behalf of Alamut in which a conflict of interest entailing a material risk of damage to the interests of one or more clients has arisen or may arise. Alamut maintains and operates effective organisational and administrative arrangements with a view to taking all reasonable steps to identify, manage, and, where possible, prevent conflicts. In particular, attention is drawn to the need for all staff and employees to declare any outside business interests (see the ‘Outside Business Interests Request Form’ in the Appendices of this manual). If these arrangements are not sufficient to ensure with reasonable confidence, that risk of damage to the interests of a client will be prevented, the firm must clearly disclose the general nature and/or sources of conflicts to the client and the steps taken to mitigate those risks before undertaking business on behalf of the client. Any such disclosure is a measure of last resort and shall be used only where the established arrangements are not sufficient to ensure, with reasonable confidence, that the risks of damage to the interest of the client will be prevented. The disclosure must clearly state that the organisational and administrative arrangements established by the firm to prevent or manage that conflict are not sufficient to ensure, with reasonable confidence, that the risks of damage to the interests of the client will be prevented. The disclosure must include specific description of the conflicts of interest that arise in the provision of services, taking into account the nature of the client to whom the disclosure is being made. The description must explain the general nature and sources of conflicts of interest, as well as the risks to the client that arise as a result of the conflicts of interest and the steps undertaken to mitigate these risks, in sufficient detail to enable that client to take an informed decision with respect to the service(s) in the context of which the conflicts of interest arise. With this in mind, reference should be made to the firm’s Conflicts of Interest Policy which identifies the circumstances which either constitute or may give rise to a conflict of interest together with the procedures to be followed and measures to be adopted in order to prevent or manage such conflicts. Further details can be found in Articles 33 and 34 of the MiFID Org Regulation (2017/565 as amended) which are directly binding upon the firm.

Conflicts of interest arising from gifts, hospitality or personal account dealing are covered elsewhere in this compliance manual. All staff are required to keep all information confidential. It is imperative that all information be treated in a sensitive manner as any leak of information may have a material adverse effect on the firm’s interests or the best interests of its clients. All staff are required to report any breaches of confidentiality to the Compliance Officer at the earliest instance. Staff are reminded that any breaches under this section may, in addition to disciplinary action, carry civil or criminal penalties. Where appropriate a Chinese wall will be established to cover situations where information held by a person in the course of carrying out one part of the business needs to be withheld from, or not to be used for, persons with or for whom it acts in the course of carrying on another part of the business.

## 4.15 Whistleblowing

The Governing Body is committed to maintaining the highest standards of honesty, openness and accountability, and recognises that all Members and other members of staff have an important part to play in achieving this goal. Employees will usually be the first to know when someone inside, or connected with, an organisation may be doing something improper, but may feel apprehensive about voicing their concerns. This may be because they feel that speaking up would be disloyal to their colleagues or to the organisation itself. Or it may be because they do not think that their concerns will be taken seriously or they are afraid that they will be penalised in some way. However, the Firm does not believe that it is in anyone's interest for employees with knowledge of wrongdoing to remain silent. The Firm takes all malpractice very seriously, whether it is committed by senior managers, staff members, suppliers or contractors. This Manual sets out a procedure by which staff can report their concerns.

The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (the “Dodd-Frank Act”) The Dodd-Frank Act, among other requirements, has established new whistleblower provisions that extend beyond the provisions of the Sarbanes Oxley Act of 2002 which encouraged employees to report wrongdoing internally. Under the Dodd-Frank Act, employees are encouraged to report directly to the SEC. The SEC may also pay an award, subject to limitations, to eligible whistleblowers. Employees are protected from retaliation by employers provided the employee is acting honestly. The Dodd-Frank Act establishes a private cause of action in the event that an employee who becomes a whistleblower is discharged or discriminated against.

The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 amends the Employment Rights Act 1996 to give protection from victimisation and dismissal to individuals who make certain disclosures in the public interest. In normal circumstances a "qualifying disclosure" is one which satisfies the three criteria below: a. It is made in good faith; b. It is made in the reasonable belief that the information disclosed tends to reveal one or more of the following:

- That a criminal offence has been, is being or is likely to be committed;

- That there has been, is or is likely to be a failure to comply with a legal obligation;

- That the health or safety of any individual has been, is being or is likely to be endangered;

- That the environment has been, is being or is likely to be damaged; and

- That there is information that shows that one of the above has been, is being or is likely to be

concealed. c. It is made to one of the following:

- The employer (or the person specified by the employer under any internal whistleblowing

procedure);

- Where the disclosure concerns the actions of a person other than the employer, that person;

- If the disclosure is made in the course of obtaining legal advice, a legal adviser (the requirement

of “good faith” does not apply here);

- In the case of employers of Non Departmental Public Bodies, the relevant Government Minister;

and

- Where the employee additionally believes that the allegation, and any information contained in

the allegation, is substantially true, a person or body prescribed by the Secretary of State.

Notwithstanding The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 or the Dodd-Frank Act, all staff members remain obligated to follow compliance reporting procedures, as outlined in this document and any other Firm policy documents. Reportable activities It is not possible to give an exhaustive list of the activities that constitute malpractice but, broadly speaking, the Firm would expect you to report the following:  Criminal offences;  Failure to comply with legal obligations;  Miscarriages of justice;  Actions which endanger the health or safety of staff members or the public;  Actions which cause damage to the environment; and  Actions which are intended to conceal any of the above.

It will not always be clear that a particular action falls within one of these categories and Members and other employees must use their own judgement in this regard. However, the Firm would prefer staff members to report their concerns rather than keep them to themselves. If a report is made in good faith, then, even if it is not confirmed by an investigation, the initial concern will be valued and the member of staff will not be liable to disciplinary action. If a false report is made, maliciously or for personal gain, then disciplinary action may result. This procedure is not a substitute for the Firm’s grievance procedure. This should be used if members of staff have a complaint or concern in relation to any internal procedure or action which affects their employment directly.

Making a report A report may be made either verbally or in writing. The Firm would normally expect concerns to be raised internally with a designated Member. The most appropriate individual to report to will depend on the seriousness of the alleged malpractice and who the reporting member of staff believes is involved. If they have not already done so, the Member may request that the reporting member of staff clearly sets down their concerns in writing, together with any evidence supporting their concerns. Any suspicion of Money Laundering or related Financial Crime must be reported to the MLRO. The Firm does not expect the individual making the report to have absolute proof of any alleged malpractice. However, they will need to be able to show the reason(s) for their concern. The Firm will do everything in its power to keep the identity of the reporting individual secret if they so wish. However, there may be circumstances where their identity needs to be disclosed, for example, if the report becomes the subject of a criminal investigation, wherein they may be needed as a witness, or where their identity needs to be disclosed to the regulatory authorities. Should this be the case, the matter will be discussed with the individual at the earliest possible opportunity.

Investigating reports Once a report has been made, the Firm will acknowledge receipt of it within five business days. The Firm will endeavour to deal with any concerns raised under this procedure quickly and efficiently. The Firm will need to make preliminary enquiries to decide whether a full investigation is necessary. If such an investigation is deemed necessary then, depending on the nature of the misconduct, the initial concerns will be either:  Investigated internally (by senior management); or  Referred to the appropriate external person (for example, the Firm’s external auditors, the FCA or the police) for investigation. Subject to any legal constraints, the Firm will inform the reporting member of staff of the outcome of the preliminary enquiries, full investigation and any further action that has been taken. If the reporting member of staff is unhappy with the outcome of an investigation, the Firm would prefer that a separate report be submitted explaining why this is the case. The fresh concerns will subsequently be investigated where there is good reason to do so. The reporting individual may not think that this process is appropriate and may want to raise their concerns with an external organisation, such as a regulator. It is, of course, open for them to do so, provided that they have sufficient evidence to support their claim.

## 4.16 MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code

The MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code applies to MIFIDPRU firms such as Alamut and is incorporated in SYSC 19G. The Code is applied on a proportional basis in line with SYSC 19G.2.5. The aim of the Code is to ensure that firms have gender-neutral risk-focussed remuneration policies which are consistent with, and promote, effective risk management and do not expose them to excessive risk. The term ‘remuneration’ encompasses any form of remuneration including salaries, discretionary pension benefits, and benefits of any kind including ‘variable remuneration’. With regard to the latter, a MIFIDPRU investment firm must not pay variable remuneration to members of the management body who do not perform any executive function in the firm. Some elements of the Code will apply to a firm on an individual basis whilst, where relevant, other elements will apply on a consolidated basis i.e. where a UK parent entity exists then the parent and all investment firms etc. in the group will be regarded as if they all formed a single MIFIDPRU investment firm – SYSC 19G.1.16 onwards provides further information. As referenced above the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code is set out in SYSC 19G. However not all of SYSC 19G will apply to all firms. SYSC 19G.1.7 includes a table which sets out the application of SYSC 19G for a:

- SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm

- Non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm falling within the exemption in SYSC 19G.1.1(2)

- Non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm not falling within the exemption in SYSC 19G.1.1(2)

The SYSC 19G.1.1(2) exemption for non-SNI firms is available where a firm’s on-balance sheet assets and off-balance sheet items (as defined in Annex 1 of the UK CRR – see ‘Useful Links’) – over the preceding 4- year period is a rolling average of: 1) £100m or less; or 2) £300m or less and (i) the exposure value of the firm’s on and off-balance sheet trading book business is £150m or less and (ii) the exposure value of the firm’s on and off-balance sheet derivative business is £100m or less As a CPMI the above thresholds are assessed on both the firm’s MiFID business and non-MiFID business. The firm is classified as an ‘SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm’. As such only the following elements of the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code apply:  SYSC 19G.2 (Remuneration policies and practices)  SYSC 19G.3.1R to SYSC 19G.3.3R (Oversight of remuneration policies and practices)  SYSC 19G.3.6R to SYSC 19G.3.8G (Control functions)  SYSC 19G.4.1R to SYSC 19G.4.5R and SYSC 19G.4.7G(1) and SYSC 19G.4.7G(2) (Fixed and variable components of remuneration)  SYSC 19G.6.1R (Remuneration and capital)  SYSC 19G.6.2R (Exceptional government intervention)  SYSC 19G.6.5R to SYSC 19G.6.6G (Assessment of performance)

## 4.17 AIFMD Remuneration Code

The AIFMD Remuneration Code applies to Alamut and is incorporated in SYSC 19B. The Code is applied on a proportional basis. As the firm is also subject to the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code (SYSC 19G) we are required to follow both Codes. Should a differing provisions arise with the effect that only one of them can be complied with then we are required to comply with the most stringent of the relevant provision – SYSC 19G.1.21 provides some examples. The aim of the Code is to ensure that firms have risk-focussed remuneration policies which are consistent with, and promote, effective risk management and do not encourage risk-taking which is inconsistent with the risk profile of the AIF. The term ‘remuneration’ encompasses any form of remuneration including salaries, discretionary pension benefits and benefits of any kind. The Code is based upon 9 Principles (which can be found in SYSC 19B). For the record the FCA has adopted a ‘proportionate approach’ in applying the Code in that not all the Principles are necessarily relevant to all firms (See ‘Useful Links’ below re the FCA’s ‘Remuneration Guide: AIFMD’). Specifically, Principles 5(e) (SYSC 19B.1.17 “retention”); 5(f) (SYSC 19B.1.18 “deferral”); and 5(g) (SYSC 19B.1.19 & 20 “performance adjustment”) – which together are known as the ‘Pay-out Process Rules’ – can be disapplied when proportionate. However when considering ‘proportionality’ under the AIFMD Remuneration Code it is important to bear in mind the comments made at the beginning of this section i.e. as the firm is subject to two Remuneration Codes it is required to comply with the most stringent of the relevant provisions.

In general the Code applies to ‘AIFM Remuneration Code staff’. The latter comprises categories of staff including senior management, risk takers, control functions and any employee receiving total remuneration that takes them into the same remuneration bracket as senior management and risk takers, whose professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm as an AIFM or on the AIFs. For the purpose of implementing the Code, control functions (AIFM Remuneration Principle 4) are defined by ESMA as “staff (other than senior management) responsible for risk management, compliance, internal audit and similar functions within an AIFM (e.g. the CFO to the extent that he/she is responsible for the preparation of the financial statements)”. Unless it can be demonstrated to the contrary, in its guidance ESMA includes the following as AIFM Remuneration Code staff (referred to in the guidance as ‘identified staff’):  Executive and non-executive members of the governing body of the AIFM  senior management  control functions  staff responsible for heading portfolio management, administration, marketing and human resources  other risk takers. The above will also extend to the relevant staff of any entity which Alamut may have delegated portfolio management or risk management activities to and whose professional activities have a material impact on the risk profiles of the AIF. Alamut maintains a record of its AIFM Remuneration Code staff. The AIF annual report requires certain remuneration disclosures to be made – see ‘AIFM Specific requirements’ chapter.

## 4.18 MiFID Remuneration Code

Unlike the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code, the MiFID Remuneration Code (SYSC 19F) is not directed towards ‘risk takers’ but rather to a wider range of staff – specifically to those ‘relevant persons’ with an impact (directly or indirectly) on the investment and ancillary services provided by the firm or on its corporate behaviour. ‘Relevant persons’ are defined in Article 2 of the MiFID Org Regulation (see ‘Useful Links’) but in brief captures:  all directors/partners of the firm (and tied agents where applicable)  employees, or persons acting under a contract for services, that are involved in the provision of investment services and activities  natural persons directly involved in the provision of services to the firm under an outsourcing arrangement for the purposes of the provision by the firm of investment services and activities

The purpose of the Code is to prevent potential conflicts so as to ensure that clients are treated fairly and that their interests are not impaired by the remuneration practices by the firm in the short, medium or ling terms. As such, remuneration (and any similar incentives) must not be solely or predominantly based upon quantitative commercial criteria but must take into account appropriate qualitative criteria reflecting compliance with applicable regulations, the fair treatment of clients and the quality of services provided to clients. To enhance the above, a balance between the ‘fixed’ and ‘variable’ components of remuneration will be maintained at all times with the purpose of ensuring that the remuneration structure does not favour the interests of the firm, or its relevant persons, against the interests of clients. Although it is a requirement to take advice from the compliance function when approving the firm’s remuneration policy, MiFID imposes responsibility on the senior management for the day-to-day implementation of the remuneration policy and for the monitoring of compliance risks provided to clients.

## 4.19 Useful links

FCA Remuneration Policy Statement Template https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/policy/remuneration-policy-statement-template.docx

Remuneration Guide: AIFMD https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/finalised-guidance/fg14-02.pdf

ESMA Remuneration Guidelines: AIFMD https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/2015/11/2013 232_aifmd_guidelines_on_remuneration_-_en.pdf

FSA/FCA Guidance on the risks to customers from financial incentives https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/finalised-guidance/fsa-fg13-01.pdf

FCA Guidance on risks to customers from performance management fees https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/finalised-guidance/fg15-10.pdf

MiFID Org Regulation (2017/565) https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2017/565/contents

SI 2018/1403 (amending EU 2017/565) https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2018/1403/contents/made

Whistleblowing https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/documents/whistleblowing-poster.pdf

AIFMD Level 2 Regulation (231/2013) https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32013R0231&from=EN

SI 2019/328 (amending the above Regulation 231/2013) https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2019/328/contents/made

UK CRR https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2013/575/contents

sections.title: Purpose
sections.content:
To:  Encourage firms’ directors or partners and senior executives to take appropriate practical responsibility for their firms’ arrangements on matters likely to be of interest to the FCA;  Increase certainty through taking reasonable care to organise and control business affairs responsibly and effectively through adequate risk management systems;  Vest responsibility for managing an effective and responsible organisation in specific directors/partners and senior managers.

This chapter should be read in conjunction with the section on the ‘Senior Managers and Certification Regime (“SMCR”) in chapter 1 and MIFIDPRU 7.2 – 7.3.

sections.title: Senior Management Responsibilities
sections.content:
As a MIFIDPRU investment firm, Alamut is required to have a clear organisational structure in place with well defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility. Directors or partners and senior managers of a firm must take appropriate practical responsibility for their firm’s arrangements, which are likely to be of interest to the FCA and must take responsibility for effective and responsible organisation of their firm. The management body (defined in the Handbook but essentially the governing body and senior personnel) must:  Consist of at least two independent minds to formulate and implement the policies of the firm  Have overall responsibility for the firm  Approve and have oversight of implementation of the firm’s strategic objectives, risk strategy and internal governance  Ensure the integrity of the firm’s accounting and financial reporting systems, including financial and operational controls and compliance with the regulatory system  Oversee the process of disclosure and communications  Have responsibility for effective oversight of senior management  Monitor and periodically assess the effectiveness of the firm’s governance arrangements  Possess adequate collective knowledge, skills and experience  Commit sufficient time to perform their functions

The senior personnel of a firm must be of sufficiently good repute and sufficiently experienced as to ensure the sound and prudent management of the firm. Where necessary, adequate resources will be employed for the induction and training of members of the management body. It is a requirement that members of the management body do not hold more directorships than is appropriate taking into account individual circumstances and the nature, scale and complexity of the firm’s activities (SYSC 4.3A.5). For the record, for a firm that is classed as a ‘significant SYSC firm’* there are prescribed limitations on the number of directorships that can be held (although that limitation does not apply to members of the management body that represent the UK) - see SYSC 4.3A.6.

- Being any firm where one or more of the following conditions are met:

 its total assets exceed £530 million  its total liabilities exceed £380 million  the annual fees and commission income it receives in relation to the regulated activities carried on by the firm exceeds £160 million in the 12-month period immediately preceding the date the firm carries out the assessment under this rule;  the client money that it receives or holds exceeds £425 million  the assets belonging to its clients that it holds in the course of, or in connection with, its regulated activities exceeds £7.8 billion

sections.title: Nomination Committee
sections.content: Alamut does not have a nomination committee. As such it is required to engage a broad set of qualities and competences when recruiting members to the management body. Please see Alamut’s policy on promoting diversity on the management body (SYSC 4.3A.10).

sections.title: Remuneration Committee
sections.content: As an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm Alamut is not required to have a remuneration committee. Nevertheless the firm’s management body adopts and periodically reviews the remuneration policy and has overall responsibility for overseeing its implementation.

sections.title: Risk Committee
sections.content: As an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm Alamut is not required to have a risk committee. Nevertheless the firm’s management body adopts and periodically reviews the remuneration policy and has overall responsibility for overseeing its implementation. See also ‘Risk Control’ below.

sections.title: Systems and Controls
sections.content:
What are Systems and Controls? The Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls module requires that a firm must take reasonable care to establish and maintain such systems and controls that are appropriate to its business. The type of systems and controls employed by the regulated firm will depend on a variety of factors including:  The nature, scale and complexity of the firm’s business;  The diversity of its operations, including geographical diversity;  Volume and size of transactions;  Degree of risk associated with each area of its operation.

A firm should carry out a regular review of its systems and controls. For Alamut, the Compliance Officer will carry out this review during periodic compliance monitoring.

sections.title: Systems and Controls Relevant for Alamut
sections.content:
A full outline of the systems and controls in place is included in the firm’s Senior Management & Systems and Controls Document. Below is a brief overview of the primary systems and controls that have been put into place for Alamut which include: General Organisational Requirements A firm must have robust governance arrangements, which include a clear organisational structure with well defined, transparent and consistent lines of responsibility, effective processes to identify, manage, monitor and report the risks it is or might be exposed to. This includes internal control mechanisms such as sound administrative and accounting procedures and effective control and safeguard arrangements for information processing systems. In this respect, Alamut will apply an appropriate chain of direct management (control), which concludes with the governing body. Information Handling Alamut must have in place adequate systems and procedures to safeguard the security integrity and confidentiality of information, taking into account the nature of the information in question. Business Continuity Alamut must take reasonable steps to ensure continuity and regularity on the performance of its regulated activities. Alamut must establish, implement and maintain an adequate business continuity policy (‘BCP’) aimed at ensuring, in the case of an interruption to their systems and procedures, that any losses are limited. Where this is not possible, Alamut will enable the timely recovery of such data, functions, services and activities. The BCP will be tested on a regular basis proportionate to the nature of the firm. Alamut’s Business Continuity Policy is retained on file by the Compliance Officer. Accounting Policies A firm must have in place accounting policies and procedures which enable them, at the request of the FCA, to deliver to the FCA, in a timely manner, financial reports which reflect a true and fair view of Alamut’s financial position and which comply with all applicable accounting standards and rules. Alamut will ensure that its internal control mechanisms, etc., permit the verification of its compliance with rules adopted in accordance with requirements at all times. Regular Monitoring Alamut must monitor and evaluate the adequacy and effectiveness of their systems, internal control mechanisms and arrangements. This forms part of the firm’s Compliance Monitoring Programme.

Persons who effectively direct the Business Alamut must ensure that management is undertaken by at least two persons “of sufficiently good repute and sufficiently experienced as to ensure the sound and prudent management of the firm”. Responsibility of Senior Personnel When allocating functions internally the firm must ensure that senior personnel are responsible for ensuring the firm complies with its regulatory obligations. In particular senior personnel must assess and periodically review the effectiveness of the policies, arrangements and procedures put in place to comply with Alamut’s regulatory obligations and take appropriate measures to address any deficiencies. A firm’s arrangements should be such as to furnish its governing body with the information it needs to play its part in identifying, measuring and controlling risks of regulatory concern. Three factors will be the relevance, reliability and timeliness of that information. Senior personnel should receive on a frequent basis, at least annually, written reports on the compliance function and the risk management policies and procedures. This should cover the adequacy and effectiveness of measures taken to address any deficiencies in those policies including failures by relevant persons to comply with such arrangements or processes and mechanisms or follow such policies and procedures.

sections.title: Data Security and Confidentiality
sections.content: Firms must have in place systems and procedures to safeguard the security, integrity and confidentiality of information, taking into account the nature of the information in question. The Compliance Officer is responsible for data security arrangements within the Firm. The Firm has a documented data security procedure that describes these arrangements, and this is reviewed on an annual basis. The Firm has adopted a proportionate, Risk Based Approach to data security, that takes into account its customer base, business and risk profile.  All staff members are required to lock their PCs/laptops when not in use.  All staff members are required to make use of confidential waste bins for the disposal of confidential or sensitive data.  Access to IT systems containing customer data, both through laptops and desktops, is controlled using individual user accounts that are password protected.  The Firm employs anti-spyware software and firewalls to protect its IT systems.  All confidential and sensitive data held by the Firm in paper form is stored in lockable filing cabinets.  All information collected for KYC purposes may only be accessed by staff members who require this information to carry out their roles.  Systems prevent unauthorised access to buildings and IT systems when staff members leave the Firm.  All laptops and other portable devices containing customer data are encrypted.  The Firm’s staff training program is tailored to ensure that staff members understand its data security procedures.  Staff members have been made aware that all data security breaches must be reported to the Compliance Officer.  The Firm takes a Risk Based Approach to staff recruitment, with higher vetting standards for staff members who will have access to confidential or sensitive data.  When staff members leave the Firm’s employment, all their IT access rights are permanently disabled.  If data is lost, it is the Firm’s policy to inform affected customers of the data loss in writing, unless the data is encrypted, or where there is law enforcement or regulatory advice to the contrary. Confidentiality is of fundamental importance to the maintenance of the Firm’s integrity, reputation and professional standing. Staff members must not discuss with partners, shareholders, clients, contacts, family or friends any information they may have about the Firm’s clients or any business in which the Firm is involved. Employees have a duty to safeguard and treat as confidential all non-public information concerning the Firm as well as all investors/clients.

sections.title: Data protection
sections.content:
The General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’) imposes directly binding obligations on the processing of personal data. Like most EU-derived Regulations, post Brexit there is a UK version of the GDPR as well as the original EU version. The UK version mirrors the EU version save that the former is in a UK context e.g. whilst EU GDPR applies in the context of the activities of an establishment of a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ in the EU or of EU data subjects by a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ that is not established in the EU, the UK version applies to the

activities of a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ established in the UK or to a ‘controller’ or ‘processor’ of UK data subjects that is not established in the UK. Article 5 of the GDPR establishes the following principles relating to the processing of personal data:  Fairly and lawfully processed in a transparent manner;  Collected for specific and legitimate purposes, and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes;  Adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary;  Accurate and up to date;  Stored for no longer than is necessary;  Processed in a manner that ensures security of personal data, including protection against accidental loss and unauthorised or unlawful processing.

sections.title: Employees and Agents
sections.content: A firm’s systems and controls should enable it to satisfy itself of the suitability of anyone who acts for it. The staff of Alamut have been appropriately and properly assessed for suitability. In accordance with existing industry practice, references have been taken up where required. Only personnel with the skills, knowledge and expertise necessary for the role are employed. All relevant persons are aware of the procedures to be followed for the proper discharge of their responsibilities. Alamut must ensure that the performance of multiple functions does not and is not likely to prevent those persons from discharging any particular function soundly, honestly and professionally. The Compliance Officer monitors and evaluates the adequacy and effectiveness of Alamut’s systems, internal control mechanisms and arrangements concerning the segregation of duties.

sections.title: Compliance, Internal Audit, Financial Crime & Risk
sections.content: Compliance The beginning of this Compliance Manual contains the information about Alamut’s compliance arrangements. Internal Audit Due to the nature, scale and lack of complexity of Alamut’s business it is not deemed appropriate or proportionate to have an independent internal audit. To the extent that it applies, internal audit remains the responsibility of the Senior Management of the firm. Financial Crime Alamut’s policies and procedures include systems and controls that enable it to identify, assess, monitor and manage money laundering risk. To ensure this happens, regular assessments of the adequacy of these systems and controls are undertaken. Risk Control The management body of Alamut has overall responsibility for risk management and is required to devote sufficient time to the consideration of risk. Alamut maintains its risk management policies and procedures which identify the risks relating to Alamut’s activities, process and systems. Effective arrangements, processes and systems have been adopted to manage the risks that can arise from the investment process, business systems, internal and external sources, and staff. Losses to a client can have an affect on the reputation of Alamut and in some cases prompt regulatory, legal action or compensation. Further details of risk are detailed in Alamut’s ICARA which details applicable risks and mitigating factors and has been approved by the Board. The ICARA is reviewed on at least an annual basis or more frequently where major changes to the business or applicable provisions arise. Alamut’s risks are covered in the ICARA – see chapter on ‘Financial Resources’. Alamut monitors:  The adequacy and effectiveness of their management policies and procedures;  The level of compliance by them and their relevant persons with the arrangements, processes and mechanisms adopted; and  The adequacy and effectiveness of measures taken to address any deficiencies in those policies, procedures, arrangements, processes and mechanisms, including failures by the relevant persons to comply with such arrangements or processes and mechanisms or follow such policies and procedures.

sections.title: Outsourcing
sections.content:
When relying on a third-party for the performance of operational functions which are critical for the performance of regulated activities on a continuous and satisfactory basis, Alamut must ensure that reasonable steps are taken to avoid undue operational risk. For our MiFID activities, and pragmatically for all our activities, we are subject to both the relevant sections of SYSC 8 of the Handbook and the directly binding rules in Articles 30 – 32 of the MiFID Org Regulation (2017/565 as amended) – see ‘Useful Links’. An operational function is important or critical if a defect or failure in its performance would materially impair the continuing compliance of Alamut with the conditions and obligations of its authorisation, or it other obligations under the regulatory system, or its financial performance, or the soundness or the continuity of its relevant services and activities. Article 30 of the MiFID Org Regulation provides further, limited, guidance on non-critical functions. If Alamut outsources critical or important operational functions it will remain fully responsible for discharging all of its obligations under the regulatory system. The respective rights and obligations of Alamut and the service provider must be clearly allocated and set out in a written agreement. Alamut must ensure the following conditions are met:  The outsourcing must not result in the delegation by senior personnel of their responsibility;  The relationship and obligations of Alamut towards its client under the regulatory system must not be altered;  The conditions with which Alamut must comply in order to be authorised, and remain so, must not be undermined; and  None of the other conditions subject to which Alamut was authorised must be removed or modified. The use of due diligence on the service provider and periodic reporting as to whether the service provider is meeting the relevant undertakings and regulatory obligations is important. Written agreements between the firm and the relevant service provider must comply with Article 31(2) & (3) of the MiFID Org Regulation. Additional requirements arise under Article 32 when a service provider is based in a third country. The Board retains oversight on a periodic basis to ensure that non-material outsourced functions continue to remain appropriate. It should be noted that there are no specific SEC outsourcing rules or requirements. However, a US investment adviser has a fiduciary duty to its clients, and it is incumbent upon an investment adviser to ensure that neither its client’s assets nor personally identifiable information is at risk when placed in the trust of an outside service provider.

Specific to the firm when ‘managing an AIF’ The agreement with the service provider should be in line with Article 75 of Regulation 231/2013 (AIFMD Level 2 Regulation as amended) which is binding on the firm. It is a requirement to notify the FCA of any such delegation before the arrangements become effective, providing the FCA with objective reasons for the delegation (FUND 3.10). An AIFM is not permitted to delegate its functions to the extent that it becomes a ‘letter-box’ entity – FUND 3.10.8 provides further guidance on this. At all times an AIFM remains responsible for those activities it has delegated. As such, effective monitoring of any delegate is required and, if requested by the Regulator, we have to be able to demonstrate that we can withdraw the delegation with immediate effect when this is in the interest of the investor. See also chapter on ‘AIFM specific requirements’ in this manual.

Note that an AIFM not only remains responsible for those activities it has delegated but is also responsible for ensuring compliance with the AIFMD (AIFMD Article 5).

Further information on outsourcing can be found in FUND 3.10 and, as per FUND 3.10.13, in Articles 75 to 82 of the AIFMD Level 2 Regulation.

sections.title: Record Keeping
sections.content: Alamut should have appropriate systems and controls in place to fulfil its regulatory and statutory obligations with respect to adequacy, access, periods of retention and security of records. In particular, it must be possible for any amendments to be readily ascertained when compared to the original(s) and, aside from this, it must not be possible for records to be otherwise altered or manipulated. Alamut must retain all records in a medium that allow the storage of information in a way accessible for future reference by the FCA for a minimum period in respect of MiFID business of five years. Other legislative or regulatory requirements may require records to be held for a longer period. Schedule 1 to each module of the Handbook sets out the record keeping requirements of that module e.g. SYSC Schedule 1.2, COBS Schedule 1.3, etc. See SYSC 9. In addition, Article 72 of the MiFID Org Regulation (‘Retention of Records’) is directly binding on the firm. Section 204 of the Advisers Act requires investment advisers to make and keep records and to make and disseminate such reports as the SEC may prescribe by rule. The SEC has indicated in the Implementing Release that ERAs will be subject to recordkeeping requirements that will be the subject of future SEC rulemaking. A projected list of the books and records to be maintained by the Firm pursuant to Section 204 is contained in Appendix O.

sections.title: Conflicts of Interest and Control of Information
Whistleblowing
MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code
sections.content:
The MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code applies to MIFIDPRU firms such as Alamut and is incorporated in SYSC 19G. The Code is applied on a proportional basis in line with SYSC 19G.2.5. The aim of the Code is to ensure that firms have gender-neutral risk-focussed remuneration policies which are consistent with, and promote, effective risk management and do not expose them to excessive risk. The term ‘remuneration’ encompasses any form of remuneration including salaries, discretionary pension benefits, and benefits of any kind including ‘variable remuneration’. With regard to the latter, a MIFIDPRU investment firm must not pay variable remuneration to members of the management body who do not perform any executive function in the firm. Some elements of the Code will apply to a firm on an individual basis whilst, where relevant, other elements will apply on a consolidated basis i.e. where a UK parent entity exists then the parent and all investment firms etc. in the group will be regarded as if they all formed a single MIFIDPRU investment firm – SYSC 19G.1.16 onwards provides further information. As referenced above the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code is set out in SYSC 19G. However not all of SYSC 19G will apply to all firms. SYSC 19G.1.7 includes a table which sets out the application of SYSC 19G for a:

- SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm

- Non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm falling within the exemption in SYSC 19G.1.1(2)

- Non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm not falling within the exemption in SYSC 19G.1.1(2)

The SYSC 19G.1.1(2) exemption for non-SNI firms is available where a firm’s on-balance sheet assets and off-balance sheet items (as defined in Annex 1 of the UK CRR – see ‘Useful Links’) – over the preceding 4- year period is a rolling average of: 1) £100m or less; or 2) £300m or less and (i) the exposure value of the firm’s on and off-balance sheet trading book business is £150m or less and (ii) the exposure value of the firm’s on and off-balance sheet derivative business is £100m or less As a CPMI the above thresholds are assessed on both the firm’s MiFID business and non-MiFID business. The firm is classified as an ‘SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm’. As such only the following elements of the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code apply:  SYSC 19G.2 (Remuneration policies and practices)  SYSC 19G.3.1R to SYSC 19G.3.3R (Oversight of remuneration policies and practices)  SYSC 19G.3.6R to SYSC 19G.3.8G (Control functions)  SYSC 19G.4.1R to SYSC 19G.4.5R and SYSC 19G.4.7G(1) and SYSC 19G.4.7G(2) (Fixed and variable components of remuneration)  SYSC 19G.6.1R (Remuneration and capital)  SYSC 19G.6.2R (Exceptional government intervention)  SYSC 19G.6.5R to SYSC 19G.6.6G (Assessment of performance)

sections.title: AIFMD Remuneration Code
sections.content:
The AIFMD Remuneration Code applies to Alamut and is incorporated in SYSC 19B. The Code is applied on a proportional basis. As the firm is also subject to the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code (SYSC 19G) we are required to follow both Codes. Should a differing provisions arise with the effect that only one of them can be complied with then we are required to comply with the most stringent of the relevant provision – SYSC 19G.1.21 provides some examples. The aim of the Code is to ensure that firms have risk-focussed remuneration policies which are consistent with, and promote, effective risk management and do not encourage risk-taking which is inconsistent with the risk profile of the AIF. The term ‘remuneration’ encompasses any form of remuneration including salaries, discretionary pension benefits and benefits of any kind. The Code is based upon 9 Principles (which can be found in SYSC 19B). For the record the FCA has adopted a ‘proportionate approach’ in applying the Code in that not all the Principles are necessarily relevant to all firms (See ‘Useful Links’ below re the FCA’s ‘Remuneration Guide: AIFMD’). Specifically, Principles 5(e) (SYSC 19B.1.17 “retention”); 5(f) (SYSC 19B.1.18 “deferral”); and 5(g) (SYSC 19B.1.19 & 20 “performance adjustment”) – which together are known as the ‘Pay-out Process Rules’ – can be disapplied when proportionate. However when considering ‘proportionality’ under the AIFMD Remuneration Code it is important to bear in mind the comments made at the beginning of this section i.e. as the firm is subject to two Remuneration Codes it is required to comply with the most stringent of the relevant provisions.

In general the Code applies to ‘AIFM Remuneration Code staff’. The latter comprises categories of staff including senior management, risk takers, control functions and any employee receiving total remuneration that takes them into the same remuneration bracket as senior management and risk takers, whose professional activities have a material impact on the risk profile of the firm as an AIFM or on the AIFs. For the purpose of implementing the Code, control functions (AIFM Remuneration Principle 4) are defined by ESMA as “staff (other than senior management) responsible for risk management, compliance, internal audit and similar functions within an AIFM (e.g. the CFO to the extent that he/she is responsible for the preparation of the financial statements)”. Unless it can be demonstrated to the contrary, in its guidance ESMA includes the following as AIFM Remuneration Code staff (referred to in the guidance as ‘identified staff’):  Executive and non-executive members of the governing body of the AIFM  senior management  control functions  staff responsible for heading portfolio management, administration, marketing and human resources  other risk takers. The above will also extend to the relevant staff of any entity which Alamut may have delegated portfolio management or risk management activities to and whose professional activities have a material impact on the risk profiles of the AIF. Alamut maintains a record of its AIFM Remuneration Code staff. The AIF annual report requires certain remuneration disclosures to be made – see ‘AIFM Specific requirements’ chapter.

sections.title: MiFID Remuneration Code
sections.content:
Unlike the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code, the MiFID Remuneration Code (SYSC 19F) is not directed towards ‘risk takers’ but rather to a wider range of staff – specifically to those ‘relevant persons’ with an impact (directly or indirectly) on the investment and ancillary services provided by the firm or on its corporate behaviour. ‘Relevant persons’ are defined in Article 2 of the MiFID Org Regulation (see ‘Useful Links’) but in brief captures:  all directors/partners of the firm (and tied agents where applicable)  employees, or persons acting under a contract for services, that are involved in the provision of investment services and activities  natural persons directly involved in the provision of services to the firm under an outsourcing arrangement for the purposes of the provision by the firm of investment services and activities

The purpose of the Code is to prevent potential conflicts so as to ensure that clients are treated fairly and that their interests are not impaired by the remuneration practices by the firm in the short, medium or ling terms. As such, remuneration (and any similar incentives) must not be solely or predominantly based upon quantitative commercial criteria but must take into account appropriate qualitative criteria reflecting compliance with applicable regulations, the fair treatment of clients and the quality of services provided to clients. To enhance the above, a balance between the ‘fixed’ and ‘variable’ components of remuneration will be maintained at all times with the purpose of ensuring that the remuneration structure does not favour the interests of the firm, or its relevant persons, against the interests of clients. Although it is a requirement to take advice from the compliance function when approving the firm’s remuneration policy, MiFID imposes responsibility on the senior management for the day-to-day implementation of the remuneration policy and for the monitoring of compliance risks provided to clients.

chapters.title: REGULATORY CAPITAL AND LIQUIDITY
chapters.summary: 5. REGULATORY CAPITAL AND LIQUIDITY
chapters.content:
5. REGULATORY CAPITAL AND LIQUIDITY

## 5.1 Introduction

As a full-scope UK AIFM that also undertakes permitted additional MiFID type activities in addition to ‘managing an AIF’ the firm falls within the definition of a ‘collective portfolio management investment firm’ (CPMI). The firm is also an ‘FCA investment firm’ (which the Handbook refers to as a ‘MIFIDPRU investment firm’). For prudential purposes Alamut is therefore subject to both IPRU-INV 11 for its activities relating ‘to managing an AIF’ and to MIFIDPRU for its MiFID business. However on occasion MIFIDPRU will apply to all of the firm’s activities e.g. the calculation of the fixed overheads requirement (FOR – MIFIDPRU 4.5). Fortunately the two prudential regimes run in parallel so that a capital instrument or a liquid asset may be used to meet either or both sets of requirements provided that it meets the conditions in both MIFIDPRU and IPRU-INV 11. For MIFIDPRU it will be necessary to determine whether the firm is a ‘SNI’ or an ‘non-SNI’ MIFIDPRU investment firm.

## 5.2 SNI or non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm

As mentioned above all firms subject to MIFIDPRU will need to determine whether they are an ‘SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm’ or a ‘non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm’ – the impact of MIFIDPRU is less onerous for the former type (‘SNI’ is an abbreviation for a firm that is ‘small and non-interconnected’). Alamut is categorised as an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm for its MiFID business. To be categorised as an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm a firm needs to satisfy the following conditions: 1) its average AUM, calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.7.5, is less than £1.2 billion 2) its average client orders handled (COH), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.10.19, is less than: a. £100 million per day for cash trades; and b. £1 billion per day for derivatives trades 3) its average assets safeguarded and administered relating to MiFID business (ASA), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.9.8 is zero 4) its average client money held relating to MiFID business (CMH), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.8.13, is zero 5) it does not have permission to deal on own account 6) its on- and off-balance sheet total is less than £100 million 7) its total annual gross revenue from investment services and/or activities is less than £30 million, calculated as an average on the basis of the annual figures from the two-year period immediately preceding the given financial year 8) it has not been classified as a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm due to the effect of MIFIDPRU 10.2 (Categorisation of clearing firms as non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firms) 9) its average daily trading flow (DTF), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.15.4, is zero 10) it is not appointed to act as a depositary in accordance with FUND 3.11.10R(2) or COLL 6.6A.8R(3)(b)(i).

With regard to the calculation of the DTF (item 9 above) it should be noted that the definition includes not only transactions that a firm enters into when ‘dealing on own account’ but also when executing orders in the firm’s own name. The FCA stated in Policy Statement PS21/9, page 35 “For example, a portfolio manager might execute a trade in its own name with the intention of subsequently allocating the relevant financial instruments among multiple client portfolios. Irrespective of whether such a trade constitutes dealing on own account, following the clarification in our rules, the value of the trade would always be recorded in K-DTF as it was executed in the name of the firm”.

Note that where there are two or more MIFIDPRU investment firms in a group (see below) that exceed any of the thresholds on a combined basis then each of these firms will be treated as a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm.

Normally when calculating the above AUM, COH and ASA then end-of-day values must be used. In contrast, intra-day values apply when calculating CMH. With regard to the latter, an end-of-day value is permitted in certain circumstances when an error has arisen – see MIFIDPRU 1.2.7. The Guidance in MIFIDPRU 1.2.8(2) explains that a firm that mistakenly accepts an amount that satisfies the CMH definition will breach the zero threshold even if it has subsequently returned that amount to the relevant client.

For (a) ‘on- and off-balance sheet total’ and (b) total ‘annual gross revenue’ a firm must use the values recorded at the end of the last financial year for which accounts have been finalised and approved by its management body. Should accounts not have been finalised and approved after 6 months from the end of the last financial year then the firm must use provisional accounts.

For the record, the FCA permits a firm to use an alternative approach (which will require notification to the FCA and is subject to meeting certain conditions) when measuring the average AUM and/or COH – see MIFIDPRU 1.2.4 for further details.

When a firm no longer satisfies the all the conditions i.e., 1) to 10) above, it ceases to be an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm with immediate effect and needs to notify the FCA. However, if the thresholds exceeded relate to one or more of conditions 1), 2), 6) and 7) above - and if the firm still meets all the remaining conditions – then instead of ‘immediate’ the cessation will occur 3 months after the date when it exceeded the relevant threshold (see MIFIDPRU 1.2.14 – 1.2.17). Similarly, if a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm subsequently satisfies all the conditions (for at least 6 months) it can be reclassified as an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm subject to notification to the FCA (MIFIDPRU 1.2.13).

Group consolidation (and Group capital test) Although the prudential obligations under MIFIDPRU are initially directed at MIFIDPRU investment firms on an individual basis, prudential consolidation needs to be considered (‘consolidated situation’) where there is an ‘investment firm group’. The latter is a group that consists of a UK incorporated parent undertaking and its subsidiaries, at least one of which must be a MIFIDPRU investment firm. MIFIDPRU 2.4.3 – 2.4.5 provides some limited guidance for a MIFIDPRU investment firm (or its UK parent entity) when identifying whether it forms part of an investment firm group. Effectively this means that where there is a ‘consolidated situation’ then the UK parent entity and its relevant financial undertakings form a single MIFIDPRU investment firm. Where relevant, tied agents are included in a ‘consolidated situation’. See MIFIDPRU 2.4 (‘Investment firm groups: general’) and MIFIDPRU 2.5 (‘Prudential consolidation’) for further information.

The ‘default’ position is the that where an investment firm group exists then prudential consolidation in line with MIFIDPRU 2.5 will apply. However, such a group can seek permission from the FCA to apply the ‘group capital test’ instead under which the UK parent entity holds own funds instead. The FCA will need to be satisfied that the group structure is ‘sufficiently simple to justify using the group capital test and that this approach would not pose significant risks to clients or to the market. Further details can be found in MIFIDPRU 2.4.17 – 2.4.19 and MIFIDPRU 2.6.

## 5.3 Regulatory Capital Requirement
