A CPMI firm such as Alamut needs to adopt a two-stage approach when calculating is prudential requirements – firstly as a MIFIDPRU firm (under MIFIDPRU) and then as an AIFM (under IPRU-INV 11) to ensure that both the amount of capital and the quality of the capital satisfies both obligations.

## 5.4 Stage one (MIFIDPRU)

At the basic level a MIFIDPRU investment firm must at all times maintain ‘own funds’ that are at least equal to its ‘own funds requirement’.

Own funds ‘Own funds’ of a firm are the sum of its:  common equity tier 1 capital (‘CET 1’)  additional tier 1 capital (‘AT 1’)  tier 2 capital

Note that the above MIFIDPRU definition of own funds (under MIFIDPRU 3.2.1) differs from that applicable IPRU-INV 11 being “has the meaning in article 4(1)(118) of the CRR, as it applied on 31 December 2021” which translates to the sum of Tier 1 capital and Tier 2 capital. However in practical terms there is no real difference as Tier 1 capital consists of the sum of CET 1 and AT 1.

At all times a firm must have own funds that satisfy all of the following conditions:  the firm’s common equity tier 1 capital must be equal to or greater than 56% of the firm’s own funds requirement  the sum of the firm’s common equity tier 1 capital and additional tier 1 capital must be equal to or greater than 75% of the firm’s own funds requirement  the firm’s own funds must be equal to or greater than 100% of the firm’s own funds requirement

Common equity tier 1 capital includes, but is not limited to, retained earnings, and issued capital instruments. The Handbook does not directly provide a definition and instead we need to refer to Articles 26 to 29 of the CRR, but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.3

Additional tier 1 capital are those instruments that do not qualify as common equity tier 1 but that meet the conditions set out in Article 51 onwards of the CRR, but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.4.

Tier 2 capital captures instruments and subordinated loans as appears in Article 62 onwards of the CRR, but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.5.

A firm is required to comply with the general requirements relating to own funds as set out in Articles 73 to 79 of the CRR but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.6.

The FCA requires advanced notification of any intention to issue additional tier 1 or tier 2 capital, whilst notification/permission applies to any intention to reduce own funds instruments – see MIFIDPRU 3.6 (MIFIDPRU 3.6.3 sets out the conditions to be met when permission can be “deemed to have been granted”).

Own funds requirement The own funds requirement of an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm is the higher of:  its permanent minimum capital requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.4); or  its fixed overheads requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.5).

The own funds requirement of a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm is the highest of:  its permanent minimum capital requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.4); or  its fixed overheads requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.5); or  its K-factor requirement (under MIFIDPRU 4.6).

## 5.5 Permanent Minimum Capital Requirement

The permanent minimum capital requirement is £75,000 for a firm that:  does not have permission to hold client money or client assets in connection with MiFID business  does not act as a depositary. and whose activities are limited to one or more of:  reception and transmission of orders in relation to one or more financial instruments  execution of orders on behalf of clients  portfolio management  investment advice  placing of financial instruments without a firm commitment basis

## 5.6 Fixed Overheads Requirement (FOR)

The FOR of a MIFIDPRU firm is an amount equal to one quarter of the firm’s relevant expenditure (calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.5.3), using the figures in its most recent audited annual financial

statements (if audited financial statements are not available then unaudited annual financial statements can be used but the calculation must be updated when audited figures are available). Please see MIFIDPRU 4.5 for further information.

## 5.7 K-factor Requirement

As mentioned earlier, the K-factor requirement is only applicable to non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firms and does not apply to SNI MIFIDPRU investment firms. A CPMI firm is not required to include its activities associated with ‘managing an AIF’ (‘collective portfolio management activities’) when calculating K-factor metrics (IPRU-INV 11.6.6). There are a total of nine ‘K-factors’, although not all of them will be relevant to every firm e.g., ‘K-CMH’ is only applicable to firms that handle client money:  K-AUM (Assets Under Management) requirement  K-CMH (Client Money Handled) requirement  K-ASA (Assets Safeguarded and Administered) requirement  K-COH (Client Orders Handled) requirement  K-NPR (Net Position Risk) requirement  K-CMG (Clearing Margin Given) requirement;  K-TCD (Trading Counterparty Default) requirement  K-DTF (Daily Trading Flow) requirement  K-CON (Concentration Risk) requirement The calculation of the factors is outside the scope of this Compliance Manual and instead reference should be made to MIFIDPRU 4.7 to 4.16 (and MIFIDPRU 5 with regard to K-CON) for further information.

## 5.8 Basic Liquid Assets Requirement

A MIFIDPRU firm must hold an amount of ‘core liquid assets’ equal to the sum of:  one third of the amount of its fixed overhead requirement and  1.6% of the total amount of any guarantees provided to clients (by way of example a firm that executes orders on behalf of a client may guarantee the settlement of any resulting transactions between the client and a third party) ‘Core liquid assets’ include items such as short-term deposits at a UK-authorised credit institution or shares or units in a short-term Money Market Fund. MIFIDPRU 6.3 provides further information.

## 5.9 Stage 2 (IPRU-INV)

Own funds At all times the Firm must maintain own funds (see below) of at least the higher of [(1) or (2)] plus (3) (IPRU-INV 11.2.1): 1. The funds under management requirement 2. The fixed overheads requirement (‘FOR’) as calculated under IPRU-INV 11.3.3A 3. Either additional own funds or suitable professional indemnity insurance to cover professional liability risks The funds under management requirement, which is subject to a maximum of €10m, (IPRU-INV 11.3.2) in 1 above is the sum of: i. the base own funds requirement (€125,000); plus ii. 0.02% of the amount by which the funds under management figure exceed €250m (‘funds’ in this context means AIFs or UCITS and includes funds where a firm has delegated the management function but excludes funds managed as a delegate). The professional liability risks to be covered must be at least those set out in IPRU-INV 11.3.12. The firm will also have to meet the qualitative requirements, including the need for a loss database and the implementation of risk management policies and procedures that are set out in IPRU-INV 11.3.13. Where additional own funds (‘professional negligence capital requirement’) is the selected route then the amount held must be at least equal to 0.01% of the value of the portfolios of AIFs managed. This figure is recalculated at the end of each financial year (unless the value of the portfolio of AIFs managed increases ‘significantly’). Note that the FCA has power to request a higher amount and can also authorise a lower amount, subject to the conditions in COBS 11.3.14(4) & (5). Where professional indemnity insurance is the selected route then additional own funds must be held to cover any defined excess in the PII (‘PII capital requirement’). The terms of the policy must be in accordance with IPRU-INV 11.3.15 e.g. coverage for an individual claim should at least be equal to 0.7% of the value of the portfolio of AIFs managed. Note that an amount of own funds will still need to be held to cover any excess or exclusions in the policy - see IPRU-INV 11.3.16. For the purposes of IPRU(INV) 11 own funds must be calculated in line with CRR Article 4(1)(118) (being Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital).

At all times the Firm must hold liquid assets which equal or exceed: The higher of either:

- the funds under management requirement less the base own funds requirement (€125,000) or

- the fixed overheads requirement (calculated in line with IPRU-INV 11.3.3A)

plus whichever is applicable of:

- The professional negligence requirement or PII capital requirement.

Liquid assets are assets which: i. Are readily convertible into cash within one month; and ii. Have not been invested in speculative positions Examples of acceptable liquid assets include cash, readily realisable investments that are not held for short- term resale and debtors. The Firm’s minimum capital resources is the higher of Stage 1 or Stage 2, subject to meeting the relevant conditions set out in both (see above).

## 5.10 Internal Capital Adequacy and Risk Assessment (ICARA)

Although a MIFIDPRU investment firm is required at all times to maintain ‘own funds’ that are at least equal to its ‘own funds requirement’ (see above) it is also subject to the ‘overall financial adequacy rule’ (MIFIDPRU 7.4.7), meaning that a firm must, at all times, hold own funds and liquid assets (core and noncore) which are adequate, both as to their amount and their quality, to ensure that:  the firm is able to remain financially viable throughout the economic cycle, with the ability to address any material potential harm that may result from its ongoing activities and  the firm’s business can be wound down in an orderly manner, minimising harm to consumers or to other market participants The amount of own funds that a firm needs to hold at any given time to comply with the above ‘overall financial adequacy rule’ is referred to as the ‘own funds threshold requirement’. MIFIDPRU 7.6.4(5) includes a diagram which summarises the process that a firm should undertake to determine its own funds threshold requirement.

The amount of liquid assets that a firm needs to hold at any given time to comply with the above ‘overall financial adequacy rule’ is referred to as the ‘liquid assets threshold requirement’. MIFIDPRU 7.7.5(3) includes a diagram which summarises the process that a firm should undertake to determine its liquid assets threshold requirement. Subject to the obligation that a firm’s ‘basic liquid assets requirement’ must be made up of ‘core liquid assets’, it is permissible for the liquid assets threshold requirement to consist of any combination of core liquid assets (except ‘trade receivables’) and ‘non-core liquid assets’ as defined in MIFIDPRU 7.7.8 (e.g., financial instruments), provided that the firm applies an appropriate haircut in accordance with MIFIDPRU 7.7.11. The FCA must be notified ‘immediately’ if the firm’s liquid assets (i.e. core liquid assets and non-core liquid assets) fall below the above liquid assets threshold requirement or if they fall below the liquid assets wind- down trigger (i.e. the firm’s ‘basic liquid assets requirement’, or any other amount specified by the FCA).

ICARA process To this end MIFIDPRU investment firms are required to apply the ‘ICARA process’, being the systems, controls and procedures in place to:  identify, monitor and, if proportionate, reduce all material potential harms that may result from the ongoing operation of, or winding down of, the firm’s business and  assess whether the firm should hold additional own funds and/or liquid assets to address material potential harms There follows an overview of the approach that can be adopted by a MIFIDPRU investment firm but reference to MIFIDPRU 7 will provide further detail.

Risk survey As a starting point, a firm must assess its business model and identify all ‘material harms’ that could result from the ongoing operation of the firm’s business and the winding-down of the firm’s business. MIFIDPRU 7.4.14 and MIFIDPRU 7 Annex 1 provide guidance to firms to assist in this process. It will then be necessary to consider whether e.g., a change in systems and controls would mitigate identified harms or, alternatively, whether additional own funds and/or liquid assets would be the required mitigant.

Stress testing Firms should consider relevant severe but plausible stresses that could affect the firm’s business and consider whether the firm would still have sufficient own funds and liquid assets to meet the overall financial adequacy rule (reference to Finalised Guidance FG20/1 – see ‘Useful Links’ - sets out the FCA’s expectations in relation to stress testing. The FCA suggests that more complex businesses or operating models should also undertake reverse stress testing (MIFIDPRU 7 Annex 1, 1.15-1.20 provides further guidance).

Wind-down planning and wind-down triggers The FCA requires MIFIDPRU investment firms to identify the steps and resources that would be required to ensure the orderly wind-down and termination of the firm’s business in a realistic timescale and to evaluate the potential harms arising from winding down the firm’s business and identify how to mitigate them. The analysis will allow the firm to assess the amount of own funds and liquid assets that would be required for an orderly wind-down. Under no circumstances can the assessment result in own funds being less than the fixed overheads requirement nor in liquid assets being less than the basic liquid assets requirement (‘wind-down triggers’). See MIFIDPRU 7.5 10 for further details as well as the FCA’s ‘Finalised Guidance FG20/1’ and ‘Wind- down planning guide’ (see ‘Useful Links’).

Notification to FCA of certain levels of own funds A MIFIDPRU investment firm must notify the FCA immediately where its own funds fall below any of the following of the firm’s: early warning indicator (being 100% of a firm’s ‘own funds threshold requirement’) own funds threshold requirement own funds wind-down trigger, or the firm considers that there is a reasonable likelihood that its own funds will fall below that level in the foreseeable future See MIFIDPRU 7.6.11-13. MIFIDPRU 7.6.15 incorporates a table which sets out the points at which the FCA would normally intervene and what actions it would normally take if there is a risk of a firm breaching requirements that apply to its own funds.

Reviewing and documenting the ICARA process A firm must review the adequacy of its ICARA process at least once every 12 months or following any material change in the firm’s business model or operating model (MIFIDPRU 7.8.2). The review must be fully documented (‘ICARA document’) – see MIFIDPRU 7.8.7 for the minimum content of the ICARA document.

After such a review the content must be reviewed and approved “within a reasonable period of time” by the firm’s governing body – the latter must specifically review and approve the key assumptions underlying the ICARA document.

Data item MIF007 (ICARA Assessment Questionnaire) must be submitted annually to the FCA (or after any review arising from a material change in the firm’s business model or operating model) – see MIFIDPRU 9.2. The firm is required to notify the FCA of the date on which the firm will submit this data item. This date will continue to apply unless the FCA is notified accordingly - although note that the revised date must not result in the firm not submitting data item MIF007 to the FCA for more than 12 months.

Both the ICARA document and the review and approval of it by the firm’s governing body must be retained for at least 3 years from the date on which the relevant document was approved.

## 5.11 Disclosures

Remuneration - disclosures for all MIFIDPRU investment firms A MIFIDPRU investment firm is required to disclose a summary of its approach to remuneration, the objectives of its financial incentives and its decision-making procedures and governance (MIFIDPRU 8.6.2). MIFIDPRU 8.6.6 lists the minimum information that is to be disclosed by a firm when disclosing the key characteristics of its remuneration policies and practices For the financial year concerned a firm must disclose the total amount of remuneration awarded to all staff split into fixed remuneration and variable remuneration On the matter of ‘remuneration’, as a full-scope UK AIFM the firm is also subject to the AIFM Remuneration Code (SYSC 19B.1) as well as the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code (SYSC 19G) Full details of obligations can be found in MIFIDPRU 8.6.

Remuneration – further disclosures relevant to all non-SNI firms After having identified material risk takers (in line with SYSC 19G.5 – such assessment must take place at least once a year) the non-SNI firm must disclose the types of staff so identified and, for the financial year concerned, the total number of material risk takers Disclosure of total remuneration awarded, and fixed/variable awarded, split between ‘senior management’, ‘other material risk takers’ and ‘other staff’ Disclosure, split into ‘senior management’ and ‘other material risk takers’, of total guaranteed variable remuneration and the number of material risk takers receiving those awards; the total amount of severance payments awarded and the number of such recipients that were material risk takers; and the amount of the highest severance pay awarded to an individual material risk taker

Remuneration – further disclosures relevant to larger non-SNI firms Depending upon the size of a firm’s on-balance sheet assets and off-balance sheet items (see SYSC 19G.1.1(2) read with MIFIDPRU 8.6.8(6)) then additional disclosures are required, again split between ‘senior management’ and ‘other material risk takers’ and being: Amount and form of awarded variable remuneration e.g., cash, shares etc. and split into deferred and non- deferred Amount of deferred remuneration awarded for previous performance periods and details of vesting Amount of deferred remuneration due to vest in financial year in respect of which the disclosure is made, including any withheld as a result of performance adjustment Details of any exemptions used in respect of material risk takers under SYSC 19.5.9 (which exemptions can be used by some firms where the annual variable remuneration does not exceed £167,000 and where it does not represent more than one-third of that individual’s total remuneration) Full details of the additional disclosure obligations can be found in MIFIDPRU 8.6. Note that it is permissible to aggregate the information disclosed for ‘senior managers’ and ‘other material risk takers’, where splitting the information between those two categories would lead to the disclosure of information ‘about one or two people’. Where such aggregation would still lead to the disclosure about one or two people then the firm does not have to comply with some of these disclosures – see MIFIDPRU 8.6.8(7) and 8.6.11. In such an instance a statement in line with MIFIDPRU 8.6.9 must be made in the main body of the firm’s remuneration disclosure.

Risk management disclosure

A non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm, and an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm that has additional tier 1 instruments in issue must disclose its risk management objectives and policies for the categories of risk addressed by: o Own funds requirement o Concentration risk (where relevant) o Liquidity The above must include a concise statement that has been approved by the governing body describing the potential for harm associated with the business strategy together with a summary of how those risks are managed. See MIFIDPRU 8.2 for further information.

Governance arrangements disclosure A non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm is required to disclose certain information about its internal arrangements including its policy on promoting diversity – see MIFIDPRU 8.3 for the full disclosures required.

Investment policy (voting) disclosure Certain larger non-SNI firms e.g., where the value of the on-balance and off-balance items over the preceding 4-year period is a rolling average of more than £100m (but see MIFIDPRU 7.1.4 for full details) are required to disclose: The proportion of voting rights attached to shares held directly or indirectly (the latter being client shares where the firm may exercise voting rights) broken down by country or territory A complete description of voting behaviour, including an explanation of the votes and the ratio of proposals put forward by that company that the firm has approved o As explanation of the use of proxy adviser firms o A summary of the voting guidelines The above disclosures (there is an exemption for what the firm deems to be proprietary information – see MIFIDPRU 8.7.4/8.75) must be made using the template available in MIFIDPRU 8 Annex 2. Note that the disclosures are only in respect of shares with voting rights that are admitted to trading on a regulated market and where the share of those voting rights represent greater than 5% of all the voting rights. See MIFIDPRU 8.7 for further information.

Own funds & Own fund requirements disclosure A non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm, and an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm that has additional tier 1 instruments in issue, is required to disclose:  A reconciliation of common equity tier 1 items, additional tier 1 items, tier 2 items and the applicable filters and deductions made in calculating the own funds  A reconciliation of the above with the capital in the balance sheet in the audited financial statements  A description of the main features of the common equity tier 1 instruments, additional tier 1 instruments and tier 2 instruments The above disclosures must be made using the template available in MIFIDPRU 8 Annex 1. In addition, disclosure is required re:  The approach adopted in assessing the adequacy of own funds in accordance with the overall financial adequacy rule  The fixed overheads requirement  The K-factor requirement, broken down as: o The sum of the K-AUM, K-CMH and K-ASA requirements o The sum of the K-COH and K-DTF requirements o The sum of the K-NPR, K-CMG, K-TCD and K-CON requirements See MIFIDPRU 8.4 & 8.5 for further information.

## 5.12 Useful Links

UK CRR https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2013/575/contents

Finalised Guidance (FG20/1) https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/finalised-guidance/fg20-1.pdf

FCA Wind-down planning guide https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/WDPG.pdf

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: As a full-scope UK AIFM that also undertakes permitted additional MiFID type activities in addition to ‘managing an AIF’ the firm falls within the definition of a ‘collective portfolio management investment firm’ (CPMI). The firm is also an ‘FCA investment firm’ (which the Handbook refers to as a ‘MIFIDPRU investment firm’). For prudential purposes Alamut is therefore subject to both IPRU-INV 11 for its activities relating ‘to managing an AIF’ and to MIFIDPRU for its MiFID business. However on occasion MIFIDPRU will apply to all of the firm’s activities e.g. the calculation of the fixed overheads requirement (FOR – MIFIDPRU 4.5). Fortunately the two prudential regimes run in parallel so that a capital instrument or a liquid asset may be used to meet either or both sets of requirements provided that it meets the conditions in both MIFIDPRU and IPRU-INV 11. For MIFIDPRU it will be necessary to determine whether the firm is a ‘SNI’ or an ‘non-SNI’ MIFIDPRU investment firm.

sections.title: SNI or non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm
sections.content:
As mentioned above all firms subject to MIFIDPRU will need to determine whether they are an ‘SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm’ or a ‘non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm’ – the impact of MIFIDPRU is less onerous for the former type (‘SNI’ is an abbreviation for a firm that is ‘small and non-interconnected’). Alamut is categorised as an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm for its MiFID business. To be categorised as an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm a firm needs to satisfy the following conditions: 1) its average AUM, calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.7.5, is less than £1.2 billion 2) its average client orders handled (COH), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.10.19, is less than: a. £100 million per day for cash trades; and b. £1 billion per day for derivatives trades 3) its average assets safeguarded and administered relating to MiFID business (ASA), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.9.8 is zero 4) its average client money held relating to MiFID business (CMH), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.8.13, is zero 5) it does not have permission to deal on own account 6) its on- and off-balance sheet total is less than £100 million 7) its total annual gross revenue from investment services and/or activities is less than £30 million, calculated as an average on the basis of the annual figures from the two-year period immediately preceding the given financial year 8) it has not been classified as a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm due to the effect of MIFIDPRU 10.2 (Categorisation of clearing firms as non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firms) 9) its average daily trading flow (DTF), calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.15.4, is zero 10) it is not appointed to act as a depositary in accordance with FUND 3.11.10R(2) or COLL 6.6A.8R(3)(b)(i).

With regard to the calculation of the DTF (item 9 above) it should be noted that the definition includes not only transactions that a firm enters into when ‘dealing on own account’ but also when executing orders in the firm’s own name. The FCA stated in Policy Statement PS21/9, page 35 “For example, a portfolio manager might execute a trade in its own name with the intention of subsequently allocating the relevant financial instruments among multiple client portfolios. Irrespective of whether such a trade constitutes dealing on own account, following the clarification in our rules, the value of the trade would always be recorded in K-DTF as it was executed in the name of the firm”.

Note that where there are two or more MIFIDPRU investment firms in a group (see below) that exceed any of the thresholds on a combined basis then each of these firms will be treated as a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm.

Normally when calculating the above AUM, COH and ASA then end-of-day values must be used. In contrast, intra-day values apply when calculating CMH. With regard to the latter, an end-of-day value is permitted in certain circumstances when an error has arisen – see MIFIDPRU 1.2.7. The Guidance in MIFIDPRU 1.2.8(2) explains that a firm that mistakenly accepts an amount that satisfies the CMH definition will breach the zero threshold even if it has subsequently returned that amount to the relevant client.

For (a) ‘on- and off-balance sheet total’ and (b) total ‘annual gross revenue’ a firm must use the values recorded at the end of the last financial year for which accounts have been finalised and approved by its management body. Should accounts not have been finalised and approved after 6 months from the end of the last financial year then the firm must use provisional accounts.

For the record, the FCA permits a firm to use an alternative approach (which will require notification to the FCA and is subject to meeting certain conditions) when measuring the average AUM and/or COH – see MIFIDPRU 1.2.4 for further details.

When a firm no longer satisfies the all the conditions i.e., 1) to 10) above, it ceases to be an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm with immediate effect and needs to notify the FCA. However, if the thresholds exceeded relate to one or more of conditions 1), 2), 6) and 7) above - and if the firm still meets all the remaining conditions – then instead of ‘immediate’ the cessation will occur 3 months after the date when it exceeded the relevant threshold (see MIFIDPRU 1.2.14 – 1.2.17). Similarly, if a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm subsequently satisfies all the conditions (for at least 6 months) it can be reclassified as an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm subject to notification to the FCA (MIFIDPRU 1.2.13).

Group consolidation (and Group capital test) Although the prudential obligations under MIFIDPRU are initially directed at MIFIDPRU investment firms on an individual basis, prudential consolidation needs to be considered (‘consolidated situation’) where there is an ‘investment firm group’. The latter is a group that consists of a UK incorporated parent undertaking and its subsidiaries, at least one of which must be a MIFIDPRU investment firm. MIFIDPRU 2.4.3 – 2.4.5 provides some limited guidance for a MIFIDPRU investment firm (or its UK parent entity) when identifying whether it forms part of an investment firm group. Effectively this means that where there is a ‘consolidated situation’ then the UK parent entity and its relevant financial undertakings form a single MIFIDPRU investment firm. Where relevant, tied agents are included in a ‘consolidated situation’. See MIFIDPRU 2.4 (‘Investment firm groups: general’) and MIFIDPRU 2.5 (‘Prudential consolidation’) for further information.

The ‘default’ position is the that where an investment firm group exists then prudential consolidation in line with MIFIDPRU 2.5 will apply. However, such a group can seek permission from the FCA to apply the ‘group capital test’ instead under which the UK parent entity holds own funds instead. The FCA will need to be satisfied that the group structure is ‘sufficiently simple to justify using the group capital test and that this approach would not pose significant risks to clients or to the market. Further details can be found in MIFIDPRU 2.4.17 – 2.4.19 and MIFIDPRU 2.6.

sections.title: Regulatory Capital Requirement
sections.content: A CPMI firm such as Alamut needs to adopt a two-stage approach when calculating is prudential requirements – firstly as a MIFIDPRU firm (under MIFIDPRU) and then as an AIFM (under IPRU-INV 11) to ensure that both the amount of capital and the quality of the capital satisfies both obligations.

sections.title: Stage one (MIFIDPRU)
sections.content:
At the basic level a MIFIDPRU investment firm must at all times maintain ‘own funds’ that are at least equal to its ‘own funds requirement’.

Own funds ‘Own funds’ of a firm are the sum of its:  common equity tier 1 capital (‘CET 1’)  additional tier 1 capital (‘AT 1’)  tier 2 capital

Note that the above MIFIDPRU definition of own funds (under MIFIDPRU 3.2.1) differs from that applicable IPRU-INV 11 being “has the meaning in article 4(1)(118) of the CRR, as it applied on 31 December 2021” which translates to the sum of Tier 1 capital and Tier 2 capital. However in practical terms there is no real difference as Tier 1 capital consists of the sum of CET 1 and AT 1.

At all times a firm must have own funds that satisfy all of the following conditions:  the firm’s common equity tier 1 capital must be equal to or greater than 56% of the firm’s own funds requirement  the sum of the firm’s common equity tier 1 capital and additional tier 1 capital must be equal to or greater than 75% of the firm’s own funds requirement  the firm’s own funds must be equal to or greater than 100% of the firm’s own funds requirement

Common equity tier 1 capital includes, but is not limited to, retained earnings, and issued capital instruments. The Handbook does not directly provide a definition and instead we need to refer to Articles 26 to 29 of the CRR, but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.3

Additional tier 1 capital are those instruments that do not qualify as common equity tier 1 but that meet the conditions set out in Article 51 onwards of the CRR, but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.4.

Tier 2 capital captures instruments and subordinated loans as appears in Article 62 onwards of the CRR, but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.5.

A firm is required to comply with the general requirements relating to own funds as set out in Articles 73 to 79 of the CRR but as modified by MIFIDPRU 3.6.

The FCA requires advanced notification of any intention to issue additional tier 1 or tier 2 capital, whilst notification/permission applies to any intention to reduce own funds instruments – see MIFIDPRU 3.6 (MIFIDPRU 3.6.3 sets out the conditions to be met when permission can be “deemed to have been granted”).

Own funds requirement The own funds requirement of an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm is the higher of:  its permanent minimum capital requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.4); or  its fixed overheads requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.5).

The own funds requirement of a non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm is the highest of:  its permanent minimum capital requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.4); or  its fixed overheads requirement (MIFIDPRU 4.5); or  its K-factor requirement (under MIFIDPRU 4.6).

sections.title: Permanent Minimum Capital Requirement
sections.content: The permanent minimum capital requirement is £75,000 for a firm that:  does not have permission to hold client money or client assets in connection with MiFID business  does not act as a depositary. and whose activities are limited to one or more of:  reception and transmission of orders in relation to one or more financial instruments  execution of orders on behalf of clients  portfolio management  investment advice  placing of financial instruments without a firm commitment basis

sections.title: Fixed Overheads Requirement (FOR)
sections.content:
The FOR of a MIFIDPRU firm is an amount equal to one quarter of the firm’s relevant expenditure (calculated in accordance with MIFIDPRU 4.5.3), using the figures in its most recent audited annual financial

statements (if audited financial statements are not available then unaudited annual financial statements can be used but the calculation must be updated when audited figures are available). Please see MIFIDPRU 4.5 for further information.

sections.title: K-factor Requirement
sections.content: As mentioned earlier, the K-factor requirement is only applicable to non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firms and does not apply to SNI MIFIDPRU investment firms. A CPMI firm is not required to include its activities associated with ‘managing an AIF’ (‘collective portfolio management activities’) when calculating K-factor metrics (IPRU-INV 11.6.6). There are a total of nine ‘K-factors’, although not all of them will be relevant to every firm e.g., ‘K-CMH’ is only applicable to firms that handle client money:  K-AUM (Assets Under Management) requirement  K-CMH (Client Money Handled) requirement  K-ASA (Assets Safeguarded and Administered) requirement  K-COH (Client Orders Handled) requirement  K-NPR (Net Position Risk) requirement  K-CMG (Clearing Margin Given) requirement;  K-TCD (Trading Counterparty Default) requirement  K-DTF (Daily Trading Flow) requirement  K-CON (Concentration Risk) requirement The calculation of the factors is outside the scope of this Compliance Manual and instead reference should be made to MIFIDPRU 4.7 to 4.16 (and MIFIDPRU 5 with regard to K-CON) for further information.

sections.title: Basic Liquid Assets Requirement
sections.content: A MIFIDPRU firm must hold an amount of ‘core liquid assets’ equal to the sum of:  one third of the amount of its fixed overhead requirement and  1.6% of the total amount of any guarantees provided to clients (by way of example a firm that executes orders on behalf of a client may guarantee the settlement of any resulting transactions between the client and a third party) ‘Core liquid assets’ include items such as short-term deposits at a UK-authorised credit institution or shares or units in a short-term Money Market Fund. MIFIDPRU 6.3 provides further information.

sections.title: Stage 2 (IPRU-INV)
sections.content:
Own funds At all times the Firm must maintain own funds (see below) of at least the higher of [(1) or (2)] plus (3) (IPRU-INV 11.2.1): 1. The funds under management requirement 2. The fixed overheads requirement (‘FOR’) as calculated under IPRU-INV 11.3.3A 3. Either additional own funds or suitable professional indemnity insurance to cover professional liability risks The funds under management requirement, which is subject to a maximum of €10m, (IPRU-INV 11.3.2) in 1 above is the sum of: i. the base own funds requirement (€125,000); plus ii. 0.02% of the amount by which the funds under management figure exceed €250m (‘funds’ in this context means AIFs or UCITS and includes funds where a firm has delegated the management function but excludes funds managed as a delegate). The professional liability risks to be covered must be at least those set out in IPRU-INV 11.3.12. The firm will also have to meet the qualitative requirements, including the need for a loss database and the implementation of risk management policies and procedures that are set out in IPRU-INV 11.3.13. Where additional own funds (‘professional negligence capital requirement’) is the selected route then the amount held must be at least equal to 0.01% of the value of the portfolios of AIFs managed. This figure is recalculated at the end of each financial year (unless the value of the portfolio of AIFs managed increases ‘significantly’). Note that the FCA has power to request a higher amount and can also authorise a lower amount, subject to the conditions in COBS 11.3.14(4) & (5). Where professional indemnity insurance is the selected route then additional own funds must be held to cover any defined excess in the PII (‘PII capital requirement’). The terms of the policy must be in accordance with IPRU-INV 11.3.15 e.g. coverage for an individual claim should at least be equal to 0.7% of the value of the portfolio of AIFs managed. Note that an amount of own funds will still need to be held to cover any excess or exclusions in the policy - see IPRU-INV 11.3.16. For the purposes of IPRU(INV) 11 own funds must be calculated in line with CRR Article 4(1)(118) (being Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital).

At all times the Firm must hold liquid assets which equal or exceed: The higher of either:

- the funds under management requirement less the base own funds requirement (€125,000) or

- the fixed overheads requirement (calculated in line with IPRU-INV 11.3.3A)

plus whichever is applicable of:

- The professional negligence requirement or PII capital requirement.

Liquid assets are assets which: i. Are readily convertible into cash within one month; and ii. Have not been invested in speculative positions Examples of acceptable liquid assets include cash, readily realisable investments that are not held for short- term resale and debtors. The Firm’s minimum capital resources is the higher of Stage 1 or Stage 2, subject to meeting the relevant conditions set out in both (see above).

sections.title: Internal Capital Adequacy and Risk Assessment (ICARA)
sections.content:
Although a MIFIDPRU investment firm is required at all times to maintain ‘own funds’ that are at least equal to its ‘own funds requirement’ (see above) it is also subject to the ‘overall financial adequacy rule’ (MIFIDPRU 7.4.7), meaning that a firm must, at all times, hold own funds and liquid assets (core and noncore) which are adequate, both as to their amount and their quality, to ensure that:  the firm is able to remain financially viable throughout the economic cycle, with the ability to address any material potential harm that may result from its ongoing activities and  the firm’s business can be wound down in an orderly manner, minimising harm to consumers or to other market participants The amount of own funds that a firm needs to hold at any given time to comply with the above ‘overall financial adequacy rule’ is referred to as the ‘own funds threshold requirement’. MIFIDPRU 7.6.4(5) includes a diagram which summarises the process that a firm should undertake to determine its own funds threshold requirement.

The amount of liquid assets that a firm needs to hold at any given time to comply with the above ‘overall financial adequacy rule’ is referred to as the ‘liquid assets threshold requirement’. MIFIDPRU 7.7.5(3) includes a diagram which summarises the process that a firm should undertake to determine its liquid assets threshold requirement. Subject to the obligation that a firm’s ‘basic liquid assets requirement’ must be made up of ‘core liquid assets’, it is permissible for the liquid assets threshold requirement to consist of any combination of core liquid assets (except ‘trade receivables’) and ‘non-core liquid assets’ as defined in MIFIDPRU 7.7.8 (e.g., financial instruments), provided that the firm applies an appropriate haircut in accordance with MIFIDPRU 7.7.11. The FCA must be notified ‘immediately’ if the firm’s liquid assets (i.e. core liquid assets and non-core liquid assets) fall below the above liquid assets threshold requirement or if they fall below the liquid assets wind- down trigger (i.e. the firm’s ‘basic liquid assets requirement’, or any other amount specified by the FCA).

ICARA process To this end MIFIDPRU investment firms are required to apply the ‘ICARA process’, being the systems, controls and procedures in place to:  identify, monitor and, if proportionate, reduce all material potential harms that may result from the ongoing operation of, or winding down of, the firm’s business and  assess whether the firm should hold additional own funds and/or liquid assets to address material potential harms There follows an overview of the approach that can be adopted by a MIFIDPRU investment firm but reference to MIFIDPRU 7 will provide further detail.

Risk survey As a starting point, a firm must assess its business model and identify all ‘material harms’ that could result from the ongoing operation of the firm’s business and the winding-down of the firm’s business. MIFIDPRU 7.4.14 and MIFIDPRU 7 Annex 1 provide guidance to firms to assist in this process. It will then be necessary to consider whether e.g., a change in systems and controls would mitigate identified harms or, alternatively, whether additional own funds and/or liquid assets would be the required mitigant.

Stress testing Firms should consider relevant severe but plausible stresses that could affect the firm’s business and consider whether the firm would still have sufficient own funds and liquid assets to meet the overall financial adequacy rule (reference to Finalised Guidance FG20/1 – see ‘Useful Links’ - sets out the FCA’s expectations in relation to stress testing. The FCA suggests that more complex businesses or operating models should also undertake reverse stress testing (MIFIDPRU 7 Annex 1, 1.15-1.20 provides further guidance).

Wind-down planning and wind-down triggers The FCA requires MIFIDPRU investment firms to identify the steps and resources that would be required to ensure the orderly wind-down and termination of the firm’s business in a realistic timescale and to evaluate the potential harms arising from winding down the firm’s business and identify how to mitigate them. The analysis will allow the firm to assess the amount of own funds and liquid assets that would be required for an orderly wind-down. Under no circumstances can the assessment result in own funds being less than the fixed overheads requirement nor in liquid assets being less than the basic liquid assets requirement (‘wind-down triggers’). See MIFIDPRU 7.5 10 for further details as well as the FCA’s ‘Finalised Guidance FG20/1’ and ‘Wind- down planning guide’ (see ‘Useful Links’).

Notification to FCA of certain levels of own funds A MIFIDPRU investment firm must notify the FCA immediately where its own funds fall below any of the following of the firm’s: early warning indicator (being 100% of a firm’s ‘own funds threshold requirement’) own funds threshold requirement own funds wind-down trigger, or the firm considers that there is a reasonable likelihood that its own funds will fall below that level in the foreseeable future See MIFIDPRU 7.6.11-13. MIFIDPRU 7.6.15 incorporates a table which sets out the points at which the FCA would normally intervene and what actions it would normally take if there is a risk of a firm breaching requirements that apply to its own funds.

Reviewing and documenting the ICARA process A firm must review the adequacy of its ICARA process at least once every 12 months or following any material change in the firm’s business model or operating model (MIFIDPRU 7.8.2). The review must be fully documented (‘ICARA document’) – see MIFIDPRU 7.8.7 for the minimum content of the ICARA document.

After such a review the content must be reviewed and approved “within a reasonable period of time” by the firm’s governing body – the latter must specifically review and approve the key assumptions underlying the ICARA document.

Data item MIF007 (ICARA Assessment Questionnaire) must be submitted annually to the FCA (or after any review arising from a material change in the firm’s business model or operating model) – see MIFIDPRU 9.2. The firm is required to notify the FCA of the date on which the firm will submit this data item. This date will continue to apply unless the FCA is notified accordingly - although note that the revised date must not result in the firm not submitting data item MIF007 to the FCA for more than 12 months.

Both the ICARA document and the review and approval of it by the firm’s governing body must be retained for at least 3 years from the date on which the relevant document was approved.

sections.title: Disclosures
sections.content:
Remuneration - disclosures for all MIFIDPRU investment firms A MIFIDPRU investment firm is required to disclose a summary of its approach to remuneration, the objectives of its financial incentives and its decision-making procedures and governance (MIFIDPRU 8.6.2). MIFIDPRU 8.6.6 lists the minimum information that is to be disclosed by a firm when disclosing the key characteristics of its remuneration policies and practices For the financial year concerned a firm must disclose the total amount of remuneration awarded to all staff split into fixed remuneration and variable remuneration On the matter of ‘remuneration’, as a full-scope UK AIFM the firm is also subject to the AIFM Remuneration Code (SYSC 19B.1) as well as the MIFIDPRU Remuneration Code (SYSC 19G) Full details of obligations can be found in MIFIDPRU 8.6.

Remuneration – further disclosures relevant to all non-SNI firms After having identified material risk takers (in line with SYSC 19G.5 – such assessment must take place at least once a year) the non-SNI firm must disclose the types of staff so identified and, for the financial year concerned, the total number of material risk takers Disclosure of total remuneration awarded, and fixed/variable awarded, split between ‘senior management’, ‘other material risk takers’ and ‘other staff’ Disclosure, split into ‘senior management’ and ‘other material risk takers’, of total guaranteed variable remuneration and the number of material risk takers receiving those awards; the total amount of severance payments awarded and the number of such recipients that were material risk takers; and the amount of the highest severance pay awarded to an individual material risk taker

Remuneration – further disclosures relevant to larger non-SNI firms Depending upon the size of a firm’s on-balance sheet assets and off-balance sheet items (see SYSC 19G.1.1(2) read with MIFIDPRU 8.6.8(6)) then additional disclosures are required, again split between ‘senior management’ and ‘other material risk takers’ and being: Amount and form of awarded variable remuneration e.g., cash, shares etc. and split into deferred and non- deferred Amount of deferred remuneration awarded for previous performance periods and details of vesting Amount of deferred remuneration due to vest in financial year in respect of which the disclosure is made, including any withheld as a result of performance adjustment Details of any exemptions used in respect of material risk takers under SYSC 19.5.9 (which exemptions can be used by some firms where the annual variable remuneration does not exceed £167,000 and where it does not represent more than one-third of that individual’s total remuneration) Full details of the additional disclosure obligations can be found in MIFIDPRU 8.6. Note that it is permissible to aggregate the information disclosed for ‘senior managers’ and ‘other material risk takers’, where splitting the information between those two categories would lead to the disclosure of information ‘about one or two people’. Where such aggregation would still lead to the disclosure about one or two people then the firm does not have to comply with some of these disclosures – see MIFIDPRU 8.6.8(7) and 8.6.11. In such an instance a statement in line with MIFIDPRU 8.6.9 must be made in the main body of the firm’s remuneration disclosure.

Risk management disclosure

A non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm, and an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm that has additional tier 1 instruments in issue must disclose its risk management objectives and policies for the categories of risk addressed by: o Own funds requirement o Concentration risk (where relevant) o Liquidity The above must include a concise statement that has been approved by the governing body describing the potential for harm associated with the business strategy together with a summary of how those risks are managed. See MIFIDPRU 8.2 for further information.

Governance arrangements disclosure A non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm is required to disclose certain information about its internal arrangements including its policy on promoting diversity – see MIFIDPRU 8.3 for the full disclosures required.

Investment policy (voting) disclosure Certain larger non-SNI firms e.g., where the value of the on-balance and off-balance items over the preceding 4-year period is a rolling average of more than £100m (but see MIFIDPRU 7.1.4 for full details) are required to disclose: The proportion of voting rights attached to shares held directly or indirectly (the latter being client shares where the firm may exercise voting rights) broken down by country or territory A complete description of voting behaviour, including an explanation of the votes and the ratio of proposals put forward by that company that the firm has approved o As explanation of the use of proxy adviser firms o A summary of the voting guidelines The above disclosures (there is an exemption for what the firm deems to be proprietary information – see MIFIDPRU 8.7.4/8.75) must be made using the template available in MIFIDPRU 8 Annex 2. Note that the disclosures are only in respect of shares with voting rights that are admitted to trading on a regulated market and where the share of those voting rights represent greater than 5% of all the voting rights. See MIFIDPRU 8.7 for further information.

Own funds & Own fund requirements disclosure A non-SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm, and an SNI MIFIDPRU investment firm that has additional tier 1 instruments in issue, is required to disclose:  A reconciliation of common equity tier 1 items, additional tier 1 items, tier 2 items and the applicable filters and deductions made in calculating the own funds  A reconciliation of the above with the capital in the balance sheet in the audited financial statements  A description of the main features of the common equity tier 1 instruments, additional tier 1 instruments and tier 2 instruments The above disclosures must be made using the template available in MIFIDPRU 8 Annex 1. In addition, disclosure is required re:  The approach adopted in assessing the adequacy of own funds in accordance with the overall financial adequacy rule  The fixed overheads requirement  The K-factor requirement, broken down as: o The sum of the K-AUM, K-CMH and K-ASA requirements o The sum of the K-COH and K-DTF requirements o The sum of the K-NPR, K-CMG, K-TCD and K-CON requirements See MIFIDPRU 8.4 & 8.5 for further information.

sections.title: Useful Links
sections.content:
UK CRR https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2013/575/contents

Finalised Guidance (FG20/1) https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/finalised-guidance/fg20-1.pdf

FCA Wind-down planning guide https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/WDPG.pdf

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – GENERAL
chapters.summary: 6. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – GENERAL
chapters.content:
6. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – GENERAL

## 6.1 Introduction

The FCA and the SEC impose various obligations on Firms when conducting business with clients. The FCA requirements can be found in the Conduct of Business Sourcebook (“COBS ”) and the SEC requirements are set out primarily in the Advisers Act.

## 6.2 Acting Honestly, Fairly and Professionally

As a full-scope UK AIFM , Alamut must, for all AIFs managed:

(i) act honestly, fairly and with due skill care and diligence in conducting their activities; (ii) act in the best interests of the AIF it manages or the investors of the AIF it manages and the integrity of the market; (iii) treat all investors fairly; and (iv) not allow any investor in an AIF to obtain preferential treatment, unless such preferential treatment is disclosed in the relevant AIF's instrument constituting the fund.

This is similar to the SEC’s fiduciary duty of care.

## 6.3 Information Disclosure Before Providing Services

Overview COBS 2.2A requires that the Firm provides certain information to a client before it conducts investment business for that client. This includes: information about the Firm itself and the services it is authorised to provide, financial instruments and proposed investment strategies and its charges for providing these services.

In addition, a client must be provided with:

 risk warnings associated with investments in financial instruments or in respect of particular investment strategies;  information on whether a particular financial instrument is intended for retail or professional clients, taking account of the identified target market in accordance with the rules in PROD 3 (see chapter on ‘Product Governance’ in this manual);  the information required by this section in a comprehensible form in such a manner that the client is reasonably able to understand the nature and risks of the investment service and of the specific type of financial instrument that is being offered and, consequently, to take investment decisions on an informed basis. That information may be provided in a standardised format;  when distributing “PRIIPs” (Packaged Retail and Insurance-based Investment Products) any costs and associated charges that may not have been included in the Key Information Document, or Key Investor Information Document, and costs and charges relating to the provision of investment services in respect of that financial instrument.

All the required information must be provided “in good time before the provision of investment services or ancillary services”.

Information about the Firm and its services The MiFID framework prescribes the following information that must be provided, where relevant, to a client, or potential client:

 the name and address of the investment firm, and the contact details necessary to enable clients to communicate effectively with the firm;  the languages in which the client may communicate with the investment firm, and receive documents and other information from the firm;  the methods of communication to be used between the investment firm and the client including, where relevant, those for the sending and reception of orders;  a statement of the fact that the investment firm is authorised and the name and contact address of the competent authority that has authorised it;  where the investment firm is acting through a tied agent, a statement of this fact specifying the Member State in which that agent is registered;  the nature, frequency and timing of the reports on the performance of the service to be provided by the investment firm to the client in accordance with MiFID requirements (Article 25(6));  where the investment firm holds client financial instruments or client funds, a summary description of the steps which it takes to ensure their protection, including summary details of any relevant investor compensation or deposit guarantee scheme which applies to the firm by virtue of its activities in a Member State;  a description, which may be provided in summary form, of the firm’s ‘Conflicts of Interest’ Policy;  at the request of the client, further details of that ’Conflicts of Interest’ Policy in a durable medium or by means of a website (where that does not constitute a durable medium) provided that the conditions set out Article 3(2) are satisfied;  for MiFID business the default ‘durable medium’ is by way of electronic format (although retail clients can still request information on paper).

See COBS 6.1ZA for further details, including the need for information, where relevant, relating to portfolio management services; the safeguarding of clients’ financial instruments and money; and costs and charges – with regard to the latter (COBS 6.1ZA.14) the requirement does not apply to professional clients except in respect of portfolio management and investment advice.

Information about financial instruments The following information must be provided where relevant:

 a general description of the nature and risks of financial instruments, taking into account, in particular, the client’s categorisation as either a retail client, professional client or eligible counterparty. That description shall explain the nature of the specific type of instrument concerned, the functioning and performance of the financial instrument in different market conditions, including both positive and negative conditions, as well as the risks particular to that specific type of instrument in sufficient detail to enable the client to take investment decisions on an informed basis;  the description of risks referred to above shall include, where relevant to the specific type of instrument concerned and the status and level of knowledge of the client, the following elements: (a) the risks associated with that type of financial instrument including an explanation of leverage and its effects and the risk of losing the entire investment including the risks associated with insolvency of the issuer or related events, such as bail in; (b) the volatility of the price of such instruments and any limitations on the available market for such instruments; (c) information on impediments or restrictions for disinvestment, for example as may be the case for illiquid financial instruments or financial instruments with a fixed investment term, including an illustration of the possible exit methods and consequences of any exit, possible constraints and the estimated time frame for the sale of the financial instrument before recovering the initial costs of the transaction in that type of financial instruments; (d) the fact that an investor might assume, as a result of transactions in such instruments, financial commitments and other additional obligations, including contingent liabilities, additional to the cost of acquiring the instruments; (e) any margin requirements or similar obligations, applicable to instruments of that type;  where an investment firm provides a retail client or potential retail client with information about a financial instrument that is the subject of a current offer to the public and a prospectus has been published in connection with that offer, that firm shall in good time before the provision of investment services or ancillary services to clients or potential clients inform the client or potential client where that prospectus is made available to the public;  where a financial instrument is composed of two or more different financial instruments or services, the investment firm shall provide an adequate description of the legal nature of the financial instrument, the components of that instrument and the way in which the interaction between the components affects the risks of the investment;

 in the case of financial instruments that incorporate a guarantee or capital protection, the investment firm shall provide a client or a potential client with information about the scope and nature of such guarantee or capital protection. When the guarantee is provided by a third party, information about the guarantee shall include sufficient detail about the guarantor and the guarantee to enable the client or potential client to make a fair assessment of the guarantee.

See COBS 14.3A for further information.

## 6.4 Information about Compensation

Information on the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (“FSCS”) must be provided where any client is an “eligible claimant” (as defined in COMP 4.2), together with details of the maximum level of compensation (£85,000 per person for investment business).

Where a client so requests, the Firm must provide information on the formalities to be completed to obtain compensation and the conditions governing compensation (see ‘Useful Links’).

For the record, currently Alamut does not have any clients eligible to make recourse to the FSCS.

## 6.5 Client Agreements

The Firm is obliged to put in place a written agreement between the client and the Firm setting out the essential rights and obligations of each party.

The agreement must include the following (Article 58 of Delegated Regulation 2017/565):

 a description of the services, and where relevant the nature and extent of the investment advice, to be provided;  in case of portfolio management services, the types of financial instruments that may be purchased and sold and the types of transactions that may be undertaken on behalf of the client, as well as any instruments or transactions prohibited; and  a description of the main features of any safekeeping and administration of financial instruments for the account of the client to be provided (including custodianship and related services such as cash/collateral management), including where applicable the role of the firm with respect to corporate actions relating to client instruments and the terms on which securities financing transactions involving client securities will generate a return for the client.

There is no minimum period specified for retention of such client agreement save for “at least the duration of the relationship with the client”

The Advisers Act and SEC interpretations require that certain clauses be included in all investment advisory agreements with clients:

 “No assignment” – an investment advisory agreement must provide that the Firm may not assign the contract without the client’s consent.

 “Change of membership” – an investment advisory agreement must provide that the adviser will notify its client of any change in membership of a partnership or limited liability company within a reasonable time after the change.

In addition, the investment adviser’s ability to utilise certain other clauses is limited.

 “Prohibition on waiver of compliance” – an investment advisory agreement may not include any condition, stipulation or provision binding any person to waive compliance with any requirements of the Advisers Act, or any rule, regulation or order there under.

 “Prohibition on hedge clauses/disclaimer(s)” – an investment advisory agreement may not contain any hedge clause / disclaimer(s), or other contractual provision that is likely to lead a client to believe that he or she has waived any right of action against the Firm.

The Advisers Act also prohibits an investment adviser from receiving any type of advisory fee calculated as a percentage of capital gains or appreciation in the client’s account (“performance-based fee”).

However, Rule 205-3 of the Advisers Act permits an investment adviser to charge performance-based fees to: i. Clients with at least $1 million under management with the adviser or more than $2 million in net worth; and ii. Clients who are “qualified purchasers” under the Investment Company Act of 1940 (the “Company Act”(see Section 8.8)); and iii. Certain knowledgeable employees of the investment adviser.

## 6.6 Client Categorisation

Please refer to the ‘Conduct of Business – Client Categorisation’ chapter in this manual for the information that must be provided to clients in respect of their categorisation.

## 6.7 Stewardship Responsibilities

The Firm is subject to disclosure on its commitment to the Financial Reporting Council’s (“FRC”’s) Stewardship Code in respect of its MiFID business which contains the following seven Principles for an institutional investor (the Firm must publicly disclose its policy on how it will discharge its stewardship responsibilities):

 Have a robust policy on managing conflicts of interest in relation to stewardship and shall publicly disclose this policy;  Monitor its investee companies;  Establish clear guidelines on when and how they will escalate their activities as a method of protecting and enhancing shareholder value;  Be willing to act collectively with other investors where appropriate;  Have a clear policy on voting and disclosure of voting activity;  Report periodically on their stewardship and voting activities.  The Firm has published a statement of the nature of its commitment to the Stewardship Code on its website (in accordance with COBS 2.2.3R).

The Firm does not commit to the FRC’s Stewardship Code, as this is not appropriate to its business strategy. A statement to this effect, explaining the nature of the Firm’s alternative investment strategy, is published on the Firm’s website (or in another accessible form).

## 6.8 Shareholder Rights Directive (“SRD Requirements”)

SRD Requirements (COBS 2.2B) applies to the extent that investment is made in shares traded on a ‘regulated market’ (being a UK Recognised Investment Exchange and comparable markets outside the UK).

A firm is required to either develop, and publicly disclose via its website, on an annual basis an ‘engagement policy’ on e.g. how it engages with investee companies, exercises voting rights etc. (see COBS 2.2B.6 onwards) or publicly disclose “a clear and reasoned explanation” of why it has chosen not to comply (COBS 2.2B.5).

The Firm has chosen not to comply with the requirements set out in the SRD ####

Where a firm has a client(s) that is an ‘SRD Institutional Investor’ (being an institution for occupational retirement provisions or an undertaking carrying out activities of life insurance) then annual disclosures must be made to the client(s) in line with COBS 2.2B.9.

## 6.9 Agent as Client

Under COBS 2.4.3if the Firm is aware that a person (C1) with or for whom it is conducting designated investment business is acting as agent for another person (C2) in relation to that business, then the agent is the client of the Firm in respect of that business. However, this will not be the case if the Firm has agreed with C1 to treat C2 as its client or if C1 is not an authorised firm and the main purpose of the arrangement is to avoid Alamut’s duties that would otherwise be owed to C2.

For anti-money laundering purposes, if simplified due diligence (see ‘Financial Crime Chapter’) cannot be applied to the agent then the Firm is required to undertake due diligence measures on both the agent and the agent’s client.

## 6.10 Reliance on Other Firms

In the course of MiFID business Alamut is entitled to rely upon any information about a client that is transmitted to it by another firm and to rely upon any recommendations in respect of the service that have been provided to the client by the other firm as long as the other firm is a MiFID firm.

For non-MiFID business a firm must show that it is reasonable for it to rely on information provided to it in writing by another person. The view of the FCA that it is reasonable for a firm to rely on information provided in writing by an unconnected authorised person or a professional firm (i.e. an individual or entity subject to regulation by a designated professional body).

Although Alamut will be responsible for enacting the service, responsibility for the completeness and accuracy of any information transmitted about the client, and for the suitability of any advice or recommendations made by the other firm, will remain with the other firm.

## 6.11 Useful Links

COBS 2 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/2/1.html

COBS 6 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/6/?view=chapter

COBS 14.2 & 14.3 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/14/2.html https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/14/3A.html

COMP 4.2 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COMP/4/2.html

Stewardship Code https://www.frc.org.uk/getattachment/d67933f9-ca38-4233-b603-3d24b2f62c5f/UK-Stewardship-Code- (September-2012).pdf

Delegated Regulation 2017/565 http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0565&from=en

and in particular

Articles 44 to 51 on the provision of information to clients and potential clients

FSCS https://www.fscs.org.uk/what-we-cover/products/investments/

Shareholder Rights Directive (2017/828) https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017L0828&from=EN

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The FCA and the SEC impose various obligations on Firms when conducting business with clients. The FCA requirements can be found in the Conduct of Business Sourcebook (“COBS ”) and the SEC requirements are set out primarily in the Advisers Act.

sections.title: Acting Honestly, Fairly and Professionally
sections.content:
As a full-scope UK AIFM , Alamut must, for all AIFs managed:

(i) act honestly, fairly and with due skill care and diligence in conducting their activities; (ii) act in the best interests of the AIF it manages or the investors of the AIF it manages and the integrity of the market; (iii) treat all investors fairly; and (iv) not allow any investor in an AIF to obtain preferential treatment, unless such preferential treatment is disclosed in the relevant AIF's instrument constituting the fund.

This is similar to the SEC’s fiduciary duty of care.

sections.title: Information Disclosure Before Providing Services
sections.content:
Overview COBS 2.2A requires that the Firm provides certain information to a client before it conducts investment business for that client. This includes: information about the Firm itself and the services it is authorised to provide, financial instruments and proposed investment strategies and its charges for providing these services.

In addition, a client must be provided with:

 risk warnings associated with investments in financial instruments or in respect of particular investment strategies;  information on whether a particular financial instrument is intended for retail or professional clients, taking account of the identified target market in accordance with the rules in PROD 3 (see chapter on ‘Product Governance’ in this manual);  the information required by this section in a comprehensible form in such a manner that the client is reasonably able to understand the nature and risks of the investment service and of the specific type of financial instrument that is being offered and, consequently, to take investment decisions on an informed basis. That information may be provided in a standardised format;  when distributing “PRIIPs” (Packaged Retail and Insurance-based Investment Products) any costs and associated charges that may not have been included in the Key Information Document, or Key Investor Information Document, and costs and charges relating to the provision of investment services in respect of that financial instrument.

All the required information must be provided “in good time before the provision of investment services or ancillary services”.

Information about the Firm and its services The MiFID framework prescribes the following information that must be provided, where relevant, to a client, or potential client:

 the name and address of the investment firm, and the contact details necessary to enable clients to communicate effectively with the firm;  the languages in which the client may communicate with the investment firm, and receive documents and other information from the firm;  the methods of communication to be used between the investment firm and the client including, where relevant, those for the sending and reception of orders;  a statement of the fact that the investment firm is authorised and the name and contact address of the competent authority that has authorised it;  where the investment firm is acting through a tied agent, a statement of this fact specifying the Member State in which that agent is registered;  the nature, frequency and timing of the reports on the performance of the service to be provided by the investment firm to the client in accordance with MiFID requirements (Article 25(6));  where the investment firm holds client financial instruments or client funds, a summary description of the steps which it takes to ensure their protection, including summary details of any relevant investor compensation or deposit guarantee scheme which applies to the firm by virtue of its activities in a Member State;  a description, which may be provided in summary form, of the firm’s ‘Conflicts of Interest’ Policy;  at the request of the client, further details of that ’Conflicts of Interest’ Policy in a durable medium or by means of a website (where that does not constitute a durable medium) provided that the conditions set out Article 3(2) are satisfied;  for MiFID business the default ‘durable medium’ is by way of electronic format (although retail clients can still request information on paper).

See COBS 6.1ZA for further details, including the need for information, where relevant, relating to portfolio management services; the safeguarding of clients’ financial instruments and money; and costs and charges – with regard to the latter (COBS 6.1ZA.14) the requirement does not apply to professional clients except in respect of portfolio management and investment advice.

Information about financial instruments The following information must be provided where relevant:

 a general description of the nature and risks of financial instruments, taking into account, in particular, the client’s categorisation as either a retail client, professional client or eligible counterparty. That description shall explain the nature of the specific type of instrument concerned, the functioning and performance of the financial instrument in different market conditions, including both positive and negative conditions, as well as the risks particular to that specific type of instrument in sufficient detail to enable the client to take investment decisions on an informed basis;  the description of risks referred to above shall include, where relevant to the specific type of instrument concerned and the status and level of knowledge of the client, the following elements: (a) the risks associated with that type of financial instrument including an explanation of leverage and its effects and the risk of losing the entire investment including the risks associated with insolvency of the issuer or related events, such as bail in; (b) the volatility of the price of such instruments and any limitations on the available market for such instruments; (c) information on impediments or restrictions for disinvestment, for example as may be the case for illiquid financial instruments or financial instruments with a fixed investment term, including an illustration of the possible exit methods and consequences of any exit, possible constraints and the estimated time frame for the sale of the financial instrument before recovering the initial costs of the transaction in that type of financial instruments; (d) the fact that an investor might assume, as a result of transactions in such instruments, financial commitments and other additional obligations, including contingent liabilities, additional to the cost of acquiring the instruments; (e) any margin requirements or similar obligations, applicable to instruments of that type;  where an investment firm provides a retail client or potential retail client with information about a financial instrument that is the subject of a current offer to the public and a prospectus has been published in connection with that offer, that firm shall in good time before the provision of investment services or ancillary services to clients or potential clients inform the client or potential client where that prospectus is made available to the public;  where a financial instrument is composed of two or more different financial instruments or services, the investment firm shall provide an adequate description of the legal nature of the financial instrument, the components of that instrument and the way in which the interaction between the components affects the risks of the investment;

 in the case of financial instruments that incorporate a guarantee or capital protection, the investment firm shall provide a client or a potential client with information about the scope and nature of such guarantee or capital protection. When the guarantee is provided by a third party, information about the guarantee shall include sufficient detail about the guarantor and the guarantee to enable the client or potential client to make a fair assessment of the guarantee.

See COBS 14.3A for further information.

sections.title: Information about Compensation
sections.content:
Information on the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (“FSCS”) must be provided where any client is an “eligible claimant” (as defined in COMP 4.2), together with details of the maximum level of compensation (£85,000 per person for investment business).

Where a client so requests, the Firm must provide information on the formalities to be completed to obtain compensation and the conditions governing compensation (see ‘Useful Links’).

For the record, currently Alamut does not have any clients eligible to make recourse to the FSCS.

sections.title: Client Agreements
sections.content:
The Firm is obliged to put in place a written agreement between the client and the Firm setting out the essential rights and obligations of each party.

The agreement must include the following (Article 58 of Delegated Regulation 2017/565):

 a description of the services, and where relevant the nature and extent of the investment advice, to be provided;  in case of portfolio management services, the types of financial instruments that may be purchased and sold and the types of transactions that may be undertaken on behalf of the client, as well as any instruments or transactions prohibited; and  a description of the main features of any safekeeping and administration of financial instruments for the account of the client to be provided (including custodianship and related services such as cash/collateral management), including where applicable the role of the firm with respect to corporate actions relating to client instruments and the terms on which securities financing transactions involving client securities will generate a return for the client.

There is no minimum period specified for retention of such client agreement save for “at least the duration of the relationship with the client”

The Advisers Act and SEC interpretations require that certain clauses be included in all investment advisory agreements with clients:

 “No assignment” – an investment advisory agreement must provide that the Firm may not assign the contract without the client’s consent.

 “Change of membership” – an investment advisory agreement must provide that the adviser will notify its client of any change in membership of a partnership or limited liability company within a reasonable time after the change.

In addition, the investment adviser’s ability to utilise certain other clauses is limited.

 “Prohibition on waiver of compliance” – an investment advisory agreement may not include any condition, stipulation or provision binding any person to waive compliance with any requirements of the Advisers Act, or any rule, regulation or order there under.
