 “Prohibition on hedge clauses/disclaimer(s)” – an investment advisory agreement may not contain any hedge clause / disclaimer(s), or other contractual provision that is likely to lead a client to believe that he or she has waived any right of action against the Firm.

The Advisers Act also prohibits an investment adviser from receiving any type of advisory fee calculated as a percentage of capital gains or appreciation in the client’s account (“performance-based fee”).

However, Rule 205-3 of the Advisers Act permits an investment adviser to charge performance-based fees to: i. Clients with at least $1 million under management with the adviser or more than $2 million in net worth; and ii. Clients who are “qualified purchasers” under the Investment Company Act of 1940 (the “Company Act”(see Section 8.8)); and iii. Certain knowledgeable employees of the investment adviser.

sections.title: Client Categorisation
sections.content: Please refer to the ‘Conduct of Business – Client Categorisation’ chapter in this manual for the information that must be provided to clients in respect of their categorisation.

The Firm does not commit to the FRC’s Stewardship Code, as this is not appropriate to its business strategy. A statement to this effect, explaining the nature of the Firm’s alternative investment strategy, is published on the Firm’s website (or in another accessible form).

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – AIFM SPECIFIC
chapters.summary: 7. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – AIFM SPECIFIC
chapters.content:
7. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – AIFM SPECIFIC

## 7.1 Purpose

Although this Compliance Manual as a whole is relevant to Alamut’s business model and activities as an AIFM, this chapter is specific to regulatory requirements relating to the AIF.

## 7.2 Investor information (disclosure prior to investment)

For each UK AIF that an AIFM manages, and for each AIF it markets in the UK certain information must be made available to investors before they invest in the AIF. The matters that need to be disclosed are numerous and are largely a matter of good business practice e.g. a description of the objectives of the AIF, the maximum level of leverage that can be employed etc. In addition a copy of the latest annual report and the latest NAV must be provided. See FUND 3.2.2 to 3.2.4A for further details. When an unauthorised AIF is marketed to a retail client then they must be offered and, if so requested, provided with additional information to that specified under FUND 3.2 and reference should be made to COBS 18.5A.11. In addition, a Key Information Document under PRIIPs Regulation will be required (FUND 3.2.4B). It should be noted that whilst they represent different regulatory frameworks, both the AIFMD and the PRIIPs Regulation define both ‘retail client’ and ‘professional client’ as per MiFID. Therefore if the investor/potential investor is neither a ‘per se professional client’ nor an ‘elective professional client’ – the latter requiring application of both the ‘qualitative test’ and the ‘quantitative test’ – then they will be regarded as a ‘retail client’.

## 7.3 Periodic disclosure (disclosure post investment)

It is a requirement that periodic disclosures be made by the AIFM to investors in a UK AIF that it manages and in respect of any non-UK AIF that is marketed in the UK. The required disclosures relate to:  the percentage of AIF’s assets which are subject to special arrangements arising from their illiquid nature  any new arrangements for managing the liquidity of the AIF  the current risk profile of the AIF and the risk management systems employed to manage those risks and, where relevant  any changes to the maximum level of leverage that may be employed, and any right of the reuse of collateral or any guarantee granted under the leveraging arrangement  the total amount of leverage employed by that AIF Although the required disclosures can be satisfied by including them in the AIF’s annual report (see next section), note that any new arrangements for managing liquidity (for an AIF that is not an unleveraged closed-ended AIF) should be notified to investors “whenever they make changes” and “immediately” where gates, side pockets or similar special arrangements are activated or where it is decided to suspend redemptions. See FUND 3.2.5 and Articles 108 and 109 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation, as amended by SI 2019/328, for further details.

## 7.4 Annual report

It is a requirement that an AIFM for each UK AIF managed, and for each AIF marketed in the UK, that an annual report:  is made available to investors for each financial year;  be provided to investors on request; and  is made available to the FCA . Such an annual report must be made available no later than six months after the end of the financial year (unless the AIF is a non-UCITS retail scheme in which reference should be made to COLL 4.5.14. The contents must include, but is not limited to, a balance sheet (or statement of assets and liabilities), a report on the activities for the year as well as remuneration data. The latter will need to include:  The total remuneration paid to staff for the financial year, split into variable and fixed, including any carried interest paid by the AIF where relevant; and the number of beneficiaries;

 The aggregate remuneration of AIFM Remuneration Code staff, broken down by senior management and members of staff. Reference should be made to FUND 3.3 and to Articles 103 to 107 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation, as amended, for details of the required contents of the annual report.

## 7.5 Reporting to the FCA

The reporting obligation applies to Alamut as a full-scope UK AIFM firm; it does not matter whether or not an AIF is marketed in the EEA. For each AIF managed the report to the FCA must include:  the main instruments traded in;  the principal markets where Alamut as an AIFM actively trades or is a member of;  the principal exposures and most important concentrations of each AIF For each UK AIF or EEA AIF managed, and for each AIF marketed in the UK, the report to the FCA must include:  the percentage of the AIF's assets that are subject to special arrangements arising from their illiquid nature;  any new arrangements for managing the liquidity of the AIF;  the current risk profile of the AIF and the risk management systems employed by the AIFM to manage the market risk, liquidity risk, counterparty risk and other risks, including operational risk;  information on the main categories of assets in which the AIF is invested; and  the results of the stress tests performed in accordance with FUND 3.6.3 R (2) (Liquidity systems and procedures) and FUND 3.7.5 R (2)(b) (Risk management systems). Where leverage is employed on a substantial basis (defined as ‘when the exposure of an AIF as calculated according to the commitment method exceeds three times its net asset value’ – see FUND 3.4.6) then the FCA will wish to know:  the overall level of leverage employed by the AIF;  a breakdown of leverage arising from borrowing of cash or securities and leverage embedded in financial derivatives;  the extent to which the AIF's assets have been reused under leveraging arrangements; and  the identity of the five largest sources of borrowed cash or securities for the AIF, and the amounts of leverage received from each of those sources. See SUP 16.8 and FUND 3.4 for further information.

## 7.6 Liquidity management

This relates to the liquidity of an AIF (that is not an unleveraged closed-ended AIF) rather than the AIFM and applies to a full-scope UK AIFM such as Alamut. In brief the investment strategy, liquidity profile and redemption policy of each AIF must be aligned, and there must be a documented liquidity management process in place. The detailed requirements, including the need for monitoring and stress testing, can be found in Articles 46 to 49 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended. See also FUND 3.6. Alamut has suitable liquidity management policies and procedures in place and which is reviewed annually.

## 7.7 Risk management

In a similar manner to liquidity management (see above) ‘risk’ here relates to the AIFs rather than to Alamut as the AIFM. As a full-scope AIFM, and on a proportional basis, Alamut is required to maintain a permanent risk management function that is segregated, both functionally and hierarchically, from the firm’s operating units including portfolio management. The role is to implement adequate risk management systems to identify, measure, manage and monitor appropriately all risks relevant to each AIF investment strategy and to which each AIF is or may be exposed including levels of leverage, liquidity risk (as above), operational risks etc. Periodic stress testing and scenario analysis is conducted to address risks arising from potential changes in market conditions which might impact the AIF. Periodic back-testing is also undertaken to review the validity of risk measurement arrangements which include model-based forecasts and estimates.

Alamut has implemented a proportionate risk management policy, details of which can be provided by the Compliance Officer. The risk management systems are reviewed whenever necessary but in event at least once a year. References to the detailed requirements can be found in FUND 3.7 and Articles 38 – 45 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended.

## 7.8 Position disclosure obligations and ‘asset stripping’

The AIFMD imposes disclosure obligations (Articles 26 – 30) when an AIF:  acquires control of a non-listed company or issuer  acquires, holds or disposes of shares of a non-listed company but where control is not acquired. Please refer to the chapter on ‘Disclosure and Transparency (including Takeover Code)’ in the Manual for further details.

## 7.9 Investment in securitisation positions

Where there is an exposure to a securitisation that does not meet the requirements provided in the Securitisation Regulation (EU Regulation 2017/2402 as amended by SI 2019/660) then an AIFM is required to act and take appropriate action in the best interests of the AIFs investors.

## 7.10 Prime brokerage firms

Alamut has appointed Morgan Stanley and Bank of America as its prime brokers and in doing has exercised due skill, care and diligence in the selection and appointment process. The appointment is in a written contract, details of which can be provided by the Compliance Officer and is compliant with the AIFMD in that any possibility of transfer and reuse of AIF assets is provided for; it complies with the AIFs’ instrument of constitution; and the depositary has been informed of the contract. In addition there is an agreement between Alamut and the prime broker in accord with Article 91 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended concerning making available certain information to the depositary. See Article 20 and Article 91 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended (and FUND 3.8) for further information.

## 7.11 Valuation

Although the valuation of the assets of the AIFs is performed by Morgan Stanley Fund Services (MSFS) details of which have been notified to the FCA in accordance with FUND 3.9.11, Alamut retains responsibility for the valuation, as well as that of the NAV and its publication. To ensure that consistent procedures are Alamut has adopted a valuation policy in conjunction with MSFS. The valuation policy is reviewed when appropriate and at least annually. See Articles 67 – 74 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended and FUND 3.9.

## 7.12 Delegation

Delegation in respect of AIFM investment management functions (being portfolio management or risk management) and the AIFM management functions (a term which apart from portfolio management and risk management also includes e.g. valuation, marketing, fund management accounting etc. – see Annex I of the AIFMD) – other than supporting tasks such as administrative or technical functions - must be in accordance with FUND 3.10. Alamut is required to: i. be able to justify its entire delegation structure with objective reasons ii. satisfy itself that the delegate has sufficient resources and the persons involved “are of sufficiently good repute and experience” iii. ensure that any delegation of AIFM investment management functions is only conferred on an entity that is authorised for asset management and is subject to supervision iv. ensure that should AIFM investment management functions be delegated to a non-UK entity that there are cooperation agreements between the FCA and the supervisory authority of the delegate v. ensure that such delegation neither prevents the FCA from supervising Alamut nor prevents it, or the AIF, from being managed in the best interests of the investor

vi. demonstrate that the delegate is qualified and capable; was selected with all due care; and that Alamut can monitor the delegate, can give instructions at any time and can withdraw the delegation with immediate effect when in the interest of the investors. The firm’s liability towards the AIFs and its investors is not affected by any delegation or sub-delegation. The firm is required to ensure that a delegate may only sub-delegate with the consent of Alamut. Note that delegation, or sub-delegation, of AIFM investment management functions cannot be to:  the depositary (or delegate of the depositary); or  any other entity whose interests might conflict with those of Alamut (or investors of the AIF) unless suitable segregation is in place and such potential conflicts are both managed and disclosed to the investors of the AIF Alamut will review on an ongoing basis the services provided by appointed delegates and, where applicable, where sub-delegates have been appointed. Alamut will make its delegates aware that they are also required to review the services provided by each of their delegates on an ongoing basis. An AIFM is not permitted to delegate its functions to the extent that it can no longer be considered to be the AIFM of the AIF to the extent that it becomes a letterbox entity. See also FUND 3.10.9 for when an AIFM might be considered to be a ‘letterbox entity’. The FCA must be notified before any such delegation arrangements, and where relevant, before any sub- delegation arrangements, are effective Further details can be found in Articles 75 to 82 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended.

## 7.13 Depositaries

Each UK AIF must have a single depositary appointed with the assets entrusted to the depositary for safekeeping (FUND 3.11. 4). In the case of a non-UK AIF which is marketed in the UK there is no depositary requirement but there is a requirement to appoint one or more entities to carry out the duties of cash monitoring; safekeeping; and oversight (sometimes referred to as ‘depositary lite’) (FUND 3.11.33). In terms of marketing a non-UK AIF in the UK the entity/entities appointed to perform those functions, which cannot be Alamut, must either:  be firms established in the UK that have Part 4A permission of ‘acting as trustee or depositary of an AIF’ and have own funds of at least €125K where the duties are carried out in the UK; or  be entities that are not established in the UK where the duties are not carried out in the UK; or  be entities established in the UK which are carrying out duties from a non-UK branch. There is no depositary requirement for a non-UK AIF which is not marketed in the UK.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.content: Although this Compliance Manual as a whole is relevant to Alamut’s business model and activities as an AIFM, this chapter is specific to regulatory requirements relating to the AIF.

sections.title: Investor information (disclosure prior to investment)
sections.content: For each UK AIF that an AIFM manages, and for each AIF it markets in the UK certain information must be made available to investors before they invest in the AIF. The matters that need to be disclosed are numerous and are largely a matter of good business practice e.g. a description of the objectives of the AIF, the maximum level of leverage that can be employed etc. In addition a copy of the latest annual report and the latest NAV must be provided. See FUND 3.2.2 to 3.2.4A for further details. When an unauthorised AIF is marketed to a retail client then they must be offered and, if so requested, provided with additional information to that specified under FUND 3.2 and reference should be made to COBS 18.5A.11. In addition, a Key Information Document under PRIIPs Regulation will be required (FUND 3.2.4B). It should be noted that whilst they represent different regulatory frameworks, both the AIFMD and the PRIIPs Regulation define both ‘retail client’ and ‘professional client’ as per MiFID. Therefore if the investor/potential investor is neither a ‘per se professional client’ nor an ‘elective professional client’ – the latter requiring application of both the ‘qualitative test’ and the ‘quantitative test’ – then they will be regarded as a ‘retail client’.

sections.title: Periodic disclosure (disclosure post investment)
sections.content: It is a requirement that periodic disclosures be made by the AIFM to investors in a UK AIF that it manages and in respect of any non-UK AIF that is marketed in the UK. The required disclosures relate to:  the percentage of AIF’s assets which are subject to special arrangements arising from their illiquid nature  any new arrangements for managing the liquidity of the AIF  the current risk profile of the AIF and the risk management systems employed to manage those risks and, where relevant  any changes to the maximum level of leverage that may be employed, and any right of the reuse of collateral or any guarantee granted under the leveraging arrangement  the total amount of leverage employed by that AIF Although the required disclosures can be satisfied by including them in the AIF’s annual report (see next section), note that any new arrangements for managing liquidity (for an AIF that is not an unleveraged closed-ended AIF) should be notified to investors “whenever they make changes” and “immediately” where gates, side pockets or similar special arrangements are activated or where it is decided to suspend redemptions. See FUND 3.2.5 and Articles 108 and 109 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation, as amended by SI 2019/328, for further details.

sections.title: Annual report
sections.content:
It is a requirement that an AIFM for each UK AIF managed, and for each AIF marketed in the UK, that an annual report:  is made available to investors for each financial year;  be provided to investors on request; and  is made available to the FCA . Such an annual report must be made available no later than six months after the end of the financial year (unless the AIF is a non-UCITS retail scheme in which reference should be made to COLL 4.5.14. The contents must include, but is not limited to, a balance sheet (or statement of assets and liabilities), a report on the activities for the year as well as remuneration data. The latter will need to include:  The total remuneration paid to staff for the financial year, split into variable and fixed, including any carried interest paid by the AIF where relevant; and the number of beneficiaries;

 The aggregate remuneration of AIFM Remuneration Code staff, broken down by senior management and members of staff. Reference should be made to FUND 3.3 and to Articles 103 to 107 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation, as amended, for details of the required contents of the annual report.

sections.title: Reporting to the FCA
sections.content: The reporting obligation applies to Alamut as a full-scope UK AIFM firm; it does not matter whether or not an AIF is marketed in the EEA. For each AIF managed the report to the FCA must include:  the main instruments traded in;  the principal markets where Alamut as an AIFM actively trades or is a member of;  the principal exposures and most important concentrations of each AIF For each UK AIF or EEA AIF managed, and for each AIF marketed in the UK, the report to the FCA must include:  the percentage of the AIF's assets that are subject to special arrangements arising from their illiquid nature;  any new arrangements for managing the liquidity of the AIF;  the current risk profile of the AIF and the risk management systems employed by the AIFM to manage the market risk, liquidity risk, counterparty risk and other risks, including operational risk;  information on the main categories of assets in which the AIF is invested; and  the results of the stress tests performed in accordance with FUND 3.6.3 R (2) (Liquidity systems and procedures) and FUND 3.7.5 R (2)(b) (Risk management systems). Where leverage is employed on a substantial basis (defined as ‘when the exposure of an AIF as calculated according to the commitment method exceeds three times its net asset value’ – see FUND 3.4.6) then the FCA will wish to know:  the overall level of leverage employed by the AIF;  a breakdown of leverage arising from borrowing of cash or securities and leverage embedded in financial derivatives;  the extent to which the AIF's assets have been reused under leveraging arrangements; and  the identity of the five largest sources of borrowed cash or securities for the AIF, and the amounts of leverage received from each of those sources. See SUP 16.8 and FUND 3.4 for further information.

sections.title: Liquidity management
sections.content: This relates to the liquidity of an AIF (that is not an unleveraged closed-ended AIF) rather than the AIFM and applies to a full-scope UK AIFM such as Alamut. In brief the investment strategy, liquidity profile and redemption policy of each AIF must be aligned, and there must be a documented liquidity management process in place. The detailed requirements, including the need for monitoring and stress testing, can be found in Articles 46 to 49 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended. See also FUND 3.6. Alamut has suitable liquidity management policies and procedures in place and which is reviewed annually.

sections.title: Risk management
sections.content:
In a similar manner to liquidity management (see above) ‘risk’ here relates to the AIFs rather than to Alamut as the AIFM. As a full-scope AIFM, and on a proportional basis, Alamut is required to maintain a permanent risk management function that is segregated, both functionally and hierarchically, from the firm’s operating units including portfolio management. The role is to implement adequate risk management systems to identify, measure, manage and monitor appropriately all risks relevant to each AIF investment strategy and to which each AIF is or may be exposed including levels of leverage, liquidity risk (as above), operational risks etc. Periodic stress testing and scenario analysis is conducted to address risks arising from potential changes in market conditions which might impact the AIF. Periodic back-testing is also undertaken to review the validity of risk measurement arrangements which include model-based forecasts and estimates.

Alamut has implemented a proportionate risk management policy, details of which can be provided by the Compliance Officer. The risk management systems are reviewed whenever necessary but in event at least once a year. References to the detailed requirements can be found in FUND 3.7 and Articles 38 – 45 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended.

sections.title: Position disclosure obligations and ‘asset stripping’
sections.content: The AIFMD imposes disclosure obligations (Articles 26 – 30) when an AIF:  acquires control of a non-listed company or issuer  acquires, holds or disposes of shares of a non-listed company but where control is not acquired. Please refer to the chapter on ‘Disclosure and Transparency (including Takeover Code)’ in the Manual for further details.

sections.title: Investment in securitisation positions
sections.content: Where there is an exposure to a securitisation that does not meet the requirements provided in the Securitisation Regulation (EU Regulation 2017/2402 as amended by SI 2019/660) then an AIFM is required to act and take appropriate action in the best interests of the AIFs investors.

sections.title: Prime brokerage firms
sections.content: Alamut has appointed Morgan Stanley and Bank of America as its prime brokers and in doing has exercised due skill, care and diligence in the selection and appointment process. The appointment is in a written contract, details of which can be provided by the Compliance Officer and is compliant with the AIFMD in that any possibility of transfer and reuse of AIF assets is provided for; it complies with the AIFs’ instrument of constitution; and the depositary has been informed of the contract. In addition there is an agreement between Alamut and the prime broker in accord with Article 91 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended concerning making available certain information to the depositary. See Article 20 and Article 91 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended (and FUND 3.8) for further information.

sections.title: Valuation
sections.content: Although the valuation of the assets of the AIFs is performed by Morgan Stanley Fund Services (MSFS) details of which have been notified to the FCA in accordance with FUND 3.9.11, Alamut retains responsibility for the valuation, as well as that of the NAV and its publication. To ensure that consistent procedures are Alamut has adopted a valuation policy in conjunction with MSFS. The valuation policy is reviewed when appropriate and at least annually. See Articles 67 – 74 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended and FUND 3.9.

sections.title: Delegation
sections.content:
Delegation in respect of AIFM investment management functions (being portfolio management or risk management) and the AIFM management functions (a term which apart from portfolio management and risk management also includes e.g. valuation, marketing, fund management accounting etc. – see Annex I of the AIFMD) – other than supporting tasks such as administrative or technical functions - must be in accordance with FUND 3.10. Alamut is required to: i. be able to justify its entire delegation structure with objective reasons ii. satisfy itself that the delegate has sufficient resources and the persons involved “are of sufficiently good repute and experience” iii. ensure that any delegation of AIFM investment management functions is only conferred on an entity that is authorised for asset management and is subject to supervision iv. ensure that should AIFM investment management functions be delegated to a non-UK entity that there are cooperation agreements between the FCA and the supervisory authority of the delegate v. ensure that such delegation neither prevents the FCA from supervising Alamut nor prevents it, or the AIF, from being managed in the best interests of the investor

vi. demonstrate that the delegate is qualified and capable; was selected with all due care; and that Alamut can monitor the delegate, can give instructions at any time and can withdraw the delegation with immediate effect when in the interest of the investors. The firm’s liability towards the AIFs and its investors is not affected by any delegation or sub-delegation. The firm is required to ensure that a delegate may only sub-delegate with the consent of Alamut. Note that delegation, or sub-delegation, of AIFM investment management functions cannot be to:  the depositary (or delegate of the depositary); or  any other entity whose interests might conflict with those of Alamut (or investors of the AIF) unless suitable segregation is in place and such potential conflicts are both managed and disclosed to the investors of the AIF Alamut will review on an ongoing basis the services provided by appointed delegates and, where applicable, where sub-delegates have been appointed. Alamut will make its delegates aware that they are also required to review the services provided by each of their delegates on an ongoing basis. An AIFM is not permitted to delegate its functions to the extent that it can no longer be considered to be the AIFM of the AIF to the extent that it becomes a letterbox entity. See also FUND 3.10.9 for when an AIFM might be considered to be a ‘letterbox entity’. The FCA must be notified before any such delegation arrangements, and where relevant, before any sub- delegation arrangements, are effective Further details can be found in Articles 75 to 82 of the AIFMD level 2 regulation as amended.

sections.title: Depositaries
sections.content: Each UK AIF must have a single depositary appointed with the assets entrusted to the depositary for safekeeping (FUND 3.11. 4). In the case of a non-UK AIF which is marketed in the UK there is no depositary requirement but there is a requirement to appoint one or more entities to carry out the duties of cash monitoring; safekeeping; and oversight (sometimes referred to as ‘depositary lite’) (FUND 3.11.33). In terms of marketing a non-UK AIF in the UK the entity/entities appointed to perform those functions, which cannot be Alamut, must either:  be firms established in the UK that have Part 4A permission of ‘acting as trustee or depositary of an AIF’ and have own funds of at least €125K where the duties are carried out in the UK; or  be entities that are not established in the UK where the duties are not carried out in the UK; or  be entities established in the UK which are carrying out duties from a non-UK branch. There is no depositary requirement for a non-UK AIF which is not marketed in the UK.

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – INDUCEMENTS (excluding Research)
chapters.summary: 8. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – INDUCEMENTS (excluding Research)
chapters.content:
8. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – INDUCEMENTS (excluding Research)

## 8.1 Introduction

There are restrictions imposed upon the receipt or payment of commissions etc. from or to third-parties.

The Handbook rules apply differently depending upon whether:

 the activities relate to the management of the fund (non-MiFID activities); or  the activities relate to activities other than the management of the fund making use of our additional ‘top-up’ activities e.g. managing third-party accounts (MiFID activities)

For our non-MiFID activities, the inducement rules in COBS 2.3 (which broadly reflects the ‘inducements’ obligations in the AIFMD Level 2 Regulation) would normally be applicable. However when the firm is executing orders, or is placing orders, in respect of the fund (‘dealing for the fund’) the FCA has decided to impose inducement rules based upon MiFID (COBS 18 Annex 1). 8.2 Non-MiFID AIFM activities: Fees, commissions and non-monetary benefits (not associated with dealing for the fund) The Firm is prohibited from paying or accepting any fee or commission, or providing or receiving any non- monetary benefit in relation to activities associated with managing other than:  A fee, commission or non-monetary benefit paid by or to the fund (or a person on behalf of the fund); or  Proper fees necessary for the provision of designated investment business e.g. legal fees, custody fees etc. or  A third party fee, commission or non-monetary benefit provided it does not impair compliance with the Firm’s duty to act in the best interests of the fund. With regard to the third bullet point above, it is a requirement that:  The existence and amount (or where the amount cannot be ascertained then the method of calculating that amount) is clearly disclosed to investors before the provision of the service; and  The payment or provision of the non-monetary benefit is designed to enhance the quality of service to the fund To satisfy the requirement for disclosure it is acceptable (COBS 2.3.2) to:  Disclose the essential arrangements in summary form  Undertake to provide the investors with further details on request  Honour that undertaking Guidance on reasonable non-monetary benefits that are capable of enhancing the quality of the service provided to the fund and, depending on the circumstances, are capable of being paid or received without breaching the ‘fund’s best interests rule’ can be found in COBS 2.3.14 -2.3.16C.

8.3 Non-MiFID AIFM activities: Fees, commissions and non-monetary benefits (when dealing for the fund) As mentioned in the introduction above, the FCA has imposed MiFID-based ‘inducements’ rules when dealing for the fund, even though that is not a MiFID activity. When executing or placing orders with other entities for execution the Firm is prohibited from:

- Accepting and retaining any fees, commissions or monetary benefits

- Accepting any non-monetary benefits

where these are paid or provided by or on behalf of a third party. Any inducements falling within a) above must be returned to the fund “as soon as is reasonably possible after receipt” and inform investors (e.g. through the annual report) about the transfer(s). The above does not apply to minor non-monetary benefits that ‘enhance the quality of service’ provided to the fund and are of a scale and nature which will not breach the ‘fund’s best interests rule’. Guidance on acceptable minor non-monetary benefits can be found in COBS 18 Annex 1(3). Please see separate chapter ‘Inducements and Research’.

## 8.4 MiFID activities: Fees, commissions and non-monetary benefits

In relation to the provision of investment services and ancillary services, MiFID firms are restricted in paying or accepting fees, commissions or non-monetary benefits unless paid by the client or is designed to enhance the quality of service to the client and does not conflict with the firm’s duty to act in the best interests of the client.

There are more stringent requirements when the investment services are ‘independent advice’ (or, for retail clients, simply ‘advice’) or ‘portfolio management’.

## 8.5 Portfolio management and advice

UK retail clients Firms providing portfolio management services or advice (whether restricted or independent) to UK retail clients must not accept any fees, commission, monetary or non-monetary benefits which are paid by third parties unless:

- ‘acceptable minor non-monetary’ (note the use of both ‘minor’ and ‘non-monetary’); or

- third party research that is paid for by the firm out of its own resources or by means of a research

payment account (see ‘Conduct of Business – Inducements: Research’ chapter for further information). Note that the key word is ‘accept’ so acceptance of any such benefits etc. is not permissible even if the intention is to rebate the amount to the client(s). For the record, whilst MiFID II refers to ‘independent advice’ and prohibits the retention of those benefits that are not ‘acceptable minor non-monetary’ benefits, the FCA has decided to extend these requirements to prohibit ‘acceptance’ and to capture non- independent advice where UK retail clients are concerned.

Professional clients (and non-UK retail clients) Firms providing portfolio management services or independent advice to professional clients or non-UK retail clients must not accept and retain any fees, commission or monetary benefits or non-monetary benefits which are paid by third parties unless:

- ‘acceptable minor non-monetary’ (note the use of both ‘minor’ and ‘non-monetary’); or

- third party research that is paid for by the firm out of its own resources or by means of a research

payment account (see ‘Conduct of Business – Inducements: Research’ chapter for further information). Any receipts not meeting the criteria in (a) must be transferred/returned to clients and the clients duly informed e.g. via periodic reporting statements (COBS 2.3A.16).

Note that the key word is ‘retain’ so a firm is allowed to accept such benefits provided that they are rebated to the client “as soon as reasonably possible after receipt”.

The above only applies in the case of independent advice. Non-independent advice is not governed by the above and instead will fall under general inducement rules i.e. the rules applicable to other than portfolio management or advice – see below. ‘Acceptable minor non-monetary benefits’ include e.g. participation in training events. Reference to COBS 2.3A.19 should be made for examples of other benefits deemed to meet this classification and the conditions that must be met (including prior disclosure and the need to be “capable of enhancing the quality of service provided to the client”). Note that in respect of portfolio management or advice no monetary benefits can be retained.

Investment services other than portfolio management or advice Note that ‘advice’ in the context of this chapter means ‘independent advice’ except in the case of retail clients in the UK retail when it captures both ‘independent advice’ and ‘non-independent advice.

A firm is permitted to pay or accept a fee or commission or any non-monetary benefit paid by third parties in connection with the provision of other investment services (or ancillary services) provided that:

 It is a payment or benefit necessary for the provision of investment services such as custody costs or legal fees which, because of their nature, cannot give rise to conflicts or third-party research received in accordance with regulatory requirements; or

 It is designed to enhance the quality of service to the client and it does not conflict with the duty to act honestly, fairly and professionally in the best interests of the client. The existence and amount of this benefit must be disclosed to the client prior to the provision of the service. Where the amount cannot be ascertained then the method of calculating it must be provided instead. Note that the criteria for benefits that are “designed to enhance the quality of service to the client” must be met - see COBS 2.3A.8 - 9.

Note that unlike ‘portfolio management services and advice’ above, (a) here there is no reference to “acceptable minor non-monetary benefits” and (b) monetary benefits may be paid or accepted, subject to meeting the above criteria, a well as non-monetary benefits.

Disclosure Although minor non-monetary benefits (see above) can be described in a generic way, non-monetary benefits that are not ‘minor’ (only permitted for services other than portfolio management or advice) must be priced and disclosed separately. Where monetary benefits are permitted then these must also be disclosed – all disclosures are required prior to the provision of the service(s).

Where the Firm has provided the method of calculating the amount of a benefit (because it was not possible to ascertain the amount ex-ante) the client must be informed of the exact amount of the payment or benefit on an ex-post basis.

## 8.6 Record keeping

A record should be maintained:

 Of fees, commissions or non-monetary benefits and any disclosures made to investors;  Evidencing how the fees, commission or non-monetary benefits paid or received by the Firm, or that the firm intends to use, enhances (if applicable) the quality of the services provided to the fund(s) and the steps taken in order not to impair the Firm’s compliance with the duty to act honestly, fairly and professionally in the best interests of the fund(s) and investors.

Records should be maintained for at least five years.

## 8.7 Personal Gifts and Benefits

Introduction The Firm must take reasonable steps to prevent itself, or any person acting on its behalf, from:

 Accepting or offering any inducements; or  Directing or referring any actual or potential business to another person on its own initiative if it is likely to conflict with any duty the Firm has toward the fund and its investors. In this regard, you are specifically reminded of the FCA’s Principle 6 (Customers’ interests) which requires the Firm to pay due regard to the interests of its clients and treat them fairly. The Firm prohibits the offering, the giving or acceptance of any bribe intended to induce an ‘improper performance’ of a relevant function or activity. Please refer to the Firm’s anti-bribery policy.

## 8.8 Prior permission

All persons are required to seek written permission from the Compliance Officer before giving to, or accepting from, any party or intermediary a gift or benefit of £150 or more. The records of the (i) request and (ii) permission must be retained on file for at least five years.

There may be situations where you are offered a gift in circumstances where it is not possible to consult the Compliance Officer in advance, such as in a meeting. In such situations you must use your best judgment to decide whether or not to accept the gift. However, if you accept a gift or benefit, you may be required by the Compliance Officer to return it.

## 8.9 Ordinary business courtesies

The only exception to this rule is if the Firm’s member of staff gives or receives an “ordinary business courtesy”. “Ordinary business courtesies” include business lunches, dinners, drinks and other reasonable business entertainment that is neither repeated nor excessive and the main purpose of which is to further business relationships. Therefore, for example, a dinner that costs no more than £50 per person is excluded from the requirement to seek prior permission from the Compliance Officer.

## 8.10 Cash and cash equivalents

Under no circumstances may cash or cash convertible gifts be offered or accepted. Any such gift offered in the course of, or in connection with, a current or prospective business relationship must be declined, and reported immediately to the Compliance Officer.

## 8.11 Gifts and benefits register

The Compliance Officer will maintain a register of all gifts and benefits received.

## 8.12 Quarterly self-certifications

At every quarter end, a signed self-certification should be provided to the Compliance Officer. If you have not received or been offered any gifts or inducements, you must confirm this “nil return” in writing by completing and returning a signed self-certification form to the Compliance Officer. A copy of the Staff Self- Certification Form is available from the Compliance Officer.

The combined Quarterly Attestation Form can be found separately and on request from the Compliance Officer.

A key test is to consider whether the gift etc., if made known to the investors in the fund, might cause concern about the objectivity of decisions etc. taken on their behalf. In making a decision, thought must be given to the frequency of such gifts etc. which, in isolation may be of low value but collectively give cause for concern.

## 8.13 Useful Links

COBS 2.3 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/2/3.html

COBS 2.3A https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/2/3A.html

COBS 18 Annex 1 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/18/Annex1.html

sections.title: MiFID activities: Fees, commissions and non-monetary benefits
sections.content:
In relation to the provision of investment services and ancillary services, MiFID firms are restricted in paying or accepting fees, commissions or non-monetary benefits unless paid by the client or is designed to enhance the quality of service to the client and does not conflict with the firm’s duty to act in the best interests of the client.

There are more stringent requirements when the investment services are ‘independent advice’ (or, for retail clients, simply ‘advice’) or ‘portfolio management’.

sections.title: Portfolio management and advice
sections.content:
UK retail clients Firms providing portfolio management services or advice (whether restricted or independent) to UK retail clients must not accept any fees, commission, monetary or non-monetary benefits which are paid by third parties unless:

- ‘acceptable minor non-monetary’ (note the use of both ‘minor’ and ‘non-monetary’); or

- third party research that is paid for by the firm out of its own resources or by means of a research

payment account (see ‘Conduct of Business – Inducements: Research’ chapter for further information). Note that the key word is ‘accept’ so acceptance of any such benefits etc. is not permissible even if the intention is to rebate the amount to the client(s). For the record, whilst MiFID II refers to ‘independent advice’ and prohibits the retention of those benefits that are not ‘acceptable minor non-monetary’ benefits, the FCA has decided to extend these requirements to prohibit ‘acceptance’ and to capture non- independent advice where UK retail clients are concerned.

Professional clients (and non-UK retail clients) Firms providing portfolio management services or independent advice to professional clients or non-UK retail clients must not accept and retain any fees, commission or monetary benefits or non-monetary benefits which are paid by third parties unless:

- ‘acceptable minor non-monetary’ (note the use of both ‘minor’ and ‘non-monetary’); or

- third party research that is paid for by the firm out of its own resources or by means of a research

payment account (see ‘Conduct of Business – Inducements: Research’ chapter for further information). Any receipts not meeting the criteria in (a) must be transferred/returned to clients and the clients duly informed e.g. via periodic reporting statements (COBS 2.3A.16).

Note that the key word is ‘retain’ so a firm is allowed to accept such benefits provided that they are rebated to the client “as soon as reasonably possible after receipt”.

The above only applies in the case of independent advice. Non-independent advice is not governed by the above and instead will fall under general inducement rules i.e. the rules applicable to other than portfolio management or advice – see below. ‘Acceptable minor non-monetary benefits’ include e.g. participation in training events. Reference to COBS 2.3A.19 should be made for examples of other benefits deemed to meet this classification and the conditions that must be met (including prior disclosure and the need to be “capable of enhancing the quality of service provided to the client”). Note that in respect of portfolio management or advice no monetary benefits can be retained.

Investment services other than portfolio management or advice Note that ‘advice’ in the context of this chapter means ‘independent advice’ except in the case of retail clients in the UK retail when it captures both ‘independent advice’ and ‘non-independent advice.

A firm is permitted to pay or accept a fee or commission or any non-monetary benefit paid by third parties in connection with the provision of other investment services (or ancillary services) provided that:

 It is a payment or benefit necessary for the provision of investment services such as custody costs or legal fees which, because of their nature, cannot give rise to conflicts or third-party research received in accordance with regulatory requirements; or

 It is designed to enhance the quality of service to the client and it does not conflict with the duty to act honestly, fairly and professionally in the best interests of the client. The existence and amount of this benefit must be disclosed to the client prior to the provision of the service. Where the amount cannot be ascertained then the method of calculating it must be provided instead. Note that the criteria for benefits that are “designed to enhance the quality of service to the client” must be met - see COBS 2.3A.8 - 9.

Note that unlike ‘portfolio management services and advice’ above, (a) here there is no reference to “acceptable minor non-monetary benefits” and (b) monetary benefits may be paid or accepted, subject to meeting the above criteria, a well as non-monetary benefits.

Disclosure Although minor non-monetary benefits (see above) can be described in a generic way, non-monetary benefits that are not ‘minor’ (only permitted for services other than portfolio management or advice) must be priced and disclosed separately. Where monetary benefits are permitted then these must also be disclosed – all disclosures are required prior to the provision of the service(s).

Where the Firm has provided the method of calculating the amount of a benefit (because it was not possible to ascertain the amount ex-ante) the client must be informed of the exact amount of the payment or benefit on an ex-post basis.

sections.title: Record keeping
sections.content:
A record should be maintained:

 Of fees, commissions or non-monetary benefits and any disclosures made to investors;  Evidencing how the fees, commission or non-monetary benefits paid or received by the Firm, or that the firm intends to use, enhances (if applicable) the quality of the services provided to the fund(s) and the steps taken in order not to impair the Firm’s compliance with the duty to act honestly, fairly and professionally in the best interests of the fund(s) and investors.

Records should be maintained for at least five years.

sections.title: Personal Gifts and Benefits
sections.content:
Introduction The Firm must take reasonable steps to prevent itself, or any person acting on its behalf, from:

 Accepting or offering any inducements; or  Directing or referring any actual or potential business to another person on its own initiative if it is likely to conflict with any duty the Firm has toward the fund and its investors. In this regard, you are specifically reminded of the FCA’s Principle 6 (Customers’ interests) which requires the Firm to pay due regard to the interests of its clients and treat them fairly. The Firm prohibits the offering, the giving or acceptance of any bribe intended to induce an ‘improper performance’ of a relevant function or activity. Please refer to the Firm’s anti-bribery policy.

sections.title: Prior permission
sections.content:
All persons are required to seek written permission from the Compliance Officer before giving to, or accepting from, any party or intermediary a gift or benefit of £150 or more. The records of the (i) request and (ii) permission must be retained on file for at least five years.

There may be situations where you are offered a gift in circumstances where it is not possible to consult the Compliance Officer in advance, such as in a meeting. In such situations you must use your best judgment to decide whether or not to accept the gift. However, if you accept a gift or benefit, you may be required by the Compliance Officer to return it.

sections.title: Ordinary business courtesies
sections.content: The only exception to this rule is if the Firm’s member of staff gives or receives an “ordinary business courtesy”. “Ordinary business courtesies” include business lunches, dinners, drinks and other reasonable business entertainment that is neither repeated nor excessive and the main purpose of which is to further business relationships. Therefore, for example, a dinner that costs no more than £50 per person is excluded from the requirement to seek prior permission from the Compliance Officer.

sections.title: Cash and cash equivalents
sections.content: Under no circumstances may cash or cash convertible gifts be offered or accepted. Any such gift offered in the course of, or in connection with, a current or prospective business relationship must be declined, and reported immediately to the Compliance Officer.

sections.title: Gifts and benefits register
sections.content: The Compliance Officer will maintain a register of all gifts and benefits received.

sections.title: Quarterly self-certifications
sections.content:
At every quarter end, a signed self-certification should be provided to the Compliance Officer. If you have not received or been offered any gifts or inducements, you must confirm this “nil return” in writing by completing and returning a signed self-certification form to the Compliance Officer. A copy of the Staff Self- Certification Form is available from the Compliance Officer.

The combined Quarterly Attestation Form can be found separately and on request from the Compliance Officer.

A key test is to consider whether the gift etc., if made known to the investors in the fund, might cause concern about the objectivity of decisions etc. taken on their behalf. In making a decision, thought must be given to the frequency of such gifts etc. which, in isolation may be of low value but collectively give cause for concern.

sections.title: Useful Links
sections.content:
COBS 2.3 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/2/3.html

COBS 2.3A https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/2/3A.html

COBS 18 Annex 1 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/18/Annex1.html

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – INDUCEMENTS: RESEARCH
chapters.summary: 9. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – INDUCEMENTS: RESEARCH
chapters.content:
9. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – INDUCEMENTS: RESEARCH

## 9.1 Introduction

In addition to the rules on inducements (see separate chapter ‘Conduct of Business - Inducements (excluding Research)’), the Handbook specifically addresses the provision of research by third parties and effectively prohibits the receipt of research by firms executing orders, or placing orders with other entities for execution, that relate to financial instruments for, or on behalf of, the fund unless:  it is paid for by the firm out of its own resources; or  it is paid for out of a research payment account (‘RPA’) funded by a specific charge to the fund. Compliance with the above with mean that the provision of, and the acceptance of, research will not be regarded as an inducement. Although these rules on research and inducements are derived from MiFID, the FCA has decided to apply similar rules to small authorised full-scope UK AIFMs (COBS 2.3B but as modified by COBS 18 Annex 1). As such, rules (albeit not identical rules) on research will apply to both the firm’s MiFID business and AIFM business.

## 9.2 What is ‘research’?

For this purpose, ‘research’ is defined in Delegated Directive 2017/593 (see ‘Useful Links’) as: “covering research material or services concerning one or several financial instruments or other assets, or the issuers or potential issuers of financial instruments, or be closely related to a specific industry or market such that it informs views on financial instruments, assets or issuers within that sector. That type of material or services explicitly or implicitly recommends or suggests an investment strategy and provides a substantiated opinion as to the present or future value or price of such instruments or assets, or otherwise contains analysis and original insights and reach conclusions based on new or existing information that could be used to inform an investment strategy and be relevant and capable of adding value to the investment firm's decisions on behalf of clients being charged for that research” (for the purposes of this chapter “clients” can include “funds”). The FCA does not provide a list of what may constitute ‘research’ and advises that firms will have to exercise their own judgement. The FCA directs firms to Chapter 7 of ESMA Q&As, and in particular Q&As 6 to 9. (see ‘Useful Links’).

## 9.3 What is not ‘research’?

Although it is not a definitive list, COBS 2.3B.23 provides examples of what the FCA does not regard as ‘research’ e.g. corporate access services and hence cannot be paid from a RPA. In its Policy Statement PS17/14 the FCA expressed the view that certain activities can be considered as inherent to the provision of ‘execution services’ (rather than a separate ‘benefit’ that would be subject to the inducement rules) and would include those activities essential to facilitating an order and that take place between the point at which an order is received and executed by a firm and the final settlement. Examples provided include:  Transaction reporting offered by a broker as part of the execution service, provided that it does not influence best execution and is offered as a standard term of business by the broker  Working large orders  Taking trades on risk  Structuring derivative trades Note that COBS 2.3A classifies certain items that might ordinarily be thought of as ‘research’ as instead being ‘acceptable minor non-monetary benefits’ i.e. cannot be paid from an RPA and are not subject to the rules on research. These items include, but are not limited to, research on companies with a market capitalisation below £200M and third party research where it relates to fixed income, currency or commodity instruments – see COBS 2.3A.19 for further details.

## 9.4 Operation of a RPA: Budget

Where the RPA route is chosen, the operation of the ‘Account’ depends upon whether it relates to our MiFID business or our management of a fund.

Alamut and its senior management remain responsible at all times for adherence to regulatory requirements in the operation of an RPA.

## 9.5 RPA: MiFID business

Where the RPA (which is subject to the firm’s written policy) route is chosen then:  before the provision of any service, the client must be informed about the budgeted amount and their estimated amount of the research charge;  provide annual information on the total costs that each client has incurred for third party research (and inform clients that they are entitled to further information – see below re “on request”);  agree with the client (via general terms of business or the IMA) the budgeted research and the frequency at which it will be deducted Once a research budget is set, research charges can be collected either via the ‘accounting method’ (whereby regular amounts are taken from a client’s account to fund their share of the RPA) or the ‘transactional method’ (whereby a charge for research is made alongside the execution commission). With regard to the latter, it is the view of the FCA that this does not allow brokers providing research to retain research charges alongside a transaction commission paid by that firm’s clients. The charge must go to the RPA and can then be paid out to the relevant broker The total amount of research charges collected from clients must not exceed the established research budget (and as far as is relevant, the budget must not be used to fund any research generated internally). Provided that the client charge is only collected when due and payable then the monies will not be regarded as client money (although a firm that collects these charges by deducting them from those clients’ resources will need to comply with CASS 8 (“Mandates”)). Any surplus in the RPA at the end of a period must either be rebated to the relevant clients or used as an offset against the budget for the next period. No increase in the research budget is allowed unless the client receives clear information on such a proposed increase “in good time” before such increases are intended to take place. The firm is required to disclose to a client “on request” a summary of:  the providers paid from this account;  the total amount they were paid over a defined period;  the benefits and services received by the firm; and  how the total amount spent from the account compares to the budget set by the firm for that period, noting any rebate or carry-over if residual funds remain in the account. Alamut and its senior management remain responsible at all times for adherence to regulatory requirements in the operation of the RPA COBS 2.3B provides further information on the requirements relating to a RPA, including the need to regularly assess the research budget and the quality of research received, and oversight on an RPA.

## 9.6 RPA: Management of the fund

Where the RPA route is chosen then Alamut must agree with the fund (but only where the fund has its ‘own governing body which is independent of the firm’ – see COBS 18 Annex 1(4.5) for guidance on this term), via general terms of business or the IMA, the budgeted research and the frequency at which it will be deducted from the fund’s resources over the year. Once a research budget is set, research charges can be collected either via the ‘accounting method’ (whereby regular amounts are taken from the fund’s account to fund their share of the RPA) or the ‘transactional method’ (whereby a charge for research is made alongside the execution commission. With regard to the latter, it is the view of the FCA that this does not allow brokers providing research to retain research charges alongside a transaction commission paid by that firm’s clients (i.e. the fund). The charge must go to the RPA and can then be paid out to the relevant broker The total amount of research charges collected from the fund must not exceed the established research budget (and as far as is relevant, the budget must not be used to fund any research generated internally). Provided that the fund charge is only collected when due and payable then the monies will not be regarded as client money (although a firm that collects these charges by deducting them from the fund’s resources will need to consider whether there is a need to

comply with CASS 8 (“Mandates”)). Any surplus in the RPA at the end of a period must either be rebated to the relevant fund or used as an offset against the budget for the next period. Alamut and its senior management remain responsible at all times for adherence to regulatory requirements in the operation of the RPA. COBS 2.3B, as modified by COBS 18 Annex 1, provides further information on the requirements relating to a RPA, including the need to regularly assess the research budget and the quality of research received, and oversight of an RPA.

Disclosure of RPA budget Alamut is required to publish:  the budgeted amount for research; and  the amount of the estimated research charge for each fund An increase in the budget is only permitted where a firm “has provided clear information about the increase in good time before it is to take effect”. The above information, including any budget increase, must be made available to both prospective investors and to existing investors.

Disclosure to investors For each fund managed, information must be provided to investors on the total costs the fund has incurred for third-party research in the most recent annual accounting period. The FCA suggests (“may wish to”) that a full-scope AIFM of an unauthorised AIF (for an authorised AIF the information must be published “in the annual long report of the authorised fund”) publishes this information in the annual report of the AIF. The FCA does not prescribe the method of publishing this information for small AIFMs. However a fund’s annual report would seem the most appropriate place. In addition, the following information must be made available in summary form “on request” for the most recent annual accounting period:  the providers paid from the account;  the total amount each provider was paid;  the benefits and services received by the firm; and  how the total amount spent from the account compares to the budget set by the firm, noting any rebate or carry-over if residual monies are held in the account

RPA Statement Although there is no requirement for a Policy in respect of managing a fund (i.e. the non-MiFID business), Alamut is required to set out in writing:  how the firm will comply with need to regularly assess the quality of research purchased, based upon robust criteria, and its ability to contribute to better investment decisions;  how research purchased through the research payment account may benefit the fund, taking into account its investment objective, policy and strategy;  the approach the firm will take to allocate the costs of research fairly among the funds it manages;  the manner in which, and the frequency at which, the research charge will be deducted from the assets of the fund; and  a statement as to where up-to-date information on where the details relating to the RPA budget and charges as required under the previous section above (‘Disclosure of RPA budget’) can be obtained The FCA suggests (“may wish to”) that a full-scope AIFM of an unauthorised AIF (in contrast, for an authorised AIF “the information must be published in the fund’s prospectus”) makes this information available as part of the prior disclosure obligation (“a description of all fees … etc.” - FUND 3.2.2(9)).

## 9.7 Alamut’s approach

Alamut has adopted the RPA approach and operates the RPA using the accounting method/transactional method. Alamut does not operate a RPA and instead funds all research from its own resources.

## 9.8 Firms providing execution services and research

Firms that provide execution services must separately identify the charges for execution (and which must only reflect the cost of execution) and the charges for any research, benefit or service. The latter charges must not be linked to the levels of payment for execution services. The requirement for separate pricing to be provided to recipient firms applies regardless of the activities the latter are carrying out.

## 9.9 Useful Links

Delegated Directive 2017/593 http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017L0593&from=EN and in particular Article 13 Research Article 12(2) & (3) Acceptable minor non-monetary benefits Recital 28 Definition of ‘research’ for purposes of RPA model Article 11 Inducements (see also MiFID II Article 24(9))

ESMA Q&As on Investment Research (section 7) https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/esma35-43- 349_mifid_ii_qas_on_investor_protection_topics.pdf

COBS 2.3B https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/2/3B.html

COBS 18 Annex 1 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/18/Annex1.html

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - CLIENT CATEGORISATION
chapters.summary: 10. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - CLIENT CATEGORISATION
chapters.content:
10. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - CLIENT CATEGORISATION

## 10.1 Introduction

COBS 3.3.1A requires the Firm to notify new clients of their categorisation as a Retail Client, Professional Client or an Eligible Counterparty. Such notification must be made prior to the provision of any services. The Firm must also advise a client (in a ‘durable medium’) of their right to request a different categorisation and about any limitations to the level of client categorisation that a different categorisation would entail. Categorisation, taking into account a client’s knowledge and experience, is considered below.

The Firm is authorised by the FCA to undertake Regulated Activities with or for Professional Clients and Eligible Counterparties only. It is therefore important that the Firm establishes the correct categorisation for a client before it acts for them.

The Compliance Officer maintains the client categorisation records for a period of at least five years after ceasing to carry on business with or for that client (COBS 3.8.2R).

## 10.2 Retail Client

COBS 3.4.1R states that a Retail Client is a client who is not a Professional Client or an Eligible Counterparty. The Firm is not permitted to deal with or for Retail Clients.

A local public authority or municipality which (in either case) does not manage public debt must be categorised as a retail client unless the body can be treated as an elective professional client (see below). Note that the criteria for treating a client as an elective professional client is different for such bodies.

## 10.3 Professional Client

COBS 3.5.1R states that a Professional Client is a client who is a Per se Professional Client or an Elective Professional Client.

Per se Professional Client As per COBS 3.5.2R a per se Professional Client is any of the following:

 Credit institution;  Investment firm;  Other authorised or regulated financial institution;  Insurance company;  Collective investment scheme or the management company of such a scheme;  Pension fund or the management company of a pension fund;  Commodity or commodity derivatives dealer;  A local authority; or  Any other institutional investor.

In relation to MiFID Business or Equivalent Third Country Business, a large undertaking meeting two of the following three size requirements:

 Balance sheet total of €20m;  Net turnover of €40m; and  Own funds of €2m.

In addition to the above, MiFID II also classifies the following as Per se Professional Clients:

 A national or regional government, including a public body that manages public debt at national or regional level, a central bank, an international or supranational institution (such as the World Bank, the IMF, the ECB, the EIB) or another similar international organisation; and  Another institutional investor whose main activity is to invest in financial instruments (in relation to the firm's MiFID or equivalent third country business) or designated investments (in relation to the firm's

other business). This includes entities dedicated to the securitisation of assets or other financing transactions.

Elective Professional Client (excluding local public authorities or municipalities) Where a client does not fall within the definition of a per se Professional Client the Firm may treat them as an Elective Professional Client if they have agreed to be classified as such and who complies with COBS 3.5.3R (1) (the "qualitative test"), (2) (the "quantitative test" and (3) (which concerns an exchange of letters between the client and the Firm), or, in the event of undertaking non-MiFID Business for that client, COBS 3.5.3 R (1) and (3).

Note that, although, as per COBS 4.12.4 (Note 4), only a qualitative test is required for recipients of financial promotions relating to non-mainstream pooled investments (see chapter ‘Conduct of Business – communicating with clients, including financial promotions’), those recipients will still be ‘retail’ if the Firm subsequently undertakes any regulated activities for such recipients e.g. ‘arranging’ unless the quantitative test is also applied.

Essentially, where the Firm wishes to categorise a client as an Elective Professional Client (and the client agrees to be so categorised), the Firm is obliged to:

 Undertake an adequate assessment to ensure that the client has sufficient expertise, knowledge and experience with regard to the financial instruments and/or service the Firm proposes to provide and that the client is, therefore, capable of understanding the impact of the Firm’s decisions and the inherent risks involved (the “qualitative test”);  Obtain in writing that the client wishes to be classified as a Professional Client either generally or in respect of the services and/or financial instruments contemplated;  Provide a clear written warning to the client regarding the protections and investor compensation rights they should expect to lose, but that would otherwise be afforded to them as a Retail Client, and that the client has acknowledged and accepted this in writing in a separate document; and  Is able to meet, in the event of undertaking MiFID Business, at least two of the three following:

Quantitative Tests:

 The client has carried out transactions, in significant size, on the relevant market at an average frequency of ten per quarter over the previous four quarters;  The size of the client’s financial instrument portfolio, defined as including cash deposits and financial instruments, exceeds €500,000;  The client works, or has worked in, the financial sector for at least one year in a professional position which requires knowledge of the transactions or services envisaged.

Elective Professional Client (local public authorities or municipalities) The obligations above also apply to a UK local public authority or municipality save for the need to comply with an enhanced “quantitative test”:

Quantitative Tests:

 The size of the client’s financial instrument portfolio, defined as including cash deposits and financial instruments, exceeds £10m;

In addition at least one of the following criteria must be met:

 The client has carried out transactions, in significant size, on the relevant market at an average frequency of ten per quarter over the previous four quarters; or

 The person authorised to carry out transactions on behalf of the client works, or has worked in, the financial sector for at least one year in a professional position which requires knowledge of the transactions or services envisaged; or  The client is an ‘administering authority’ of the Local Government Pension Scheme within the meaning of the version of Schedule 3 of The Local Government Pension Scheme Regulations 2013 or, (in relation to Scotland) within the meaning of the version of Schedule 3 of The Local Government Pension Scheme (Scotland) Regulations 2014 in force as at 1 January 2018, and is acting in that capacity.

The “quantitative test” must always be used when assessing whether a local public authority or municipality can be treated as an elective profession regardless of whether the Firm intends to conduct MiFID or equivalent third country business or other regulated activities.

The two tests must be applied separately and independently in relation to the client’s:

 Business in the course of or connected to its administration of a pension scheme; and  Other business as a local public authority or municipality.

Where the client is a non-UK local public authority or municipality a revised “quantitative test” is applied:

 Where the local public authority or municipality is established in an EEA State and the EEA State has adopted alternative or additional criteria to those listed in the fifth paragraph to section II.1 of Annex II to MiFID, those criteria as set out in the law or measures of that EEA State; or  In any other case the same “quantitative test” that is applied to MiFID business excluding local public authorities or municipalities.

In all cases, where the client is an entity the “qualitative test” should be performed in relation to the person authorised to carry out transactions on its behalf.

## 10.4 Eligible Counterparty

As stated in COBS 3.6.1R, an Eligible Counterparty Client is either a per se Eligible Counterparty or an Elective Eligible Counterparty. The Firm may only undertake “Eligible Counterparty Business” (dealing on own account, execution of orders on behalf of clients, reception and transmission of orders or ancillary services as per MiFID Annex I B directly related to these services or arranging in relation to any non-MiFID business) for an Eligible Counterparty and may not, therefore, undertake investment advice or investment management for such a client.

Per se Eligible Counterparty As stated in COBS 3.6.2R, a per se Eligible Counterparty is an Eligible Counterparty that the Firm has chosen to categorise as such and does not provide investment advice or investment management and is an entity as defined in COBS 3.6.2R (1) to (10).

Elective Eligible Counterparty An Elective Eligible Counterparty is an Eligible Counterparty that the Firm has chosen to categorise as an Elective Eligible Counterparty and is an entity that is:

 A per se Professional Client (but not if the client is only categorised as such because it is an “other institutional investor”; and  Is a body corporate (including a LLP) which has (or any of whose holding companies or subsidiaries has) called up share capital of at least £10m (or equivalent other currency).

Confirmation that they wish to be categorised as such must be obtained from the client and they need to be provided with a clear warning of the consequences of such a course of action, including any protections they may lose.

Note that an Elective Professional Client cannot be treated as an Elective Eligible Counterparty.

## 10.5 Providing Clients with a Higher Level of Protection

COBS 3.7.1R gives a client the right to be treated in such a way that higher levels of protection are afforded to it. A client that the Firm has categorised as a Professional Client may request to be classified as a Retail Client and similarly in the case of a client categorised as an Eligible Counterparty may request to be classified as a Retail Client or Professional Client. However, if a client requests to be treated as a Retail Client, the Firm would be unable to undertake business with that client as it does not have ‘retail’ as a customer type on its Part 4A.

## 10.6 Policies, Procedures and Records

COBS 3.8.1R requires the Firm to implement appropriate written internal policies and procedures to categorise its clients.

COBS 3.8.2R requires the Firm to keep records of the client’s category, evidence of the despatch of notice of categorisation sent to the client and a copy of the agreement entered into by the client. These records must be kept by the Firm for a minimum of five years after the client ceases to be a client of the Firm (COBS 3.8.2R (3)(c)). As noted earlier, the Compliance Officer will retain all client records for a minimum of five years.

## 10.7 SEC client classification requirements

The SEC has not established any equivalent client classification rules under the Advisers Act. However, the pooled investment vehicles (or funds) that an investment adviser typically manages will meet the definition of an “investment company” under the Company Act. A fund that does not qualify for an exclusion from the definition of an investment company may not offer or sell a security in the US without registering under Section 8 of the Company Act. A registered investment company is subject to technical, complex and extensive substantive regulation of its activities including its ability to employ leverage and sell securities short. Therefore, an adviser will seek to qualify a fund that it manages for an exclusion from the definition of investment company under Section 3(c)(1) or Section 3(c)(7) of the Company Act. This would require the investment adviser to make a determination of the status of the prospective investors in a fund that it manages, in order to determine if the fund qualifies for one of the exclusions.

10.8 Fund Exclusion from the definition of investment company under Section 3(c)(1) and Section 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act

Section 3(c)(1)

Section 3(c)(1) of the Company Act excludes a pooled investment vehicle from the definition of an investment company if it is not making a public offering and there are no more than 100 beneficial owners of its outstanding securities (a “Section 3(c)(1) fund”). Investors in a Section 3(c)(1) fund are typically “Accredited Investors”, as defined in the Securities Act generally as follows:

 A natural person with an individual net worth, or joint net worth with his or her spouse, at the time of purchase in excess of $1 million excluding the primary residence of such person; or  A natural person with an individual income in excess of $200,000, or in excess of $300,000 with his or her spouse, in each of the two most recent years and who has a reasonable expectation of an income in excess of $200,000 individually, or in excess of $300,000 with his or her spouse, in the current year; or  Any executive officer, director or general partner of the issuer of the securities offered.

Section 3(c)(7)

Section 3(c)(7) of the Company Act excludes any pooled investment vehicle from the definition of an investment company if it does not make any public offering and its outstanding securities are held

exclusively by “Qualified Purchasers” (a “Section 3(c)(7) fund”). The Company Act defines a Qualified Purchaser generally as follows:  Any natural person who owns not less than $5 million in investments (as defined);  or any other person (e.g. an institutional investor) that owns and invests on a discretionary basis not less than $25 million in investments.

## 10.9 Useful Links

COBS 3 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/3/1.html

MiFID Delegated Regulation 2017/565 http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0565&from=EN

and in particular

Information concerning client categorisation including need to newly categorise existing clients Article 45
