chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – SUITABILITY
chapters.summary: 11. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – SUITABILITY
chapters.content:
11. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – SUITABILITY

## 11.1 Introduction

In respect of its MiFID business, COBS 9A.2 requires the Firm, where it provides Personal Recommendations or Portfolio Management, to ensure the advice provided or decisions made are suitable for the client’s circumstances, investment objectives and attitude to investment risk.

The FCA Handbook provides the following guidance on ‘assessing suitability’:

i. A transaction may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the associated financial instruments, the type of transaction, the characteristics of the order or the frequency of the trading. ii. A series of transactions, each of which are suitable when viewed in isolation may be unsuitable if the recommendation or the decisions to trade are made with a frequency that is not in the best interests of the client. iii. In the case of portfolio management, a transaction might be unsuitable if it would result in an unsuitable portfolio.

When providing Personal Recommendations or Portfolio Management involving switching investments for a retail client, an analysis of the costs and benefits must be undertaken so that it can be reasonably demonstrated that the benefits of switching are greater than the costs. In addition, adequate policies and procedures are required to be in place to ensure that the Firm can evidence an understanding of the nature, features (including costs and risks) of the investment services and financial instruments selected for clients and that assessment can be made on whether equivalent investment services or financial instruments can meet the client’s profile.

## 11.2 Requirements

The Firm is authorised to manage investments for and provide advice to Professional Clients. It should be noted that investment advice and investment management is not Eligible Counterparty Business.

Sufficient information must be gathered from clients to ensure that advice provided or a decision to trade:

i. Meets the client’s investment objectives, including tolerance to risk; ii. Is such that the client is able to financially bear any related investment risks consistent with then above objectives; and iii. The client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks involved.

Further guidance on obtaining the above information can be found in COBS 9A.2.6 - 2.8.

If the above information is not obtained then the Firm is not permitted to recommend any services of financial instruments to the client.

Where the client is categorised as a professional client then the Firm is entitled to assume that the client has the necessary experience and knowledge in iii) above.

Where the client is a per se professional client then in terms of investment advice the Firm is entitled to assume that the client is able financially to bear any related investment risks in ii) above.

## 11.3 Reliability of Information

Reasonable steps should be taken that the information so collected, and maintained, is reliable. Such steps include (but not limited to):

a. ensuring clients are aware of the importance of providing accurate and up-to-date information; b. ensuring all tools, such as risk assessment profiling tools or tools to assess a client’s knowledge and experience, employed in the suitability assessment process are fit-for-purpose and are appropriately designed for use with their clients, with any limitations identified and actively mitigated through the suitability assessment process; c. ensuring questions used in the process are likely to be understood by clients, capture an accurate reflection of the client’s objectives and needs, and the information necessary to undertake the suitability assessment; and d. taking steps, as appropriate, to ensure the consistency of client information, such as by considering whether there are obvious inaccuracies in the information provided by clients.

## 11.4 Subject of Suitability Assessment

Where a client is:

 A legal person; or  A group of two or more natural persons; or  Where one or more natural persons are represented by another natural person

Then reference should be made to the Firm’s separate policy that has been established as to who should be subject to the suitability assessment and how it will be done.

Where a client is:

 A natural person represented by another natural person; or  A legal person requesting to be treated as an Elective Professional Client

Then the assessment of:

 The financial situation and investment objectives shall be those of the natural person who will be the client (and not that of their representative) or of the legal person as appropriate; but  The knowledge and experience shall be that of the natural person’s representative or that of the person authorised to carry out transactions on behalf of the underlying client.

## 11.5 Periodic Assessments

Before providing investment advice a client must be informed “in good time” whether the Firm will provide them with a periodic assessment of the suitability of the recommended financial instruments.

When a periodic assessment is to be provided then the following must be disclosed to the client:

 the frequency and extent of the periodic suitability assessment and where relevant, the conditions that trigger that assessment;  the extent to which the information previously collected will be subject to reassessment; and  the way in which an updated recommendation will be communicated to the client.

Any such periodic assessment will be undertaken on a risk basis but will be at least annually.

## 11.6 Suitability Reports and Periodic Reports

When providing investment advice to a retail client a suitability report must be provided specifying the advice given and how that advice meets the preferences, objectives and other characteristics of the client. The report must be provided before the transaction is concluded. There is an exception which permits the report to be provided post the transaction if distance communications prevents the prior delivery of the suitability report. However the client will need to consent to this and needs to be given the option of delaying the transaction – see COBS 9A.3.2(3). See the below paragraph for when we commit to carrying out a period assessment of suitability.

## 11.7 Retail Clients

When providing portfolio management to a retail client then the periodic report (see the ‘Conduct of Business – Reporting to clients’ chapter in this Compliance Manual) must contain an updated statement of how the client’s investments meet the preferences, objectives and other characteristics of the client. Note

that this requirement also applies when a client is informed that we will carry out a periodic assessment of suitability (see also COBS 9A.3.3).

It should be borne in mind that the Handbook places restrictions on the ‘retail distribution’ of certain investments (and ‘retail distribution’ can include distribution to e.g. HNWs etc.):

 Restricted mass market investments (“RMMI”) as per COBS 4.12A and the restriction on promotion – see also the ‘Conduct of Business – Communicating with clients, including financial promotions’ chapter in this Compliance Manual;  Non-mass market investments (“NMMI”) as per COBS 4.12B and the restriction on promotion – see also above mentioned chapter in this Compliance Manual;  Contingent convertible instruments and CoCo funds as per COBS 22.3 and the restriction on sales and promotions;  Mutual society shares as per COBS 22.2 and the requirements in relation to dealing and arranging activities; and  Esoteric investments such as binaries, CFDs and cryptoasset derivatives (see COBS 22.4 – COBS 22.6)

In terms of discretionary portfolio management for a retail client, COBS 9A.2.22 requires firms to “exercise particular care” to ensure that a transaction involving one of the above investments is suitable and in the client’s best interests having regard to the FCA’s view that “such investments pose particular risks of inappropriate distribution”. Therefore any decision to purchase such an investment should be supported by “detailed and robust” justification.

In terms of advice, COBS 9A.2.22(2) states that a personal recommendation “will generally incorporate a financial promotion” and therefore a firm should be satisfied that an exemption is available before recommending such an investment.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
In respect of its MiFID business, COBS 9A.2 requires the Firm, where it provides Personal Recommendations or Portfolio Management, to ensure the advice provided or decisions made are suitable for the client’s circumstances, investment objectives and attitude to investment risk.

The FCA Handbook provides the following guidance on ‘assessing suitability’:

i. A transaction may be unsuitable for a client due to the risks of the associated financial instruments, the type of transaction, the characteristics of the order or the frequency of the trading. ii. A series of transactions, each of which are suitable when viewed in isolation may be unsuitable if the recommendation or the decisions to trade are made with a frequency that is not in the best interests of the client. iii. In the case of portfolio management, a transaction might be unsuitable if it would result in an unsuitable portfolio.

When providing Personal Recommendations or Portfolio Management involving switching investments for a retail client, an analysis of the costs and benefits must be undertaken so that it can be reasonably demonstrated that the benefits of switching are greater than the costs. In addition, adequate policies and procedures are required to be in place to ensure that the Firm can evidence an understanding of the nature, features (including costs and risks) of the investment services and financial instruments selected for clients and that assessment can be made on whether equivalent investment services or financial instruments can meet the client’s profile.

sections.title: Requirements
sections.content:
The Firm is authorised to manage investments for and provide advice to Professional Clients. It should be noted that investment advice and investment management is not Eligible Counterparty Business.

Sufficient information must be gathered from clients to ensure that advice provided or a decision to trade:

i. Meets the client’s investment objectives, including tolerance to risk; ii. Is such that the client is able to financially bear any related investment risks consistent with then above objectives; and iii. The client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks involved.

Further guidance on obtaining the above information can be found in COBS 9A.2.6 - 2.8.

If the above information is not obtained then the Firm is not permitted to recommend any services of financial instruments to the client.

Where the client is categorised as a professional client then the Firm is entitled to assume that the client has the necessary experience and knowledge in iii) above.

Where the client is a per se professional client then in terms of investment advice the Firm is entitled to assume that the client is able financially to bear any related investment risks in ii) above.

sections.title: Reliability of Information
sections.content:
Reasonable steps should be taken that the information so collected, and maintained, is reliable. Such steps include (but not limited to):

a. ensuring clients are aware of the importance of providing accurate and up-to-date information; b. ensuring all tools, such as risk assessment profiling tools or tools to assess a client’s knowledge and experience, employed in the suitability assessment process are fit-for-purpose and are appropriately designed for use with their clients, with any limitations identified and actively mitigated through the suitability assessment process; c. ensuring questions used in the process are likely to be understood by clients, capture an accurate reflection of the client’s objectives and needs, and the information necessary to undertake the suitability assessment; and d. taking steps, as appropriate, to ensure the consistency of client information, such as by considering whether there are obvious inaccuracies in the information provided by clients.

sections.title: Subject of Suitability Assessment
sections.content:
Where a client is:

 A legal person; or  A group of two or more natural persons; or  Where one or more natural persons are represented by another natural person

Then reference should be made to the Firm’s separate policy that has been established as to who should be subject to the suitability assessment and how it will be done.

Where a client is:

 A natural person represented by another natural person; or  A legal person requesting to be treated as an Elective Professional Client

Then the assessment of:

 The financial situation and investment objectives shall be those of the natural person who will be the client (and not that of their representative) or of the legal person as appropriate; but  The knowledge and experience shall be that of the natural person’s representative or that of the person authorised to carry out transactions on behalf of the underlying client.

sections.title: Periodic Assessments
sections.content:
Before providing investment advice a client must be informed “in good time” whether the Firm will provide them with a periodic assessment of the suitability of the recommended financial instruments.

When a periodic assessment is to be provided then the following must be disclosed to the client:

 the frequency and extent of the periodic suitability assessment and where relevant, the conditions that trigger that assessment;  the extent to which the information previously collected will be subject to reassessment; and  the way in which an updated recommendation will be communicated to the client.

Any such periodic assessment will be undertaken on a risk basis but will be at least annually.

sections.title: Suitability Reports and Periodic Reports
sections.content: When providing investment advice to a retail client a suitability report must be provided specifying the advice given and how that advice meets the preferences, objectives and other characteristics of the client. The report must be provided before the transaction is concluded. There is an exception which permits the report to be provided post the transaction if distance communications prevents the prior delivery of the suitability report. However the client will need to consent to this and needs to be given the option of delaying the transaction – see COBS 9A.3.2(3). See the below paragraph for when we commit to carrying out a period assessment of suitability.

sections.title: Retail Clients
sections.content:
When providing portfolio management to a retail client then the periodic report (see the ‘Conduct of Business – Reporting to clients’ chapter in this Compliance Manual) must contain an updated statement of how the client’s investments meet the preferences, objectives and other characteristics of the client. Note

that this requirement also applies when a client is informed that we will carry out a periodic assessment of suitability (see also COBS 9A.3.3).

It should be borne in mind that the Handbook places restrictions on the ‘retail distribution’ of certain investments (and ‘retail distribution’ can include distribution to e.g. HNWs etc.):

 Restricted mass market investments (“RMMI”) as per COBS 4.12A and the restriction on promotion – see also the ‘Conduct of Business – Communicating with clients, including financial promotions’ chapter in this Compliance Manual;  Non-mass market investments (“NMMI”) as per COBS 4.12B and the restriction on promotion – see also above mentioned chapter in this Compliance Manual;  Contingent convertible instruments and CoCo funds as per COBS 22.3 and the restriction on sales and promotions;  Mutual society shares as per COBS 22.2 and the requirements in relation to dealing and arranging activities; and  Esoteric investments such as binaries, CFDs and cryptoasset derivatives (see COBS 22.4 – COBS 22.6)

In terms of discretionary portfolio management for a retail client, COBS 9A.2.22 requires firms to “exercise particular care” to ensure that a transaction involving one of the above investments is suitable and in the client’s best interests having regard to the FCA’s view that “such investments pose particular risks of inappropriate distribution”. Therefore any decision to purchase such an investment should be supported by “detailed and robust” justification.

In terms of advice, COBS 9A.2.22(2) states that a personal recommendation “will generally incorporate a financial promotion” and therefore a firm should be satisfied that an exemption is available before recommending such an investment.

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – APPROPRIATENESS
chapters.summary: 12. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – APPROPRIATENESS
chapters.content:
12. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – APPROPRIATENESS

## 12.1 General

The concept of ‘appropriateness’ needs to be considered in certain circumstances when:

- firm is providing investment services in the course of MiFID business other than

– making a personal recommendation and – managing investments (for which reference should be made to the ‘Suitability’ chapter); or

- when a firm arranges or deals in certain instruments for retail clients other than in the course of MiFID

business.

## 12.2 Application

The appropriateness test applies when a firm is performing MiFID business (COBS 10.A) and provides any of the following services for either a retail client or a professional client:

 reception and transmission of orders (“RTO”);  execution of orders on behalf of clients;  underwriting; and  placing.

The test also applies in respect of non-MiFID business when a firm arranges or deals in relation to non- readily realisable securities, speculative illiquid securities, derivatives or warrants with or for a retail client and the firm is aware, or ought reasonably to be aware, that the application or order is in response to a direct offer financial promotion. As Alamut does not conduct business with retail clients this will not be relevant for us.

## 12.3 Requirement

When any of the above services are provided, Alamut needs to ask the client to provide sufficient information regarding their relevant knowledge and experience to allow us to assess whether the service or product is appropriate for the client.

When assessing appropriateness, Alamut must determine whether the client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks involved. However, it can be assumed that a professional client has the necessary experience and knowledge of the service/product for which the client has been classified as professional client. Further details can be found in COBS 10A.2.3.

A suitable warning to the client must be given should a firm, on the basis of information received, consider that the service/product is not appropriate. If a client elects not to provide the necessary information then they must be warned that this will not allow the Firm to determine appropriateness. Should the client still insist on continuing with the transaction then it is for the Firm to decide whether to do so – see COBS 10A.3.3 (or COBS 10.3.3 for any non-MiFID business).

## 12.4 When not Required

There is no requirement to assess appropriateness when the service is provided at the initiative of the client and it relates to certain non-complex instruments such as:

 Shares trading on a regulated market or MTF (or equivalent third country market) – except those that embed a derivative;  Bonds or other forms of securitised debt trading on a regulated market or MTF (or equivalent third country market) – except those that embed a derivative or incorporate a structure which makes it difficult for the client to understand;  UCITS, excluding structured UCITS.

The above (which is not exhaustive and reference should be made to COBS 10A.4 in respect of MiFID business and to COBS 10.4 in respect of non-MiFID business) is only relevant for the services of execution

and/or reception and transmission of client orders where the Firm complies with its obligations in relation to conflicts of interest. COBS 10A.5 provides guidance on assessing ‘appropriateness’ and “at the initiative of the client” (and COBS 10.5 is respect of non-MiFID business).

The concept of ‘appropriateness’ is not relevant if the Firm has assessed ‘suitability’ under COBS (see previous Chapter on ‘suitability’) or if it relies on a recommendation made by another investment firm.

## 12.5 Records

Records relating to appropriate assessments must be maintained for at least five years and in accord with COBS 10A.7.

## 12.6 Useful Links

COBS 10A (MiFID business) https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/10A/1.html

COBS 10 (non-MiFID business) https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/10/1.html

Non-readily realisable security https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/glossary/G3363.html

Speculative illiquid security https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/glossary/G4619s.html

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – COMMUNICATING WITH CLIENTS, INCLUDING FINANCIAL PROMOTIONS
chapters.summary: 13. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – COMMUNICATING WITH CLIENTS, INCLUDING FINANCIAL PROMOTIONS
chapters.content:
13. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – COMMUNICATING WITH CLIENTS, INCLUDING FINANCIAL PROMOTIONS

## 13.1 Introduction

Section 21 of FSMA imposes a restriction on the communication of Financial Promotions by unauthorised persons. A person must not, in the course of business, communicate an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity (a Financial Promotion) unless:

 They are an Authorised Person; or  An Authorised Person approves the content of the Financial Promotion.

Strictly speaking, an authorised firm is not required to formally ‘approve’ its own Financial Promotions (although it is a requirement that an authorised firm must have procedures in place to ensure compliance with the FCA’s requirements set out in COBS 4.10) but by doing so will allow the material to be used by non-authorised persons without the latter breaching the above mentioned section 21 of FSMA. However this firm will internally approve all Financial Promotions – see ‘Approving and Signing-off Financial Promotions’ below.

The Compliance Officer is responsible for approving the Firm’s Financial Promotions.

There is no restriction on the media of communication to which the FCA requirements on Financial Promotion apply and they therefore include, for example, printed materials, personal visits or telephone calls and internet or other electronic media communications.

The rules in the Handbook on Communicating with Clients and Financial Promotions are contained in COBS 4.

## 13.2 Overview

COBS 4.2.1R requires the Firm to ensure any communications with existing and potential clients are “fair, clear and not misleading”.

The Firm should ensure that the Financial Promotion (which should be identifiable as such):

1. Clearly states whether a product or service places a client’s capital at risk; 2. Quotes a yield figure that gives a balanced impression of the short and long term prospects of the investment; 3. Where it promotes an investment or service whose charging structure is complex or in relation to which the Firm will receive more than one element of remuneration, this includes the information necessary to ensure that it is fair, clear and not misleading and contains sufficient information to take into account the needs of the recipients; 4. That names the FCA (and/or the PRA) as its regulator and, if it refers to matters not regulated by the FCA, makes clear that those matters are not regulated by the FCA (and/or the PRA); and 5. Where it offers packaged products or stakeholder products not produced by the Firm, gives a fair, clear and not misleading impression of the product provider or the manager of the underlying investments. 6. Does not use terms such as ‘guaranteed’, ‘secure’ etc. unless that term is capable of being “fair, clear and not misleading” and provides all necessary information to justify the term (this requirement also applies to communications as well as to financial promotions).

## 13.3 MiFID provisions

In addition to the general requirements relating to “fair, clear and not misleading”, MiFID (specifically Article 44 of Regulation 2017/565 – the “MiFID Org Regulation”) imposes the following requirements on information, including marketing communications, disseminated to both professional and retail clients:

(a) the information includes the name of the investment firm;

(b) the information is accurate and always gives a fair and prominent indication of any relevant risks when referencing any potential benefits of an investment service or financial instrument; (c) the information uses a font size in the indication of relevant risks that is at least equal to the predominant font size used throughout the information provided, as well as a layout ensuring such indication is prominent; (d) the information is sufficient for, and presented in a way that is likely to be understood by, the average member of the group to whom it is directed, or by whom it is likely to be received; (e) the information does not disguise, diminish or obscure important items, statements or warnings; (f) the information is consistently presented in the same language throughout all forms of information and marketing materials that are provided to each client, unless the client has accepted to receive information in more than one language; (g) the information is up-to-date and relevant to the means of communication used.

The MiFID Org Regulation imposes further requirements (replicated in COBS 4.5A.7 to COBS 4.5A.15) where the information in question:

 Includes comparative information Any such comparison must be meaningful and presented in a balanced way; information sources must be specified; and key facts and assumptions included.

 Makes a reference to tax It must state that the tax treatment depends upon individual circumstances and may be subject to change.

 Contains an indication of past performance or simulated past performance Past performance must not be the most prominent feature and must cover the preceding 5 years (or the whole period when the instrument/service has been offered or the financial index established where less than 5 years), or such longer period as the firm may decide, and based on complete 12-month periods.

Simulated performance must be based upon actual performance of instruments or indices that are the same, or substantially so, as the instrument concerned.

A prominent warning must appear that past performance is not a reliable indicator of future performance (and, where relevant, that the figures are based upon simulated past performance).

 Contains information on future performance The information must be supported by objective data and not based upon, or refer to, simulated past performance and accompanied by a prominent warning that such forecasts are not a reliable indicator of future performance.

Any information in marketing communications must be consistent with any information provided to clients in the course of carrying on investment business.

The above bullet points are summaries - COBS 4.5A provides further information on the MiFID provisions such as source of information etc. Should the financial promotion relate to FIIAs (‘funds investing in inherently illiquid assets’ - being essentially non-UCITS retail schemes with at least 50% in assets such as property or non-freely traded shares etc.) and be directed at retail clients (which would include e.g. High Net Worth individuals or Sophisticated investors) then a specific risk warning is required – see COBS 4.5A.17 for further details.

## 13.4 Communications to Retail Clients (non-MiFID business)

In addition to the general requirements relating to “fair, clear and not misleading”, the Handbook imposes requirements on information, including marketing communications (including approval of), disseminated to, or likely to be received by, retail clients (a term which would include e.g. High Net Worth individuals or Sophisticated investors). It must be ensured that such information:

i. includes the name of the firm and, where relevant (e.g. in the unlikely event that the firm does not have the required expertise in relation to the marketing communication as per COBS 4.10.9A(3)) the name of the firm approving that communication and the date the financial promotion was approved by that firm; ii. is accurate and always gives a fair and prominent indication of any relevant risks when referencing any potential benefits of relevant business or a relevant investment; iii. is sufficient for, and presented in a way that is likely to be understood by, the average member of the group to whom it is directed, or by whom it is likely to be received; iv. does not disguise, diminish or obscure important items, statements or warnings. v. uses a font size in the indication of relevant risks that is at least equal to the predominant font size used throughout the information provided, as well as a layout that ensures that such indication is prominent; vi. is consistently presented in the same language throughout all forms of information and marketing materials that are provided to each client, unless the client has agreed to receive information in more than one language; and vii. is up-to-date and relevant to the means of communication used.

Further obligations arise when the information in question:

 Includes comparative information (COBS 4.5.6)  Makes a reference to tax (COBS 4.5.7)  Is made via a website, mobile application or other digital medium (COBS 4.5.2A)  Authorised fund managers’ communications in relation to benchmarks (COBS 4.5.11A – COBS 4.5.15

Any information contained in a financial promotion must be consistent with any information provided to a retail client in the course of carrying on investment business.

Should the financial promotion relate to FIIAs (‘funds investing in inherently illiquid assets’ - being essentially non-UCITS retail schemes with at least 50% in assets such as property or non-freely traded shares etc.) and be directed at retail clients (which would include e.g. High Net Worth individuals or Sophisticated investors) then a specific risk warning is required – see COBS 4.5.16 for further details. The above is a summary - COBS 4.5 provides further information on these provisions.

## 13.5 Past, Simulated Past and Future Performance (non-MiFID business)

The Handbook contains prescriptive requirements in respect of both the approval and communication of a financial promotion where it is likely to be received by a retail client.

These requirements concern communications which include:

 Past performance  Simulated past performance  Future performance

Such requirements include, but are not limited to, the need for a warning that past performance (and a future forecast) is not a reliable indicator of future performance.

Reference should be made to COBS 4.6 for further information.

## 13.6 Role of the Compliance Officer

Compliance Officer Sign-off and Approval Under s21 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 there is a general prohibition on a person making a financial promotion unless:

 It is made by an ‘authorised person’ (such as ALAMUT); or

 If made by an unauthorised person then it is approved by an ‘authorised person’

It will therefore be appreciated that whilst ‘sign-off’ and ‘approval’ might be interchangeable in normal day-to-day conversation, in a strict regulatory sense the firm would only be ‘approving’ a financial promotion if so requested by, or is to be used by, a non-authorised firm (the ‘approval’ is effectively confirming that the material produced by the other firm meets the FCA Handbook requirements), whilst the firm would be simply signing-off its own financial promotions. For the record an Appointed Representative falls within the definition of an ‘unauthorised person’.

Should ALAMUT ever ‘approve’ a financial promotion then the Handbook requires the firm to e.g. monitor the continuing compliance of that promotion and seek quarterly attestations from the other firm – with the ultimate sanction of the firm withdrawing its approval of the financial promotion. Full details of the obligations arising under the ‘approval and confirmation’ process can be found in COBS 4.10.

Any references to ‘communicating a financial promotion’ in this chapter can also be read, where relevant, as ‘approving a financial promotion’.

All Promotional Material must be submitted to the Compliance Officer prior to its first use. The Compliance Officer must make a written record indicating that he has reviewed and signed-off the Promotional Material as being in accord with regulatory requirements, along with the date of sign-off.

Employees intending to submit articles for external publication, or to participate in speaking engagements, must obtain prior approval of the Compliance Officer, and submit any written materials for review by the Compliance Officer.

Actual Performance If the Promotional Material includes any specific numerical or statistical information about the past performance of any actual accounts (including rates of return), the Compliance Officer must review all actual performance data for the same time period of all reasonably comparable accounts in order to determine that the reference in the Promotional Material to actual performance is representative of actual performance for the same time period of all reasonably comparable accounts.

Ratings Where Promotional Material contains any ratings, the Compliance Officer will review the Promotional Material to check that the material explains the ratings, including the limitations of such ratings.

Reprints Where Promotional Material contains any reprints of articles, the Compliance Officer will review the reprints to check that they have been supplemented with appropriate disclosures and disclaimers.

Promotional Recordkeeping The Compliance Officer is responsible for reviewing and signing off/approving all Promotional Material and keeping the material (together with any supporting documentation) for five years from the date of last use. There are specific record keeping obligations when a financial promotion relates to either a ‘restricted mass market investment’ (RMMI) or to a ‘non-mass market investment’ (NMMI) (see below for further information for these types of investment) – see COBS 4.11.4 and 4.11.5.

Electronic Communication The content standards and supervisory review requirements apply equally to Promotional Material distributed by email or other electronic means.

## 13.7 Direct Offer Financial Promotions (MiFID business)

COBS 4.7 addresses Direct Offer Financial Promotions (DOFP). These are promotions that offer the client (whether retail or professional) the opportunity to enter into an agreement with a firm whereby that firm will offer services or financial instruments to the client, and which specifies the manner of response or

includes a form to make the response. Conversely, a communication that does not contain details of how to apply or to make an offer, or an application form will not be a DOFP. The following must be included in any direct offer marketing communication as is relevant to the offer:

 Information about the Firm and its services for clients and potential clients;  Information about financial instruments, including general nature and the risks involved;  Information concerning safeguarding of client financial instruments and funds;  Information on costs and associated charges.

Please see COBS 4.7.-1A and Articles 47 to 50 of Delegated Regulation 2017/565 (see ‘Useful Links’) – which are reproduced in COBS 6.1ZA and COBS 14.3A - for further information on what has to be included in the above.

Consideration must also be given to the need for an ‘appropriateness assessment’ before sending out any direct offer financial promotion – see COBS 10A and the chapter on ‘Appropriateness’ in this manual.

See also below re direct offer financial promotions in respect of ‘Restricted mass market investments’ and of ‘Non-mass market investments’.

## 13.8 Direct Offer Financial Promotions (non-MiFID business)

COBS 4.7 addresses Direct Offer Financial Promotions (DOFP) which are addressed to, or likely to be received by, a retail client. These are promotions that offer the client to enter into an agreement with a firm whereby that firm will offer services or financial instruments to the client and which specifies the manner of response or includes a form to make the response. Conversely, a communication that does not contain details of how to apply or to make an offer, or an application form will not be a DOFP.

Various information must be included in any such direct offer marketing communication as is relevant to the offer such as:

 Information about the Firm and its services for clients and potential clients  Information about financial instruments, including general nature and the risks involved  Information concerning safeguarding of client financial instruments and funds

Please see COBS 4.7.1 (which refers the reader to other sections of the Handbook) for further details.

See also below re direct offer financial promotions in respect of ‘Restricted mass market investments’ and of ‘Non-mass market investments’.

## 13.9 Warrants and Derivatives

The Handbook imposes stricter requirements on the promotion of the above to retail clients. Before any direct offer financial promotion is made in respect of these types of instruments, consideration must be given to the need to undertake an ‘appropriateness assessment’ (refers to separate chapter on ‘Appropriateness’ in this manual). See COBS 4.7.6 for further information including restrictions on certain types of investments marketed to retail clients.

## 13.10 Non-written Financial Promotions

For the record only, this refers to ‘cold calling’ e.g. a financial promotion made by way of a telephone conversation which was not initiated by the prospective customer. These rules (COBS 4.8) do not apply to e.g. non-retail communications and as we do not undertake cold calling it will not be applicable to the Firm.

## 13.11 Promotion of ‘restricted mass market investments’ (RMMI)

A ‘RMMI’ is any of the following:

 a non-readily realisable security;  a P2P agreement;  a P2P portfolio.

The Handbook definition of a ‘non-readily realisable security’ is a security that is not a ‘readily realisable security’. For the purposes of the above, the definition of a ‘non-readily realisable security’ includes, but is not limited to, a security which is not e.g. a public or government security or a security listed on a UK exchange. Note that the definition (a) excludes an NMMI (see next section below) and (b) the definition of a ‘readily realisable security’ is different for the purposes of the below NMMI regime. See ‘Useful Links’ for the full Handbook definition of both a RMMI and a non-readily realisable security.

The financial promotion of any RMMI is subject to the requirement that it:

 includes a prescribed form of risk warning (COBS 4.12A.10 onwards)  does not include any form of incentive for retail clients (COBS 4.12A.8 provides some examples of what would be regarded as ‘incentives’)

On the other hand a direct offer financial promotion (‘DOFP’ - see ‘Direct offer financial promotions’ section above for what does, and does not, constitute a DOFP) requires the following conditions to be met (COBS 4.12A.15):

1. The need for a cooling off period (COBS 4.12A.18 subject to COBS 4.12A.15(2)) 2. A personalised risk warning (COBS 4.12A.20 subject to COBS 4.12A.15(2)) 3. Categorisation (e.g. ‘high net worth’ etc.) (COBS 4.12A.21) 4. Appropriateness (COBS 4.12A.28 subject to COBS 4.12A.15(3))

Condition 1 relates to the period before communicating the DOFP.

Conditions 2 and 3 above need to be satisfied before communicating the DOFP.

Condition 3 captures the following categorisations of retail clients:

 high net worth investor  certified sophisticated investor  self-certified sophisticated investor  restricted investor

See COBS 4 Annexes 2R to 5R for further details including draft ‘statements’ to be completed by such persons that meet the relevant requirements.

Condition 4 is only relevant if the recipient of the offer makes an application in response to the DOFP. In such circumstances the application can only be processed once the firm has assessed that the RMMI is ‘appropriate’ for the retail client in accordance with COBS 10 (non-MiFID business) or COBS 10A (MiFID business) as is appropriate. Where the RMMI is not deemed appropriate based upon a series of questions answered by the retail client then although ‘broad reasons’ for that assessment can be given it is forbidden to inform the client which particular answers led to that assessment (COBS 4.12A.31 - 35).

Risk warnings/summaries must be ‘prominent’. COBS 4.12A.37 & COBS 4.12A.38 advises that:

 the FCA has issued guidance on prominence in financial promotions

 the FCA expects firms to take into account the latest version of the international Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) where relevant

See ‘Useful Links’ for links to this material.

Page 29 of FCA Policy Statement PS22/10 (see ‘Useful Links’) provides a diagrammatic example of how firms could apply the rules from issuance of the ‘financial promotion’ to provision of a DOFP.

Reference should be made to COBS 4.12A.36 – 45 for further details concerning risk warnings and summaries.

## 13.12 Promotions of ‘non-mass market investments’ (‘NMMI’)

A NMMI is either of the following:

 a non-mainstream pooled investment (‘NMPI’)  a speculative illiquid security (‘SIS’)

An example of a NMPI would be an unregulated collective investment scheme.

A SIS is a debenture or preference share which meets the conditions set out in COBS 4.12B.50.

See ‘Useful links’ for the Handbook definitions of ‘NMPI’ and ‘SIS’ – COBS 4.12B.50 onwards also expands upon the definition of a SIS.

The communication of, or approval of, a financial promotion relating to a NMMI cannot be mass marketed to retail investors (“likely to be received by a retail client”) unless they fall within certain exemptions. This is in contrast to a RMMI (see above) which can be marketed to retail subject to that promotion (a) including a prescribed risk warning and (b) not including any form of incentive (albeit that a direct offer financial promotion – see above - of a RMMI is subject to more onerous requirements).

The exemptions referred to above for a NMMI are those that appear in the table in COBS 4.12B.7 – eleven such exemptions are currently listed - and include for example a ‘self-certified sophisticated investor’, a ‘non-retail client’ and ‘eligible employees’. It is important to note that these exemptions will have specific requirements which must be adhered to e.g. a self-certified sophisticated investor or a certified high net worth investor may receive promotions concerning any NMMI – but subject to the firm making a suitability assessment of that particular client before the promotion is made (COBS 4.12B.9 provides some guidance on this assessment).

In addition to the need for the above suitability assessment before promotion to a self-certified sophisticated investor or to a certified high net worth investor, the following conditions are also applicable for these two categories of investors and also for certified sophisticated investors (under COBS 4.12B.11 it is expected that the firm must “take reasonable steps” to establish that the retail client in question does indeed fall within one of these three categories – COBS 4.12B.38 to COBS 4.12B.45 provide further details on the categorisation of such clients) :

Before communicating the financial promotion to these three categories of investors:

 A personalised risk warning and risk summary is required (COBS 4.12B.14 and COBS 4 Annex 1R) and with the client being invited to specify whether or not they wish to continue the investment journey  There is a need for a cooling off period where the retail client requests a view of a financial promotion (COBS 4.12B.15) and with the client being invited to specify whether or not they wish to continue the investment journey

On communication of the financial promotion:

 There is a restriction on offering any form of incentives (COBS 4.12B.18 provides some examples of what would be regarded as ‘incentives’)  Provision of a prescribed risk warning within the communication is required (COBS 4.12B.21)

Note that there are some exemptions for the promotion of long-term asset funds (see COBS 4.12B.5(7)).

Risk warnings/summaries must be ‘prominent’. COBS 4.12B advises that:

 The FCA has issued guidance on prominence in financial promotions

 The FCA expects firms to take into account the latest version of the international Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) where relevant

See ‘Useful Links’ for access to this material.

There are additional requirements concerning charges and commission when the financial promotion relates to a SIS such stating the total amount of capital raised that will be paid out in costs, fees, charges and commissions and other expenses to a third party – see COBS 4.12B.32 to COBS 4.12B.37 for further details.

The application of which provisions apply to which type of client and investment (e.g. some provisions exclude long-term asset funds – see ‘Useful Links’ - whilst other provisions apply to all NMMI) can be complex – the table in COBS 14.12B.5 provides a useful overview.

It should also be borne in mind that if a NMMI is also an AIF then consideration should be given to AIFMD marketing requirements (see below).

## 13.13 UK UCITS Schemes

In addition to the general financial promotion requirements set out in this chapter, additional requirements apply when marketing UK UCITS schemes.

Specific information included in the marketing communication must:

 make no statement that contradicts or diminishes the significance of the information contained in the prospectus and the key investor information document for the scheme;  indicate that a prospectus exists for the scheme and that the key investor information document is available; and  specify where and in which language such information or documents may be obtained by investors or potential investors or how they may obtain access to them.

Where a UCITS scheme may invest more than 35% of its scheme property in transferable securities and money market instruments issued or guaranteed by the UK or an EEA State, one or more of its local authorities, a third country or a public international body to which the UK or one or more EEA States belong, the Firm must ensure that a marketing communication relating to the scheme contains a prominent statement drawing attention to the investment policy and indicating the particular States, local authorities, third countries or public international bodies in the securities of which the scheme intends to invest or has invested more than 35% of its scheme property.

Where a UCITS scheme invests principally in units in collective investment schemes, deposits or derivatives, or replicates a stock or debt securities index in accordance with COLL 5.2.31 R (Schemes replicating an index) the Firm must ensure that a marketing communication relating to the scheme contains a prominent statement drawing attention to the investment policy.

Where the net asset value of a UCITS scheme has, or is likely to have, high volatility owing to its portfolio composition or the portfolio management techniques that are or may be used, the Firm must ensure that a marketing communication relating to the scheme contains a prominent statement drawing attention to that characteristic.

If the marketing communication relates to a feeder UCITS then it must contain a statement that the feeder UCITS permanently invests at least 85% in value of its assets in units of its master UCITS.

See COBS 4.13 for further information.

## 13.14 Marketing and the AIFMD

The AIFMD introduced the concept of ‘marketing’ which sits alongside the UK’s ‘financial promotion’ (and ‘scheme promotion’) restrictions and this section must be read in conjunction with the other parts of this chapter.

Whilst there can be an overlap between ‘marketing’ and ‘financial promotion’ the two concepts are not the same. As an example an AIFM making a communication by way of a draft version of an AIF private placement memorandum is likely to be making a ‘financial promotion’ but would not be regarded as ‘marketing’ that AIF provided that the document cannot be used by a potential investor to make an investment (PERG 8.37.6 & PERG 8.37.14) - although care would need to be exercised to avoid the AIFM being accused of attempting to circumvent the AIFMD marketing regime.

An AIFM markets an AIF when the AIFM makes a direct or indirect offering or placement of units or shares of an AIF managed by it to or with an investor domiciled or with a registered office in the UK or Gibraltar or when another person makes such an offering or placement at the initiative of, or on behalf of, the AIFM (PERG 8.37(4)(1)(a)).

An investment firm markets an AIF when it makes a direct or indirect offering or placement of units or shares of the AIF to or with an investor domiciled or with a registered office in the UK or Gibraltar at the initiative of, or on behalf of, the AIFM of that AIF (PERG 8.37(4)(1)(b)).

To constitute ‘marketing’ the activity has to be at the initiative of the AIFM (or on the AIFM’s behalf). The marketing rules do not apply to situations where marketing is at the initiative of the investor (sometimes referred to as ‘reverse solicitation’). The FCA has advised that a confirmation from the investor that the ‘offering or placement’ was made at its initiative should be sufficient to demonstrate that this is the case but warns firms that they cannot rely on such confirmation if this has been obtained to circumvent the AIFMD requirements (PERG 8.37.11).

13.14.1 Marketing in the UK The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Regulations 2013 as amended places restrictions on the marketing of an AIF in the UK in that certain conditions need to be satisfied including notification to the FCA. Note that the marketing of an AIF to a retail client also has to comply with the ‘financial promotion’ (and scheme promotion) restrictions outlined elsewhere in this chapter. See FUND 3.12 (“Marketing a UK AIF in the UK”), FUND 10.5 (“National private placement”) and Part 6 of the UK AIFM Regulations 2013 for further information.

13.14.2 Marketing in the EEA Under the EU AIFMD it is at the discretion of each Member State on whether to allow the marketing of an AIF in its jurisdiction under their national private placement regime (NPPR). It is therefore necessary to seek local advice on current restrictions and requirements under the relevant NPPR.

## 13.15 Other Considerations

Any promotion must also consider the need for a key information document (“KID”) to be provided in accord with the Packaged Retail and Insurance-based Investment Products Regulation (”PRIIPs”) – see COBS 14.2. A KID will be required when the UK recipient is a retail client i.e. the recipient is neither a per se professional client nor an elective professional (the latter requiring the application of both the “qualitative test” and the “quantitative test” – see chapter ‘Conduct of Business – Client Categorisation’). There will be a similar obligation in respect of EEA investors under EEA PRIIPs.

## 13.16 Communications via Social Media

The use of social networking and social media websites may constitute a general financial promotion. Therefore, the Firm prohibits any use of social media websites (e.g. Facebook, Twitter) in relation to the Firm and its business and practices. All Employees of the Firm are prohibited from:  Divulging the Firm’s name or their position at the Firm on social media websites, except on professional networking sites (e.g. LinkedIn);  Using the e-mail function on any social media website when communicating information that would be required to be retained under the FCA record-keeping rules;  Posting of any confidential or non-public information on social media about both the Firm’s business activities and its client(s) or any business activities of the client account(s).

Any information that a member or employee intends to place on a social media website relating to the Firm, or its business, must be pre-approved by the Compliance Officer.

## 13.17 Approving and Signing-off Financial Promotions

COBS 4.10 concerns the need for a firm to have controls in place to ensure that all communications about investments and all Financial Promotions are in compliance with the FCA Handbook.

COBS 4.10.2R requires the Firm to ensure that Financial Promotions are approved if these Financial Promotions are communicated by unauthorised Persons. The Firm’s policy is that it will internally ‘approve’ all Financial Promotions to be communicated by the Firm itself and also those which a third party may wish to communicate.

The Firm’s Compliance Officer is responsible for internally approving all Financial Promotions.

The Compliance Officer will internally review the Financial Promotion taking into account the applicable Communication and Financial Promotion Rules in COBS 4. All Financial Promotions must be reviewed and approved by the Compliance Officer before they are issued. The Compliance Officer maintains a file of all final versions of all Financial Promotions and completed checklists to evidence these reviews.

## 13.18 Record Keeping of Financial Promotions

The Firm will keep records of Financial Promotions it makes and/or approves for at least five years.

With reference to NMMI and RMMI Financial Promotions records must include evidence that proves reasonable care has been taken to ensure that the Financial Promotion has only been made to those entitled to receive them. In particular, if the Firm has relied upon the ‘excluded communications’ exemption (see above), then the particular exemption in either the Financial Promotions Order or the Promotion of Collective Schemes Order must be clearly identified together with the reason why the Firm is satisfied that the exemption applies.

The Firm maintains a record of potential investors targeted, as well as supporting evidence of their eligibility under the exemptions.

See COBS 4.11 for further details, particularly for the record keeping requirements relating to the marketing of NMMI and of RMMI.

## 13.19 Useful Links
