sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
Section 21 of FSMA imposes a restriction on the communication of Financial Promotions by unauthorised persons. A person must not, in the course of business, communicate an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity (a Financial Promotion) unless:

 They are an Authorised Person; or  An Authorised Person approves the content of the Financial Promotion.

Strictly speaking, an authorised firm is not required to formally ‘approve’ its own Financial Promotions (although it is a requirement that an authorised firm must have procedures in place to ensure compliance with the FCA’s requirements set out in COBS 4.10) but by doing so will allow the material to be used by non-authorised persons without the latter breaching the above mentioned section 21 of FSMA. However this firm will internally approve all Financial Promotions – see ‘Approving and Signing-off Financial Promotions’ below.

The Compliance Officer is responsible for approving the Firm’s Financial Promotions.

There is no restriction on the media of communication to which the FCA requirements on Financial Promotion apply and they therefore include, for example, printed materials, personal visits or telephone calls and internet or other electronic media communications.

The rules in the Handbook on Communicating with Clients and Financial Promotions are contained in COBS 4.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
COBS 4.2.1R requires the Firm to ensure any communications with existing and potential clients are “fair, clear and not misleading”.

The Firm should ensure that the Financial Promotion (which should be identifiable as such):

1. Clearly states whether a product or service places a client’s capital at risk; 2. Quotes a yield figure that gives a balanced impression of the short and long term prospects of the investment; 3. Where it promotes an investment or service whose charging structure is complex or in relation to which the Firm will receive more than one element of remuneration, this includes the information necessary to ensure that it is fair, clear and not misleading and contains sufficient information to take into account the needs of the recipients; 4. That names the FCA (and/or the PRA) as its regulator and, if it refers to matters not regulated by the FCA, makes clear that those matters are not regulated by the FCA (and/or the PRA); and 5. Where it offers packaged products or stakeholder products not produced by the Firm, gives a fair, clear and not misleading impression of the product provider or the manager of the underlying investments. 6. Does not use terms such as ‘guaranteed’, ‘secure’ etc. unless that term is capable of being “fair, clear and not misleading” and provides all necessary information to justify the term (this requirement also applies to communications as well as to financial promotions).

sections.title: MiFID provisions
sections.content:
In addition to the general requirements relating to “fair, clear and not misleading”, MiFID (specifically Article 44 of Regulation 2017/565 – the “MiFID Org Regulation”) imposes the following requirements on information, including marketing communications, disseminated to both professional and retail clients:

(a) the information includes the name of the investment firm;

(b) the information is accurate and always gives a fair and prominent indication of any relevant risks when referencing any potential benefits of an investment service or financial instrument; (c) the information uses a font size in the indication of relevant risks that is at least equal to the predominant font size used throughout the information provided, as well as a layout ensuring such indication is prominent; (d) the information is sufficient for, and presented in a way that is likely to be understood by, the average member of the group to whom it is directed, or by whom it is likely to be received; (e) the information does not disguise, diminish or obscure important items, statements or warnings; (f) the information is consistently presented in the same language throughout all forms of information and marketing materials that are provided to each client, unless the client has accepted to receive information in more than one language; (g) the information is up-to-date and relevant to the means of communication used.

The MiFID Org Regulation imposes further requirements (replicated in COBS 4.5A.7 to COBS 4.5A.15) where the information in question:

 Includes comparative information Any such comparison must be meaningful and presented in a balanced way; information sources must be specified; and key facts and assumptions included.

 Makes a reference to tax It must state that the tax treatment depends upon individual circumstances and may be subject to change.

 Contains an indication of past performance or simulated past performance Past performance must not be the most prominent feature and must cover the preceding 5 years (or the whole period when the instrument/service has been offered or the financial index established where less than 5 years), or such longer period as the firm may decide, and based on complete 12-month periods.

Simulated performance must be based upon actual performance of instruments or indices that are the same, or substantially so, as the instrument concerned.

A prominent warning must appear that past performance is not a reliable indicator of future performance (and, where relevant, that the figures are based upon simulated past performance).

 Contains information on future performance The information must be supported by objective data and not based upon, or refer to, simulated past performance and accompanied by a prominent warning that such forecasts are not a reliable indicator of future performance.

Any information in marketing communications must be consistent with any information provided to clients in the course of carrying on investment business.

The above bullet points are summaries - COBS 4.5A provides further information on the MiFID provisions such as source of information etc. Should the financial promotion relate to FIIAs (‘funds investing in inherently illiquid assets’ - being essentially non-UCITS retail schemes with at least 50% in assets such as property or non-freely traded shares etc.) and be directed at retail clients (which would include e.g. High Net Worth individuals or Sophisticated investors) then a specific risk warning is required – see COBS 4.5A.17 for further details.

sections.title: Communications to Retail Clients (non-MiFID business)
sections.content:
In addition to the general requirements relating to “fair, clear and not misleading”, the Handbook imposes requirements on information, including marketing communications (including approval of), disseminated to, or likely to be received by, retail clients (a term which would include e.g. High Net Worth individuals or Sophisticated investors). It must be ensured that such information:

i. includes the name of the firm and, where relevant (e.g. in the unlikely event that the firm does not have the required expertise in relation to the marketing communication as per COBS 4.10.9A(3)) the name of the firm approving that communication and the date the financial promotion was approved by that firm; ii. is accurate and always gives a fair and prominent indication of any relevant risks when referencing any potential benefits of relevant business or a relevant investment; iii. is sufficient for, and presented in a way that is likely to be understood by, the average member of the group to whom it is directed, or by whom it is likely to be received; iv. does not disguise, diminish or obscure important items, statements or warnings. v. uses a font size in the indication of relevant risks that is at least equal to the predominant font size used throughout the information provided, as well as a layout that ensures that such indication is prominent; vi. is consistently presented in the same language throughout all forms of information and marketing materials that are provided to each client, unless the client has agreed to receive information in more than one language; and vii. is up-to-date and relevant to the means of communication used.

Further obligations arise when the information in question:

 Includes comparative information (COBS 4.5.6)  Makes a reference to tax (COBS 4.5.7)  Is made via a website, mobile application or other digital medium (COBS 4.5.2A)  Authorised fund managers’ communications in relation to benchmarks (COBS 4.5.11A – COBS 4.5.15

Any information contained in a financial promotion must be consistent with any information provided to a retail client in the course of carrying on investment business.

Should the financial promotion relate to FIIAs (‘funds investing in inherently illiquid assets’ - being essentially non-UCITS retail schemes with at least 50% in assets such as property or non-freely traded shares etc.) and be directed at retail clients (which would include e.g. High Net Worth individuals or Sophisticated investors) then a specific risk warning is required – see COBS 4.5.16 for further details. The above is a summary - COBS 4.5 provides further information on these provisions.

sections.title: Past, Simulated Past and Future Performance (non-MiFID business)
sections.content:
The Handbook contains prescriptive requirements in respect of both the approval and communication of a financial promotion where it is likely to be received by a retail client.

These requirements concern communications which include:

 Past performance  Simulated past performance  Future performance

Such requirements include, but are not limited to, the need for a warning that past performance (and a future forecast) is not a reliable indicator of future performance.

Reference should be made to COBS 4.6 for further information.

sections.title: Role of the Compliance Officer
sections.content:
Compliance Officer Sign-off and Approval Under s21 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 there is a general prohibition on a person making a financial promotion unless:

 It is made by an ‘authorised person’ (such as ALAMUT); or

 If made by an unauthorised person then it is approved by an ‘authorised person’

It will therefore be appreciated that whilst ‘sign-off’ and ‘approval’ might be interchangeable in normal day-to-day conversation, in a strict regulatory sense the firm would only be ‘approving’ a financial promotion if so requested by, or is to be used by, a non-authorised firm (the ‘approval’ is effectively confirming that the material produced by the other firm meets the FCA Handbook requirements), whilst the firm would be simply signing-off its own financial promotions. For the record an Appointed Representative falls within the definition of an ‘unauthorised person’.

Should ALAMUT ever ‘approve’ a financial promotion then the Handbook requires the firm to e.g. monitor the continuing compliance of that promotion and seek quarterly attestations from the other firm – with the ultimate sanction of the firm withdrawing its approval of the financial promotion. Full details of the obligations arising under the ‘approval and confirmation’ process can be found in COBS 4.10.

Any references to ‘communicating a financial promotion’ in this chapter can also be read, where relevant, as ‘approving a financial promotion’.

All Promotional Material must be submitted to the Compliance Officer prior to its first use. The Compliance Officer must make a written record indicating that he has reviewed and signed-off the Promotional Material as being in accord with regulatory requirements, along with the date of sign-off.

Employees intending to submit articles for external publication, or to participate in speaking engagements, must obtain prior approval of the Compliance Officer, and submit any written materials for review by the Compliance Officer.

Actual Performance If the Promotional Material includes any specific numerical or statistical information about the past performance of any actual accounts (including rates of return), the Compliance Officer must review all actual performance data for the same time period of all reasonably comparable accounts in order to determine that the reference in the Promotional Material to actual performance is representative of actual performance for the same time period of all reasonably comparable accounts.

Ratings Where Promotional Material contains any ratings, the Compliance Officer will review the Promotional Material to check that the material explains the ratings, including the limitations of such ratings.

Reprints Where Promotional Material contains any reprints of articles, the Compliance Officer will review the reprints to check that they have been supplemented with appropriate disclosures and disclaimers.

Promotional Recordkeeping The Compliance Officer is responsible for reviewing and signing off/approving all Promotional Material and keeping the material (together with any supporting documentation) for five years from the date of last use. There are specific record keeping obligations when a financial promotion relates to either a ‘restricted mass market investment’ (RMMI) or to a ‘non-mass market investment’ (NMMI) (see below for further information for these types of investment) – see COBS 4.11.4 and 4.11.5.

Electronic Communication The content standards and supervisory review requirements apply equally to Promotional Material distributed by email or other electronic means.

sections.title: Direct Offer Financial Promotions (MiFID business)
sections.content:
COBS 4.7 addresses Direct Offer Financial Promotions (DOFP). These are promotions that offer the client (whether retail or professional) the opportunity to enter into an agreement with a firm whereby that firm will offer services or financial instruments to the client, and which specifies the manner of response or

includes a form to make the response. Conversely, a communication that does not contain details of how to apply or to make an offer, or an application form will not be a DOFP. The following must be included in any direct offer marketing communication as is relevant to the offer:

 Information about the Firm and its services for clients and potential clients;  Information about financial instruments, including general nature and the risks involved;  Information concerning safeguarding of client financial instruments and funds;  Information on costs and associated charges.

Please see COBS 4.7.-1A and Articles 47 to 50 of Delegated Regulation 2017/565 (see ‘Useful Links’) – which are reproduced in COBS 6.1ZA and COBS 14.3A - for further information on what has to be included in the above.

Consideration must also be given to the need for an ‘appropriateness assessment’ before sending out any direct offer financial promotion – see COBS 10A and the chapter on ‘Appropriateness’ in this manual.

See also below re direct offer financial promotions in respect of ‘Restricted mass market investments’ and of ‘Non-mass market investments’.

sections.title: Direct Offer Financial Promotions (non-MiFID business)
sections.content:
COBS 4.7 addresses Direct Offer Financial Promotions (DOFP) which are addressed to, or likely to be received by, a retail client. These are promotions that offer the client to enter into an agreement with a firm whereby that firm will offer services or financial instruments to the client and which specifies the manner of response or includes a form to make the response. Conversely, a communication that does not contain details of how to apply or to make an offer, or an application form will not be a DOFP.

Various information must be included in any such direct offer marketing communication as is relevant to the offer such as:

 Information about the Firm and its services for clients and potential clients  Information about financial instruments, including general nature and the risks involved  Information concerning safeguarding of client financial instruments and funds

Please see COBS 4.7.1 (which refers the reader to other sections of the Handbook) for further details.

See also below re direct offer financial promotions in respect of ‘Restricted mass market investments’ and of ‘Non-mass market investments’.

sections.title: Warrants and Derivatives
sections.content: The Handbook imposes stricter requirements on the promotion of the above to retail clients. Before any direct offer financial promotion is made in respect of these types of instruments, consideration must be given to the need to undertake an ‘appropriateness assessment’ (refers to separate chapter on ‘Appropriateness’ in this manual). See COBS 4.7.6 for further information including restrictions on certain types of investments marketed to retail clients.

sections.title: Non-written Financial Promotions
sections.content: For the record only, this refers to ‘cold calling’ e.g. a financial promotion made by way of a telephone conversation which was not initiated by the prospective customer. These rules (COBS 4.8) do not apply to e.g. non-retail communications and as we do not undertake cold calling it will not be applicable to the Firm.

sections.title: Promotion of ‘restricted mass market investments’ (RMMI)
sections.content:
A ‘RMMI’ is any of the following:

 a non-readily realisable security;  a P2P agreement;  a P2P portfolio.

The Handbook definition of a ‘non-readily realisable security’ is a security that is not a ‘readily realisable security’. For the purposes of the above, the definition of a ‘non-readily realisable security’ includes, but is not limited to, a security which is not e.g. a public or government security or a security listed on a UK exchange. Note that the definition (a) excludes an NMMI (see next section below) and (b) the definition of a ‘readily realisable security’ is different for the purposes of the below NMMI regime. See ‘Useful Links’ for the full Handbook definition of both a RMMI and a non-readily realisable security.

The financial promotion of any RMMI is subject to the requirement that it:

 includes a prescribed form of risk warning (COBS 4.12A.10 onwards)  does not include any form of incentive for retail clients (COBS 4.12A.8 provides some examples of what would be regarded as ‘incentives’)

On the other hand a direct offer financial promotion (‘DOFP’ - see ‘Direct offer financial promotions’ section above for what does, and does not, constitute a DOFP) requires the following conditions to be met (COBS 4.12A.15):

1. The need for a cooling off period (COBS 4.12A.18 subject to COBS 4.12A.15(2)) 2. A personalised risk warning (COBS 4.12A.20 subject to COBS 4.12A.15(2)) 3. Categorisation (e.g. ‘high net worth’ etc.) (COBS 4.12A.21) 4. Appropriateness (COBS 4.12A.28 subject to COBS 4.12A.15(3))

Condition 1 relates to the period before communicating the DOFP.

Conditions 2 and 3 above need to be satisfied before communicating the DOFP.

Condition 3 captures the following categorisations of retail clients:

 high net worth investor  certified sophisticated investor  self-certified sophisticated investor  restricted investor

See COBS 4 Annexes 2R to 5R for further details including draft ‘statements’ to be completed by such persons that meet the relevant requirements.

Condition 4 is only relevant if the recipient of the offer makes an application in response to the DOFP. In such circumstances the application can only be processed once the firm has assessed that the RMMI is ‘appropriate’ for the retail client in accordance with COBS 10 (non-MiFID business) or COBS 10A (MiFID business) as is appropriate. Where the RMMI is not deemed appropriate based upon a series of questions answered by the retail client then although ‘broad reasons’ for that assessment can be given it is forbidden to inform the client which particular answers led to that assessment (COBS 4.12A.31 - 35).

Risk warnings/summaries must be ‘prominent’. COBS 4.12A.37 & COBS 4.12A.38 advises that:

 the FCA has issued guidance on prominence in financial promotions

 the FCA expects firms to take into account the latest version of the international Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) where relevant

See ‘Useful Links’ for links to this material.

Page 29 of FCA Policy Statement PS22/10 (see ‘Useful Links’) provides a diagrammatic example of how firms could apply the rules from issuance of the ‘financial promotion’ to provision of a DOFP.

Reference should be made to COBS 4.12A.36 – 45 for further details concerning risk warnings and summaries.

sections.title: Promotions of ‘non-mass market investments’ (‘NMMI’)
sections.content:
A NMMI is either of the following:

 a non-mainstream pooled investment (‘NMPI’)  a speculative illiquid security (‘SIS’)

An example of a NMPI would be an unregulated collective investment scheme.

A SIS is a debenture or preference share which meets the conditions set out in COBS 4.12B.50.

See ‘Useful links’ for the Handbook definitions of ‘NMPI’ and ‘SIS’ – COBS 4.12B.50 onwards also expands upon the definition of a SIS.

The communication of, or approval of, a financial promotion relating to a NMMI cannot be mass marketed to retail investors (“likely to be received by a retail client”) unless they fall within certain exemptions. This is in contrast to a RMMI (see above) which can be marketed to retail subject to that promotion (a) including a prescribed risk warning and (b) not including any form of incentive (albeit that a direct offer financial promotion – see above - of a RMMI is subject to more onerous requirements).

The exemptions referred to above for a NMMI are those that appear in the table in COBS 4.12B.7 – eleven such exemptions are currently listed - and include for example a ‘self-certified sophisticated investor’, a ‘non-retail client’ and ‘eligible employees’. It is important to note that these exemptions will have specific requirements which must be adhered to e.g. a self-certified sophisticated investor or a certified high net worth investor may receive promotions concerning any NMMI – but subject to the firm making a suitability assessment of that particular client before the promotion is made (COBS 4.12B.9 provides some guidance on this assessment).

In addition to the need for the above suitability assessment before promotion to a self-certified sophisticated investor or to a certified high net worth investor, the following conditions are also applicable for these two categories of investors and also for certified sophisticated investors (under COBS 4.12B.11 it is expected that the firm must “take reasonable steps” to establish that the retail client in question does indeed fall within one of these three categories – COBS 4.12B.38 to COBS 4.12B.45 provide further details on the categorisation of such clients) :

Before communicating the financial promotion to these three categories of investors:

 A personalised risk warning and risk summary is required (COBS 4.12B.14 and COBS 4 Annex 1R) and with the client being invited to specify whether or not they wish to continue the investment journey  There is a need for a cooling off period where the retail client requests a view of a financial promotion (COBS 4.12B.15) and with the client being invited to specify whether or not they wish to continue the investment journey

On communication of the financial promotion:

 There is a restriction on offering any form of incentives (COBS 4.12B.18 provides some examples of what would be regarded as ‘incentives’)  Provision of a prescribed risk warning within the communication is required (COBS 4.12B.21)

Note that there are some exemptions for the promotion of long-term asset funds (see COBS 4.12B.5(7)).

Risk warnings/summaries must be ‘prominent’. COBS 4.12B advises that:

 The FCA has issued guidance on prominence in financial promotions

 The FCA expects firms to take into account the latest version of the international Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) where relevant

See ‘Useful Links’ for access to this material.

There are additional requirements concerning charges and commission when the financial promotion relates to a SIS such stating the total amount of capital raised that will be paid out in costs, fees, charges and commissions and other expenses to a third party – see COBS 4.12B.32 to COBS 4.12B.37 for further details.

The application of which provisions apply to which type of client and investment (e.g. some provisions exclude long-term asset funds – see ‘Useful Links’ - whilst other provisions apply to all NMMI) can be complex – the table in COBS 14.12B.5 provides a useful overview.

It should also be borne in mind that if a NMMI is also an AIF then consideration should be given to AIFMD marketing requirements (see below).

sections.title: UK UCITS Schemes
sections.content:
In addition to the general financial promotion requirements set out in this chapter, additional requirements apply when marketing UK UCITS schemes.

Specific information included in the marketing communication must:

 make no statement that contradicts or diminishes the significance of the information contained in the prospectus and the key investor information document for the scheme;  indicate that a prospectus exists for the scheme and that the key investor information document is available; and  specify where and in which language such information or documents may be obtained by investors or potential investors or how they may obtain access to them.

Where a UCITS scheme may invest more than 35% of its scheme property in transferable securities and money market instruments issued or guaranteed by the UK or an EEA State, one or more of its local authorities, a third country or a public international body to which the UK or one or more EEA States belong, the Firm must ensure that a marketing communication relating to the scheme contains a prominent statement drawing attention to the investment policy and indicating the particular States, local authorities, third countries or public international bodies in the securities of which the scheme intends to invest or has invested more than 35% of its scheme property.

Where a UCITS scheme invests principally in units in collective investment schemes, deposits or derivatives, or replicates a stock or debt securities index in accordance with COLL 5.2.31 R (Schemes replicating an index) the Firm must ensure that a marketing communication relating to the scheme contains a prominent statement drawing attention to the investment policy.

Where the net asset value of a UCITS scheme has, or is likely to have, high volatility owing to its portfolio composition or the portfolio management techniques that are or may be used, the Firm must ensure that a marketing communication relating to the scheme contains a prominent statement drawing attention to that characteristic.

If the marketing communication relates to a feeder UCITS then it must contain a statement that the feeder UCITS permanently invests at least 85% in value of its assets in units of its master UCITS.

See COBS 4.13 for further information.

sections.title: Marketing and the AIFMD
sections.content:
The AIFMD introduced the concept of ‘marketing’ which sits alongside the UK’s ‘financial promotion’ (and ‘scheme promotion’) restrictions and this section must be read in conjunction with the other parts of this chapter.

Whilst there can be an overlap between ‘marketing’ and ‘financial promotion’ the two concepts are not the same. As an example an AIFM making a communication by way of a draft version of an AIF private placement memorandum is likely to be making a ‘financial promotion’ but would not be regarded as ‘marketing’ that AIF provided that the document cannot be used by a potential investor to make an investment (PERG 8.37.6 & PERG 8.37.14) - although care would need to be exercised to avoid the AIFM being accused of attempting to circumvent the AIFMD marketing regime.

An AIFM markets an AIF when the AIFM makes a direct or indirect offering or placement of units or shares of an AIF managed by it to or with an investor domiciled or with a registered office in the UK or Gibraltar or when another person makes such an offering or placement at the initiative of, or on behalf of, the AIFM (PERG 8.37(4)(1)(a)).

An investment firm markets an AIF when it makes a direct or indirect offering or placement of units or shares of the AIF to or with an investor domiciled or with a registered office in the UK or Gibraltar at the initiative of, or on behalf of, the AIFM of that AIF (PERG 8.37(4)(1)(b)).

To constitute ‘marketing’ the activity has to be at the initiative of the AIFM (or on the AIFM’s behalf). The marketing rules do not apply to situations where marketing is at the initiative of the investor (sometimes referred to as ‘reverse solicitation’). The FCA has advised that a confirmation from the investor that the ‘offering or placement’ was made at its initiative should be sufficient to demonstrate that this is the case but warns firms that they cannot rely on such confirmation if this has been obtained to circumvent the AIFMD requirements (PERG 8.37.11).

13.14.1 Marketing in the UK The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Regulations 2013 as amended places restrictions on the marketing of an AIF in the UK in that certain conditions need to be satisfied including notification to the FCA. Note that the marketing of an AIF to a retail client also has to comply with the ‘financial promotion’ (and scheme promotion) restrictions outlined elsewhere in this chapter. See FUND 3.12 (“Marketing a UK AIF in the UK”), FUND 10.5 (“National private placement”) and Part 6 of the UK AIFM Regulations 2013 for further information.

13.14.2 Marketing in the EEA Under the EU AIFMD it is at the discretion of each Member State on whether to allow the marketing of an AIF in its jurisdiction under their national private placement regime (NPPR). It is therefore necessary to seek local advice on current restrictions and requirements under the relevant NPPR.

sections.title: Other Considerations
sections.content: Any promotion must also consider the need for a key information document (“KID”) to be provided in accord with the Packaged Retail and Insurance-based Investment Products Regulation (”PRIIPs”) – see COBS 14.2. A KID will be required when the UK recipient is a retail client i.e. the recipient is neither a per se professional client nor an elective professional (the latter requiring the application of both the “qualitative test” and the “quantitative test” – see chapter ‘Conduct of Business – Client Categorisation’). There will be a similar obligation in respect of EEA investors under EEA PRIIPs.

sections.title: Communications via Social Media
sections.content:
The use of social networking and social media websites may constitute a general financial promotion. Therefore, the Firm prohibits any use of social media websites (e.g. Facebook, Twitter) in relation to the Firm and its business and practices. All Employees of the Firm are prohibited from:  Divulging the Firm’s name or their position at the Firm on social media websites, except on professional networking sites (e.g. LinkedIn);  Using the e-mail function on any social media website when communicating information that would be required to be retained under the FCA record-keeping rules;  Posting of any confidential or non-public information on social media about both the Firm’s business activities and its client(s) or any business activities of the client account(s).

Any information that a member or employee intends to place on a social media website relating to the Firm, or its business, must be pre-approved by the Compliance Officer.

sections.title: Approving and Signing-off Financial Promotions
sections.content:
COBS 4.10 concerns the need for a firm to have controls in place to ensure that all communications about investments and all Financial Promotions are in compliance with the FCA Handbook.

COBS 4.10.2R requires the Firm to ensure that Financial Promotions are approved if these Financial Promotions are communicated by unauthorised Persons. The Firm’s policy is that it will internally ‘approve’ all Financial Promotions to be communicated by the Firm itself and also those which a third party may wish to communicate.

The Firm’s Compliance Officer is responsible for internally approving all Financial Promotions.

The Compliance Officer will internally review the Financial Promotion taking into account the applicable Communication and Financial Promotion Rules in COBS 4. All Financial Promotions must be reviewed and approved by the Compliance Officer before they are issued. The Compliance Officer maintains a file of all final versions of all Financial Promotions and completed checklists to evidence these reviews.

sections.title: Record Keeping of Financial Promotions
sections.content:
The Firm will keep records of Financial Promotions it makes and/or approves for at least five years.

With reference to NMMI and RMMI Financial Promotions records must include evidence that proves reasonable care has been taken to ensure that the Financial Promotion has only been made to those entitled to receive them. In particular, if the Firm has relied upon the ‘excluded communications’ exemption (see above), then the particular exemption in either the Financial Promotions Order or the Promotion of Collective Schemes Order must be clearly identified together with the reason why the Firm is satisfied that the exemption applies.

The Firm maintains a record of potential investors targeted, as well as supporting evidence of their eligibility under the exemptions.

See COBS 4.11 for further details, particularly for the record keeping requirements relating to the marketing of NMMI and of RMMI.

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – DEALING AND MANAGING
chapters.summary: 14. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – DEALING AND MANAGING
chapters.content:
14. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – DEALING AND MANAGING

## 14.1 Purpose

To ensure that in any dealing with or management of clients’ funds, including the AIFs, that Acronym acts at all times with fairness and due regard to the interests of the client. The main requirements of the FCA Rules are intended to ensure that:  All decisions are properly recorded;  The requirements of different clients are dealt with fairly and in proper order; and  Best execution is obtained in a transaction.

Acronym has implemented documented Investment Management procedures which identify the method in which Acronym manages a client’s account. These procedures, together with the Compliance Manual and relevant investment management agreement, will indicate the scope of Acronym’s investment management powers, abilities and activities. Additionally, all portfolio managers are required to have an understanding of each client’s Investment Mandate. It is the investment management teams’ responsibility to ensure that all investment decisions taken are in conformity with these documents, particularly the investment objectives, restrictions and limitations contained therein. Consequently, all investment decisions must be recorded and be made available for periodic review. As a CPMI firm we are subject to the AIFMD ‘Best Execution’ requirements when managing the assets of the funds and to the MiFID ‘Best Execution’ requirements when managing the assets of third-parties when using our permitted additional MiFID activities. For practical purposes we will adopt the (‘higher-bar’) MiFID requirements, unless indicated otherwise in this chapter. For this reason, references to a ‘client’ or ‘clients’ should be read as ‘the fund(s)’ where applicable (again, unless indicated otherwise in this chapter).

## 14.2 Best Execution - summary

A firm that executes its own orders, rather than passing orders to e.g. a broker to execute them is under an obligation to execute orders on terms most favourable to their client and to take “all sufficient steps” to obtain, when executing orders, the best possible result for its clients taking into account the execution factors:  price;  costs;  speed;  likelihood of execution and settlement;  size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order

When determining the relative importance of these execution factors we must take into account the following execution criteria:

 the characteristics of the client including the categorisation of the client as retail or professional;  the characteristics of the client order, including where the order involves a securities financing transaction;  the characteristics of financial instruments that are the subject of that order;  the characteristics of the execution venues to which that order can be directed.

For the purposes of the above, ‘execution venues’ includes a regulated market (being a UK RIE), an MTF (whether a UK MTF or a non-UK facility which would be a UK MTF if in the UK), an OTF (whether a UK OTF or a non-UK facility which would be a UK OTF if in the UK), a systematic internaliser (as defined in the FCA Handbook), or a market maker or other liquidity provider or an entity that performs a similar function in a third country to the function performed by any of the forgoing. Although reference is made to ‘execution’, the obligation to act in accordance with best interests of the client, including the need to consider both the execution factors and execution criteria applies to firms that:  Execute orders  Receive and transmit orders to other entities for execution  Provide portfolio management and places order with other entities for execution

The above applies to all financial instruments, although it is accepted that the application of the obligation will not be the same for each financial instrument e.g. a bespoke OTC financial instrument vs. shares traded on a regulated market.

## 14.3 Best Execution – the role of price

The ‘best possible result’ for a retail client must be determined in terms of the total consideration (being the price of the security and the costs related to execution (COBS 11.2A.9). On the other hand “the FCA would expect that price will merit a high relative importance in obtaining the best possible result for professional clients” (COBS 11.2A.26). The above is modified to the extent that a firm follows specific client instructions. However it must be appreciated that any such client instruction does not absolve the firm from its overall best execution obligations – the firm will be treated as having satisfied its best execution obligations only in respect of the part of the order to which the client instruction relates. When executing orders or taking decisions to deal (including passing or transmitting orders to other entities for execution) in OTC products, including bespoke products, a firm must check the fairness of the price proposed to the client by gathering market data used in the estimation of the product and, where possible, by comparing with similar or comparable products.

## 14.4 Order Execution Policy

To comply with the obligation to “take all sufficient steps” Acronym has established an order execution policy. For each class of financial instruments the policy provides information on:  the different execution venues that the firm uses to execute orders; and  the factors affecting the choice of execution venue.

MiFID requires the firm to provide “appropriate information” on its order execution policy which:

 explains clearly how orders will be executed for its clients; and  includes sufficient details, and be provided in a way that can be easily understood by clients

Information on the Policy These requirements are expanded upon in the Level 2 Regulation in that the following details are to be provided to the client “in good time prior to the provision of the service”:  an account of the relative importance of the execution factors in accordance with the execution criteria, or the process by which the firm determines the relative importance of those factors;  a list of execution venues – specifying which venues are used for each class of financial instruments for retail client orders, professional client orders and securities financing transactions;  a list of factors used to select an execution venue, including qualitative factors, and the relative importance of each factor;  how the execution factors of cost, price, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of the order are considered as part of all sufficient steps to obtain the best possible result for the client;  where applicable, information that the firm executes orders outside a trading venue, the consequences, for example counterparty risk arising from execution outside a trading venue, and, upon client request, additional information about the consequences of this means of execution;  a clear and prominent warning that any specific instruction from a client may prevent the firm from taking the steps that it has designed and implemented in its execution policy to obtain the best possible result for the execution of those orders in respect of the elements covered by those instructions;  a summary of the selection process for execution venues, execution strategies employed, the procedures and process used to analyse the quality of execution obtained and how the firms monitor and verify that the best possible results were obtained;

The above must be provided in a ’durable medium’ e.g. in paper form or by means of a website.

Other considerations The following additional items need to be considered:  where the firm applies different fees depending on the execution venue, the firm must explain these differences in sufficient detail in order to allow the client to understand the advantages and the disadvantages of the choice of a single execution venue;  where the firm invites clients to choose an execution venue, fair, clear and not misleading information must be provided to prevent the client from choosing one execution venue rather than another on the sole basis of the price policy applied by the firm;  firms are only permitted to receive third-party payments that comply with MiFID and need to tell clients about the inducements that the firm may receive from the execution venues. The information shall specify the fees charged by the investment firm to all counterparties involved in the transaction, and where the fees vary depending on the client, the information shall indicate the maximum fees or range of the fees that may be payable;  where an investment firm charges more than one participant in a transaction, the firm shall inform its client of the value of any monetary or non-monetary benefits received by the firm;  where a client makes reasonable and proportionate requests for information about its policies or arrangements and how they are reviewed to an investment firm, that investment firm shall answer clearly and within a reasonable time;  Where orders are executed for retail clients they must be provided with a summary of the relevant policy, focussed on the total cost they incur and provide a link to the most recent quality of execution data published (see below) for each venue listed;  inclusion of a single execution venue in the policy is permitted, but only where the firm can demonstrate that this allows it to obtain best execution on a consistent basis. This should be supported by the data that execution venues and liquidity providers publish in accordance with MiFID requirements and by internal analysis.

Further details can be found in COBS 11.2A.25, 26 & 29.

Client consent All clients must give prior consent to the execution policy. However where there is the possibility that orders may be executed outside a trading venue then:  the client must be informed about this possibility; and  the client must give express prior consent before an order can be executed outside a trading venue (for this purpose being a UK RIE or an OTF/MTF as defined in ‘execution venue’ above or an EU regulated market). In the case of a fund, the consent must come from the fund where there is a governing body that can provide such consent.

Review and monitoring The effectiveness of both the order execution arrangements and execution policy must be monitored to identify, and to correct, any deficiencies. In particular the firm must assess on a regular basis whether the execution venues included in the policy provide for the best possible result. To this end, account will need to be taken of information on the quality of execution published by:  Execution venues  Market makers and liquidity providers  Other investment firms that execute client orders

It is a requirement that the policy is reviewed at least on an annual basis as well as the firm’s order execution arrangements. A review will also be necessary whenever there is a “material change” that may affect the firm’s ability to obtain the best possible result for clients. For these purposes a “material change” is defined as a significant event that could impact parameters of best execution such as cost, price, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of the order (‘execution factors’).

At all times the firm must be able to demonstrate to clients, on request, that it has executed in accordance with the order execution policy and to the FCA, on request, that it is executing orders on terms most favourable to the client.

## 14.5 Order Execution - summary

A firm that does not execute its own orders but rather places orders with other investment firms for execution e.g. a broker is still required to comply with the MiFID obligation to act in the best interest of the client when placing such orders. The impact of this is that Acronym is under an obligation to ensure that it takes “all sufficient steps” to obtain the best possible result taking into account the execution factors:  price;  costs;  speed;  likelihood of execution and settlement;  size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order  When determining the relative importance of these execution factors we must take into account the following execution criteria:  the characteristics of the client including the categorisation of the client as retail or professional;  the characteristics of the client order, including where the order involves a securities financing transaction;  the characteristics of financial instruments that are the subject of that order;  the characteristics of the execution venues to which that order can be directed.

## 14.6 The role of price

Although the above execution factors and criteria are to be considered, where a retail client is involved, the best possible result will be determined in terms of the total consideration, representing both the price of the security and the costs relating to execution. Ordinarily the FCA would expect price to merit a high relative importance in obtaining the best possible result for professional clients. However, the firm is not required to take these steps to the extent that it follows specific instructions from a client when passing or transmitting orders to another entity for execution. When taking decisions to deal (including passing or transmitting orders to other entities for execution) in OTC products, including bespoke products, a firm must check the fairness of the price proposed to the client by gathering market data used in the estimation of the product and, where possible, by comparing with similar or comparable products.

## 14.7 Order Execution Policy

To comply with the obligation to “take all sufficient steps” Acronym has established an order execution policy. For each class of financial instruments the policy provides information on the entities chosen for the execution of orders. MiFID requires the firm to provide:  information on the established policy; and  “appropriate information” about the firm and its services and the entities chosen for execution.

Upon reasonable request, the firm must provide clients, or potential clients, with information about the entities chosen for execution.

Other considerations MiFID requires the following additional items considered, although not all may be relevant to the firm’s model of passing orders to other firms for execution:  where the firm applies different fees depending on the execution venue, the firm must explain these differences in sufficient detail in order to allow the client to understand the advantages and the disadvantages of the choice of a single execution venue;

 where the firm invites clients to choose an execution venue, fair, clear and not misleading information must be provided to prevent the client from choosing one execution venue rather than another on the sole basis of the price policy applied by the firm;  firms are only permitted to receive third-party payments that comply with MiFID and need to tell clients about the inducements that the firm may receive from the execution venues. The information shall specify the fees charged by the investment firm to all counterparties involved in the transaction, and where the fees vary depending on the client, the information shall indicate the maximum fees or range of the fees that may be payable;  where an investment firm charges more than one participant in a transaction, the firm shall inform its client of the value of any monetary or non-monetary benefits received by the firm;  where a client makes reasonable and proportionate requests for information about its policies or arrangements and how they are reviewed to an investment firm, that investment firm shall answer clearly and within a reasonable time;  Where orders are executed for retail clients they must be provided with a summary of the relevant policy, focussed on the total cost they incur and provide a link to the most recent quality of execution published (see below) for each venue listed;  inclusion of a single entity for execution in the policy is permitted, but only where the firm can demonstrate that this allows it to obtain best execution on a consistent basis. This should be supported by the data that execution venues and liquidity providers publish in accordance with MiFID requirements and by internal analysis.

Further details can be found in COBS 11.2A.34 - 36.

Review and monitoring The effectiveness of the order execution policy and the execution quality of the entities identified in the policy must be monitored to identify, and to correct, any deficiencies. It is a requirement that the policy and arrangements are reviewed at least on an annual basis. A review will also be necessary whenever there is a “material change” that may affect the firm’s ability to obtain the best possible result for clients. For these purposes a “material change” is defined as a significant event that could impact parameters of best execution such as cost, price, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of the order (‘execution factors’). At all times the firm must be able to demonstrate to clients, on request, that it has executed in accordance with the order execution policy and to the FCA, on request, that it is executing orders on terms most favourable to the client.

## 14.8 Record keeping

An investment firm is required to immediately record and retain minimum specified details in relation to every initial decision to deal taken and every initial order received from a client.

Section 1 of Annex IV of MiFID Delegated Regulation 2017/565 (see ‘Useful Links’) – which is reproduced in COBS 11.5A.4 - sets out up to 16 data items to be recorded, as are applicable, including, but not limited to:

 A designation to identify the trader responsible for the firm making the investment decision;  A designation to identify the algorithm responsible in the firm for the investment decision;  The date and exact time (in accordance with MiFID II Article 50 – “Synchronisation of business clocks”) of receipt of the order/when the decision to deal was made.

An investment firm is required to immediately record and retain minimum specified details relating to transactions and order processing after making a decision to deal or after receiving a client order.

Section 2 of Annex IV of MiFID Delegated Regulation 2017/565 – which is reproduced in COBS 11.5A.5 - sets out up to 40 data items to be recorded, as are applicable, including, but not limited to:

 Short selling flag;

 A designation to identify the trader responsible for the execution;  The date and exact time (in accordance with MiFID II Article 50 – “Synchronisation of business clocks”) of: submission of the order for execution or decision to deal; any message transmitted to, and received from, the trading venue in relation to any events affecting the order; any message transmitted to or received from another investment firm in relation to any events affecting an order.

Recording of AIF subscription and redemption orders Aside from the need to record portfolio transactions, Acronym must take “all reasonable steps” to ensure that fund subscription and redemption orders are recorded without undue delay after receipt of any such order. The following must be recorded:  the relevant AIF;  the person giving or transmitting the order;  the person receiving the order;  the date and time of the order;  the terms and means of payment;  the type of the order;  the date of execution of the order;  the number of units or shares or equivalent amounts subscribed or redeemed;  the subscription or, where relevant, redemption price for each unit or share or, where relevant, the amount of capital committed and paid;  the total subscription or redemption value of the units or shares;  the gross value of the order including charges for subscription, or the net amount after charges for redemption All records relating to this section must be retained for at least five years (the last three items above “shall be recorded as soon as available”.

## 14.9 Client order handling

When a firm is “carrying out client orders”:  the orders must be executed promptly and accurately recorded and allocated;  comparable client orders should be carried out sequentially and promptly unless the characteristics of the order or market conditions make this impractical or against the client’s interests; AND  retail clients must be informed about any material difficulty relevant to the proper carrying out of orders promptly upon becoming aware of the difficulty.

In the context of these requirements “carrying out client orders” includes:  the execution of orders on behalf of clients;  placing orders with other entities for execution that arise from decisions to deal when providing portfolio management or collective portfolio management;  the transmission of client orders to other entities for execution when providing the service of reception and transmission. In keeping with Acronym’s policies and procedures it is prohibited to misuse information relating to pending orders.

## 14.10 Aggregation and Allocation

Orders must not be aggregated unless:  It is unlikely that aggregation will work overall to the disadvantage of any client;  It is disclosed to each client whose order is aggregated that aggregation may work to its disadvantage in relation to a particular order; and  An order allocation policy is established and implemented providing for the fair allocation of such aggregated orders, including how the volume and price of orders determines allocations and the treatment of partial executions. Partially executed aggregated orders must be allocated in accordance with the above order allocation policy. Where relevant, partially executed orders involving a client order and an own account order must give priority to the client when allocating (unless the firm can demonstrate that without the combination it would have been unable to carry out the order on such advantageous terms, or at all, in which case it may allocate in accordance with its order allocation policy).

## 14.11 Client limit orders

A client limit order in respect of shares admitted to trading on a regulated market or on a trading venue which is not immediately executed under prevailing market conditions must be made public immediately so as to facilitate the earliest possible execution of that order (unless the client has expressly instructed otherwise). Firms can comply with this obligation by transmitting the client limit order to a trading venue. The above obligation is disapplied for transactions that are ‘large in scale’ – COBS 11.4.5.

## 14.12 Short selling and EMIR

See the relevant chapters in this manual on ‘Short Selling’ and ‘EMIR Requirements’.

## 14.13 Algorithmic and High-Frequency Trading

Introduction MiFID II introduces requirements on firms that engage in algorithmic trading to ensure that adequate systems and controls are in place to prevent the sending of erroneous orders or acting in any way that could create or contribute to a disorderly market. Firms engaging in high frequency algorithmic trading must maintain accurate and time sequenced records of all placed orders, including cancellations, executed orders and quotations on trading venues. Firms providing direct electronic access (DEA) to a trading venue (which must be notified to the FCA) must properly assess the suitability of clients using this service and monitor their use to prevent risks to the firm or the creation of a disorderly market. There is also an obligation on trading venues to ensure that members or participants engaging in algorithmic trading cannot create or contribute to disorderly trading e.g. by having the ability to slow down order flow or limiting the ratio of unexecuted orders to transactions that may be entered into the system.

What is ‘algorithmic trading’? This is defined in MiFID as “trading in financial instruments where a computer algorithm automatically determines individual parameters of orders such as whether to initiate the order, the timing, price or quantity of the order or how to manage the order after its submission, with limited or no human intervention, and does not include any system that is only used for the purpose of routing orders to one or more trading venues or for the processing of orders involving no determination of any trading parameters or for the confirmation of orders or the post-trade processing of executed transactions”.

What is ‘high-frequency trading’? This is an algorithmic trading technique characterised by:  infrastructure intended to minimise network and other types of latencies, including at least one of the following facilities for algorithmic order entry: co-location, proximity hosting or high-speed direct electronic access;  system-determination of order initiation, generation, routing or execution without human intervention for individual trades or orders; and  high message intraday rates which constitute orders, quotes or cancellations;

Requirements Obligations on firms include, but are not limited to:  clear lines of accountability re development, deployment and subsequent updates of trading algorithms and resolution of identified problems  need for compliance staff to have at least a general understanding of how the firm’s algorithmic trading systems and trading algorithms operate  sufficient staff with the necessary skills and knowledge  need for clearly delineated methodologies to develop and test systems

 conformance testing with relevant trading venue(s)  predefined limits on e.g. number of instruments being traded and strategy positions  annual self-assessment and validation (including stress testing)  need for ‘kill functionality’ i.e. ability to cancel immediately all or any of the firm’s unexecuted orders  automated surveillance system  business continuity  periodic review of DEA clients (where relevant)  in the case of high frequency algorithmic trading the need for recording prescribed details Further details can be found in MAR 7A and Delegated Regulation 2017/589 (see ‘Useful Links’).

## 14.14 Securitisations

The UK Securitisation Regulation (EU 2017/2402 as amended) provides a framework for securitisations and imposes requirements on both those involved in the securitisation process and on those that invest in securitisations. Before investment in a securitisation position an ‘institutional investor‘ (a term which includes UK UCITS, AIFMs that manage and/or market AIFs in the UK and investment firms as defined in Article 4(1)(2A) of the UK Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) as amended is required to undertake appropriate due-diligence to verify that the originator, sponsor or original lender retains, on an ongoing basis, a material net economic interest of at least 5%. In addition Acronym must verify that loans within the securitisation were granted on the basis of sound and well-defined criteria (unless the originator or original lender is a UK credit institution or investment firm as defined in the CRR). Acronym is required to demonstrate that it has a thorough understanding of any such positions and for this reason has in place policies and procedures for the analysis and recording of positions taken. The detailed requirements on due-diligence, including the need for monitoring, stress testing and the requirement to take any necessary corrective action, can be found in Article 5 of the Securitisation Regulation. Note that whilst the firm may not meet the definition of ‘institutional investor’ when carrying out its MiFID activities i.e. activities other than when ‘managing an AIF’ it may still be required to comply with the above obligations in respect of activities undertaken for its clients. Although the above is based upon the UK post-Brexit version of the EU Securitisation Regulation it should be noted that the latter would apply if a UK AIFM markets an AIF in the EU.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.content:
To ensure that in any dealing with or management of clients’ funds, including the AIFs, that Acronym acts at all times with fairness and due regard to the interests of the client. The main requirements of the FCA Rules are intended to ensure that:  All decisions are properly recorded;  The requirements of different clients are dealt with fairly and in proper order; and  Best execution is obtained in a transaction.

Acronym has implemented documented Investment Management procedures which identify the method in which Acronym manages a client’s account. These procedures, together with the Compliance Manual and relevant investment management agreement, will indicate the scope of Acronym’s investment management powers, abilities and activities. Additionally, all portfolio managers are required to have an understanding of each client’s Investment Mandate. It is the investment management teams’ responsibility to ensure that all investment decisions taken are in conformity with these documents, particularly the investment objectives, restrictions and limitations contained therein. Consequently, all investment decisions must be recorded and be made available for periodic review. As a CPMI firm we are subject to the AIFMD ‘Best Execution’ requirements when managing the assets of the funds and to the MiFID ‘Best Execution’ requirements when managing the assets of third-parties when using our permitted additional MiFID activities. For practical purposes we will adopt the (‘higher-bar’) MiFID requirements, unless indicated otherwise in this chapter. For this reason, references to a ‘client’ or ‘clients’ should be read as ‘the fund(s)’ where applicable (again, unless indicated otherwise in this chapter).

sections.title: Best Execution - summary
sections.content:
A firm that executes its own orders, rather than passing orders to e.g. a broker to execute them is under an obligation to execute orders on terms most favourable to their client and to take “all sufficient steps” to obtain, when executing orders, the best possible result for its clients taking into account the execution factors:  price;  costs;  speed;  likelihood of execution and settlement;  size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order

When determining the relative importance of these execution factors we must take into account the following execution criteria:

 the characteristics of the client including the categorisation of the client as retail or professional;  the characteristics of the client order, including where the order involves a securities financing transaction;  the characteristics of financial instruments that are the subject of that order;  the characteristics of the execution venues to which that order can be directed.

For the purposes of the above, ‘execution venues’ includes a regulated market (being a UK RIE), an MTF (whether a UK MTF or a non-UK facility which would be a UK MTF if in the UK), an OTF (whether a UK OTF or a non-UK facility which would be a UK OTF if in the UK), a systematic internaliser (as defined in the FCA Handbook), or a market maker or other liquidity provider or an entity that performs a similar function in a third country to the function performed by any of the forgoing. Although reference is made to ‘execution’, the obligation to act in accordance with best interests of the client, including the need to consider both the execution factors and execution criteria applies to firms that:  Execute orders  Receive and transmit orders to other entities for execution  Provide portfolio management and places order with other entities for execution

The above applies to all financial instruments, although it is accepted that the application of the obligation will not be the same for each financial instrument e.g. a bespoke OTC financial instrument vs. shares traded on a regulated market.

sections.title: Best Execution – the role of price
sections.content: The ‘best possible result’ for a retail client must be determined in terms of the total consideration (being the price of the security and the costs related to execution (COBS 11.2A.9). On the other hand “the FCA would expect that price will merit a high relative importance in obtaining the best possible result for professional clients” (COBS 11.2A.26). The above is modified to the extent that a firm follows specific client instructions. However it must be appreciated that any such client instruction does not absolve the firm from its overall best execution obligations – the firm will be treated as having satisfied its best execution obligations only in respect of the part of the order to which the client instruction relates. When executing orders or taking decisions to deal (including passing or transmitting orders to other entities for execution) in OTC products, including bespoke products, a firm must check the fairness of the price proposed to the client by gathering market data used in the estimation of the product and, where possible, by comparing with similar or comparable products.

sections.title: Order Execution Policy
sections.content:
To comply with the obligation to “take all sufficient steps” Acronym has established an order execution policy. For each class of financial instruments the policy provides information on:  the different execution venues that the firm uses to execute orders; and  the factors affecting the choice of execution venue.

MiFID requires the firm to provide “appropriate information” on its order execution policy which:

 explains clearly how orders will be executed for its clients; and  includes sufficient details, and be provided in a way that can be easily understood by clients

Information on the Policy These requirements are expanded upon in the Level 2 Regulation in that the following details are to be provided to the client “in good time prior to the provision of the service”:  an account of the relative importance of the execution factors in accordance with the execution criteria, or the process by which the firm determines the relative importance of those factors;  a list of execution venues – specifying which venues are used for each class of financial instruments for retail client orders, professional client orders and securities financing transactions;  a list of factors used to select an execution venue, including qualitative factors, and the relative importance of each factor;  how the execution factors of cost, price, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of the order are considered as part of all sufficient steps to obtain the best possible result for the client;  where applicable, information that the firm executes orders outside a trading venue, the consequences, for example counterparty risk arising from execution outside a trading venue, and, upon client request, additional information about the consequences of this means of execution;  a clear and prominent warning that any specific instruction from a client may prevent the firm from taking the steps that it has designed and implemented in its execution policy to obtain the best possible result for the execution of those orders in respect of the elements covered by those instructions;  a summary of the selection process for execution venues, execution strategies employed, the procedures and process used to analyse the quality of execution obtained and how the firms monitor and verify that the best possible results were obtained;

The above must be provided in a ’durable medium’ e.g. in paper form or by means of a website.

Other considerations The following additional items need to be considered:  where the firm applies different fees depending on the execution venue, the firm must explain these differences in sufficient detail in order to allow the client to understand the advantages and the disadvantages of the choice of a single execution venue;  where the firm invites clients to choose an execution venue, fair, clear and not misleading information must be provided to prevent the client from choosing one execution venue rather than another on the sole basis of the price policy applied by the firm;  firms are only permitted to receive third-party payments that comply with MiFID and need to tell clients about the inducements that the firm may receive from the execution venues. The information shall specify the fees charged by the investment firm to all counterparties involved in the transaction, and where the fees vary depending on the client, the information shall indicate the maximum fees or range of the fees that may be payable;  where an investment firm charges more than one participant in a transaction, the firm shall inform its client of the value of any monetary or non-monetary benefits received by the firm;  where a client makes reasonable and proportionate requests for information about its policies or arrangements and how they are reviewed to an investment firm, that investment firm shall answer clearly and within a reasonable time;  Where orders are executed for retail clients they must be provided with a summary of the relevant policy, focussed on the total cost they incur and provide a link to the most recent quality of execution data published (see below) for each venue listed;  inclusion of a single execution venue in the policy is permitted, but only where the firm can demonstrate that this allows it to obtain best execution on a consistent basis. This should be supported by the data that execution venues and liquidity providers publish in accordance with MiFID requirements and by internal analysis.

Further details can be found in COBS 11.2A.25, 26 & 29.

Client consent All clients must give prior consent to the execution policy. However where there is the possibility that orders may be executed outside a trading venue then:  the client must be informed about this possibility; and  the client must give express prior consent before an order can be executed outside a trading venue (for this purpose being a UK RIE or an OTF/MTF as defined in ‘execution venue’ above or an EU regulated market). In the case of a fund, the consent must come from the fund where there is a governing body that can provide such consent.

Review and monitoring The effectiveness of both the order execution arrangements and execution policy must be monitored to identify, and to correct, any deficiencies. In particular the firm must assess on a regular basis whether the execution venues included in the policy provide for the best possible result. To this end, account will need to be taken of information on the quality of execution published by:  Execution venues  Market makers and liquidity providers  Other investment firms that execute client orders

It is a requirement that the policy is reviewed at least on an annual basis as well as the firm’s order execution arrangements. A review will also be necessary whenever there is a “material change” that may affect the firm’s ability to obtain the best possible result for clients. For these purposes a “material change” is defined as a significant event that could impact parameters of best execution such as cost, price, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of the order (‘execution factors’).

At all times the firm must be able to demonstrate to clients, on request, that it has executed in accordance with the order execution policy and to the FCA, on request, that it is executing orders on terms most favourable to the client.

sections.title: Order Execution - summary
sections.content: A firm that does not execute its own orders but rather places orders with other investment firms for execution e.g. a broker is still required to comply with the MiFID obligation to act in the best interest of the client when placing such orders. The impact of this is that Acronym is under an obligation to ensure that it takes “all sufficient steps” to obtain the best possible result taking into account the execution factors:  price;  costs;  speed;  likelihood of execution and settlement;  size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order  When determining the relative importance of these execution factors we must take into account the following execution criteria:  the characteristics of the client including the categorisation of the client as retail or professional;  the characteristics of the client order, including where the order involves a securities financing transaction;  the characteristics of financial instruments that are the subject of that order;  the characteristics of the execution venues to which that order can be directed.

sections.title: The role of price
sections.content: Although the above execution factors and criteria are to be considered, where a retail client is involved, the best possible result will be determined in terms of the total consideration, representing both the price of the security and the costs relating to execution. Ordinarily the FCA would expect price to merit a high relative importance in obtaining the best possible result for professional clients. However, the firm is not required to take these steps to the extent that it follows specific instructions from a client when passing or transmitting orders to another entity for execution. When taking decisions to deal (including passing or transmitting orders to other entities for execution) in OTC products, including bespoke products, a firm must check the fairness of the price proposed to the client by gathering market data used in the estimation of the product and, where possible, by comparing with similar or comparable products.

sections.title: Order Execution Policy
sections.content:
To comply with the obligation to “take all sufficient steps” Acronym has established an order execution policy. For each class of financial instruments the policy provides information on the entities chosen for the execution of orders. MiFID requires the firm to provide:  information on the established policy; and  “appropriate information” about the firm and its services and the entities chosen for execution.

Upon reasonable request, the firm must provide clients, or potential clients, with information about the entities chosen for execution.

Other considerations MiFID requires the following additional items considered, although not all may be relevant to the firm’s model of passing orders to other firms for execution:  where the firm applies different fees depending on the execution venue, the firm must explain these differences in sufficient detail in order to allow the client to understand the advantages and the disadvantages of the choice of a single execution venue;

 where the firm invites clients to choose an execution venue, fair, clear and not misleading information must be provided to prevent the client from choosing one execution venue rather than another on the sole basis of the price policy applied by the firm;  firms are only permitted to receive third-party payments that comply with MiFID and need to tell clients about the inducements that the firm may receive from the execution venues. The information shall specify the fees charged by the investment firm to all counterparties involved in the transaction, and where the fees vary depending on the client, the information shall indicate the maximum fees or range of the fees that may be payable;  where an investment firm charges more than one participant in a transaction, the firm shall inform its client of the value of any monetary or non-monetary benefits received by the firm;  where a client makes reasonable and proportionate requests for information about its policies or arrangements and how they are reviewed to an investment firm, that investment firm shall answer clearly and within a reasonable time;  Where orders are executed for retail clients they must be provided with a summary of the relevant policy, focussed on the total cost they incur and provide a link to the most recent quality of execution published (see below) for each venue listed;  inclusion of a single entity for execution in the policy is permitted, but only where the firm can demonstrate that this allows it to obtain best execution on a consistent basis. This should be supported by the data that execution venues and liquidity providers publish in accordance with MiFID requirements and by internal analysis.

Further details can be found in COBS 11.2A.34 - 36.

Review and monitoring The effectiveness of the order execution policy and the execution quality of the entities identified in the policy must be monitored to identify, and to correct, any deficiencies. It is a requirement that the policy and arrangements are reviewed at least on an annual basis. A review will also be necessary whenever there is a “material change” that may affect the firm’s ability to obtain the best possible result for clients. For these purposes a “material change” is defined as a significant event that could impact parameters of best execution such as cost, price, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of the order (‘execution factors’). At all times the firm must be able to demonstrate to clients, on request, that it has executed in accordance with the order execution policy and to the FCA, on request, that it is executing orders on terms most favourable to the client.
