sections.title: Algorithmic and High-Frequency Trading
sections.content:
Introduction MiFID II introduces requirements on firms that engage in algorithmic trading to ensure that adequate systems and controls are in place to prevent the sending of erroneous orders or acting in any way that could create or contribute to a disorderly market. This is an algorithmic trading technique characterised by:  infrastructure intended to minimise network and other types of latencies, including at least one of the following facilities for algorithmic order entry: co-location, proximity hosting or high-speed direct electronic access;  system-determination of order initiation, generation, routing or execution without human intervention for individual trades or orders; and  high message intraday rates which constitute orders, quotes or cancellations;

Requirements Obligations on firms include, but are not limited to:  clear lines of accountability re development, deployment and subsequent updates of trading algorithms and resolution of identified problems  need for compliance staff to have at least a general understanding of how the firm’s algorithmic trading systems and trading algorithms operate  sufficient staff with the necessary skills and knowledge  need for clearly delineated methodologies to develop and test systems

 conformance testing with relevant trading venue(s)  predefined limits on e.g. number of instruments being traded and strategy positions  annual self-assessment and validation (including stress testing)  need for ‘kill functionality’ i.e. ability to cancel immediately all or any of the firm’s unexecuted orders  automated surveillance system  business continuity  periodic review of DEA clients (where relevant)  in the case of high frequency algorithmic trading the need for recording prescribed details Further details can be found in MAR 7A and Delegated Regulation 2017/589 (see ‘Useful Links’).

sections.title: Securitisations
sections.content: The UK Securitisation Regulation (EU 2017/2402 as amended) provides a framework for securitisations and imposes requirements on both those involved in the securitisation process and on those that invest in securitisations. Before investment in a securitisation position an ‘institutional investor‘ (a term which includes UK UCITS, AIFMs that manage and/or market AIFs in the UK and investment firms as defined in Article 4(1)(2A) of the UK Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) as amended is required to undertake appropriate due-diligence to verify that the originator, sponsor or original lender retains, on an ongoing basis, a material net economic interest of at least 5%. In addition Acronym must verify that loans within the securitisation were granted on the basis of sound and well-defined criteria (unless the originator or original lender is a UK credit institution or investment firm as defined in the CRR). Acronym is required to demonstrate that it has a thorough understanding of any such positions and for this reason has in place policies and procedures for the analysis and recording of positions taken. The detailed requirements on due-diligence, including the need for monitoring, stress testing and the requirement to take any necessary corrective action, can be found in Article 5 of the Securitisation Regulation. Note that whilst the firm may not meet the definition of ‘institutional investor’ when carrying out its MiFID activities i.e. activities other than when ‘managing an AIF’ it may still be required to comply with the above obligations in respect of activities undertaken for its clients. Although the above is based upon the UK post-Brexit version of the EU Securitisation Regulation it should be noted that the latter would apply if a UK AIFM markets an AIF in the EU.

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - RECORDING OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATIONS AND ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATIONS
chapters.summary: 15. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - RECORDING OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATIONS AND ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATIONS
chapters.content:
15. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - RECORDING OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATIONS AND ELECTRONIC COMMUNICATIONS

## 15.1 Application

The Handbook (SYSC 10A) imposes recording obligations when a firm carries out certain activities in investments which are financial instruments but only in respect to those activities carried on from an establishment (including a branch) maintained by the Firm in the UK.

## 15.2 Activities Subject to Recording

The recording obligation applies to a wide range of activities, although not all of these will be relevant to Alamut’s business model:

 arranging (bringing about) deals in investments;  dealing in investments as agent;  dealing in investments as principal;  managing investments;  managing a UK UCITS to the extent that this comprises the function of investment management referred to in Annex II of the UCITS Directive;  managing an AIF to the extent that this comprises the function of portfolio management referred to in Annex I of the AIFMD;  establishing, operating or winding up a collective investment scheme to the extent that this comprises scheme management activity.

Note that:

 the above is greater in scope than the MiFID requirement which is to “include the recording of telephone conversations or electronic communications relating to, at least, transactions concluded when dealing on own account and the provision of client order services that relate to the reception, transmission and execution of client orders”; and  the scope captures both MiFID and non-MiFID firms.

## 15.3 Financial Instruments

As a collective portfolio management investment firm the activities we undertake are a mixture of non- MiFID (‘managing an AIF’) and MiFID (‘managing investments’, ‘arranging (bringing about) deals in investments’ etc.).

Whilst ‘managing an AIF’ or ‘managing investments’ only financial instruments that are:

a. admitted to trading on a trading venue; or b. traded on a trading venue; or c. instruments for which a request has been made for admission to trading on a trading venue; or d. instruments covered by paragraph (a), (b) or (c), but the price or value of which depends on, or has an effect on, the price or value of a financial instrument referred to in those paragraphs

are captured by these rules. ‘Trading venue’ here means a UK RIE or an EEA regulated market or an MTF or OTF.

However, when we are ‘arranging (bringing about) deals in investments’ and/or ‘dealing as agent’ the rules apply to all financial instruments.

For the avoidance of doubt, whilst ‘managing an AIF’ can cover a wide range of activities the FCA has confirmed that the intention is to only capture the transactional side of portfolio management i.e. conversations that relate to transactions undertaken or intended to be undertaken.

## 15.4 Recording Obligation

Alamut is required to “take all reasonable steps” to record telephone conversations, and to keep a copy of all electronic communications, that relate to the above activities and financial instruments.

The recording obligation applies to all such telephone conversations and electronic communications that are made with or sent from or received on equipment:

 provided by the Firm to an employee or contractor; or  the use of which by an employee or contractor has been accepted or permitted by the Firm.

It is prohibited for any employee or contractor to use privately-owned equipment which Alamut in unable to record or copy.

A record is maintained of those individuals who either have phones etc. provided by the Firm and, where relevant, privately-owned equipment that has been approved for use.

Alamut has a recording policy in place which:

 identifies the telephone conversations and electronic communications, including relevant internal telephone conversations and electronic communications, that are subject to the recording requirement; and  sets out the procedures to be followed and measures to be adopted to ensure that relevant conversations and communications are recorded where exceptional circumstances arise and the Firm is unable to record the conversation/communication on devices issued, accepted or permitted by the Firm. Evidence of such circumstances shall be retained and shall be accessible to the FCA.

## 15.5 Notifying Clients

All clients, whether new or existing, must be informed before the provision of any investment services:

 that communications or conversations between them and Alamut will be recorded if they are related to the above activities; and  a copy of the recording will be available on request for a period of five years (and, where so requested by the FCA, for a period of up to seven years).

## 15.6 Other Communications

Any client instructions given otherwise than by telephone must be made in a durable medium such as by mail, faxes, emails or documentation of a client instruction issued at a meeting.

Any face-to-face conversation with a client may be recorded by using written minutes or notes. At a minimum, the following must be recorded as relevant:

 date and time of meeting(s);  location of meeting(s);  identity of attendees;  initiator of the meeting(s); and  relevant information about the order(s), including price, volume, type of order and when it will be transmitted and executed.

## 15.7 Recording: Controls

All records arising from the recording obligation will be stored in an appropriate manner which ensures that they cannot be altered or deleted and that allows them to be replayed or copied.

As mentioned above, records will be retained for at least five years from the date that the relevant record is created (although the FCA has discretion to request a period of up to seven years).

Persons should be aware that:

 the Firm periodically monitors conversations that are subject to the recording obligation together with the associated records of transactions and orders; and  records will be readily accessible and available to clients on request.

## 15.8 Useful Links

SYSC 10A https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/SYSC/10A/1.html

Definition of ‘financial instruments’ https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/glossary/G1519.html

FCA Policy Statement PS17/14 – includes advice on recording obligations (Chapter 19) https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/policy/ps17-14.pdf

Delegated Regulation 2017/565 (‘MiFID Org Regulation) https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2017/565/contents and in particular

Minimum content of a firm’s recording policy Article 76(1)

Record of individuals with firm’s devices/privately-owned devices Article 76(4)

Need for training employees in procedures surrounding recording Article 76(5)

Need for periodic monitoring of records (‘risk based and proportionate’) Article 76(6)

Information to clients re recording Article 76(8)

Face-to-face conversations with clients Article 76(9)

UK version of MiFID Org Regulation https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2017/565/contents

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - PERSONAL ACCOUNT DEALING
chapters.summary: 16. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - PERSONAL ACCOUNT DEALING
chapters.content:
16. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS - PERSONAL ACCOUNT DEALING

## 16.1 Purpose

Alamut allows Personal Account Dealing (“PA Dealing” or “PAD”) provided that it falls within the permitted framework (‘PA Dealing’ Policy) as set out later in this Chapter. Staff’s primary function at Alamut is to ensure they operate in the best interests of the Firm and underlying clients. Hence excessive PA Dealing may raise conflicts of interest or personal finance issues where staff trade beyond their means. These factors may directly affect employees’ abilities to perform their role. Alamut has a vested interest to ensure that Staff maintain adequate financial stability so as to limit any incentives to fraud or competing interests between Staff and Alamut.

Personal transactions that involve the misuse or improper disclosure of confidential information relating to clients or transactions relating to clients are prohibited, as will be any transaction where the individual is prohibited from entering into it under the Market Abuse Regulation (see the Market Abuse chapter of this manual for further information).

## 16.2 What is a personal transaction?

For the purposes of these rules a ‘personal transaction’ extends further than a ‘relevant person’ (see below).

A personal transaction is any trade carried out by or on behalf of the relevant person where at least one of the following criteria are met:

(a) the relevant person is acting outside the scope of the activities he carries out in this professional capacity; (b) the trade is carried out for the account of any of the following persons: (i) the relevant person; (ii) any person with who he has a family relationship, or with whom he has close links; (iii) a person in respect of whom the relevant person has a direct or indirect material interest in the outcome of the trade, other than obtaining a fee or commission for the execution of the trade.

## 16.3 What is a ‘relevant person’?

The definition of a relevant person is quite wide-ranging as it captures:

 a director, partner or equivalent, manager or tied agent/appointed representative of the Firm;  a director, partner or equivalent, or manager of any tied agent/appointed representative of the Firm;  an employee of the Firm or of a tied agent/appointed representative of the Firm, as well as any other natural person whose services are placed at the disposal and under the control of the Firm or a tied agent/appointed representative of the Firm and who is involved in the provision by the Firm of investment services and activities;  a natural person who is directly involved in the provision of services to the investment firm or to its tied agent/appointed representative under an outsourcing arrangement for the purpose of the provision by the Firm of investment services and activities.

## 16.4 Personal Account Dealing Notice

All PA Dealing is subject to the conditions and procedures outlined in the PAD Notice, a copy of which is given to all new employees or Members on commencement of service. The notice must be signed, dated and returned to the Compliance Officer as an indication that the employee/Member has read the notice and agrees to abide by the undertakings detailed therein. The Firm’s PAD Notice is attached in Appendix C

## 16.5 Restricted List Procedure

The Restricted List contains securities of companies in which PAD is prohibited. Dealing in Restricted List securities may be prohibited because the Firm is advising or contemplating advising, or doing business with or for, a client company, because a Firm’s senior manager is on the Governing Body of that company, to avoid the appearance of a conflict of interest or for any other reason. It is the responsibility of each Member and employee to check the Restricted List before undertaking PAD. This can be obtained from the Compliance Officer.

If the Firm or members of staff have Inside Information about a security, this security should be recorded on the Insider List, rather than the Restricted List. Whereas the Restricted List is made available to all staff, the Insider List is confidential and kept by the Compliance Officer.

## 16.6 Exemptions

The Firm’s PA Dealing requirements will not apply for any personal transactions:

 effected under a discretionary portfolio management service where there is no prior communication in connection with the transaction between the portfolio manager and the relevant person or other person for whose account the transaction is executed;  in undertakings in UCITS (or in AIFs that are subject to supervision under the law of the UK which requires an equivalent level of risk spreading in their assets) where the relevant person and any other person for whose account the transactions are effected are not involved in the management of that undertaking;  in undertakings in UCITS that comply with the UCITS Directive (or are subject to EEA law that requires an equivalent level of risk spreading in their assets);  personal transactions in life policies

## 16.7 Successive Personal Transactions

Where successive personal transactions are carried out on behalf of a person in accordance with prior instructions given by that person, the obligations under this chapter do not apply:

 separately to each successive transaction if those instructions remain in force and unchanged; or  to the termination or withdrawal of such instructions, provided that any financial instruments which had previously been acquired pursuant to the instructions are not disposed of at the same time as the instructions terminate or are withdrawn.

Obligations under this chapter do apply in relation to a personal transaction, or the commencement of successive personal transactions, that are carried out on behalf of the same person if those instructions are changed or if new instructions are issued.

## 16.8 Other Considerations

Notwithstanding the Firm’s ‘Personal Account Dealing’ Policy (as set out in this chapter) all Staff are reminded:

Dealings for Customers Dealings on behalf of customers must always have priority over dealings by staff on their own account or on behalf of Alamut.

Dealing through Other Persons Staff must not try to avoid the rules by putting their deals through the names of other persons whether connected to them or not. Therefore, if staff are precluded from entering into a transaction for their own account, he/she must not procure any other person to enter into such a transaction. An attempt to do so may be a criminal offence.

Availability of Funds Staff must not undertake transactions in securities unless they have sufficient funds available for settlement.

Short Term Dealings

If it is anticipated that dealing is being undertaken on considerations of a short-term nature (speculative); reference should be made to the Compliance Officer. Short-term is presumed to be a holding period of less than 30 days. Hence staff will generally be encouraged to hold investments for longer than 30 days.

Credit and Special Dealing Facilities Staff must not request or accept any credit or special dealing facilities without the specific prior consent of the Firm. If appropriate, staff are required to inform their broker of their employment with a regulated firm and provide copy of such notification to the Compliance Officer.

## 16.9 Conflicts of Interest: Investment Analysts

Staff who undertake investment analysis are not permitted to PA deal in sectors which they cover as analysts, or have knowledge of, without prior, and exceptional, clearance from the Compliance Officer. Under no circumstances will PA dealing be allowed in respect of investment research which is not publicly available until the recipients of the investment research have had a reasonable opportunity to act upon it. For this reason, Alamut requires that analysts must have 7-10 business days clearance between the date of publication of research published by Alamut, and any approved PA dealing request. Exceptions may be made – subject to prior approval by the Compliance Officer - in circumstances related to financial hardship. See COBS 12.2.21 for further guidance.

## 16.10 Remuneration Code

Staff are reminded that they are not allowed to use personal hedging strategies or insurance based products that would undermine the risk alignment effects embedded in the Firm’s remuneration arrangements.

## 16.11 Record Keeping

The Compliance Officer maintains a record of all PADs for a period of at least five years following the date of the trade.

sections.content: The Compliance Officer maintains a record of all PADs for a period of at least five years following the date of the trade.

chapters.title: CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – REPORTING TO CLIENTS
chapters.summary: 17. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – REPORTING TO CLIENTS
chapters.content:
17. CONDUCT OF BUSINESS – REPORTING TO CLIENTS

## 17.1 Introduction

Alamut must ensure that Clients receive adequate reports on the services provided to them. These include, where applicable, additional reporting where a retail client’s portfolio depreciates by 10% in accordance with COBS 16A (although until 31 December 2022 the FCA will exercise ‘forbearance’ subject to certain conditions – see ‘Useful Links’).

## 17.2 Occasional Reporting

Should the Firm carry out an order on behalf of a client, excluding when managing investments, then details should be sent to the client in accordance with COBS 16.3A. The information to be reported is set out in COBS 16A.3.1. Note that the prescriptive information therein is only obligatory for retail clients – for professional clients the requirement is to provide “essential information”.

## 17.3 Periodic Reporting

When managing investments for a client the client must be provided with a periodic statement. For retail clients this periodic statement must be provided every three month, save for when:

 the management agreement authorises a leveraged portfolio when the periodicity is at least once a month; or  the Firm provides clients with online access which allows the client to access the required information and that there is evidence that the client has done so at least once during the relevant quarter.

Note that the prescriptive content within a periodic report (COBS 16A.4.1) is only applicable for retail clients.

Where relevant, a 12-month periodicity is permitted where a client has elected to receive information on a transaction-by-transaction basis (COBS 16A.4.1).

Note that additional requirements apply in respect of retail clients – see ‘Suitability reports and Periodic reports’ within the ‘Conduct of Business – Suitability’ chapter in this Compliance Manual.

## 17.4 Information to be Provided

The need to provide periodic statements applies to both retail and professional clients. However the prescriptive content of a periodic statement (COBS 16A.4.1), is only applicable to retail clients and is as follows (as is relevant):

 the name of the investment firm;  the name or other designation of the client’s account;  a statement of the contents and the valuation of the portfolio, including details of each financial instrument held, its market value, or fair value if market value is unavailable and the cash balance at the beginning and at the end of the reporting period, and the performance of the portfolio during the reporting period;  the total amount of fees and charges incurred during the reporting period, itemising at least total management fees and total costs associated with execution, and including, where relevant, a statement that a more detailed breakdown will be provided on request;  a comparison of performance during the period covered by the statement with the investment performance benchmark (if any) agreed between the investment firm and the client;  the total amount of dividends, interest and other payments received during the reporting period in relation to the client’s portfolio;  information about other corporate actions giving rights in relation to financial instruments held in the portfolio;  for each transaction executed during the period, the information referred to in Article 59(4)(c) to (l) where relevant (see ‘Occasional reporting’ above and COBS 16A.3), unless the client elects to receive information about executed transactions on a transaction-by-transaction basis.

## 17.5 Additional Reporting (Portfolio Depreciation)

When managing investments, a retail client must be informed where the overall value of the portfolio, as determined at the beginning of each reporting period, depreciates by 10% and thereafter in multiples of 10%, no later than the end of the business day in which the threshold is exceeded (or by close of the next business day where the threshold is exceeded of a non-business day). However note that until 31 December 2022 the FCA will exercise ‘forbearance’ with regard to this obligation subject to certain conditions – see ‘Useful Links’.

In the case of retail client accounts that include positions in leveraged financial instruments or contingent liability transactions (being one that involves any actual or potential client liability that exceeds the cost of acquiring the instrument) must inform the client when each such instrument depreciates by 10%, and thereafter in multiples of 10% no later than the end of the business day in which the threshold is exceeded (where that day is a non-business day then by close of next business day). Note that unless agreed otherwise with the client, the reporting threshold is on an instrument-by-instrument basis.

## 17.6 Client Assets and Client Money

A firm holding client assets and/or client money must send a statement to the client, at least quarterly (or more frequently on a commercial-cost basis), the information specified in COBS 16A.5 (which can be combined, where relevant, with the periodic statement relating to portfolio management.

## 17.7 Useful Links

COBS 16A https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/COBS/16A/1.html?timeline=True

Delegated Regulation 2017/565 (MIFID ORG REGULATION) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0565&from=EN

and in particular

Reporting re orders other than portfolio management (‘occasional reporting’) Article 59 (see also Article 61 re ECPs)

Periodic Reporting re portfolio management Article 60 in general Article 60(3)(a) where client has access to an online system Article 60(3)(b) & 60(4) when client has elected for ‘transaction by transaction’ reporting Paragraph immediately following Article 60(3)(c) when 12-month reporting cannot be applied Article 60(3)(c) re leverage portfolios

Additional reporting re ‘10% depreciation’ – FCA forbearance https://www.fca.org.uk/news/statements/10-per-cent-depreciation-notifications-further-extension- temporary-measures Statements of client funds/instruments Article 63

UK version of MiFID Org Regulation https://www.legislation.gov.uk/eur/2017/565/contents

chapters.title: CLIENT MONEY/ASSETS
chapters.summary: 18. CLIENT MONEY/ASSETS
chapters.content:
18. CLIENT MONEY/ASSETS

## 18.1 Introduction

Acronym is permitted to control – but is not allowed to hold - client money and client assets. Any instance of such an event happening must be reported to the Compliance Officer, or in their absence a senior manager, without delay with the monies and/or assets being returned to the client or their agent as appropriate.

Definition of Client Money Although we are not permitted to hold client money or client assets it is important for all to understand what constitutes ‘client money’ and ‘client assets’ and what exemptions are available even if not relevant to our business model. Client money means any money that a firm receives from or holds for, or on behalf of, a client in the course of, or in connection with, its MiFID business and/or its designated investment business that is not MiFID business in respect of any investment agreement. There are a number of exceptions (as in money or an asset not being deemed to be client money or client assets) including delivery versus payment (‘DVP’) and money due and payable to the firm and use of title transfer collateral arrangements (‘TTCA’). Use of DVP and TTCA are restricted in nature and care should be taken to ensure systems and controls are followed robustly in order to rely on the exceptions available. DVP can be used in relation to (a) regulated collective investment schemes’ transactions and (b) transactions through commercial settlement systems (if a direct member or participant). See CASS7.11.14

- 24 for further details. The use of such an exemption is subject to obtaining the client’s written agreement.

The TTCA exemption is not permitted in respect of retail clients. Where the exemption can be used then there must be an appropriate written agreement in place. See CASS 7.11.1 – 13 (client money) and CASS 6.1 (client assets) for further details.

Money that is properly due and payable to the firm for its own account is not client money. See CASS 7.11.25 onwards for further details. Client money which has remained unclaimed for at least six years, can be paid to a registered charity of choice, so relieving the firm of any further client money obligations in respect of that money, however the firm must undertake to pay the client a sum equal to the balance paid to the charity in the event of any client claim in the future. Having said that, where the amount concerned is a de minimis of £100 (or £25 in the case of a retail client) there is no obligation should a future claim arise. Further details will be found in CASS 7.11.48 – 58. There are similar rules relating to unclaimed safe custody assets except that the period here is 12 years as opposed to six years for client money and there is no ‘de minimis’ concept. See CASS 6.2.8 – 16. Should we enter into any arrangement under which commission is rebated to a client then those rebates will not be client money until it becomes due and payable to said client. There is no obligation to pay the client any interest earned on client money – see CASS 7.11.32 - 33 A professional client can opt out of the client money rules, subject to meeting the requirements of CASS 7.10.10 – but only in respect of money held in connection with non-MiFID business (CASS 7.10.8) and nor does it apply to insurance mediation activity (CASS 7.10.11).

Definition of Client Assets As for client assets, the FCA Handbook uses the term ‘safe custody asset’. Whilst the definition of the latter depends upon whether it is relation to MiFID business or non-MiFID business, in essence it means any investment which is not the property of the firm. Where relevant, firms should also be aware of the rules for collateral (CASS 3) including the requirements to act in the client’s best interest when receiving or holding assets to secure the obligation of a client (where certain rights are given to use the asset e.g. ISDA transfer of title). Note that there are similar custody rules concerning DVP and TTCA – see CASS 6.1.12 and CASS 6.1.6 – 6.1.9. The custody rules do not apply where a firm temporarily handles a safe custody asset belonging to a client (CASS 6.1.15 – 16).

## 18.2 Controlling Client Money/Assets (Mandates) (CASS 8)

A ‘mandate’ is any means that gives a firm the ability to control a client’s assets or liabilities with the client’s consent e.g. it would allow a firm to give instructions to a client’s bank in respect of the client’s bank account. A mandate need not be in writing and can even include an authority provided by a client orally (CASS 8.2.2). Note that a ‘mandate’ means that the other party e.g. client’s bank remains responsible to the client – if the firm was responsible to the client then this would not be a ‘mandate’ but rather ‘client money/assets’ and the firm would be subject to the requirements of CASS 7 (client money rules) and/or CASS 6 (custody rules).

Recording Mandates Firms are not required to separately document non-written mandates from written mandates. However, specific information about each non-written mandate will need to be recorded in the firm’s list of mandates and the firm will need to establish systems and controls to ensure that all such information is captured. A firm’s records and internal controls must include: 1) An up-to-date list of each mandate that the firm has obtained, including a record of any conditions placed by the client or the firm's management on the use of the mandate and, where a mandate was received in non-written form in the course of its designated investment business, the details required under CASS 8.3.2C 2) A record of each transaction entered into under each mandate that the firm has; 3) Internal controls to ensure that each transaction entered into under each mandate that the firm has is carried out in accordance with any conditions placed by the client or the firm's management on the use of the mandate; 4) The details of the procedures and internal controls around the giving of instructions under the mandates that the firm has (such instructions being those referred to in CASS 8.2.1R (4)); and 5) Where the firm holds a passbook or similar documents belonging to the client, internal controls for the safeguarding (including against loss, unauthorised destruction, theft, fraud or misuse) of any passbook or similar document belonging to the client held by the firm. The up-to-date list of mandates above must be maintained in a manner that allows access to the FCA or by the firm’s auditors. The list referred to in 1) above needs to have a new entry created each time a new mandate is obtained – CASS 8.3.2F. Should a mandate be in a non-written form e.g. in a telephone call then in addition to the above the following details must also be captured: nature of the mandate (e.g. debit/credit card details), purpose (link to activity e.g. collection of premiums), how mandate instructed (verbal, written) alongside the details of the client and date mandate obtained (CASS 8.3.2C). Records need to be retained for a minimum of five years (for MiFID business, otherwise the minimum period is one year) after a particular mandate is ceased.

## 18.3 Arranging Safeguarding

Although Acronym does not safeguard or administer assets, it can make arrangements for the safeguarding of client assets (making such arrangements is also a regulated activity) with a suitable third party and so we are obliged to ensure adequate protection for client assets. For the record, simply introducing a client to another firm that has permission to safeguard and administer clients’ assets will not be a regulated activity provided that the other firm is in the UK, is not a group entity and that we are not remunerated for such introductions. All due skill, care and diligence in the selection, appointment and periodic review of the selected third- party and of the arrangements for the holding and safekeeping of those assets. CASS 6.3 outlines the minimum due diligence expected, including the restrictions and requirements on the use of third-country custodians and in the event the selected third-party custodian delegates any of its safekeeping functions. A record must be made:  As to the rationale for selection of the third-party – such record to be made on the date that the selection, or appointment, is made

 Of each periodic review of the selection and appointment of the third-party and our conclusion – such record to be made on the date of that conclusion Each of the above records must be retained for 5 years after we cease to use that third party Acronym is required to have a written agreement in place with such a ‘third-party custodian’ which at a minimum must:  Set out the binding terms of the arrangement between the firm and the third party  Be in force for the duration of that arrangement  Clearly set out the custody service(s) that the third party is contracted to provide CASS 6.3.4B provides guidance of issues that should be addressed in these arrangements. In contrast to the above, ‘proper records’ (CASS 6.1.16K) of the arrangements between Acronym and the third-party custodian should be maintained for a period of 5 years after they are made.

## 18.4 Auditors Report

The firm’s auditor is required to submit a client assets report addressed to the FCA stating whether anything has come to their attention that causes them to believe that Acronym held client money or custody assets during the period covered by the report (SUP 3.10.4).

## 18.5 Useful Links

CASS https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/CASS/1/?view=chapter

chapters.title: COMPETENCE: FITNESS and PROPRIETY
chapters.summary: 19. COMPETENCE: FITNESS and PROPRIETY
chapters.content:
19. COMPETENCE: FITNESS and PROPRIETY

## 19.1 Purpose

The firm is subject to the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR) which requires the firm to ensure the fitness and propriety of all personnel subject to the regime (see below), both before appointing a person to their role and whilst continuing in their role. In addition SYSC 5.1.5AA requires that persons giving investment advice or information about financial instruments or investment or ancillary services to clients (i.e. ‘MiFID business’), regardless of categorisation, to possess the necessary knowledge and competence to fulfil investor protection obligations.

## 19.2 The Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR)

The SMCR essentially consists of two regimes:  Senior Managers regime (persons performing such roles will need FCA approval)  Certification regime (persons performing such roles will not need FCA approval) In assessing the fitness and propriety of an individual under either regime the following factors, based upon FIT 1.3.1B, will be taken into account:  Honesty, integrity and reputation  Competence and capability  Financial soundness FIT 2.1 – FIT 2.3 provides guidance on determining the above factors in relation to an individual. Note that as a firm subject to MiFID requirements due regard will also be given to the ESMA Guidelines on assessing knowledge and competence (see ‘Assessing competence – MiFID requirements’ below). In addition to the above factors, when assessing if a person is fit and proper the firm will have regard to whether that person:  has obtained a qualification  has undergone, or is undergoing, training  possesses a level of competence or  has the personal characteristics required by general rules made by the FCA. The Training and Competence (TC) sourcebook also sets out guidance to firms in assessing the competence of individuals to perform certain customer-related functions. Even though the detailed requirements of TC do not apply (because Alamut does not have any retail clients), SYSC 5.1.4A suggests that firms may nevertheless wish to take the training and competence sourcebook into account when complying with the training and competence requirements in SYSC.

## 19.3 Senior Managers regime

In contrast to the Certification regime (see below), persons undertaking a ‘Senior Management Function’ (’SMF’) – the Compliance Oversight function (SMF16) is an example - require approval from the FCA before undertaking such a role. Although the FCA approves these roles it remains the responsibility of the firm to ensure that these persons are, and remain, ‘fit and proper’ in accordance with FIT (see above re ‘FIT factors’). The effect of FSMA s63(2A) is that all those persons holding an SMF role will need to be assessed by the firm (“at least once a year”) as to their continuing fitness and propriety for the role. Further information on the Senior Managers Regime, including the Senior Management Functions relevant to this firm, can be found in the ‘Introduction’ chapter of this manual.

## 19.4 Certification regime

The Certification regime captures those individuals who are not Senior Managers but whose roles could have a significant impact upon customers, the firm or market integrity (‘FCA certification functions’) e.g. ‘client-dealing’. Whilst persons performing these functions do not require approval from the FCA, the firm remains responsible for ensuring that these persons are, and remain, ‘fit and proper’ in accordance with FIT (see above re ‘FIT factors’) which includes ‘certification’.

Persons for these functions will need to be issued with a ‘certificate’ by the firm before assuming the role – the certificate is valid for a maximum period of 12 months after which it must be renewed if the person is to continue in that role. The certificate that is issued will:  State that the firm is satisfied that the person is fit and proper to perform the function to which the certificate relates  Set out the aspects of the firm’s affairs in which the person will be involved in performing the function Further information on the Certification regime can be found in the ‘Introduction’ chapter of this manual.

## 19.5 Persons outside of the SMCR

Whilst not all persons working for the firm may fall within either the Senior Managers regime or the Certification regime, SYSC 5.1 imposes a general requirement on firms to ensure that all personnel have the necessary skills, knowledge and expertise required to enable the firm to satisfy itself as to the suitability of anyone acting for the firm (‘competent employees rule’).

## 19.6 Code of Conduct

The Handbook contains the ‘Code of Conduct’ (COCON) which applies to:  persons subject to the SMCR e.g. Senior Management or Certification Functions and  to certain employees that fall outside the SMCR. The only employees not subject to COCON would be e.g. post room staff, receptionists etc. (see COCON 1.1.2). Those persons subject to COCON are referred to as ‘conduct rules staff’. Adherence to the Code of Conduct will be taken into account when assessing a person’s fitness and propriety – and indeed breaches of the Code may lead to disciplinary action being taken against that individual and consequent notification to the FCA about that person’s behaviour (SUP 15.11). Notifications in respect of SMF managers must be made to the FCA within seven business days of the firm becoming aware of the matter via Form D or, if the individual is ceasing to perform a controlled function, Form C (SUP 10C Annex 2). Notifications in respect of conduct rules staff other than SMF managers must be made annually, covering the 12 month period ending on the last day of August and submitted to the FCA within two months of the end of that reporting period (REP008 - Form H).

## 19.7 Assessing competence – MiFID requirements

ESMA has produced Guidelines (see ‘Useful Links’) on the assessment of knowledge and competence required under MiFID which establishes minimum standards. The Guidelines, which remain valid post Brexit, address both the criteria for knowledge and competence for staff giving information about investment products and services and criteria for knowledge and competence for staff giving investment advice. In addition the Guidelines set out the organisational requirements for the assessment, maintenance and updating of knowledge and competence. This includes relevant staff being assessed through the successful completion of “an appropriate qualification” and having gained “appropriate experience” in the provision of relevant services. “Appropriate qualification” is a qualification (or other test or training course) which meets the criteria in the Guidelines. “Appropriate experience” means a staff member having demonstrated the ability to perform the relevant services through previous work for a period of at least six months (on a full time equivalent basis). Staff that have not acquired the necessary knowledge and competence cannot provide the relevant services – although they will be permitted to do so in under supervision. Anyone supervising other staff members must have the necessary knowledge and competence and the necessary skills and resources to act as a competent advisor. Any staff member who has not acquired the necessary knowledge or competence in the provision of the relevant services cannot provide those services under supervision for a period exceeding four years (or shorter period if required by the Regulator). It is a requirement that we carry out an internal or external review, at least annually, of staff members’ development and experience needs, assess regulatory developments and take any necessary action to

comply with the requirements. This review should also ensure that staff possess an appropriate qualification and undertake appropriate continuous professional development. We must be able to provide the Regulator, on request, all records concerning knowledge and competence with sufficient information to allow the Regulator to assess and verify compliance with these Guidelines. The Guidelines provides examples of activities which would not fall within the scope of the Guidelines.

## 19.8 Recruitment

When an individual is recruited by the firm it is important that they have the necessary knowledge and skills and experience required for the role. In the case of persons intended to fulfil roles subject to either the Senior Managers regime or the Certification regime, steps will be taken to obtain information about the potential employee’s previous relevant activities and training (‘regulatory references’). SYSC 22 Annexes 1 and 2 provide more information on regulatory references, including an appropriate template. Evidence of industry qualifications and examination passes should be obtained together with confirmation from the previous employer of their training status – Trainee or Competent. In addition to ‘fitness and propriety’ needs, in the case of certification function roles the information so gathered will assist the firm when issuing a ‘certificate’. To assist with the Training & Competence requirements, staff are required to maintain a record of how they have maintained their ongoing competence. This will typically include a record of seminars and training courses attended (in-house and external) further qualifications, relevant study, reading and research.

## 19.9 Record Keeping

There is no express time limit set in the Handbook for the retention of records relating to the assessment of an Individual (FIT Sch 1; SYSC 22.9.2).

## 19.10 Useful Links

ESMA Guidelines for the assessment of knowledge and competence https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/esma71-1154262120- 153_guidelines_for_the_assessment_of_knowledge_and_competence_corrigendum.pdf

SYSC 5 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/SYSC/5/1.html

TC https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/TC/1/?view=chapter

sections.title: Purpose
sections.content: The firm is subject to the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR) which requires the firm to ensure the fitness and propriety of all personnel subject to the regime (see below), both before appointing a person to their role and whilst continuing in their role. In addition SYSC 5.1.5AA requires that persons giving investment advice or information about financial instruments or investment or ancillary services to clients (i.e. ‘MiFID business’), regardless of categorisation, to possess the necessary knowledge and competence to fulfil investor protection obligations.

sections.title: The Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR)
sections.content: The SMCR essentially consists of two regimes:  Senior Managers regime (persons performing such roles will need FCA approval)  Certification regime (persons performing such roles will not need FCA approval) In assessing the fitness and propriety of an individual under either regime the following factors, based upon FIT 1.3.1B, will be taken into account:  Honesty, integrity and reputation  Competence and capability  Financial soundness FIT 2.1 – FIT 2.3 provides guidance on determining the above factors in relation to an individual. Note that as a firm subject to MiFID requirements due regard will also be given to the ESMA Guidelines on assessing knowledge and competence (see ‘Assessing competence – MiFID requirements’ below). In addition to the above factors, when assessing if a person is fit and proper the firm will have regard to whether that person:  has obtained a qualification  has undergone, or is undergoing, training  possesses a level of competence or  has the personal characteristics required by general rules made by the FCA. The Training and Competence (TC) sourcebook also sets out guidance to firms in assessing the competence of individuals to perform certain customer-related functions. Even though the detailed requirements of TC do not apply (because Alamut does not have any retail clients), SYSC 5.1.4A suggests that firms may nevertheless wish to take the training and competence sourcebook into account when complying with the training and competence requirements in SYSC.

sections.title: Senior Managers regime
sections.content: In contrast to the Certification regime (see below), persons undertaking a ‘Senior Management Function’ (’SMF’) – the Compliance Oversight function (SMF16) is an example - require approval from the FCA before undertaking such a role. Although the FCA approves these roles it remains the responsibility of the firm to ensure that these persons are, and remain, ‘fit and proper’ in accordance with FIT (see above re ‘FIT factors’). The effect of FSMA s63(2A) is that all those persons holding an SMF role will need to be assessed by the firm (“at least once a year”) as to their continuing fitness and propriety for the role. Further information on the Senior Managers Regime, including the Senior Management Functions relevant to this firm, can be found in the ‘Introduction’ chapter of this manual.

chapters.title: REPORTING TO AND NOTIFYING THE FCA (& other bodies, as required)
chapters.summary: 20. REPORTING TO AND NOTIFYING THE FCA (& other bodies, as required)
chapters.content:
20. REPORTING TO AND NOTIFYING THE FCA (& other bodies, as required)

## 20.1 REGDATA

Most reports are submitted electronically via the FCA’s REGDATA. Although it provides a schedule of returns to be submitted, due date etc. it remains the responsibility of all authorised firms to ensure that all required reports are submitted in a timely manner.

## 20.2 Reports

The most common types of report are set out below.

## 20.3 Annual Controller’s Report (REP002)

The Firm is required to keep the FCA informed about the identity of its controllers.

A controller’s report must be submitted, via REGDATA, to the FCA annually, within four months of the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date (financial year end).

Notwithstanding this annual reporting requirement, any significant change in control during the year will require FCA notification and possibly prior FCA clearance (see below).

What is a controller? A controller is a person who:

 Holds 20% or more of the shares or voting power in the firm or a parent undertaking; or  Is able to exercise significant influence over the management of the firm through his shareholding in the firm or a parent undertaking;

Furthermore, for the purposes of calculating the above criteria levels, a person’s shares or voting power is deemed to include the votes or voting powers of another person combined if they are acting in concert. There are however some limited circumstances where shares held by a person are disregarded as counting towards the thresholds above – see definition of ‘controller’ in the Handbook Glossary.

Change of control A ‘controller’ is a person holding 20% or more the shares or voting power in the Firm (or is able to exercise significant influence over the Firm). As such, a ‘change of control’ occurs at the 20% or above level (acquiring control) or reducing below the 20% level.

Note that acquiring control requires FCA approval. Ceasing control does not require FCA approval but does require prior notification to the FCA.

There are notification obligations upon both the target firm and the controller/proposed controller. SUP 11.5.8G permits a joint notification.

See ‘Useful Links’ below for the FCA Reference Guidance re Change in Control.

Involving Compliance and FCA Any proposed change of Controller must be notified in advance to the Compliance Officer. The Firm is required to be open and co-operative with the FCA – the FCA should be involved as early as possible in any proposed change of Controller. The Compliance Officer will monitor any changes of Controller.

Giving Notice to the FCA Notification to the FCA must be made as soon as the Firm becomes aware that a person has decided to acquire control or increase/reduce control.

If the change in control takes place without the knowledge of the Firm then the notification required by the FCA must be made within 14 days of becoming aware of the change in control.

In addition to the above, the FCA requires notification when a potential change in control that has previously been notified to it has taken place or when having grounds for reasonably believing that the event will not now take place. Such notification must be made within 14 days of the change of control or having the grounds as applicable.

Controller’s Circumstances The Firm must advise the FCA, as required under SUP 11.8:

 If the Controller has been subject to any legal action or investigation which might put their integrity in question;  There has been significant deterioration in the financial position of the Controller;  There has been a substantial change or series of changes in the Controller’s governing body; or  A regulator in another EEA state has ceased to authorise the Controller.

## 20.4 Annual Close Links report (REP001)

If a firm has close links with another person, the FCA must be satisfied that those Close Links are not likely to prevent the FCA's effective supervision of the firm. A close links report must be submitted, via REGDATA, to the FCA annually, within four months of the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date (financial year end). An organisation chart must be submitted unless no close links exist. Firms have the option to elect for monthly reporting (SUP 11.9.5A), but ordinarily this will only be appropriate for firms forming parts of large groups.

The Firm must notify the FCA “as soon as reasonably practicable and no later than one month after it becomes aware” that it has become, or ceased to be, closely linked with any person (SUP 11.9.4A). If a ‘change in a close link’ also involves a ‘change of control’ then prior approval may be required – see above section on ‘Change of control’.

What is a close link? A close link is the relationship between a person ("A") and another person ("CL") which exists if:

 CL is a parent undertaking of A;  CL is a subsidiary undertaking of A;  CL is a parent undertaking of a subsidiary undertaking of A;  CL is a subsidiary undertaking of a parent undertaking of A;  CL owns or controls 20% or more of the voting rights or capital of A; or  A owns or controls 20% or more of the voting rights or capital of CL.

## 20.5 Annual Report and Accounts

The Firm’s annual report and accounts are required within 80 business days of Alamut’s accounting reference date. The accounts are to be uploaded to REGDATA via FIN-A.

## 20.6 Financial Crime Return (REP-CRIM)

This return helps the FCA to understand the financial crime risks faced by firms and their management of those risks, although not all firms have to complete this return. For some types of firm it is compulsory e.g. a UK bank or a firm that manages investments whilst for other firms it will only be a consideration for firms that have reported revenue of at least £5m at the last Accounting Reference Date e.g. firms that only ‘advise’ or ‘arrange’ – see SUP 16.23.2 for further details. The return must be submitted within 60 business days of the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date.

## 20.7 Client Money and Assets Return (CMAR)

As a CASS medium firm/CASS large firm Alamut is required to submit a completed CMAR to the FCA via REGDATA (SUP 16.14).

## 20.8 Complaints

The ‘complaints reporting rules’ in DISP 1.10 require a twice-yearly report to be provided to the FCA concerning complaints received from eligible complainants. By virtue of DISP 1.1A.39 they also apply to MiFID complaints, except that information must be included about such complaints received from retail clients, professional clients and, where relevant, eligible counterparties rather than eligible complainants.

Reports must be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods.

The relevant reporting periods are:

 The six months immediately following the Firm’s Accounting References Date; and  The six months immediately preceding the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date.

## 20.9 Notifications to the FCA

Principle 11 comprises a requirement for the Firm to disclose to the FCA anything relating to the Firm of which the FCA would reasonably expect notice. Various chapters in the FCA Handbook also contain specific notification requirements for FCA. The following list is not exhaustive and staff members should refer to the Compliance Officer if in any doubt.

What Notifications should be made to the FCA A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that any of the following has or may occur in the foreseeable future (‘matters having a serious regulatory impact’):

 If Alamut fails to satisfy one or more of the threshold conditions;  Any matter which could have a significant adverse impact on Alamut’s reputation;  Any matter which could affect Alamut’s ability to continue to provide adequate services to its customers and which could result in a serious detriment to a customer; and  Any matter in respect of the firm, which could result in serious financial consequences to the financial system or to other firms.

Aside from the above scenarios which require immediate notification, that notifications of other events should be made is not explicitly prescribed in the FCA Handbook and is left to Alamut’s discretion. However, the FCA has issued guidance as to what such notifications may comprise. These include, for example, any significant failure in Alamut’s systems and controls, including those reported to the Firm by the Firm's auditor, any proposed restructuring, reorganisation or business expansion which could have a significant impact on Alamut’s risk profile or resources, or any action which Alamut proposes to take which would result in a material change in its capital adequacy or insolvency.

SUP 15.3 provides guidance on other Notifiable Events.

The FCA expects a firm to discuss relevant matters with it at an early stage, before making any internal or external commitments. The important notifications are as follows:

Breaches of rules and other requirements in or under FSMA A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that the following has or may occur (this is a rule and not guidance):

 A significant breach of a rule (which includes a Principle) or Statement of Principle;  A breach of any requirement imposed by the Act or by regulations or an order made under the Act by the Treasury;  The bringing of a prosecution for, or a conviction of, any offence under the Act;  A breach of any on-shored regulations which were previously European regulations e.g. MiFIR etc.;

by (or against) the firm or any of its directors/partners, officers, employees, approved persons, certification employees or appointed representatives. SUP 15.3.11 provides further details.

Any notification should include information about any circumstances relevant to the breach or offence, identification of the rule or requirement or offence, and any information, which Alamut has taken or intends to take to rectify or remedy the breach or prevent any future potential occurrence.

Civil, Criminal or Disciplinary Proceedings against a Firm A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that the following has or may occur (this is a rule and not guidance):

 Civil proceedings against Alamut, whereby the amount of the claim is significant in relation to Alamut’s financial resources or its reputation;  Any action that is brought against Alamut under section 71 or section 138D of FSMA (Action for damages);  Disciplinary measures or sanctions that have been imposed on Alamut by any statutory or regulatory authority, professional organisation or trade body (other than the FCA) or Alamut becomes aware that one of those bodies has started an investigation into its affairs; and  If Alamut is prosecuted for, or convicted of, any offence involving fraud or dishonesty, or any penalties imposed on it for tax evasion.

Notification should include details of the matter and an estimate of the likely financial consequences, if any.

Fraud, Errors and other Irregularities (provided the event is significant) A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that the following has or may occur (this is a rule and not guidance):

 If Alamut becomes aware that an employee may have committed a fraud against one of its customers;  If Alamut becomes aware that a person, whether or not employed by it, may have committed a fraud against it;  If Alamut considers that any person, whether or not employed by it, is acting with intent to commit a fraud against it;  If Alamut identifies irregularities in its accounting or other records, whether or not there is evidence of fraud; or  If Alamut suspects that one of its employees may be guilty of serious misconduct concerning his honesty or integrity and which is connected with Alamut regulated activities or ancillary activities.  SUP 15.3.18G provides guidance on ‘significant matters’.

Insolvency, bankruptcy and winding up A firm must notify the FCA immediately of any of the following events:

 If a meeting is called to consider a resolution for the winding up of Alamut or if a petition is presented for the winding up of Alamut;  If an application has been made to dissolve the Firm or to strike it off the Register of Companies;  If a presentation of a petition for the winding up of Alamut is made;  If a composition or arrangement is made, or if any proposals for the making of such composition or arrangement are made with any one or more of Alamut creditors;  If an application has been made for the appointment of an administrator or trustee in bankruptcy to Alamut;  If a receiver to Alamut has been appointed;  If an application for an interim order against Alamut under section 252 of the Insolvency Act 1986 has been made; and  Anything equivalent to the above in respect of Alamut in a jurisdiction outside the UK.

Firm Details

A firm is required to check the accuracy of its ‘firm details’ held by the FCA within 60 business days of its accounting reference date. The ‘firm details’ include e.g. its registered office, details of the Firm’s auditor, contact point for complaints etc. – SUP 16 Annex 16A provides a definitive list of ‘firm details’.

If any details are incorrect then the corrected firm details must be submitted to the FCA via Connect. However if the Firm details are correct the this must be confirmed to the FCA, again within the 60 business days deadline.

Further details can be found in SUP 16.10, including what actions to take should the FCA’s systems not be available.

Full-scope AIFMD activities As a full-scope AIFM, Alamut must notify the FCA before implementing any material changes to the conditions under which it was granted permission to manage an AIF. Changes the FCA would expect to be notified of include:

 The AIFM being appointed to manage another AIF;  The appointment of a different depositary for an AIFM we manage; and  The appointment of any new senior personnel where Alamut is not required to apply for FCA approval as a Senior Management Function.

Small AIFM activities As a small AIFM, Alamut must immediately notify the FCA if it no longer meets the AIFMD threshold exemptions and so no longer qualifies as a small AIFM. In such a scenario we must also apply to the FCA for a variation of permission to become a full-scope UK AIFM within 30 calendar days. It is also necessary to notify the FCA before we start to manage a new AIF or new investment compartment of an AIF.

RVECA/SEF activities The FCA require notification for changes to senior personnel or for any changes to the jurisdiction in which the fund is marketed or to market a new fund.

Small Registered UK AIFM As a small registered UK AIFM Alamut must immediately notify the FCA if it no longer meets the AIFMD threshold exemptions and so no longer qualifies as a small AIFM, or if it fails to meet the conditions to be a small registered AIFM. It is also necessary to notify the FCA before we start to manage a new AIF or new investment compartment of an AIF (SUP 15.3.30).

Other FCA Notifications SUP 15 sets out other events that the FCA would require notification of including, but not limited to:

Change in Name Change of Alamut’s name or any business name under which it carries out (given with reasonable advance) or any business name under which Alamut carries on a regulated or ancillary activity either from an establishment in the UK or with or for clients in the UK.

Change in Address or Telephone number Change in the Firm's principal place of business and/or telephone number in the UK including date of the change (again, given with reasonable advance notice).

Other Regulators The FCA needs to be notified immediately should Alamut become subject to, or cease to be subject to, the supervision of any overseas regulator.

Material outsourcing

Entering into, or significantly changing a material outsourcing arrangement (as defined in the Handbook Glossary).

Market abuse See SUP 15.10 concerning the reporting of suspicious transactions or orders.

## 20.10 AIFMD Specific Reporting

 The following minimum reporting applies to all AIFMs i.e. small authorised UK AIFMs and small registered UK AIFMs (reporting under AIFMD Article 3(3()(d)) and full-scope UK AIFMs (reporting under AIFMD Article 24(1)). For each AIF managed:

- main instruments in which trading;

- principal markets of which a member or where actively trades;

- principal exposures and most important concentrations.

Additional information re full-scope AIFMs includes:

 For each UK and EEA AIF managed and each AIF marketed in the EEA:

- percentage of AIF assets subject to special arrangements as a result of their illiquid nature;

- any new arrangements for managing the liquidity of the AIF;

- current risk profile and risk management systems employed to manage market risk, liquidity risk,

counterparty risk and other risks, including operational risk;

- information on the main categories of assets in which invested;

- results of required stress tests (see FUND 3.6.3(2) and FUND 3.7.5(2)(b).

 For each AIF that employs leverage on a substantial basis

- overall level of leverage;

- breakdown of leverage as a result of borrowing cash or securities and that embedded in financial

derivatives;

- extent to which AIF’s assets have been reused;

- identity of five largest sources of borrowed cash or securities and amounts of leverage from each

of those sources.

‘Substantial basis’ is defined as when the exposure of an AIF as calculated according to the commitment method (as set out in Article 8 of AIFMD Level 2 Regulation 231/2013) exceeds three times its net asset value.

Submission is by way of completion of AIF001 (AIFM-specific) and AIF002 (AIF-specific) via REGDATA as is relevant to the AIFM . However, firms have the option of submitting the data in XML format instead, using REGDATA to upload and submit the file.

See SUP 16.18, the ESMA Guidelines on reporting and FUND 3.4 for further details

## 20.11 Financial Returns

These reports are intended to help the FCA analyse a firm's financial condition and performance and to understand their business. The FCA also uses these reports to identify new developments across the financial services industry and its constituent sectors.

There are a number of these returns, although the precise returns that need to be submitted, and their frequency, depend upon a firm’s activities (‘RAG Number’ – SUP 16.12.4). The returns that need to be submitted by Alamut will be shown on REGDATA.

## 20.12 SEC: Filing of Form ADV

As an Exempt Reporting Adviser, the Firm is required to file the following items of Part 1 of Form ADV (“Form ADV”) with the SEC:

1 – Identifying Information; 2.B. –Reporting by Exempt Reporting Advisers; 3 – Form of Organization; 6 – Other Business Activities; 7 – Financial Industry Affiliations and Private Fund Reporting; 10 – Control Persons; 11 – Disclosure Information; and

Corresponding sections of Schedules A, B, C and D.

The Form ADV must be filed with the SEC electronically via the Investment Adviser Registration Depository (“IARD”).

The SEC maintains the information submitted on the ADV 1 and makes it publicly available. By accepting the ADV 1, however, the SEC does not make a finding that the information contained therein is accurate or has been completed correctly.

Amending and Updating Form ADV The Form ADV must be kept up-to-date in accordance with the instructions below and it should be noted that only the Compliance Officer or his or her designee has the authority to amend and/or update the Form ADV. The Firm must file an amendment to its Form ADV with the SEC via the IARD as follows:

1. At least annually, within ninety days of the end of the Firm’s fiscal year; and 2. More frequently, if required by the Form ADV instructions.

The Firm must file an amendment promptly for the following:

1. Any inaccuracies in Items 1 (Identification Information), 3 (Form of Organization), and 11 (Disciplinary Information); and 2. Any material inaccuracies in Item 10 (Control Persons).

The Compliance Officer will have overall responsibility for ensuring the following:

1. The Firm’s Form ADV remains accurate and complete in accordance with the requirements of the form; 2. All required amendments are made and filed on a timely basis; 3. The Firm pays applicable annual filing fees; 4. The Firm retains the Form ADV and all amendments; and 5. The Form ADV is delivered or appropriately offered.
