sections.title: REGDATA
sections.content: Most reports are submitted electronically via the FCA’s REGDATA. Although it provides a schedule of returns to be submitted, due date etc. it remains the responsibility of all authorised firms to ensure that all required reports are submitted in a timely manner.

sections.title: Reports
sections.content: The most common types of report are set out below.

sections.title: Annual Controller’s Report (REP002)
sections.content:
The Firm is required to keep the FCA informed about the identity of its controllers.

A controller’s report must be submitted, via REGDATA, to the FCA annually, within four months of the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date (financial year end).

Notwithstanding this annual reporting requirement, any significant change in control during the year will require FCA notification and possibly prior FCA clearance (see below).

What is a controller? A controller is a person who:

 Holds 20% or more of the shares or voting power in the firm or a parent undertaking; or  Is able to exercise significant influence over the management of the firm through his shareholding in the firm or a parent undertaking;

Furthermore, for the purposes of calculating the above criteria levels, a person’s shares or voting power is deemed to include the votes or voting powers of another person combined if they are acting in concert. There are however some limited circumstances where shares held by a person are disregarded as counting towards the thresholds above – see definition of ‘controller’ in the Handbook Glossary.

Change of control A ‘controller’ is a person holding 20% or more the shares or voting power in the Firm (or is able to exercise significant influence over the Firm). As such, a ‘change of control’ occurs at the 20% or above level (acquiring control) or reducing below the 20% level.

Note that acquiring control requires FCA approval. Ceasing control does not require FCA approval but does require prior notification to the FCA.

There are notification obligations upon both the target firm and the controller/proposed controller. SUP 11.5.8G permits a joint notification.

See ‘Useful Links’ below for the FCA Reference Guidance re Change in Control.

Involving Compliance and FCA Any proposed change of Controller must be notified in advance to the Compliance Officer. The Firm is required to be open and co-operative with the FCA – the FCA should be involved as early as possible in any proposed change of Controller. The Compliance Officer will monitor any changes of Controller.

Giving Notice to the FCA Notification to the FCA must be made as soon as the Firm becomes aware that a person has decided to acquire control or increase/reduce control.

If the change in control takes place without the knowledge of the Firm then the notification required by the FCA must be made within 14 days of becoming aware of the change in control.

In addition to the above, the FCA requires notification when a potential change in control that has previously been notified to it has taken place or when having grounds for reasonably believing that the event will not now take place. Such notification must be made within 14 days of the change of control or having the grounds as applicable.

Controller’s Circumstances The Firm must advise the FCA, as required under SUP 11.8:

 If the Controller has been subject to any legal action or investigation which might put their integrity in question;  There has been significant deterioration in the financial position of the Controller;  There has been a substantial change or series of changes in the Controller’s governing body; or  A regulator in another EEA state has ceased to authorise the Controller.

sections.title: Annual Close Links report (REP001)
sections.content:
If a firm has close links with another person, the FCA must be satisfied that those Close Links are not likely to prevent the FCA's effective supervision of the firm. A close links report must be submitted, via REGDATA, to the FCA annually, within four months of the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date (financial year end). An organisation chart must be submitted unless no close links exist. Firms have the option to elect for monthly reporting (SUP 11.9.5A), but ordinarily this will only be appropriate for firms forming parts of large groups.

The Firm must notify the FCA “as soon as reasonably practicable and no later than one month after it becomes aware” that it has become, or ceased to be, closely linked with any person (SUP 11.9.4A). If a ‘change in a close link’ also involves a ‘change of control’ then prior approval may be required – see above section on ‘Change of control’.

What is a close link? A close link is the relationship between a person ("A") and another person ("CL") which exists if:

 CL is a parent undertaking of A;  CL is a subsidiary undertaking of A;  CL is a parent undertaking of a subsidiary undertaking of A;  CL is a subsidiary undertaking of a parent undertaking of A;  CL owns or controls 20% or more of the voting rights or capital of A; or  A owns or controls 20% or more of the voting rights or capital of CL.

sections.title: Annual Report and Accounts
sections.content: The Firm’s annual report and accounts are required within 80 business days of Alamut’s accounting reference date. The accounts are to be uploaded to REGDATA via FIN-A.

sections.title: Financial Crime Return (REP-CRIM)
sections.content: This return helps the FCA to understand the financial crime risks faced by firms and their management of those risks, although not all firms have to complete this return. For some types of firm it is compulsory e.g. a UK bank or a firm that manages investments whilst for other firms it will only be a consideration for firms that have reported revenue of at least £5m at the last Accounting Reference Date e.g. firms that only ‘advise’ or ‘arrange’ – see SUP 16.23.2 for further details. The return must be submitted within 60 business days of the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date.

sections.title: Client Money and Assets Return (CMAR)
sections.content: As a CASS medium firm/CASS large firm Alamut is required to submit a completed CMAR to the FCA via REGDATA (SUP 16.14).

sections.title: Complaints
sections.content:
The ‘complaints reporting rules’ in DISP 1.10 require a twice-yearly report to be provided to the FCA concerning complaints received from eligible complainants. By virtue of DISP 1.1A.39 they also apply to MiFID complaints, except that information must be included about such complaints received from retail clients, professional clients and, where relevant, eligible counterparties rather than eligible complainants.

Reports must be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods.

The relevant reporting periods are:

 The six months immediately following the Firm’s Accounting References Date; and  The six months immediately preceding the Firm’s Accounting Reference Date.

sections.title: Notifications to the FCA
sections.content:
Principle 11 comprises a requirement for the Firm to disclose to the FCA anything relating to the Firm of which the FCA would reasonably expect notice. Various chapters in the FCA Handbook also contain specific notification requirements for FCA. The following list is not exhaustive and staff members should refer to the Compliance Officer if in any doubt.

What Notifications should be made to the FCA A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that any of the following has or may occur in the foreseeable future (‘matters having a serious regulatory impact’):

 If Alamut fails to satisfy one or more of the threshold conditions;  Any matter which could have a significant adverse impact on Alamut’s reputation;  Any matter which could affect Alamut’s ability to continue to provide adequate services to its customers and which could result in a serious detriment to a customer; and  Any matter in respect of the firm, which could result in serious financial consequences to the financial system or to other firms.

Aside from the above scenarios which require immediate notification, that notifications of other events should be made is not explicitly prescribed in the FCA Handbook and is left to Alamut’s discretion. However, the FCA has issued guidance as to what such notifications may comprise. These include, for example, any significant failure in Alamut’s systems and controls, including those reported to the Firm by the Firm's auditor, any proposed restructuring, reorganisation or business expansion which could have a significant impact on Alamut’s risk profile or resources, or any action which Alamut proposes to take which would result in a material change in its capital adequacy or insolvency.

SUP 15.3 provides guidance on other Notifiable Events.

The FCA expects a firm to discuss relevant matters with it at an early stage, before making any internal or external commitments. The important notifications are as follows:

Breaches of rules and other requirements in or under FSMA A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that the following has or may occur (this is a rule and not guidance):

 A significant breach of a rule (which includes a Principle) or Statement of Principle;  A breach of any requirement imposed by the Act or by regulations or an order made under the Act by the Treasury;  The bringing of a prosecution for, or a conviction of, any offence under the Act;  A breach of any on-shored regulations which were previously European regulations e.g. MiFIR etc.;

by (or against) the firm or any of its directors/partners, officers, employees, approved persons, certification employees or appointed representatives. SUP 15.3.11 provides further details.

Any notification should include information about any circumstances relevant to the breach or offence, identification of the rule or requirement or offence, and any information, which Alamut has taken or intends to take to rectify or remedy the breach or prevent any future potential occurrence.

Civil, Criminal or Disciplinary Proceedings against a Firm A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that the following has or may occur (this is a rule and not guidance):

 Civil proceedings against Alamut, whereby the amount of the claim is significant in relation to Alamut’s financial resources or its reputation;  Any action that is brought against Alamut under section 71 or section 138D of FSMA (Action for damages);  Disciplinary measures or sanctions that have been imposed on Alamut by any statutory or regulatory authority, professional organisation or trade body (other than the FCA) or Alamut becomes aware that one of those bodies has started an investigation into its affairs; and  If Alamut is prosecuted for, or convicted of, any offence involving fraud or dishonesty, or any penalties imposed on it for tax evasion.

Notification should include details of the matter and an estimate of the likely financial consequences, if any.

Fraud, Errors and other Irregularities (provided the event is significant) A firm must notify the FCA immediately if it becomes aware or has reason to believe that the following has or may occur (this is a rule and not guidance):

 If Alamut becomes aware that an employee may have committed a fraud against one of its customers;  If Alamut becomes aware that a person, whether or not employed by it, may have committed a fraud against it;  If Alamut considers that any person, whether or not employed by it, is acting with intent to commit a fraud against it;  If Alamut identifies irregularities in its accounting or other records, whether or not there is evidence of fraud; or  If Alamut suspects that one of its employees may be guilty of serious misconduct concerning his honesty or integrity and which is connected with Alamut regulated activities or ancillary activities.  SUP 15.3.18G provides guidance on ‘significant matters’.

Insolvency, bankruptcy and winding up A firm must notify the FCA immediately of any of the following events:

 If a meeting is called to consider a resolution for the winding up of Alamut or if a petition is presented for the winding up of Alamut;  If an application has been made to dissolve the Firm or to strike it off the Register of Companies;  If a presentation of a petition for the winding up of Alamut is made;  If a composition or arrangement is made, or if any proposals for the making of such composition or arrangement are made with any one or more of Alamut creditors;  If an application has been made for the appointment of an administrator or trustee in bankruptcy to Alamut;  If a receiver to Alamut has been appointed;  If an application for an interim order against Alamut under section 252 of the Insolvency Act 1986 has been made; and  Anything equivalent to the above in respect of Alamut in a jurisdiction outside the UK.

Firm Details

A firm is required to check the accuracy of its ‘firm details’ held by the FCA within 60 business days of its accounting reference date. The ‘firm details’ include e.g. its registered office, details of the Firm’s auditor, contact point for complaints etc. – SUP 16 Annex 16A provides a definitive list of ‘firm details’.

If any details are incorrect then the corrected firm details must be submitted to the FCA via Connect. However if the Firm details are correct the this must be confirmed to the FCA, again within the 60 business days deadline.

Further details can be found in SUP 16.10, including what actions to take should the FCA’s systems not be available.

Full-scope AIFMD activities As a full-scope AIFM, Alamut must notify the FCA before implementing any material changes to the conditions under which it was granted permission to manage an AIF. Changes the FCA would expect to be notified of include:

 The AIFM being appointed to manage another AIF;  The appointment of a different depositary for an AIFM we manage; and  The appointment of any new senior personnel where Alamut is not required to apply for FCA approval as a Senior Management Function.

Small AIFM activities As a small AIFM, Alamut must immediately notify the FCA if it no longer meets the AIFMD threshold exemptions and so no longer qualifies as a small AIFM. In such a scenario we must also apply to the FCA for a variation of permission to become a full-scope UK AIFM within 30 calendar days. It is also necessary to notify the FCA before we start to manage a new AIF or new investment compartment of an AIF.

RVECA/SEF activities The FCA require notification for changes to senior personnel or for any changes to the jurisdiction in which the fund is marketed or to market a new fund.

Small Registered UK AIFM As a small registered UK AIFM Alamut must immediately notify the FCA if it no longer meets the AIFMD threshold exemptions and so no longer qualifies as a small AIFM, or if it fails to meet the conditions to be a small registered AIFM. It is also necessary to notify the FCA before we start to manage a new AIF or new investment compartment of an AIF (SUP 15.3.30).

Other FCA Notifications SUP 15 sets out other events that the FCA would require notification of including, but not limited to:

Change in Name Change of Alamut’s name or any business name under which it carries out (given with reasonable advance) or any business name under which Alamut carries on a regulated or ancillary activity either from an establishment in the UK or with or for clients in the UK.

Change in Address or Telephone number Change in the Firm's principal place of business and/or telephone number in the UK including date of the change (again, given with reasonable advance notice).

Other Regulators The FCA needs to be notified immediately should Alamut become subject to, or cease to be subject to, the supervision of any overseas regulator.

Material outsourcing

Entering into, or significantly changing a material outsourcing arrangement (as defined in the Handbook Glossary).

Market abuse See SUP 15.10 concerning the reporting of suspicious transactions or orders.

sections.title: AIFMD Specific Reporting
Financial Returns
sections.content:
These reports are intended to help the FCA analyse a firm's financial condition and performance and to understand their business. The FCA also uses these reports to identify new developments across the financial services industry and its constituent sectors.

There are a number of these returns, although the precise returns that need to be submitted, and their frequency, depend upon a firm’s activities (‘RAG Number’ – SUP 16.12.4). The returns that need to be submitted by Alamut will be shown on REGDATA.

sections.title: SEC: Filing of Form ADV
sections.content:
As an Exempt Reporting Adviser, the Firm is required to file the following items of Part 1 of Form ADV (“Form ADV”) with the SEC:

1 – Identifying Information; 2.B. –Reporting by Exempt Reporting Advisers; 3 – Form of Organization; 6 – Other Business Activities; 7 – Financial Industry Affiliations and Private Fund Reporting; 10 – Control Persons; 11 – Disclosure Information; and

Corresponding sections of Schedules A, B, C and D.

The Form ADV must be filed with the SEC electronically via the Investment Adviser Registration Depository (“IARD”).

The SEC maintains the information submitted on the ADV 1 and makes it publicly available. By accepting the ADV 1, however, the SEC does not make a finding that the information contained therein is accurate or has been completed correctly.

Amending and Updating Form ADV The Form ADV must be kept up-to-date in accordance with the instructions below and it should be noted that only the Compliance Officer or his or her designee has the authority to amend and/or update the Form ADV. The Firm must file an amendment to its Form ADV with the SEC via the IARD as follows:

1. At least annually, within ninety days of the end of the Firm’s fiscal year; and 2. More frequently, if required by the Form ADV instructions.

The Firm must file an amendment promptly for the following:

1. Any inaccuracies in Items 1 (Identification Information), 3 (Form of Organization), and 11 (Disciplinary Information); and 2. Any material inaccuracies in Item 10 (Control Persons).

The Compliance Officer will have overall responsibility for ensuring the following:

1. The Firm’s Form ADV remains accurate and complete in accordance with the requirements of the form; 2. All required amendments are made and filed on a timely basis; 3. The Firm pays applicable annual filing fees; 4. The Firm retains the Form ADV and all amendments; and 5. The Form ADV is delivered or appropriately offered.

chapters.title: DISCLOSURE AND SUSTAINABILITY
chapters.summary: 21. DISCLOSURE AND SUSTAINABILITY
chapters.content:
21. DISCLOSURE AND SUSTAINABILITY

## 21.1 Introduction

The ESG sourcebook sets out rules and guidance concerning a firm’s approach to Environmental, Social and Governance matters. Essentially it combines climate-related disclosures consistent with the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the application of the UK’s Sustainability Disclosure Requirements (SDR) and is divided into the following parts:  ESG 1: Purpose and application  ESG 1A: Application of ESG 2  ESG 2: Disclosure of climate-related financial information (TCFD))  ESG 3: Application of ESG 4 and ESG 5  ESG 4: Sustainability labelling, naming and marketing (including anti-greenwashing)  ESG 5: Disclosures of sustainability-related information The scope of the ESG regime re ESG 4 & ESG 5 currently only applies to UK funds and to UK firms – overseas funds and firms are not in scope. In contrast, whilst the scope of ESG 2 is also limited to UK firms there is no ‘UK funds’ limitation i.e. it captures a firm’s approach to managing sustainability risks and opportunities in respect of UK and overseas funds managed from the UK. ESG 1.2 contains a useful table setting out the application of ESG 2, ESG 4 and ESG 5 to various types of firms. The ESG Sourcebook makes reference to ‘asset managers’. Unlike under ESG 2, for the purposes of ESG 4 and ESG 5 this terms captures managers of UK UCITS and managers of AIFs – but not portfolio managers.

## 21.2 TCFD

The TCFD was created by the Financial Stability Board to develop recommendations on the types of information that companies should disclose to support investors, lenders, and insurance underwriters in appropriately assessing and pricing a specific set of risks—risks related to climate change. Such disclosures are at entity level and product level. The Final Report of the TCFD includes its four recommendations and eleven recommended disclosures (page 14 of the Final Report – see ‘Useful Links’).

## 21.3 Application of disclosure obligations (ESG 2)

The below table sets out the application of the ESG 2 disclosure obligations (“climate-related reports”) by type of firm (but see ESG 1.1A.1 for full details) in relation to the TCFD in-scope business carried on from an establishment in the UK (see table below). However, firms are exempt from the disclosures in ESG 2 if and for as long as the assets under management in relation to TCFD in-scope business amount to less than £5bn calculated as a 3-year rolling average on an annual assessment. The disclosure obligations under ESG2 concern a:  TCFD entity report (ESG 2.2)  TCFD product report (ESG 2.3)

Type of firm TCFD in-scope business Any firm Portfolio management UK UCITS ManCo (or the scheme itself Managing a UK UCITS if no separate ManCo) Full-scope UK AIFM Managing an AIF Small authorised UK AIFM Managing an AIF

For the purposes of ESG 2 the Handbook refers to the entities in the above table as ‘asset managers’. For the record, TCFD in-scope business can also be undertaken by ‘asset owners’, a term which includes operators of various pension schemes and firms that provide insurance-based investment products.

## 21.4 Disclosure: TCFD Entity report (ESG 2.2)

As mentioned in the ‘Introduction’ ESG 2.2 will apply to portfolio managers, managers of UK UCITS and to UK AIFMs although reference should be made to the application tables in ESG 1.2.4 and ESG 1A.1.1.

A firm that is subject to the disclosure obligations must prepare and publish its TCFD entity report (which must cover a reporting period of 12 months starting no earlier than 1 January of the previous calendar year – ESG 2.1.2) by 30 June each calendar year regarding the overall assets managed/administered by a firm in respect of its TCFD in-scope business. Although the reporting period may be changed in subsequent yearly reports, there must be no period of time after 1 January 2022 which is not covered by a firm’s TCFD entity report. ESG 2.1 and ESG 2.2 provide relevant information on the structuring of the Entity Report based upon the four recommendations (Governance, Strategy, Risk Management and Metrics and Targets) set out on page 14 of the TCFD Final Report (see ‘Useful Links’). The table therein also includes ‘recommended disclosures’ to support those four recommended areas. The TCFD Entity Report must include a statement, signed by a member of senior management of the firm, confirming that the disclosures in the report, including any third party or group disclosures cross- referenced in it, comply with the requirements under ESG 2.2.

## 21.5 Disclosure: TCFD Product report (ESG 2.3)

A TCFD product report can be either:  A public TCFD product report; or  An on-demand product report

## 21.6 TCFD Product report: Public TCFD product report

The report will apply to products such as authorised funds and closed-ended investment funds (but see ‘Useful Links’ for the full definitions of ‘Public TCFD product report’ and ‘TCFD product’. However, whilst this captures UK UCITS note from the definition of ‘Public TCFD product report’ (see ‘Useful Links’) that as far as unauthorised AIFs are concerned only those that are listed on a recognised investment exchange (including an investment trust) are subject to the Public TCFD product report obligation. A firm that is subject to this disclosure obligation must prepare and publish a Public TCFD product report by 30 June each calendar year in respect of a TCFD product. ESG 2.3 concerns the TCFD product report, including the content of such a report – see ESG 2.3.9 onwards. ESG 2.3.1 explains where the Public TCFD product report (or a prominent cross-reference and hyper link) must appear e.g. in a periodic client report. The one exception is where a firm is a UK AIFM of an unauthorised AIF listed on a recognised investment exchange in which case the report must be included in its TCFD entity report.

## 21.7 TCFD Product report: On-demand product report and underlying asset data

An ‘on-demand’ product report is in respect of either:  Investments which a firm provides portfolio management for; or  An unauthorised AIF which is not listed on a recognised investment exchange A firm must provide on-demand information “within a reasonable period of time” when such a request is received from a person entitled to make such a request under ESG 2.3.5 (being a client in e.g. an unlisted and unauthorised AIF which is not subject to the Public TCFD product report obligation but who requires such information in order to satisfy climate-related financial disclosure obligations). In addition, clients can also request underlying asset data e.g. ISINs, name, holding size etc. (“as reasonably required by the relevant person”) to allow them to produce their own amalgamated scenario analysis and otherwise satisfy their own disclosure obligations in relation to climate-related information. See ESG 2.3.9 onwards regarding the content of a TCFD product report e.g. total carbon footprint. The entitlement is limited to one request for an on-demand TCFD product report or underlying asset data or both in respect of each TCFD product in each of the firm’s reporting periods (see ESG 2.3.6 for further details).

## 21.8 Publication

Climate-related reports (i.e. the TCFD entity report and the public TCFD product report) must be published in a way that makes it easy for prospective readers to locate and access them – at a minimum by making the most recent of these reports available in a prominent place on the main website for the business of the firm.

## 21.9 Portfolio management and the application of ESG

The rest of this chapter considers obligations arising under ESG 4 (Sustainability labelling, naming and marketing) and ESG 5 (Disclosure of sustainability-related information). Whilst ‘portfolio management’ constitutes “TCFD in-scope business”, and hence is subject to the TCFD disclosure obligations (subject to the above £5bn threshold) in ESG 2, generally ESG 4 and ESG 5 do not currently apply to portfolio managers apart from ESG 4.1.1(1) (the prohibition on the use of certain sustainability labels) and ESG 4.3.1 (anti-greenwashing) both of which apply to all firms. The FCA is expected to consult on the application of the ESG disclosure regime to such firms in 2024 (PS23/16, para 2.4 refers).

## 21.10 Sustainability labels

ESG 4.1.1 lists four ‘sustainability labels’ that can be used by a firm subject to meeting both the general criteria in ESG 4.2.4 – ESG 4.2.11 and the relevant specific criteria in ESG 4.2.12 onwards. The below lists these four labels together with where the relevant specific criteria for each label can found:  Sustainability focus (ESG 4.2.13)  Sustainability improvers (ESG 4.2.14)  Sustainability impact (ESG 4.2.16)  Sustainability mixed goals (ESG 4.2.18) Note that the above does not apply to a feeder fund and instead reference should be made to ESG 4.1.2. The use of a label is subject to ongoing monitoring of the product in line with ESG 4.2.20. In addition to meeting the general criteria and the above relevant specific criteria the use of any of these labels is also subject to meeting the requirements in ESG 4.1 which include, but are not limited to:  Use of the relevant graphic prescribed by the FCA (ESG 4.1.3)  Recording the basis on which the label has been used (ESG 4.1.6)  Notifying the FCA before using, revising or ceasing the use of that label (ESG 4.1.7)  Where the product information is publicly available then additional information must be published on the firm’s website etc. (ESG 4.1.8)  Keeping the use of the label under review (ESG 4.1.11 & 12)  Notifying clients where a label has been amended or revised (ESG 4.1.13)

## 21.11 Naming & Marketing (including Anti-greenwashing)

An asset manager undertaking sustainability in-scope business (see above) for retail clients in relation to a sustainability product (see above) that uses sustainability-related terms (‘relevant terms’ – ESG 4.3.2) such as ‘green’, ‘responsible’, ‘social’, ‘impact’ etc. are subject to particular rules and requirements including information that needs to be published on the firm’s website or relevant digital medium (see ESG 4.3.4 – ESG 4.3.8). The above rules apply from when a sustainability label is first used in relation to a sustainability product or 2 December 2024. However it is permissible to use any of these ‘relevant terms’ to make short factual statements that are not financial promotions or to make statements in a context not intended to refer to or describe the sustainability characteristics of a sustainable product e.g. the use of ‘financial impact’ would be acceptable. The ‘anti-greenwashing’ rule (ESG 4.3.1) applies to all FCA authorised firms (regardless of whether the firm undertakes in-scope business or not) when:  communicating with a UK client in relation to a product or service  communicating or approving a financial promotion directed to a UK person Any reference to the environmental or social characteristics (‘sustainability characteristics’) of that product or service must be fair, clear and not misleading and be consistent with product or service in question. The rule applies from 31 May 2024 (ESG TP1(1.8)).

## 21.12 Sustainability disclosures

When undertaking sustainability in-scope business (see above) in respect of a sustainability product (see above) - excluding a feeder fund – a firm that uses either:  A sustainability label (see ‘Sustainability labels’ above); or  A sustainability-related term (see ‘Naming and Marketing including Anti-greenwashing’ above)

Must prepare:  A consumer-facing disclosure (for retail clients); and  A pre-contractual disclosure Consumer-facing disclosures Where a sustainability label is used the content will include e.g. a summary of the metrics used and the product’s sustainability objective (‘sustainability goal’) – however see ESG 5.2.2 for the full content. Further requirements such as publication of the disclosure, clarity and record retention etc. can be found in ESG 5.2.3 – ESG 5.2.10. If a sustainability label is not used but a ‘relevant term’ (see ‘Naming and Marketing (including Anti- greenwashing)’ above) is used then selected elements of ESG 5.2 are required to be disclosed as well as certain additional information based upon ESG 4.3 – see ESG 5.2.1(2). Pre-contractual disclosures Where a sustainability label is used the disclosure must include e.g. the sustainability label used, the product’s sustainability objective, the metrics used etc. – see ESG 5.3.3 and ESG 5.3.6 for further details. If a sustainability label is not used but a ‘relevant term’ (see ‘Naming and Marketing (including Anti- greenwashing)’ above) is used then selected elements of ESG 5.3.3 are required to be disclosed as well as certain additional information based upon ESG 4.3 – see ESG 5.3.2(2). Pre-contractual disclosures must be included in either:  The product’s prospectus or, if not applicable, then within the prior disclosures required under AIFMD Article 23(1) (replicated in FUND 3.2.2)) (‘pre-contractual materials’); or  Where there are no pre-contractual materials then in Part A of the product’s public-product-level sustainability report (see below). See also ESG 5.3.9 re ‘prominence’. These sustainability disclosures must be kept under review in line with ESG 5.1.2. The disclosures obligation applies from either the date on which a sustainability label is first used or 2 December 2024.

## 21.13 Sustainability reports

Product-level reports When undertaking sustainability in-scope business (see above) in respect of a sustainability product (see above) - excluding a feeder fund – a firm that uses either:  A sustainability label (see ‘Sustainability labels’ above); or  A sustainability-related term (see ‘Naming and Marketing including Anti-greenwashing’ above) Must prepare:  Part A of a public product-level sustainability report (where the product does not have ‘pre- contractual materials’ that relate to it (see ‘Pre-contractual disclosures’ above for definition) which must be made available to clients before they invest; and  Part B of a public product-level sustainability report. When a firm is required to prepare a public TCFD product report (see above) then the contents of that report, or a hyperlink to it, must be included in Part B – see ESG 5.4.4 Note that Part B is only relevant to unauthorised AIFs that are listed or to UK UCITS – it does not apply to unauthorised AIFs that are not listed. Public product-level sustainability report Where a Part A public product-level sustainability report is required i.e. where there are no ‘pre- contractual materials’ (see above) it must contain content that is based upon the pre-contractual disclosures (see above) – specifically ESG 5.3.3 and ESG 5.3.6 See ESG 5.5.1 onwards re publication and the need to keep the information under review. The content of Part B is dependent upon whether a ‘sustainability label’ is used (in which case the content is addressed in ESG 5.5.6 – ESG 5.5.11) or a ‘suitability-related term’ is used (in which case the content is as per ESG 5.5.5(2)(b)). Reference should be made to ESG 2.3.1 – ESG 2.3.4 as to publication (reading ‘public TCFD product report’ as ‘public product-level sustainability report). Part A and Part B must be kept under review in line with ESG 5.5.4 and ESG 5.5.12 respectively. On-demand product-level sustainability information On-demand requests can only be made in respect of unauthorised AIFs that are not listed (see application table in ESG 1.2.4).

A manager is required to prepare and provide ‘on-demand sustainability information’ to a person who requests it in order to satisfy sustainability-related disclosure obligations (although managers are encouraged by the FCA to consider making available this information regardless the person’s eligibility to request such information) – see 5.5.13 – ESG 5.5.16 for further details. Entity level report In addition to the above, a sustainability entity report must be prepared by a manager where the assets under management/administration in respect of its sustainability in-scope business amount to £5bn or more (as a 3-year rolling average on an annual assessment) regardless of whether it uses a sustainability label or a sustainability-related term. When a firm is required to prepare a TCFD entity report (see above) then contents of that report, or a hyperlink to it, must be included – see ESG 5.4.5. The report will include information such as the manager’s approach to governance with respect to managing sustainability risks , how sustainability-related risks are identified an managed etc. See ESG 5.6 for further details. In a similar manner that applies to a firm’s TCFD entity report (See “Disclosure: TCFD Entity report (ESG 2.2)” above) the sustainability entity report must include a statement, signed by a member of senior management, that the disclosures therein are in accordance with Handbook requirements (ESG 5.6.8). ESG 5.4 contains further information on the preparation and timing of sustainability reports.

## 21.14 Useful links

Definition: On-demand information https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/glossary/G3569o.html?date=2023-10-31

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The ESG sourcebook sets out rules and guidance concerning a firm’s approach to Environmental, Social and Governance matters. Essentially it combines climate-related disclosures consistent with the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the application of the UK’s Sustainability Disclosure Requirements (SDR) and is divided into the following parts:  ESG 1: Purpose and application  ESG 1A: Application of ESG 2  ESG 2: Disclosure of climate-related financial information (TCFD))  ESG 3: Application of ESG 4 and ESG 5  ESG 4: Sustainability labelling, naming and marketing (including anti-greenwashing)  ESG 5: Disclosures of sustainability-related information The scope of the ESG regime re ESG 4 & ESG 5 currently only applies to UK funds and to UK firms – overseas funds and firms are not in scope. In contrast, whilst the scope of ESG 2 is also limited to UK firms there is no ‘UK funds’ limitation i.e. it captures a firm’s approach to managing sustainability risks and opportunities in respect of UK and overseas funds managed from the UK. ESG 1.2 contains a useful table setting out the application of ESG 2, ESG 4 and ESG 5 to various types of firms. The ESG Sourcebook makes reference to ‘asset managers’. Unlike under ESG 2, for the purposes of ESG 4 and ESG 5 this terms captures managers of UK UCITS and managers of AIFs – but not portfolio managers.

sections.title: TCFD
sections.content: The TCFD was created by the Financial Stability Board to develop recommendations on the types of information that companies should disclose to support investors, lenders, and insurance underwriters in appropriately assessing and pricing a specific set of risks—risks related to climate change. Such disclosures are at entity level and product level. The Final Report of the TCFD includes its four recommendations and eleven recommended disclosures (page 14 of the Final Report – see ‘Useful Links’).

sections.title: Application of disclosure obligations (ESG 2)
sections.content:
The below table sets out the application of the ESG 2 disclosure obligations (“climate-related reports”) by type of firm (but see ESG 1.1A.1 for full details) in relation to the TCFD in-scope business carried on from an establishment in the UK (see table below). However, firms are exempt from the disclosures in ESG 2 if and for as long as the assets under management in relation to TCFD in-scope business amount to less than £5bn calculated as a 3-year rolling average on an annual assessment. The disclosure obligations under ESG2 concern a:  TCFD entity report (ESG 2.2)  TCFD product report (ESG 2.3)

Type of firm TCFD in-scope business Any firm Portfolio management UK UCITS ManCo (or the scheme itself Managing a UK UCITS if no separate ManCo) Full-scope UK AIFM Managing an AIF Small authorised UK AIFM Managing an AIF

For the purposes of ESG 2 the Handbook refers to the entities in the above table as ‘asset managers’. For the record, TCFD in-scope business can also be undertaken by ‘asset owners’, a term which includes operators of various pension schemes and firms that provide insurance-based investment products.

sections.title: Disclosure: TCFD Entity report (ESG 2.2)
sections.content:
As mentioned in the ‘Introduction’ ESG 2.2 will apply to portfolio managers, managers of UK UCITS and to UK AIFMs although reference should be made to the application tables in ESG 1.2.4 and ESG 1A.1.1.

A firm that is subject to the disclosure obligations must prepare and publish its TCFD entity report (which must cover a reporting period of 12 months starting no earlier than 1 January of the previous calendar year – ESG 2.1.2) by 30 June each calendar year regarding the overall assets managed/administered by a firm in respect of its TCFD in-scope business. Although the reporting period may be changed in subsequent yearly reports, there must be no period of time after 1 January 2022 which is not covered by a firm’s TCFD entity report. ESG 2.1 and ESG 2.2 provide relevant information on the structuring of the Entity Report based upon the four recommendations (Governance, Strategy, Risk Management and Metrics and Targets) set out on page 14 of the TCFD Final Report (see ‘Useful Links’). The table therein also includes ‘recommended disclosures’ to support those four recommended areas. The TCFD Entity Report must include a statement, signed by a member of senior management of the firm, confirming that the disclosures in the report, including any third party or group disclosures cross- referenced in it, comply with the requirements under ESG 2.2.

sections.title: Disclosure: TCFD Product report (ESG 2.3)
sections.content: A TCFD product report can be either:  A public TCFD product report; or  An on-demand product report

sections.title: TCFD Product report: Public TCFD product report
sections.content: The report will apply to products such as authorised funds and closed-ended investment funds (but see ‘Useful Links’ for the full definitions of ‘Public TCFD product report’ and ‘TCFD product’. However, whilst this captures UK UCITS note from the definition of ‘Public TCFD product report’ (see ‘Useful Links’) that as far as unauthorised AIFs are concerned only those that are listed on a recognised investment exchange (including an investment trust) are subject to the Public TCFD product report obligation. A firm that is subject to this disclosure obligation must prepare and publish a Public TCFD product report by 30 June each calendar year in respect of a TCFD product. ESG 2.3 concerns the TCFD product report, including the content of such a report – see ESG 2.3.9 onwards. ESG 2.3.1 explains where the Public TCFD product report (or a prominent cross-reference and hyper link) must appear e.g. in a periodic client report. The one exception is where a firm is a UK AIFM of an unauthorised AIF listed on a recognised investment exchange in which case the report must be included in its TCFD entity report.

sections.title: TCFD Product report: On-demand product report and underlying asset data
sections.content: An ‘on-demand’ product report is in respect of either:  Investments which a firm provides portfolio management for; or  An unauthorised AIF which is not listed on a recognised investment exchange A firm must provide on-demand information “within a reasonable period of time” when such a request is received from a person entitled to make such a request under ESG 2.3.5 (being a client in e.g. an unlisted and unauthorised AIF which is not subject to the Public TCFD product report obligation but who requires such information in order to satisfy climate-related financial disclosure obligations). In addition, clients can also request underlying asset data e.g. ISINs, name, holding size etc. (“as reasonably required by the relevant person”) to allow them to produce their own amalgamated scenario analysis and otherwise satisfy their own disclosure obligations in relation to climate-related information. See ESG 2.3.9 onwards regarding the content of a TCFD product report e.g. total carbon footprint. The entitlement is limited to one request for an on-demand TCFD product report or underlying asset data or both in respect of each TCFD product in each of the firm’s reporting periods (see ESG 2.3.6 for further details).

sections.title: Publication
sections.content: Climate-related reports (i.e. the TCFD entity report and the public TCFD product report) must be published in a way that makes it easy for prospective readers to locate and access them – at a minimum by making the most recent of these reports available in a prominent place on the main website for the business of the firm.

sections.title: Portfolio management and the application of ESG
sections.content: The rest of this chapter considers obligations arising under ESG 4 (Sustainability labelling, naming and marketing) and ESG 5 (Disclosure of sustainability-related information). Whilst ‘portfolio management’ constitutes “TCFD in-scope business”, and hence is subject to the TCFD disclosure obligations (subject to the above £5bn threshold) in ESG 2, generally ESG 4 and ESG 5 do not currently apply to portfolio managers apart from ESG 4.1.1(1) (the prohibition on the use of certain sustainability labels) and ESG 4.3.1 (anti-greenwashing) both of which apply to all firms. The FCA is expected to consult on the application of the ESG disclosure regime to such firms in 2024 (PS23/16, para 2.4 refers).

sections.title: Sustainability labels
sections.content: ESG 4.1.1 lists four ‘sustainability labels’ that can be used by a firm subject to meeting both the general criteria in ESG 4.2.4 – ESG 4.2.11 and the relevant specific criteria in ESG 4.2.12 onwards. The below lists these four labels together with where the relevant specific criteria for each label can found:  Sustainability focus (ESG 4.2.13)  Sustainability improvers (ESG 4.2.14)  Sustainability impact (ESG 4.2.16)  Sustainability mixed goals (ESG 4.2.18) Note that the above does not apply to a feeder fund and instead reference should be made to ESG 4.1.2. The use of a label is subject to ongoing monitoring of the product in line with ESG 4.2.20. In addition to meeting the general criteria and the above relevant specific criteria the use of any of these labels is also subject to meeting the requirements in ESG 4.1 which include, but are not limited to:  Use of the relevant graphic prescribed by the FCA (ESG 4.1.3)  Recording the basis on which the label has been used (ESG 4.1.6)  Notifying the FCA before using, revising or ceasing the use of that label (ESG 4.1.7)  Where the product information is publicly available then additional information must be published on the firm’s website etc. (ESG 4.1.8)  Keeping the use of the label under review (ESG 4.1.11 & 12)  Notifying clients where a label has been amended or revised (ESG 4.1.13)

sections.title: Naming & Marketing (including Anti-greenwashing)
sections.content: An asset manager undertaking sustainability in-scope business (see above) for retail clients in relation to a sustainability product (see above) that uses sustainability-related terms (‘relevant terms’ – ESG 4.3.2) such as ‘green’, ‘responsible’, ‘social’, ‘impact’ etc. are subject to particular rules and requirements including information that needs to be published on the firm’s website or relevant digital medium (see ESG 4.3.4 – ESG 4.3.8). The above rules apply from when a sustainability label is first used in relation to a sustainability product or 2 December 2024. However it is permissible to use any of these ‘relevant terms’ to make short factual statements that are not financial promotions or to make statements in a context not intended to refer to or describe the sustainability characteristics of a sustainable product e.g. the use of ‘financial impact’ would be acceptable. The ‘anti-greenwashing’ rule (ESG 4.3.1) applies to all FCA authorised firms (regardless of whether the firm undertakes in-scope business or not) when:  communicating with a UK client in relation to a product or service  communicating or approving a financial promotion directed to a UK person Any reference to the environmental or social characteristics (‘sustainability characteristics’) of that product or service must be fair, clear and not misleading and be consistent with product or service in question. The rule applies from 31 May 2024 (ESG TP1(1.8)).

sections.title: Sustainability disclosures
sections.content:
When undertaking sustainability in-scope business (see above) in respect of a sustainability product (see above) - excluding a feeder fund – a firm that uses either:  A sustainability label (see ‘Sustainability labels’ above); or  A sustainability-related term (see ‘Naming and Marketing including Anti-greenwashing’ above)

Must prepare:  A consumer-facing disclosure (for retail clients); and  A pre-contractual disclosure Consumer-facing disclosures Where a sustainability label is used the content will include e.g. a summary of the metrics used and the product’s sustainability objective (‘sustainability goal’) – however see ESG 5.2.2 for the full content. Further requirements such as publication of the disclosure, clarity and record retention etc. can be found in ESG 5.2.3 – ESG 5.2.10. If a sustainability label is not used but a ‘relevant term’ (see ‘Naming and Marketing (including Anti- greenwashing)’ above) is used then selected elements of ESG 5.2 are required to be disclosed as well as certain additional information based upon ESG 4.3 – see ESG 5.2.1(2). Pre-contractual disclosures Where a sustainability label is used the disclosure must include e.g. the sustainability label used, the product’s sustainability objective, the metrics used etc. – see ESG 5.3.3 and ESG 5.3.6 for further details. If a sustainability label is not used but a ‘relevant term’ (see ‘Naming and Marketing (including Anti- greenwashing)’ above) is used then selected elements of ESG 5.3.3 are required to be disclosed as well as certain additional information based upon ESG 4.3 – see ESG 5.3.2(2). Pre-contractual disclosures must be included in either:  The product’s prospectus or, if not applicable, then within the prior disclosures required under AIFMD Article 23(1) (replicated in FUND 3.2.2)) (‘pre-contractual materials’); or  Where there are no pre-contractual materials then in Part A of the product’s public-product-level sustainability report (see below). See also ESG 5.3.9 re ‘prominence’. These sustainability disclosures must be kept under review in line with ESG 5.1.2. The disclosures obligation applies from either the date on which a sustainability label is first used or 2 December 2024.

sections.title: Sustainability reports
sections.content:
Product-level reports When undertaking sustainability in-scope business (see above) in respect of a sustainability product (see above) - excluding a feeder fund – a firm that uses either:  A sustainability label (see ‘Sustainability labels’ above); or  A sustainability-related term (see ‘Naming and Marketing including Anti-greenwashing’ above) Must prepare:  Part A of a public product-level sustainability report (where the product does not have ‘pre- contractual materials’ that relate to it (see ‘Pre-contractual disclosures’ above for definition) which must be made available to clients before they invest; and  Part B of a public product-level sustainability report. When a firm is required to prepare a public TCFD product report (see above) then the contents of that report, or a hyperlink to it, must be included in Part B – see ESG 5.4.4 Note that Part B is only relevant to unauthorised AIFs that are listed or to UK UCITS – it does not apply to unauthorised AIFs that are not listed. Public product-level sustainability report Where a Part A public product-level sustainability report is required i.e. where there are no ‘pre- contractual materials’ (see above) it must contain content that is based upon the pre-contractual disclosures (see above) – specifically ESG 5.3.3 and ESG 5.3.6 See ESG 5.5.1 onwards re publication and the need to keep the information under review. The content of Part B is dependent upon whether a ‘sustainability label’ is used (in which case the content is addressed in ESG 5.5.6 – ESG 5.5.11) or a ‘suitability-related term’ is used (in which case the content is as per ESG 5.5.5(2)(b)). Reference should be made to ESG 2.3.1 – ESG 2.3.4 as to publication (reading ‘public TCFD product report’ as ‘public product-level sustainability report). Part A and Part B must be kept under review in line with ESG 5.5.4 and ESG 5.5.12 respectively. On-demand product-level sustainability information On-demand requests can only be made in respect of unauthorised AIFs that are not listed (see application table in ESG 1.2.4).

A manager is required to prepare and provide ‘on-demand sustainability information’ to a person who requests it in order to satisfy sustainability-related disclosure obligations (although managers are encouraged by the FCA to consider making available this information regardless the person’s eligibility to request such information) – see 5.5.13 – ESG 5.5.16 for further details. chapters.title: DISCLOSURE and TRANSPARENCY (including Takeover Code)
chapters.summary: 22. DISCLOSURE and TRANSPARENCY (including Takeover Code)
chapters.content:
22. DISCLOSURE and TRANSPARENCY (including Takeover Code)

## 22.1 Introduction

This chapter details the FCA’s Disclosure and Transparency Rules (“DTR”) on disclosure and transparency as well as giving consideration to the Takeover Code where relevant. In other jurisdictions there may be different disclosure requirements and separate advice should be sought. Within the EU, the Transparency (Obligations) Directive and the Takeovers Directive provide some similarity among EU members over the rules for disclosure and transparency.

Where securities are admitted to trading on any regulated market (being a UK Recognised investment Exchange) or ‘prescribed market’ (for the purposes of these Rules this will capture a market which is established under the rules of a UK Recognised Investment Exchange e.g. AIM), regardless of whether the issuer is established in the UK or not, then the FCA’s DTR rules would apply.

## 22.2 Disclosure

The disclosure rules in DTR 1, 2, 3 and 4 apply to issuers of financial instruments admitted to trading on a regulated market and therefore are not applicable to Alamut. Additional scrutiny should be given to the City Code which requires disclosure where an Issuer is party to a takeover.

Although not part of the Handbook, the FCA has endorsed the Takeover Code (“Code”). The Code is enforced by the Panel on Takeovers and Mergers (“Takeover Panel”). Disclosure is required when shares are acquired in a company subject to a takeover bid. Rule 8 of the Code (disclosure of dealings and positions) and Rule 9 (mandatory offer) are of particular relevance when stake building. Under Rule 8 disclosure must be made by a person with an interest of 1% or more in either the target company or the company making the offer. When investments are managed on a discretionary basis then it is the manager, and not the person on whose behalf the relevant securities are managed, that is subject to the disclosure requirement. Rule 9 requires that an offer be made to all shareholders when a stake reaches 30% or more of the voting rights.

Note that any reporting required under the Code will be in addition to any obligations under DTR.

## 22.3 Transparency

The transparency rules are contained within DTR 5 and include the requirement to notify the issuer of voting rights held. Note that this obligation applies to shareholders of issuers with shares that are traded on a regulated market and to shareholders of issuers whose shares are traded on a prescribed market e.g. AIM.

Certain obligations under DTR 5 apply to issuers e.g. DTR 5.6 ‘disclosure by issuers’.

A person must notify the issuer of the percentage of its voting rights (held as a shareholder or are held or are deemed to be held through direct or indirect holdings of financial instruments falling within DTR 5.3.1(1) (see also below) if the percentage of those voting rights reaches, exceeds or falls below 3% and each 1% threshold thereafter up to 100 % (although see below re managed accounts). In the case of non- UK issuers threshold starts at 5% and increases in 5% intervals up to 30% and thereafter apply at the 50% & 75% levels (DTR 5.1.2 (1)). The latter thresholds are based upon the EU Transparency Directive, as amended, whilst the former arise from the FCA applying super-equivalent rules which have continued post the Brexit transitional.

DTR 5.2 provides examples (‘cases’) where a person will be regarded as an indirect holder of shares e.g. voting rights by a person undertaking investment management and who may exercise voting rights at their discretion.

DTR 5.3 provides rules and guidance on financial instruments which on maturity give a holder, under a formal agreement, a right or discretion to acquire shares to which voting rights are attached or which are referenced to such shares and with an ‘economic effect’ similar to those said financial instruments whether or not they confer a right to a physical settlement (DTR 5.3.1(1)).

Where notification includes financial instruments within DTR 5.3.1(1) then said notification must include a breakdown by type and distinguish between those instruments that confer a right to physical settlement or cash settlement (DTR 5.3.5). Both ESMA and the FCA have published an ‘indicative list’ of financial instruments that are subject to notification requirements e.g. warrants, futures etc. which satisfy the conditions of Article 13(1) of the Transparency Directive/DTR 5.3.1 (see ‘Useful Links).

If the notification relates to shares admitted to trading on a regulated market, the person should also file a copy of notification with the FCA at the same time (DTR 5.9). Note that this could give rise to a dual notification requirement to both the FCA and to the relevant EU competent authority e.g. if shares are traded on both a UK regulated market and on an EU regulated market (notification to Home Member State is required under Article 19(3) of the Transparency Directive).

## 22.4 Voting Rights which Can Be Disregarded

Certain voting rights can be disregarded if they fall into one of the exemptions in DTR 5.1.3 e.g. shares held by a nominee in that capacity.

By virtue of DTR 5.1.5R threshold levels will only require notification at 5%, 10% and each whole percent thereafter where the voting rights attached to a share belong to someone else and is managed under an agreement in or evidenced in writing. This is also applicable to the voting rights attached to shares which may be exercisable by a person in the capacity as the operator of:

i. an authorised unit trust scheme; ii. an authorised contractual scheme; iii. a recognised scheme; or iv. a UCITS scheme.

For details of what should be included in the notification to the issuer (not later than four trading days in the case of a non-UK issuer and two trading days in all other cases) - see DTR 5.8.3. If the notification to the issuer relates to shares admitted to trading on a regulated market then a copy of such notification must also be filed with the FCA at the same time (DTR 5.8.2(2) and DTR 5.9).

Notification is by way of the FCA’s ESS (Electronic Submission System) - market makers have to complete form TR-2 which is to be emailed to the FCA.

## 22.5 AIFMD Disclosure Obligations and ‘Asset Stripping’

Purpose The AIFMD imposes notification and disclosure obligations (Articles 26 – 30) upon an AIFM when an AIF (or AIFs):  acquires control of a non-listed company or issuer  acquires, holds or disposes of shares of a non-listed company but control is not acquired

There is an exemption for small and medium-sized enterprises or an SPV with the purpose of purchasing, holding or administering real estate.

Control For a non-listed company ‘control’ means holding more than 50% of the voting rights of the company. For such an entity, any voting rights of an undertaking controlled by the AIF, or by someone acting on behalf of the AIF (or on behalf of an undertaking controlled by the AIF) must be taken into account.

For an issuer ‘control’ means holding the percentage of voting rights which confers control in the EEA State in which the issuer has its registered office.

Notification of major holdings re non-listed companies

As the AIFM Alamut must notify the FCA of the proportion of voting rights held by the AIF any time when that proportion reaches, exceeds or falls below the thresholds of 10%, 20%, 30%, 50% and 75%.

When an AIF acquires control of such an entity the Alamut must notify the following of such controls:  The non-listed company;  The company’s shareholders where the identities and addresses can be ascertained;  The FCA as the competent authority of the AIFM.

The notification must include:  the resulting situation in terms of voting rights in the company  the date on which control was acquired  the conditions subject to which control was acquired, including information about the identity of the shareholders involved, anyone entitled to exercise voting rights on their behalf and the chain of undertakings through which voting rights are effectively held  a request to the board to inform its employees, or their representatives, of the above “without due delay” (and use best efforts to ensure this is done). However, the directors are not obliged to comply with the request where communication of the information “would seriously harm the functioning of the non-listed company or would be seriously prejudicial to it”.

Notifications under the above section must be made as soon as possible and, in any event, no later than ten working days after the control or relevant threshold occurred.

Disclosure on acquisition of control of issuer or non-listed company Where an AIF acquires control of an issuer or a non-listed company then Alamut must disclose additional information to the following:  The company or issuer;  The company’s shareholders where the identities and addresses can be ascertained;  The FCA as the competent authority of the AIFM.

The additional information is:  Identity of the AIFM(s) that manage the AIF(s) that have acquired control;  Policy for preventing and managing conflicts of interest between the AIFM(s)/AIF(s) and the company or issuer;  Specific safeguards to ensure that any agreements between the AIFM(s)/AIF(s) and the company or issuer is concluded at arm’s length;  The policy for external and internal communication relating to the company or issuer, in particular as regards the employees.

The notification must include a request to the board to inform its employees, or their representatives, of the above “without undue delay” (and use best efforts to ensure this is done). However, the directors are not obliged to comply with the request where communication of the information “would seriously harm the functioning of the non-listed company or would be seriously prejudicial to it”.

Additional disclosure on acquisition of control of non-listed companies When an AIF acquires control of a non-listed company, Alamut as AIFM of the AIF must disclose its intentions within 20 working days (starting on the day control was acquired) as to the future business of the non-listed company and the likely repercussions on employment by the company, including any material change in the conditions of employment. The disclosure must be to:  The non-listed company  The company’s shareholders where the identities and addresses can be ascertained

The additional notification must include a request to the board to inform its employees, or their representatives, of the above intentions (and use best efforts to ensure this is done). However, the directors

are not obliged to comply with the request where communication of the information “would seriously harm the functioning of the non-listed company or would be seriously prejudicial to it”.

Alamut must also provide both the FCA and the AIF’s investors with information on the financing of the acquisition.

Annual report of AIFs exercising control of non-listed companies Where the AIF acquires control of a non-listed company then the AIF’s annual report must include:  fair review of the development of the company’s business representing the situation at the end of the period covered by the annual report;  any important events that have occurred since the end of the financial year of the AIF;  the company’s likely future development; and in relation to the company’s acquisition or disposal of its own shares: i. the reasons for acquisitions made during the financial year; ii. the number and nominal value or, in the absence of a nominal value, the accountable par of the shares acquired and disposed of during the financial year and the proportion of the subscribed capital which they represent; iii. in the case of acquisition or disposal for a value, the consideration for the shares; and iv. the number and nominal value or, in the absence of a nominal value, the accountable par of all the shares acquired and held by the company and the proportion of the subscribed capital which they represent.

However, the above obligation will not apply if (a) the non-listed company is obliged under law to prepare its own annual report and if the AIFM ensures that the required information is included in that report and (b) the report is made available to the employees or their representatives within the period in which the annual report must be drawn up.

Where the information is included in the company’s annual report then the information must also be made available to the AIF’s investors in so far as already available within six months following the end of the AIF’s financial year and in any event no later than the date on which the annual report of the company must be drawn up.

Where the information is included in the AIF’s annual report then the AIFM is required to make best efforts to ensure that the directors of the non-listed company makes that information available to its employees no later than six months following the end of the AIF’s financial year.

Asset stripping prevention on acquiring control Restrictions are placed upon the actions of the AIFM of any AIF that acquires control of a non-listed company or an issuer for a period of twenty-four months from that date. The restrictions are:

the AIFM must not facilitate, support or instruct any distribution, capital reduction, share redemption or acquisition by the company or issuer of its own shares; in so far as the AIFM is authorised to vote on behalf of the AIF at the meetings of the governing body of the company or issuer, must not vote in favour of a distribution, capital reduction, share redemption or acquisition by the company or issuer of its own shares;  in any event the AIFM must use its best efforts to prevent distributions, capital reductions, share redemptions or the acquisition by the company or issuer of its own shares.

Should Alamut find itself in a position where the above requirements apply then the relevant definitions of the terms used e.g. ‘capital reduction’ can be found in Part 5 of the UK’s AIFMD Regulations (see link below).

## 22.6 Other

Please note that there are separate FCA requirements in respect of acquisitions of, or reductions in, UK authorised firms – see the ‘Change of Control’ section of this manual.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
This chapter details the FCA’s Disclosure and Transparency Rules (“DTR”) on disclosure and transparency as well as giving consideration to the Takeover Code where relevant. In other jurisdictions there may be different disclosure requirements and separate advice should be sought. Within the EU, the Transparency (Obligations) Directive and the Takeovers Directive provide some similarity among EU members over the rules for disclosure and transparency.

Where securities are admitted to trading on any regulated market (being a UK Recognised investment Exchange) or ‘prescribed market’ (for the purposes of these Rules this will capture a market which is established under the rules of a UK Recognised Investment Exchange e.g. AIM), regardless of whether the issuer is established in the UK or not, then the FCA’s DTR rules would apply.

sections.title: Disclosure
sections.content:
The disclosure rules in DTR 1, 2, 3 and 4 apply to issuers of financial instruments admitted to trading on a regulated market and therefore are not applicable to Alamut. Additional scrutiny should be given to the City Code which requires disclosure where an Issuer is party to a takeover.

Although not part of the Handbook, the FCA has endorsed the Takeover Code (“Code”). The Code is enforced by the Panel on Takeovers and Mergers (“Takeover Panel”). Disclosure is required when shares are acquired in a company subject to a takeover bid. Rule 8 of the Code (disclosure of dealings and positions) and Rule 9 (mandatory offer) are of particular relevance when stake building. Under Rule 8 disclosure must be made by a person with an interest of 1% or more in either the target company or the company making the offer. When investments are managed on a discretionary basis then it is the manager, and not the person on whose behalf the relevant securities are managed, that is subject to the disclosure requirement. Rule 9 requires that an offer be made to all shareholders when a stake reaches 30% or more of the voting rights.

Note that any reporting required under the Code will be in addition to any obligations under DTR.

sections.title: Transparency
sections.content:
The transparency rules are contained within DTR 5 and include the requirement to notify the issuer of voting rights held. Note that this obligation applies to shareholders of issuers with shares that are traded on a regulated market and to shareholders of issuers whose shares are traded on a prescribed market e.g. AIM.

Certain obligations under DTR 5 apply to issuers e.g. DTR 5.6 ‘disclosure by issuers’.

A person must notify the issuer of the percentage of its voting rights (held as a shareholder or are held or are deemed to be held through direct or indirect holdings of financial instruments falling within DTR 5.3.1(1) (see also below) if the percentage of those voting rights reaches, exceeds or falls below 3% and each 1% threshold thereafter up to 100 % (although see below re managed accounts). In the case of non- UK issuers threshold starts at 5% and increases in 5% intervals up to 30% and thereafter apply at the 50% & 75% levels (DTR 5.1.2 (1)). The latter thresholds are based upon the EU Transparency Directive, as amended, whilst the former arise from the FCA applying super-equivalent rules which have continued post the Brexit transitional.

DTR 5.2 provides examples (‘cases’) where a person will be regarded as an indirect holder of shares e.g. voting rights by a person undertaking investment management and who may exercise voting rights at their discretion.

DTR 5.3 provides rules and guidance on financial instruments which on maturity give a holder, under a formal agreement, a right or discretion to acquire shares to which voting rights are attached or which are referenced to such shares and with an ‘economic effect’ similar to those said financial instruments whether or not they confer a right to a physical settlement (DTR 5.3.1(1)).

Where notification includes financial instruments within DTR 5.3.1(1) then said notification must include a breakdown by type and distinguish between those instruments that confer a right to physical settlement or cash settlement (DTR 5.3.5). Both ESMA and the FCA have published an ‘indicative list’ of financial instruments that are subject to notification requirements e.g. warrants, futures etc. which satisfy the conditions of Article 13(1) of the Transparency Directive/DTR 5.3.1 (see ‘Useful Links).

If the notification relates to shares admitted to trading on a regulated market, the person should also file a copy of notification with the FCA at the same time (DTR 5.9). Note that this could give rise to a dual notification requirement to both the FCA and to the relevant EU competent authority e.g. if shares are traded on both a UK regulated market and on an EU regulated market (notification to Home Member State is required under Article 19(3) of the Transparency Directive).

sections.title: Voting Rights which Can Be Disregarded
sections.content:
Certain voting rights can be disregarded if they fall into one of the exemptions in DTR 5.1.3 e.g. shares held by a nominee in that capacity.

By virtue of DTR 5.1.5R threshold levels will only require notification at 5%, 10% and each whole percent thereafter where the voting rights attached to a share belong to someone else and is managed under an agreement in or evidenced in writing. This is also applicable to the voting rights attached to shares which may be exercisable by a person in the capacity as the operator of:

i. an authorised unit trust scheme; ii. an authorised contractual scheme; iii. a recognised scheme; or iv. a UCITS scheme.

For details of what should be included in the notification to the issuer (not later than four trading days in the case of a non-UK issuer and two trading days in all other cases) - see DTR 5.8.3. If the notification to the issuer relates to shares admitted to trading on a regulated market then a copy of such notification must also be filed with the FCA at the same time (DTR 5.8.2(2) and DTR 5.9).

Notification is by way of the FCA’s ESS (Electronic Submission System) - market makers have to complete form TR-2 which is to be emailed to the FCA.

chapters.title: SHORT SELLING
chapters.summary: 23. SHORT SELLING
chapters.content:
23. SHORT SELLING

## 23.1 Introduction

The Short Selling Regulation (“SSR”):

 gives powers to the FCA to suspend or limit short selling  requires persons undertaking short selling to make notifications  places restrictions on uncovered short positions (‘naked short-selling’)

The above SSR is EU 236/2012 as amended post-Brexit by SI 2018/132 & SI 2021/5 (see ‘Useful Links’). As such there exists an ‘EU SSR’ and a ‘UK SSR’.

Note that the SSR is extra territorial in that the EU SSR applies to financial instruments that are admitted to trading on an EU trading venue (being an EU regulated market or an EU MTF) whilst the UK SSR applies to financial instruments admitted to trading on a UK trading venue (being a UK regulated market or a UK MTF) – in each case including when traded outside of a trading venue.

## 23.2 Share Capital

There is a restriction on uncovered short sales of a share admitted to trading on a ‘trading venue’ (Article 12).

Basically, one will have to have borrowed the share or entered into an appropriate arrangement/agreement including a ’locate’ – see Article 12(1)(a) to (c) for the precise circumstances.

A person that has a net short position in relation to the issued share capital of a company whose shares are admitted to trading on a ‘trading venue’ has to notify the relevant competent authority (as defined in Article 2(1)(j)) when the position reaches or falls below 0.1%* of the issued capital and each 0.1% above that (Article 5). Under EU SSR the relevant competent authority can be found from the ESMA FIRDS Register (‘upcoming RCA’) – see ‘Useful Links’.

- Note that at the time of this Manual both the UK SSR and the EU SSR set a threshold of 0.1%. However

the thresholds may be subject variance and reference should be made to the FCA and ESMA websites (see ‘Useful Links’) for current notification thresholds.

Should the position equal 0.5% then disclosure has to be made public, and for each 0.1% above that (Article 6).

There is an exemption from the private and public disclosure obligations above and from the restriction on uncovered short sales where the principal trading venue for the shares is outside the EU (EU SSR) or UK (UK SSR) – see ‘Useful Links’ for the lists of such exempt shares.

## 23.3 Sovereign Debt

There is a restriction on uncovered short sales in sovereign debt (as defined by EU SSR or UK SSR as relevant). Again, one will have to have borrowed the sovereign debt or entered into an appropriate arrangement/agreement – see Article 13(1)(a) to (c).

However, the restrictions do not apply if the transaction serves to hedge a long position in debt instruments of an issuer, the pricing of which has a “high correlation with the pricing of the given sovereign debt” (Article 13(2)).

A person that has a net short position relating to sovereign debt has to notify the relevant competent authority (as defined in Article 2(1)(j) – although under UK SSR this will be the FCA) when certain thresholds are reached (Article 7).

The thresholds are not specified in the Regulations and instead reference should be made to the relevant ESMA Register or FCA web page (see ‘Useful Links’).

Note that unlike share capital (see above) there is no public notification obligation for Sovereign debt.

## 23.4 Sovereign Credit Default Swaps (“CDS”)

There is a restriction on uncovered sovereign CDS.

Transactions in a sovereign CDS can only be entered into where it does not lead to an uncovered position (Article 14(1)).

The same Article also allows a competent authority to temporarily suspend this restriction in line with Article 14(2). Where such a suspension materialises then a holder of an uncovered position in a sovereign CDS has to notify the relevant competent authority at the notification thresholds applicable to sovereign debt (see ‘Sovereign debt’ above) (Article 8).

## 23.5 Useful Links

sections.content:
