 gives powers to the FCA to suspend or limit short selling  requires persons undertaking short selling to make notifications  places restrictions on uncovered short positions (‘naked short-selling’)

The above SSR is EU 236/2012 as amended post-Brexit by SI 2018/132 & SI 2021/5 (see ‘Useful Links’). As such there exists an ‘EU SSR’ and a ‘UK SSR’.

Note that the SSR is extra territorial in that the EU SSR applies to financial instruments that are admitted to trading on an EU trading venue (being an EU regulated market or an EU MTF) whilst the UK SSR applies to financial instruments admitted to trading on a UK trading venue (being a UK regulated market or a UK MTF) – in each case including when traded outside of a trading venue.

sections.title: Share Capital
sections.content:
There is a restriction on uncovered short sales of a share admitted to trading on a ‘trading venue’ (Article 12).

Basically, one will have to have borrowed the share or entered into an appropriate arrangement/agreement including a ’locate’ – see Article 12(1)(a) to (c) for the precise circumstances.

A person that has a net short position in relation to the issued share capital of a company whose shares are admitted to trading on a ‘trading venue’ has to notify the relevant competent authority (as defined in Article 2(1)(j)) when the position reaches or falls below 0.1%* of the issued capital and each 0.1% above that (Article 5). Under EU SSR the relevant competent authority can be found from the ESMA FIRDS Register (‘upcoming RCA’) – see ‘Useful Links’.

- Note that at the time of this Manual both the UK SSR and the EU SSR set a threshold of 0.1%. However

the thresholds may be subject variance and reference should be made to the FCA and ESMA websites (see ‘Useful Links’) for current notification thresholds.

Should the position equal 0.5% then disclosure has to be made public, and for each 0.1% above that (Article 6).

There is an exemption from the private and public disclosure obligations above and from the restriction on uncovered short sales where the principal trading venue for the shares is outside the EU (EU SSR) or UK (UK SSR) – see ‘Useful Links’ for the lists of such exempt shares.

sections.title: Sovereign Debt
sections.content:
There is a restriction on uncovered short sales in sovereign debt (as defined by EU SSR or UK SSR as relevant). Again, one will have to have borrowed the sovereign debt or entered into an appropriate arrangement/agreement – see Article 13(1)(a) to (c).

However, the restrictions do not apply if the transaction serves to hedge a long position in debt instruments of an issuer, the pricing of which has a “high correlation with the pricing of the given sovereign debt” (Article 13(2)).

A person that has a net short position relating to sovereign debt has to notify the relevant competent authority (as defined in Article 2(1)(j) – although under UK SSR this will be the FCA) when certain thresholds are reached (Article 7).

The thresholds are not specified in the Regulations and instead reference should be made to the relevant ESMA Register or FCA web page (see ‘Useful Links’).

Note that unlike share capital (see above) there is no public notification obligation for Sovereign debt.

sections.title: Sovereign Credit Default Swaps (“CDS”)
sections.content:
There is a restriction on uncovered sovereign CDS.

Transactions in a sovereign CDS can only be entered into where it does not lead to an uncovered position (Article 14(1)).

The same Article also allows a competent authority to temporarily suspend this restriction in line with Article 14(2). Where such a suspension materialises then a holder of an uncovered position in a sovereign CDS has to notify the relevant competent authority at the notification thresholds applicable to sovereign debt (see ‘Sovereign debt’ above) (Article 8).

chapters.title: TRANSACTION REPORTING and TRADE REPORTING
chapters.summary: 24. TRANSACTION REPORTING and TRADE REPORTING
chapters.content:
24. TRANSACTION REPORTING and TRADE REPORTING

## 24.1 Transaction Reporting - Introduction

Transaction reports are seen as an essential tool in maintain confidence in the financial markets. They play an integral role in the detection and investigation of potential market abuse.

## 24.2 Requirements

Transaction reporting applies to a firm that ‘executes transactions’ – see definition below (and to operators of trading venues when a transaction is executed on its platform by a person not subject to MiFIR).

Before Brexit the ‘original’ transaction reporting obligations arose under MiFIR (EU 600/2014). Although EU MiFIR no longer applies to UK firms, a UK transaction reporting obligation arises under the UK version of MiFIR (for the record, EU firms trading in the UK as a branch will also be subject to UK transaction reporting and, if the instrument is tradable on an EU trading venue, also to EU transaction reporting).

Transaction reporting applies to financial instruments traded on or admitted to trading on or where a request for admission to trading on a trading venue (being a regulated market an MTF or an OTF) and to financial instruments where the underlying is a financial instrument traded on a trading venue and to financial instruments where the underlying is an index or a basket composed of financial instruments traded on a trading venue (a ‘reportable financial instrument’). Note that the UK transaction reporting obligation applies when the above trading venue is a UK trading venue, a Gibraltar trading venue or an EU trading venue.

The UK transaction reporting obligations, and supporting Technical Standards, largely, but not wholly, mirror the original EU legislation.

## 24.3 Execution of a Transaction

Under MiFID II ‘execution of a transaction’ (but see below re ‘transmission of an order’) will arise when a firm undertakes any of the following activities that result in a transaction:

a. Reception and transmission of orders in relation to one or more financial instruments; b. Making an investment decision in accordance with a discretionary mandate given by a client; c. Dealing on own account; d. Execution of orders on behalf of a client; e. Transfer of financial instruments to or from accounts.

However, an investment firm will not be deemed to have ‘executed a transaction’ where it has met the conditions of having ‘transmitted an order’ (and be a ‘transmitting firm’). The conditions are:

 The order was received from its client or arises from the purchase or sale of an instrument in accordance with a discretionary mandate;  The transmitting firm has submitted details relating to the order as specified in trading Article 4(2) of RTS 22 (see ‘Useful Links); and  The receiving firm is subject to the MiFIR obligation to report transactions and agrees to either report the transaction or to transmit it to another investment firm.

## 24.4 Reporting of Transactions

The content and format of a transaction report is set out in Regulation 2017/590 (see ‘Useful Links’).

Transactions should be reported to the FCA as soon as possible and no later than the close of the following working day.

Alamut has delegated its transaction reporting tothe market counterparty to the transaction.

## 24.5 Trade reporting – Introduction

Post-trade reporting obligations (‘transparency reporting’) apply to firms that conclude transactions in certain investments outside the rules of a UK trading venue (whether on own account or on behalf of clients) and require them to make public the details of such transactions. Similar to transaction reporting above, the obligation originally arose under MiFIR (EU 600/2014). Since Brexit the trade reporting obligation arises under UK MiFIR.

## 24.6 Requirements

The post-trade reporting obligation applies to the following instrument types when they are traded outside the rules of a UK trading venue although deemed to be (capable of being) ‘traded on a trading venue’ (see below):

 Shares, depositary receipts, ETFs, certificates and other similar financial instruments  Bonds, structured finance products, emission allowances and derivatives.

Unlike Transaction Reports (see above) the details of such trades are submitted to “APAs” (Approved Publication Arrangements) and not to the FCA.

Note that the obligation does not extend to all financial instruments traded OTC but rather to those ‘traded on a trading venue’ (“TOTV”) – see ‘Useful Links on how ESMA applies that concept to OTC derivatives.

Reporting in respect of shares etc. must be as close to real-time as possible and in any case within one minute of the transaction (see RTS 1 Article 14(2)(b) for when trading is undertaken outside the trading hours of the ‘most relevant market’ and Article 15 on deferment of publication).

Reporting in respect of bonds etc. must be as close to real time as possible and in any case within 5 minutes after execution of the transaction.

Subject to meeting the relevant requirements, it is possible to apply to the FCA for authorisation to defer post-trade publication.

RTS 1 and RTS 2 (concerning shares etc. and bonds etc. respectively – see ‘Useful Links’) provides further information on publication (and deferment).

Note that the FCA has stated “we do not require UK investment firms that transact on trading venues outside the UK (albeit that such a transaction would, of course, be outside of a UK trading venue), in the EU or elsewhere, to publish details of those transactions through a UK APA”.

## 24.7 Useful Links

Definition of ‘UK regulated market’ https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/glossary/?filter-title=regulated+market

RTS 22 (2017/590) re Transaction Reporting (EU) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0590&from=EN

RTS 22 (UK version) https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/techstandards/MIFID-MIFIR/2017/reg_del_2017_590_oj/?view=chapter

ESMA Guidelines on Transaction Reporting (ESMA/2016/1452) https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/2016- 1452_guidelines_mifid_ii_transaction_reporting.pdf

ESMA Q&As on MiFIR Data Reporting

https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/esma70-1861941480- 56_qas_mifir_data_reporting.pdf

RTS 1 (2017/585) re Trade Reporting for shares etc. (EU) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0587&from=EN

and in particular

 Transactions not contributing to the price discovery process (a contributory criterion for the disapplication of the trading obligation) - Article 2  Post-trade transparency obligations - Article 12  (Dis)application of certain transactions from post-trade transparency - Article 13  Publication & Deferment - Articles 14 & 15  Details of transactions to be made public - Annex I

RTS 1 (UK version) https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/techstandards/MIFID-MIFIR/2017/reg_del_2017_585_oj/?view=chapter

RTS 2 (2017/583) re Trade Reporting for bonds, derivatives etc. (EU) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0583&from=CS

and in particular

 Post-trade transparency obligations - Article 7  Publication & Deferment - Articles 7(4) & 8  (Dis)application of certain transactions from post-trade transparency - Article 12  Details of transactions to be made available to the public - Annex II  Various meanings including ‘asset class’ and ‘sub-asset class’ - Annex III

RTS 2 (UK version) https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/techstandards/MIFID-MIFIR/2017/reg_del_2017_583_oj/?view=chapter

ESMA Opinion (ESMA70-156-117) on meaning of ‘traded on a trading venue’ (“TOTV”) concerning post- trade transparency) https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/esma70-156-117_mifir_opinion_on_totv.pdf

Authorised APAs https://www.fca.org.uk/markets/mifid-ii/data-reporting-services-providers

FCA Transparency waivers and deferrals https://www.fca.org.uk/markets/mifid-ii/transparency-calculations

chapters.title: COMMODITY DERIVATIVES: LIMITS & POSITION REPORTING (and EMISSION ALLOWANCE POSITIONS)
chapters.summary: 25. COMMODITY DERIVATIVES: LIMITS & POSITION REPORTING (and EMISSION ALLOWANCE POSITIONS)
chapters.content:
25. COMMODITY DERIVATIVES: LIMITS & POSITION REPORTING (and EMISSION ALLOWANCE POSITIONS)

## 25.1 Introduction

UK MiFID places an obligation on the FCA to set position limits on the size of a net position that a person can hold at all times in commodity derivatives.

In addition, UK MiFID places commodity derivative and/or emission allowance position reporting obligations on:

trading venues (UK regulated market, UK MTF or UK OTF) investment firms trading outside a trading venue members, participants and clients of trading venues.

## 25.2 Position Limits

The position limits apply to ‘persons’ and therefore captures all firms, regardless of whether they are MiFID or non-MiFID firms, and natural persons. The limits restrict the size of a net position that can be held at all times in commodity derivatives traded on a trading venue and to ‘economically equivalent OTC contracts’ to those commodity derivatives (see ‘Useful Links’).

## 25.3 Position Reporting

Operators of trading venues are required to provide the FCA with, at least, daily reports showing positions held by all persons (including members, participants or clients) as well as those of their clients until the end client is reached. In addition, these operators must make public weekly reports on aggregate positions held by different categories of person (with similar details to be sent to the FCA and ESMA).

In turn, Members, participants or clients of UK trading venues must report to the relevant trading venue details of their positions held through contracts traded on that venue at least daily as well as those of their clients and the clients of those clients until the end client is reached.

Investment firms trading in a commodity derivative or emission allowance outside of a trading venue must provide the FCA on a daily basis with a breakdown of:

 their positions in those commodity derivatives or emission allowances traded on a trading venue  economically equivalent OTC contracts  the positions of their clients and the clients of those clients until the end client is reached

For the avoidance of doubt, the above means that as a CPMI firm the position limits will apply to both funds and to third-party managed accounts. However, there will be no reporting obligation on a fund as that is not an ‘investment firm’. Although the Firm will be regarded as an ‘investment firm’ when it is managing third-party accounts (because when managing such accounts the Firm is using its ‘top-up’ MiFID activities), it neither trades in commodities nor does it hold its own positions to give rise to reporting. The only situation when acting as a CPMI firm that would give rise to a reporting obligation would be if it were a ‘member’ or ‘participant’ of a trading venue. Position limits are per fund/scheme/client account.

Where applicable, position reports must be submitted to the FCA, for each business day, by 21:00 GMT the following business day.

See ‘Useful Links’ for FCA Guidance on the submission of reports and current position limits.

## 25.4 Useful Links

Handbook Rules MAR 10 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/MAR/10/?view=chapter

FCA Guidance on ‘Commodity Position Reports’ https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/systems-information/commodity-position-reporting-instructions.pdf

FCA Current Position Limits https://www.fca.org.uk/markets/mifid-ii/commodity-derivatives/position-limits

FCA Q&As https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/minutes/mifidii-commodity-derivatives-qa.pdf

Market Data Processor for reporting https://www.fca.org.uk/markets/market-data-regimes/market-data-reporting-mdp

‘MiFi Regulations’ (referenced in MAR 10) – SI 2017/701 http://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2017/701/contents/made

MiFID II (2014/65) Articles 57 & 58 http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32014L0065&from=EN

See also definition of a ‘commodity derivative’:

[Article 4(1)(50), signposting to MiFIR Article 2(1)(30) which in turn signposts back to MiFID II]:

“commodity derivatives’ means those financial instruments defined in point (44)(c) of Article 4(1) of Directive 2014/65/EU; which relate to a commodity or an underlying referred to in Section C(10) of Annex I to Directive 2014/65/EU; or in points (5), (6), (7) and (10) of Section C of Annex I thereto”

Delegated Regulation 2017/565 (Article 83) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0565&from=EN

Position reporting, including threshold for weekly reporting by trading venues

Delegated Regulation 2017/591 (‘RTS 21’) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0591&from=EN

and in particular:

 Definition of a non-financial entity – Article 2(1)

 Note that the definition is on an ‘other than’ basis i.e. it is a natural or legal person other than UCITS, AIFs managed by authorised/registered AIFMs, investment firms authorised under MiFID etc. The Article also addresses the position re third-country entities

 Economically equivalent OTC contracts – Article 6

 Methodology for competent authorities to calculate position limits – Articles 9 to 21

ITS 4 re position reporting (2017/1093) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R1093&from=EN

ITS 5 on format and timing of weekly position reports (2017/953) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0953&from=EN

ESMA on Commodities and Position Limits https://www.esma.europa.eu/policy-activities/mifid-ii/commodity-derivatives

Although the Firm will be regarded as an ‘investment firm’ when it is managing third-party accounts (because when managing such accounts the Firm is using its ‘top-up’ MiFID activities), it neither trades in commodities nor does it hold its own positions to give rise to reporting. The only situation when acting as a CPMI firm that would give rise to a reporting obligation would be if it were a ‘member’ or ‘participant’ of a trading venue. Position limits are per fund/scheme/client account.

Where applicable, position reports must be submitted to the FCA, for each business day, by 21:00 GMT the following business day.

See ‘Useful Links’ for FCA Guidance on the submission of reports and current position limits.

chapters.title: EMIR REQUIREMENTS
chapters.summary: 26. EMIR REQUIREMENTS
chapters.content:
26. EMIR REQUIREMENTS

## 26.1 Introduction

The European Markets Infrastructure Regulation (“EMIR”) implements the G20 commitments to the:

 Reporting to Trade Repositories (“TR”) (OTC and Exchange Traded);  Central Clearing for certain classes of OTC derivatives;  Application of risk mitigation techniques for non-centrally cleared OTC derivatives;  Application of organisational, conduct of business and prudential requirements for Central Counterparties (“CCPs”);  Application of requirements for Trade repositories, including the duty to make certain data available to the public and relevant authorities.

Although originally an EU Regulation (EU 648/2012), since full Brexit there is also a UK EMIR (SI 2019/335) which largely mirrors to EU Regulation.

EMIR has undergone a number of amendments, most notably by way of ‘EMIR REFIT Regulation’ (EU 2019/834; SI 2019/335) which included changes to the definitions of ‘Financial Counterparties’ and to the clearing obligation. For the record there is also an ‘EMIR 2.2’ Regulation which amends EMIR, although it is only concerned with the authorisation of CCPs and the recognition of third-country CCPs.

The FCA Handbook is largely silent on EMIR although it contains a section on “Application and notifications under EMIR” in SUP 15A. Links to relevant Regulations and to ESMA Q&As can be found under ‘Useful Links’ at the end of this chapter.

## 26.2 Application of EMIR

EMIR largely applies to entities that trade OTC or Exchange Traded Derivative contracts (“ETD”)., even when they trade with counterparties that are not subject to EMIR requirements. In this case the counterparty subject to EMIR needs to identify the other counterparty in its report. As such, whilst e.g. EMIR reporting obligations will not apply directly to such counterparties, those counterparties will nevertheless have to provide the reporting entity with the necessary information. Similarly, the EU EMIR/UK EMIR reporting obligation does not apply to the non-EU/non-UK subsidiaries of firms established in the EU/UK respectively.

For the purposes of EMIR, an ‘OTC derivative’ is one which is not traded on a MiFID/UK regulated market (as relevant) or on a third-country market considered equivalent (see ‘Useful Links’).

See ‘Counterparty Categorisation’ below for circumstances when a UK entity could be subject to both EU EMIR and to UK EMIR.

## 26.3 Counterparty Categorisation

EMIR imposes obligations upon Financial Counterparties (“FC”) and Non-Financial Counterparties (“NFC”).

An FC is defined in Article 2(8) of EMIR, as amended, and includes a MiFID investment firm, a bank, an AIF that is either established in the Union (EU EMIR) or the UK (UK EMIR) or that is managed by an AIFM authorised or registered in accordance with the EU AIFMD or a UK AIFM as applicable, an insurer, an EU UCITS or UK UCITS as applicable (and where relevant its management company) and an occupational retirement scheme.

A ‘small FC’ (“FC-”) is a sub-set of an FC (those FC counterparties that are not “FC-” are categorised as “FC+”) and is exempt from the clearing obligation. The aggregate month-end average position thresholds (calculated over the previous 12 months) for a counterparty to be categorised as a FC- range from €1bn to €3bn depending upon the class of OTC derivative. These clearing thresholds, which can be found in Article 11 of Regulation 149/2013, are the same as are applicable to an NFC (UK EMIR uses the same thresholds).

An FC is not obliged to calculate its average month end positions, although this would automatically categorise the counterparty as “FC+”. Counterparties that are categorised as FC+ need to inform ESMA and its regulator (or just the FCA under UK EMIR) of the situation and establish clearing arrangements within four months of this notification.

An NFC is defined in Article 2(9) of EMIR and is an undertaking established in the EU (EU EMIR) or in the UK (UK EMIR) other than those mentioned above. There are two subsets of the NFC categorisation and which are relevant to the need to centrally clear OTC derivative contracts and to risk mitigation techniques:

 NFC+ is a Non-Financial Counterparty exceeding the clearing threshold (see ‘Useful Links’).  NFC- is a Non-Financial Counterparty under the clearing threshold.

Note that the impact of exceeding a clearing threshold for any one class of instruments e.g. OTC equity derivative contracts is different between an FC and an NFC:

 when an FC exceeds any one of the clearing thresholds this will trigger the clearing obligation for all classes of OTC derivatives  when an NFC exceeds any one of the clearing thresholds this will trigger the clearing obligation for only that one class of OTC derivatives

## 26.4 Reporting to a Trade Repository

The obligation to report derivative transactions to a TR applies to both FC and NFC counterparties (whether NFC+ or NFC-). The reporting obligation applies to both counterparties that meet the definition of an FC or an NFC so e.g. it does not apply to individuals. The minimum details to be reported is set out in Regulation 2017/104 (see ‘Useful Links’). However, note that responsibility for reporting an OTC transaction:

 between an FC and an NFC- falls upon the FC  where the counterparty is an AIF/UCITS falls upon the AIFM/Management Company

## 26.5 Mandatory Clearing

Counterparties that are subject to the clearing obligation are required to clear all OTC derivative contracts that belong to a class of derivatives that has been declared subject to the clearing obligation through a CCP (EMIR Article 4).

The date of the clearing obligation was dependent upon both the OTC class of derivative and the ‘Category’ of the counterparties as follows:

 Category 1 will include counterparties that are clearing members for at least one of the derivatives subject to the clearing obligation  Category 2 will capture counterparties that do not belong to ‘Category 1’ that are FCs (or AIFs that are non-financial counterparties) whose aggregate month-end average of outstanding gross notional amount of non-centrally cleared derivatives is above €8bn  Category 3 will be FCs (or AIFs that are non-financial counterparties) not belonging to Category 1 or Category 2  Category 4 will consist of NFCs that do not belong to any of the above three categories

As mentioned above, an FC categorised as ‘FC-‘ will be exempt from the clearing obligation.

All four counterparty categories now fall with the clearing obligation.

The class of OTC derivatives that are subject to the clearing obligation are set out in various Delegated Regulations – however ESMA (re EU EMIR) and the Bank of England (re UK EMIR) maintain separate public Registers for the Clearing Obligation – see ‘Useful Links’. Any reference to the ‘frontloading provision’ in the Register derives from EMIR Article 4(1)(b)(ii) and refers to those contracts which are subject to clearing

before the date the clearing obligation takes effect – however ‘EMIR REFIT’ removes the frontloading provision.

## 26.6 Risk Mitigation Techniques

EMIR Article 11 requires risk management of OTC derivatives that are not cleared by a CCP through:

(a) Timely confirmations; (b) Portfolio reconciliation; (c) Portfolio compression; (d) Dispute resolution; (e) Marking to market/model; (f) Margin requirements.

## 26.7 Timely Confirmations

The timely confirmation requirement applies whenever a new OTC derivatives contract is concluded between an FC or an NFC+ and which is not cleared by a CCP. This includes novated contracts and portfolio compression of previously concluded contracts.

EMIR requires that the confirmation where the counterparties are either an FC or an NFC+ is done as soon as possible and, at the latest, by the end of the business day following the date of execution of the derivative contract (‘T+1’).

For transactions where one party is an NFC- the deadline is T+2.

For transactions (whether involving a FC/NFC+ or an NFC- counterparty) concluded after 16:00 local time, or with a counterparty located in a different time zone which does not allow confirmation by the set deadline, the confirmation must be done as soon as possible and, at the latest, one business day after the deadlines referred to above. Only days which are business days in the jurisdictions of both counterparties should be counted.

An FC must have in place procedures to report on a monthly basis the number of unconfirmed OTC transactions outstanding for more than five business days. However, the FCA advises that an FC does not need to submit such a report unless it has been individually requested to do so by the FCA.

## 26.8 Portfolio Reconciliation

Both FCs and NFCs are required to agree before entering into the OTC contract arrangements under which portfolios are reconciled and both counterparties (or qualified third parties) are responsible for performing portfolio reconciliations.

The frequency at which portfolio reconciliation must be performed depends on the number of derivative contracts outstanding during the relevant period and whether the counterparty it is an FC/NFC + or an NFC- .

For an FC or NFC+ reconciliation should be performed:

1. Each business day when the counterparties have 500 or more OTC derivative contracts outstanding with each other; 2. Weekly when the counterparties have between 51 and- 499 OTC derivative contracts outstanding with each other at any time during the week; 3. Quarterly when the counterparties have 50 or less OTC derivative contracts outstanding with each other at any given time during the quarter.

For an NFC- reconciliation should be performed:

 Quarterly when the counterparties have more than 100 OTC derivative contracts outstanding with each other at any time during the quarter;  Annually when the counterparties have 100 or less derivative contracts outstanding with each other.

The counterparties should reassess the frequency at each portfolio reconciliation date. For example, if an FC is subject to a quarterly reconciliation with its counterparty and performs the first portfolio reconciliation on 15 December, the date of the next portfolio reconciliation should be 15 March the following year if the number of the outstanding contracts was 50 or less at any time in the previous reconciliation period (from 15 September to 15 December).

The portfolio reconciliation should cover the key trade terms that identify each particular derivative contract including:

(a) Valuation (see “Mark-to-market and marking-to-model” below); (b) Effective date; (c) Scheduled maturity date; (d) Any payment or settlement dates; (e) Notional value of the contract; (f) Currency of the transaction; (g) Underlying instrument; (h) Position of the counterparties; (i) Business day convention; and (j) Any relevant fixed or floating rates.

## 26.9 Portfolio Compression

FCs and NFCs with 500 or more OTC derivative contracts outstanding with a counterparty which are not centrally cleared are required to have appropriate procedures in place to consider the possibility to conduct portfolio compression (a risk mitigation technique that reduces notional exposure and eliminates trades) at least semi-annually.

If counterparties conclude that portfolio compression is not appropriate, they must be able to provide a reasonable and valid explanation to the relevant competent authority.

## 26.10 Dispute Resolution

FCs and NFCs are required to have in place detailed processes and procedures on:

 The identification, recording of and the monitoring of disputes relating to the recognition or valuation of a contract and to the exchange of collateral between counterparties; and  The resolution of disputes in a timely manner with a specific process for those disputes that are not resolved within five business days.

FCs must report to the competent authority any disputes between them for an amount or a value higher than €15 million and outstanding for at least 15 business days. This amount or value should be calculated and reported on a trade by trade basis whenever possible. However, counterparties may use a portfolio basis if the disputed valuation or collateral is calculated at the portfolio level.

A minimum requirement for FCs is to make a monthly notification of any disputes outstanding in the preceding month, unless more frequent reporting is required by the relevant national competent authority.

The Firm and its FC must report to the FCA by the 15th of each month any disputes outstanding in the previous month. The FCA does not require a nil return if there are no disputes to report in any particular month.

## 26.11 Marking-to-market and Marking-to-model

FC and NFC+ counterparties are required to mark-to-market the value of outstanding contracts daily or, where market conditions prevent them from marking-to-market, mark-to-model. Such conditions will be deemed to occur when either:

 The market is inactive (when quoted prices are not readily and regularly available and those prices available do not represent actual and regularly occurring market transactions on an arm’s length basis); or  Where the range of reasonable fair values estimates is significant and the probabilities of the various estimates cannot reasonably be assessed.

Counterparties using ‘market-to-model’ must ensure that the model:

(a) Incorporates all factors that counterparties would consider in setting a price, including using as much as possible marking-to-market information; (b) Is consistent with accepted economic methodologies for pricing financial instruments; (c) Is calibrated and tested for validity using prices from any observable current market transactions in the same instrument or based on any available observable market data; (d) Is validated and monitored independently by another division other than the division taking the risk; (e) Is duly documented and approved by the board of directors (or to a delegated committee) as frequently as necessary, following any material change and at least annually.

## 26.12 Margin Requirements

FC and NFC+ are subject to margin requirements where OTC derivatives are not cleared through a CCP.

Initial margin (“IM”) is the collateral collected by a counterparty to cover current and potential future exposures for the expected time between the last exchange of variation margin and the liquidation of positions on the default of a counterparty.

Variation margin (“VM”) is the collateral collected by a counterparty to reflect the results of daily marking- to-market/model (see above).

The requirement for VM has been in place for all entities in scope since 1 March 2017. However, the requirement for IM is phased-in over a period of time.

## 26.13 Clearing Obligation Exemption

Derivative contracts that are intragroup transactions are exempt from the clearing obligation under EMIR provided authorisation/notification requirements are fulfilled. Counterparties involved in such transactions must first notify their respective authorities in writing of their intention to apply the exemption to the relevant transactions between themselves.

Counterparties will be able to apply for this exemption to their respective competent authorities after the first technical standards on the clearing obligation enter into force.

For an FC, an intragroup transaction is:

 an OTC derivative contract entered into with another counterparty which is part of the same group, provided conditions outlined in Article 3 EMIR are met;  an OTC derivative contract entered into with an NFC which is part of the same group, provided that both counterparties are included in the same consolidation on a full basis and meet the requirements in Article 3(2)(d) EMIR;  an OTC derivative contract entered into with another counterparty where both counterparties are part of the same institutional protection scheme (as per Article 80(8) of the Banking Consolidation Directive 2006/48);

 an OTC derivative contract entered into between credit institutions affiliated to the same central body or between such credit institution and the central body.

For an NFC, an intragroup transaction is:

 an OTC derivative contract entered into with another counterparty which is part of the same group, provided that both counterparties are included in the same consolidation on a full basis and meet the requirements in Article 3(1) EMIR.

## 26.14 Collateralisation Requirement Exemption

Derivative contracts that are intragroup transactions are exempt from the collateralisation requirement provided that ‘there is no current or foreseen practical or legal impediment to the prompt transfer of own funds or repayment of liabilities between counterparties’. The counterparty to such exempt intragroup transaction is required under EMIR to publicly disclose information on the exemption

## 26.15 Useful Links

http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0105&from=EN

 Quarterly when the counterparties have more than 100 OTC derivative contracts outstanding with each other at any time during the quarter;  Annually when the counterparties have 100 or less derivative contracts outstanding with each other.

The counterparties should reassess the frequency at each portfolio reconciliation date. For example, if an FC is subject to a quarterly reconciliation with its counterparty and performs the first portfolio reconciliation on 15 December, the date of the next portfolio reconciliation should be 15 March the following year if the number of the outstanding contracts was 50 or less at any time in the previous reconciliation period (from 15 September to 15 December).

The portfolio reconciliation should cover the key trade terms that identify each particular derivative contract including:

(a) Valuation (see “Mark-to-market and marking-to-model” below); (b) Effective date; (c) Scheduled maturity date; (d) Any payment or settlement dates; (e) Notional value of the contract; (f) Currency of the transaction; (g) Underlying instrument; (h) Position of the counterparties; (i) Business day convention; and (j) Any relevant fixed or floating rates.

sections.title: Portfolio Compression
sections.content:
FCs and NFCs with 500 or more OTC derivative contracts outstanding with a counterparty which are not centrally cleared are required to have appropriate procedures in place to consider the possibility to conduct portfolio compression (a risk mitigation technique that reduces notional exposure and eliminates trades) at least semi-annually.

If counterparties conclude that portfolio compression is not appropriate, they must be able to provide a reasonable and valid explanation to the relevant competent authority.

sections.title: Dispute Resolution
sections.content:
FCs and NFCs are required to have in place detailed processes and procedures on:

 The identification, recording of and the monitoring of disputes relating to the recognition or valuation of a contract and to the exchange of collateral between counterparties; and  The resolution of disputes in a timely manner with a specific process for those disputes that are not resolved within five business days.

FCs must report to the competent authority any disputes between them for an amount or a value higher than €15 million and outstanding for at least 15 business days. This amount or value should be calculated and reported on a trade by trade basis whenever possible. However, counterparties may use a portfolio basis if the disputed valuation or collateral is calculated at the portfolio level.

A minimum requirement for FCs is to make a monthly notification of any disputes outstanding in the preceding month, unless more frequent reporting is required by the relevant national competent authority.

The Firm and its FC must report to the FCA by the 15th of each month any disputes outstanding in the previous month. The FCA does not require a nil return if there are no disputes to report in any particular month.

sections.title: Marking-to-market and Marking-to-model
sections.content:
FC and NFC+ counterparties are required to mark-to-market the value of outstanding contracts daily or, where market conditions prevent them from marking-to-market, mark-to-model. Such conditions will be deemed to occur when either:

 The market is inactive (when quoted prices are not readily and regularly available and those prices available do not represent actual and regularly occurring market transactions on an arm’s length basis); or  Where the range of reasonable fair values estimates is significant and the probabilities of the various estimates cannot reasonably be assessed.

Counterparties using ‘market-to-model’ must ensure that the model:

(a) Incorporates all factors that counterparties would consider in setting a price, including using as much as possible marking-to-market information; (b) Is consistent with accepted economic methodologies for pricing financial instruments; (c) Is calibrated and tested for validity using prices from any observable current market transactions in the same instrument or based on any available observable market data; (d) Is validated and monitored independently by another division other than the division taking the risk; (e) Is duly documented and approved by the board of directors (or to a delegated committee) as frequently as necessary, following any material change and at least annually.

sections.title: Margin Requirements
sections.content:
FC and NFC+ are subject to margin requirements where OTC derivatives are not cleared through a CCP.

Initial margin (“IM”) is the collateral collected by a counterparty to cover current and potential future exposures for the expected time between the last exchange of variation margin and the liquidation of positions on the default of a counterparty.

Variation margin (“VM”) is the collateral collected by a counterparty to reflect the results of daily marking- to-market/model (see above).

The requirement for VM has been in place for all entities in scope since 1 March 2017. However, the requirement for IM is phased-in over a period of time.

sections.title: Clearing Obligation Exemption
sections.content:
Derivative contracts that are intragroup transactions are exempt from the clearing obligation under EMIR provided authorisation/notification requirements are fulfilled. Counterparties involved in such transactions must first notify their respective authorities in writing of their intention to apply the exemption to the relevant transactions between themselves.

Counterparties will be able to apply for this exemption to their respective competent authorities after the first technical standards on the clearing obligation enter into force.

For an FC, an intragroup transaction is:

 an OTC derivative contract entered into with another counterparty which is part of the same group, provided conditions outlined in Article 3 EMIR are met;  an OTC derivative contract entered into with an NFC which is part of the same group, provided that both counterparties are included in the same consolidation on a full basis and meet the requirements in Article 3(2)(d) EMIR;  an OTC derivative contract entered into with another counterparty where both counterparties are part of the same institutional protection scheme (as per Article 80(8) of the Banking Consolidation Directive 2006/48);

 an OTC derivative contract entered into between credit institutions affiliated to the same central body or between such credit institution and the central body.

For an NFC, an intragroup transaction is:

 an OTC derivative contract entered into with another counterparty which is part of the same group, provided that both counterparties are included in the same consolidation on a full basis and meet the requirements in Article 3(1) EMIR.

sections.title: Collateralisation Requirement Exemption
sections.content: Derivative contracts that are intragroup transactions are exempt from the collateralisation requirement provided that ‘there is no current or foreseen practical or legal impediment to the prompt transfer of own funds or repayment of liabilities between counterparties’. The counterparty to such exempt intragroup transaction is required under EMIR to publicly disclose information on the exemption

chapters.title: COMPLAINTS
chapters.summary: 27. COMPLAINTS
chapters.content:
27. COMPLAINTS

## 27.1 Introduction

Firms must have effective and transparent procedures for the fair and prompt handling of complaints. Furthermore firms are required to recognise that complaints need to be resolved .

## 27.2 Complaint vs. MiFID complaint

A complaint can be regarded as any oral or written expression of dissatisfaction, whether justified or not, concerning the provision of a financial service and which alleges that the complainant has suffered (or may suffer) material distress or material inconvenience. A MiFID complaint is basically a complaint that relates to MiFID business, although the Handbook definition refers to a complaint “to which Article 26 of the MiFID Org Regulation applies”. See also DISP 1.1A.3. As a collective portfolio management investment firm our business involves both MiFID and non-MiFID activities and hence we could receive ‘a complaint’ (whether from an ‘eligible complainant’ or not) in respect of our non-MiFID business or ‘a MiFID complaint’. In order to avoid having to follow different complaints regimes, we will take a pragmatic approach and adopt the higher MiFID processes for all complaints. However, as will be seen below, complaints will need to be differentiated for the purposes of informing persons about any rights they may have under the Financial Ombudsman Service (if an ‘eligible complainant’).

## 27.3 MiFID complainant vs. Eligible complainant

The MiFID complaints handling requirements apply to clients’ or potential clients’ complaints. As such they are applicable to retail clients, professional clients and to eligible counterparties. An ‘eligible complainant’ is a UK concept and refers to either a ‘consumer’ (see below), a ‘micro- enterprise’ as defined in the Handbook, small charities (less than £6.5m annual income) and a trustee of a small trust (less than £5m net asset value) – see DISP 2.7.3 for further information. A consumer is any natural person acting outside of their trade, business or profession. Therefore a consumer, and hence an eligible complainant, can be an individual that is either a retail client or a professional client. The distinction is important as only an eligible complainant is permitted to refer a complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) where eight weeks have elapsed since receipt of the complaint. As such, all MiFID firms are required to follow the MiFID complaints handling rules when a complaint is made by a client (regardless of whether that client is retail or professional or eligible counterparty). In addition where the complaint is made by an eligible complainant then a MiFID firm must also follow the UK-based complaints handling rules. DISP 1 (‘Treating complainants fairly’) sets out the procedures to be followed by a firm when a complaint is received. As a generalisation, the rules on the handling of MiFID complaints is addressed in DISP 1.1A. All the rules in DISP 1.1A (some of which will refer to other rules in DISP 1 as a whole) will apply when a MiFID complainant is also an eligible complainant. These rules will be a combination of the MiFID complaints rules and the UK’s rules concerning eligible complainants. However not all the rules in DISP 1.1A will apply when a MiFID complainant is not an eligible complainant – DISP 1.1A.6 clarifies which rules are relevant (essentially only those provisions marked “UK”). These rules will be based on the MiFID complaints rules. The application of DISP 1 as a whole to a MiFID firm can be complex and reference should be made to the Table in DISP 1 Annex 2. By way of example, the UK-based ‘Consumer awareness rules’ (DISP 1.2) do not apply to a MiFID firm - although similar, albeit less onerous, ‘Consumer awareness rules’ are captured in DISP 1.1A.10 – 1.1A.11. For the record, the firm does not currently undertake business with clients that would be classified as eligible complainants.

## 27.4 Complaints handling, oversight and monitoring

The Firm has established, and maintains, complaints management policies and procedures for the prompt handling of clients’ complaints. The policy has been endorsed by the Firm’s management body. All staff should make themselves familiar with these policies and procedures. The Firm is required to establish a ‘complaints management function’ responsible for the investigation of complaints. This role is carried out by the compliance function. The Firm’s compliance function, even where it is not the appointed ‘complaints management function’, is specifically charged with responsibility for analysis of complaints and complaints-handling data to ensure that risks and issues are identified and resolved e.g. identification of root causes and the impact upon other processes or products, including those not directly complained of. The compliance monitoring undertaken by the Firm addresses this requirement. For the avoidance of doubt, responsibility for implementation of the complaints management policy and for monitoring compliance with it rests with the Firm’s senior management.

## 27.5 Complaints awareness

Details of the Firm’s complaints handling process must be published (e.g. in brochures or the Firm’s website) and made available to clients or potential clients on request or when acknowledging a complaint (see below). The details will include information about the complaints management policy and the contact details of the complaint management function (see below). Where there are potential eligible complainants then information about FOS, and its website address, must be provided (“in a clear, comprehensible and easily accessible way”) on both the firm’s website and in the general conditions of the contracts with eligible complainants. Such information will include the right of an eligible complainant to approach the FOS when eight weeks have elapsed since receipt of the complaint.

## 27.6 Procedures

All complaints Any complaints received from a client should be referred to the Compliance Officer. In practical terms there should be no major difference in the Firm’s approach to the resolution of complaints from any complainant. It may be that a complaint is received by the firm on an issue which is actually the responsibility of another firm (or possibly another firm is jointly responsible with us for the alleged matter). In such instances the complaint should be forwarded (“promptly”) to the other and the complainant informed accordingly in line with DISP 1.7.1. All complaints must be acknowledged promptly, confirming receipt of the complaint and that it is being dealt with. The acknowledgement must be accompanied by a copy of the Firm’s published complaints handling process (see ‘Complaints awareness’ above). A complaint must be investigated competently, diligently and impartially, obtaining additional information as necessary. The complaint must be assessed fairly, consistently and promptly, giving due attention to the need to determine whether it should be upheld and what remedial action and/or redress may be appropriate. The complainant must be kept informed of the progress being made in the resolution of their complaint. The Firm must comply promptly where any offer of remedial action or redress is accepted by the complainant. When communicating the Firm’s position on the complaint to the client they must be informed about their options (if an eligible complainant this will be reference to the FOS; they may also be able to take civil action – DISP 1.1A.30). ESMA and the EBA have issued joint Guidelines for complaints-handling to which reference should be made when dealing with a complaint.

Eligible complainants Additional requirements apply when a complaint is received from an eligible complainant. If the complainant is an eligible complainant then when communicating to the client they should be informed that if the complaint is not resolved they may be entitled to refer it to the FOS (see “Complaints awareness” above). In general this option will only apply when either the Firm has sent its final position to the complainant or when eight weeks have elapsed since the Firm received the complaint (DISP 2.8.1).

When communicating the Firm’s position on the complaint to the client they must be informed about their options, including that if dissatisfied then they may refer the complaint to the FOS. In this situation the communication should be accompanied by a copy of the FOS’s standard explanatory leaflet and confirmation of their website address. There are time limits after which the FOS cannot consider a complaint. The above communication to an eligible complainant must indicate whether the Firm consents to waiving these time limits (see DISP 1.1A.31(4) and DISP 2.8.2 for further details). Note that an alternative approach is permitted where a complaint is resolved by close of the third business day following the day on which it was received (DISP 1.1A.23). As mentioned above, reference to DISP 1 Annex 2 (‘Application of DISP 1 to type of respondent/complaint’) may be useful.

## 27.7 Reporting (and publication)

The ‘complaints reporting rules’ in DISP 1.10 require a twice-yearly report to be provided to the FCA concerning complaints received from eligible complainants. By virtue of DISP 1.1A.38 they also apply to MiFID complaints, except that information must be included about such complaints received from retail clients, professional clients and, where relevant, eligible counterparties rather than just those received from eligible complainants. It will be appreciated that an eligible complainant (requiring a report under DISP 1.10) could make a complaint about MiFID business (which needs to be reported under DISP 1.1A.38) which, on the face of it, would mean that the same complaint would be reported twice to the FCA. To avoid such double counting, the effect of DISP 1.1A.39 is to modify the reporting rules so that information about complaints will be in respect of retail clients, professional clients and, where relevant, eligible counterparties rather than from eligible complainants. Reports must be submitted to the FCA within 30 business days of the end of the relevant reporting periods. The relevant reporting periods are:  The six months immediately following the firm’s accounting references date; and  The six months immediately preceding the firm’s accounting reference date Although unlikely to be relevant to us, for the record a Firm is required to publish on its website a summary of the complaints data (informing the FCA that it has done so) where 500 or more complaints are submitted in one of the reports above. See DISP 1.10A for further details.

## 27.8 Record keeping

A firm must keep records for every complaint it receives, including the measures taken for resolution (DISP 1.1A.37). Records of all complaints and the Firm’s responses will be kept by the Compliance Officer.

## 27.9 Useful Links

DISP https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/DISP/INTRO/?view=chapter

Complaints Return (pdf) https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/form/disp/DISP_01_ann_01_20171117.pdf?date=2030-01-01

FOS Leaflets https://payments.financial-ombudsman.org.uk/

ESMA/EBA Guidelines https://www.eba.europa.eu/documents/10180/732334/JC+2014+43+-+Joint+Committee+- +Final+report+complaints-handling+guidelines.pdf/312b02a6-3346-4dff-a3c4-41c987484e75

MiFID Org Regulation http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0565&from=DA

chapters.title: PRODUCT GOVERNANCE
chapters.summary: 28. PRODUCT GOVERNANCE
chapters.content:
28. PRODUCT GOVERNANCE

## 28.1 Purpose

MiFID introduced product governance requirements which fall upon both the ‘manufacturer’ of a financial instrument (including structured products) and the ‘distributor’ of a financial instrument (as will be seen below, ‘distributor’ also includes the provision of portfolio management).

‘Product governance’ means the systems and controls firms have in place for the design, approval, marketing and ongoing management of products throughout their lifecycle to ensure they meet legal and regulatory requirements.

Although Alamut does not undertake any MiFID activities, it is important to be aware of these requirements as our products may be distributed by a MiFID firm and consequently may approach Alamut in order to comply with their own MiFID obligations. In addition, PROD 1.3.2 requires non-MiFID firms to take account of these rules as if they were guidance.

Reference should be made to PROD 3 for further information.

## 28.2 What is a ‘manufacturer’?

A ‘manufacturer’ is a firm which creates, develops, issues and/or designs investments, including when advising corporate issuers on the launch of new investments.

A firm is expected to comply “in a way that is appropriate and proportionate” with the requirements set out in the Handbook (PROD 3.1.2).

## 28.3 What is a distributor?

A ‘distributor’ is a firm that offers, recommends or sells investments or provides investment services to clients. In line with this definition, ESMA has confirmed that a ‘distributor’ will also capture the investment services of portfolio management and reception and transmission of orders.

As per the above, the principle of proportionality also applies to the rules impacting upon a distributor (PROD 3.1.2).

## 28.4 Manufacture of Products

A manufacturer will be required to put in place a product approval process for each financial instrument and for any significant adaptation of existing financial instruments. This product approval process must:

 specify an identified target market of end clients (i.e. the person or group of persons at the end of the supply chain) within the relevant category (as per COBS 3) of clients;  ensure all relevant risks to the identified target market are assessed;  ensure that the intended distribution strategy is consistent with the identified target market;  take reasonable steps to ensure that the product is distributed to the identified target market.

The charging structure of each product must be considered including whether the charging structure is appropriately transparent for the target market. It is also a requirement to consider whether the product may represent a threat to the orderly function or stability of financial markets before deciding to proceed with the launch. Scenario analysis (‘product testing’) must be undertaken to assess the risk of poor outcomes for end clients and the circumstances in which those poor outcomes may occur. Conflicts of interest must be managed in the overall context of ‘manufacturing’ (including remuneration) and in respect of the design of each new product.

Interaction with distributors: pre-sale A manufacturer must make available to any distributor (and must make clear if it is not intended for end client use):

 all appropriate information on the product;  all appropriate information on the product approval process;  the identified target market, including any target market assessments undertaken;  information about the appropriate channels for distribution.

A manufacturer whose products are distributed through other firms must determine the needs and characteristics of the clients for whom the product is compatible based on:

 their theoretical knowledge of, and past experience with, the products in question;  the financial markets;  the needs, characteristics and objectives of potential end clients.

Interaction with distributors: post-sale A distributor is required to provide the manufacturer of each distributed product information on sales, including any sales outside the target market, and the outcome of the reviews they are required to undertake (see ‘Distribution of products and investment services’ below). A summary on types of client, complaints received and response from clients to questions suggested by the manufacturer for the purposes of obtaining feedback would be acceptable as opposed to reporting the details of every sale.

Review and oversight Products should be regularly reviewed for consistency in meeting identified needs, appropriateness of distribution strategy etc. This will include the communication to end clients (or to the distributor if the identity of the end clients is unknown) of any contractual ‘breakpoints’ such as the end of a long tie-in period that may have a material impact on the end client.

All staff involved in the manufacture of products must possess the necessary expertise to understand the characteristics and risks of those products.

Oversight of the development and periodic review of governance arrangements must sit with the person allocated the compliance oversight function. Compliance reports to the management body must include information about the manufactured products, including information on the distribution strategy. See PROD 3.2 for further information.

## 28.5 Distribution of Products and Investment Services

A distributor (as defined in the context of ‘product governance’) must:

 understand the products it ‘distributes’ to clients;  assess the compatibility of the products with the needs of clients, taking into account the manufacturers identified target market of end clients;  ensure the products are ‘distributed’ only when this is in the best interest of the client.

The above does not apply to eligible counterparty business (see PROD 1.3.3).

Interaction with the manufacturer: pre-sale Distributors will need to obtain from the manufacturer sufficient information to gain the necessary understanding and knowledge of the product in order to ensure said product will be distributed in accordance with the needs, characteristics and objectives of the target market. A distributor should ask the manufacturer for additional information or any necessary training in order to assist in understanding of the product or investment service - a distributor should not distribute the product or investment service if it does not sufficiently understand it.

‘All reasonable steps’ must be taken by distributors to comply with the Handbook requirements (PROD 3.3) where the product manufacturer is not subject to the Handbook (or to equivalent requirements in another

EEA State). The ‘all reasonable steps’ requirement applies proportionately depending on the complexity of the financial instrument in question and the degree to which publicly available information is obtainable.

Interaction with the manufacturer: post-sale A distributor must provide the manufacturer of each distributed product information on sales, including any sales outside the target market, and the outcome of the reviews mentioned below. A summary on types of client, complaints received and response from clients to questions suggested by the manufacturer for the purposes of obtaining feedback would be acceptable as opposed to reporting the details of every sale.

Target market Distributors must:

 determine the target market for the investment product even if the target market was not defined by the manufacturer (for the avoidance of doubt, where a firm is both a manufacturer and a distributor then only one target market assessment is required); and  identify any groups of end clients for which the financial instrument or investment service is not compatible.

When deciding the range of financial instruments and investment services to be distributed, and the target market, distributors must also ensure that all applicable rules are complied with including (but not limited to):

 Suitability  Appropriateness  Inducements  Conflicts of interest  Disclosure

All relevant staff must possess the necessary expertise to understand: the characteristics and risks of the products that will be distributed; the investment services provided by the Firm; and the needs, characteristics and objectives of the identified target market.

Review and oversight A distributor must regularly review the investment products distributed and investment services provided for consistency in meeting identified needs and whether the distribution strategy remains appropriate.

Similar to manufacturers, the oversight of the development and periodic review of governance arrangements must sit with the person allocated the compliance oversight function. Compliance reports to the management body must include information about the distribution of financial products by the Firm and the investment services provided. Distributors must regularly review the financial instruments distributed and the investment services provided to assess whether said instruments or services remain consistent with the requirements of the target market.

Where a distributor becomes aware that it has (a) wrongly identified the target market or (b) an instrument or service no longer meets the circumstances of the target market e.g. the instrument becomes very illiquid or volatile due to market changes then it must take ‘appropriate steps’ including at least reconsidering the target market and/or updating its product governance arrangements.

Where a firm distributes financial instruments to clients which are not end clients then it must also consider whether, in addition to acting as a distributor, it is undertaking a manufacturing role (in which case it will also need to comply with PROD 3.2). See PROD 3.3 for further information.

## 28.6 Interaction with RPPD Guide

There is similarity with the current “RPPD” (The Responsibilities of Providers and Distributors for the Fair Treatment of Customers) Regulatory Guide in the Handbook, albeit that the MiFID provisions go beyond what is in the RPPD. PROD 1.3.15 confirms that “a firm to which PROD 3 applies need not apply the guidance in RPPD for matters covered by PROD if the firm has complied with PROD 3”.

## 28.7 Useful Links

PROD 3 https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/PROD/3/?view=chapter

MiFID II (2014/65) (Article 16(3) & Article 24(2)) http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017R0567&from=EN

Delegated Directive 2017/593: Article 9 (‘Manufacturers’) & Article 10 (‘Distributors’)

and in particular

Application to Distributors Article 10 is applied to both “the range of financial instruments issued by themselves or other firms and services they intend to offer or recommend to clients….” http://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:32017L0593&from=EN

ESMA Guidelines on MiFID II product governance requirements https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/library/esma35-43- 620_guidelines_on_mifid_ii_product_governance_requirements_0.pdf

sections.title: Purpose
sections.content:
MiFID introduced product governance requirements which fall upon both the ‘manufacturer’ of a financial instrument (including structured products) and the ‘distributor’ of a financial instrument (as will be seen below, ‘distributor’ also includes the provision of portfolio management).

‘Product governance’ means the systems and controls firms have in place for the design, approval, marketing and ongoing management of products throughout their lifecycle to ensure they meet legal and regulatory requirements.

Although Alamut does not undertake any MiFID activities, it is important to be aware of these requirements as our products may be distributed by a MiFID firm and consequently may approach Alamut in order to comply with their own MiFID obligations. In addition, PROD 1.3.2 requires non-MiFID firms to take account of these rules as if they were guidance.

Reference should be made to PROD 3 for further information.

sections.title: What is a ‘manufacturer’?
sections.content:
A ‘manufacturer’ is a firm which creates, develops, issues and/or designs investments, including when advising corporate issuers on the launch of new investments.

A firm is expected to comply “in a way that is appropriate and proportionate” with the requirements set out in the Handbook (PROD 3.1.2).
